Across the later stages, once strong cooperative norms and high total contributions are established, they increasingly choose non-contribution, indicating that when they believe others’ cooperation is stable, they see little downside in free-riding. Overall, their strategy is flexible exploitation: cooperate enough to be included and occasionally praised, but defect whenever the group environment appears cooperative and forgiving.

This player is a stable unconditional high contributor. They consistently allocate their full endowment to the public fund, even when others contribute less on average and even when some group members clearly free-ride or under-contribute. There is no evidence that they condition their behavior on others’ past contributions, or that they adjust toward the group average; instead, they maintain a maximal cooperative stance. This suggests a strong internal norm of full cooperation or efficiency-seeking, with low concern for immediate relative payoff and low sensitivity to exploitation risk. They appear willing to accept being among the highest contributors and do not show signs of strategic hedging or risk-averse contribution behavior. Their fairness concept, if any, is not enforced via adjusting contributions downward; they tolerate inequality in contributions while continuing to cooperate. Whether they understand the payoff structure in detail is unknown, but their repeated full contributions indicate either a principled or simple “always contribute everything” rule.

This player is a stable, moderately high contributor who prioritizes cooperation over strict payoff maximization. They consistently give more than the group average but do not maximize contribution. Their behavior suggests a fairness or “do my part” norm: they neither exploit others’ generosity nor sacrifice to the extreme. There is no observable conditional adjustment to others’ behavior; they keep the same contribution level despite variation in others’ contributions and in the pattern of punishment and reward around them. This points to a simple, rule-like strategy (e.g., a fixed notion of a “fair” contribution) rather than fine-grained conditional cooperation. They tolerate some inequality and apparent free-riding by low contributors, indicating either low sensitivity to exploitation, a focus on own stable rule, or a belief that the environment is too short/uncertain to justify complex adaptation. Risk attitude toward the public good is moderate: they give enough to benefit from the multiplier but retain a substantial private portion.

This player is a moderately high to high contributor who moves toward full cooperation when they see generally strong group contributions. They start by giving somewhat less than the emerging group norm, then increase to the cooperative maximum once they observe that many others are also contributing at or near that level. Their behavior is conditionally cooperative rather than purely altruistic: they appear willing to contribute generously when others do the same, and do not exploit the group even when they could gain more by free-riding. They tolerate some variation in others’ contributions, since they continue to give fully even when a few group members contribute noticeably less. They seem willing to accept personal payoff losses relative to low contributors in order to support the public fund, suggesting that short-term payoff maximization is not their only concern. Their risk tolerance with respect to cooperation is moderate: they adjust upward in response to evidence of general cooperativeness, but there is insufficient data to know how they behave in an initially uncooperative or deteriorating group (unknown).

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator in the observed environment. They contribute their entire endowment each time, without adjusting to the fact that others, on average, contribute less. They show no evidence of conditional cooperation or reciprocity; their contribution does not respond to variation in others’ contributions across periods. They act as if maximizing group welfare or adhering to an internal fairness/efficiency norm, despite the low marginal per capita return that makes full contribution individually costly. They tolerate being among the highest contributors without withdrawing, suggesting low sensitivity to relative disadvantage and a willingness to risk being exploited for the sake of maintaining a cooperative standard. Whether this reflects principled altruism, experiment-naïveté, or a fixed heuristic is unknown, but their behavior is consistent with a “set-and-forget” cooperative rule rather than a responsive strategy.

This player is a moderately high contributor who adjusts downward in response to the emerging group norm and payoff information. They start above the minimum cooperative level and close to the group average, indicating some willingness to support the public fund rather than free-ride. They later reduce their contribution while still staying within the broad cooperative band of the group, suggesting conditional cooperation rather than unconditional altruism. Their pattern implies attention to others’ contributions and a desire not to be either a sucker (far above others) or an exploiter (far below others). They appear sensitive to efficiency: the multiplication of the public fund is understood, but they balance it against private payoff, indicating moderate risk tolerance and a pragmatic, payoff-conscious approach rather than purely norm-driven behavior.

This player is a moderately strong contributor who adjusts downward when they see that others are, on average, giving more than them. Their initial contribution level is clearly cooperative and above what a purely self-interested, non-cooperative type would give, but it is not at the very top of the group. After observing that several peers contribute the maximum and that the group as a whole is highly cooperative, they reduce their own contribution rather than matching or increasing it. This pattern suggests conditional cooperation with a self-serving bias: they are willing to support the public good but are comfortable free-riding at the margin when they see others sustaining high contributions. They appear sensitive to relative standing—when they are slightly below the group’s contribution elite, they do not try to “keep up,” indicating weaker fairness or equality norms compared to strong altruists. Their behavior is consistent with someone who values payoff optimization within a cooperative environment more than strict contribution fairness. Risk tolerance is unknown beyond a mild willingness to deviate downward from the emerging high-cooperation norm.

This player is a high, prosocial contributor who stays above group average and close to the maximum feasible level. Contributions are relatively stable across rounds with only a mild downward adjustment, suggesting conditional cooperation rather than blind altruism. They appear to follow a fairness norm where strong contributors—especially those at or near the top—are the reference point. Individuals contributing clearly less than the strong contributors are tolerated rather than sanctioned, indicating a cooperative but non‑moralistic stance. They seem willing to give up substantial private payoff to support the public good despite the relatively low marginal per-capita return, implying low payoff-maximizing selfishness and higher weight on group efficiency and/or norm adherence. Risk tolerance regarding being exploited is moderate: they do not immediately drop to low contributions despite heterogeneity in others’ behavior, but they also do not push to full contribution throughout, suggesting they balance idealism with cautious adjustment.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator within the observed environment. They consistently contribute their entire endowment, despite the marginal per-coin return from the public fund being lower than the private return. This indicates a strong group-efficiency orientation and a willingness to sacrifice personal payoff for collective gain. There is no evidence that they modulate contributions based on others’ average contributions, on prior punishment or reward, or on payoff comparisons; their behavior aligns more with an internal norm (“I should fully cooperate”) than with conditional strategies such as matching or punishing free-riders via reduced contributions. Their risk tolerance appears high with respect to exploitation risk: they continue to maximize contributions even when others’ contributions are somewhat lower and heterogeneous, suggesting they do not require assurance of universal high cooperation to maintain their own. Whether they are driven more by fairness, pure altruism, or a preference for socially efficient outcomes is unknown, but they clearly prioritize the public fund over their own immediate earnings and are behaviorally robust to moderate deviations by others.

This player is a moderately cooperative, slightly below-norm contributor in a generally high-cooperation environment. They consistently contribute a substantial portion of their endowment but stay somewhat below the prevailing group level. Their contributions are stable rather than erratic, suggesting a simple, steady rule rather than sharp conditional responses to specific individuals. They do not appear to chase perfect fairness or exactly match others; instead, they seem comfortable free-riding a little on a cooperative group while still contributing enough to share in the cooperative norm. Risk tolerance regarding others’ cooperation is moderate: they neither defect aggressively nor over-contribute to lead the group.

This player is a predominantly free-riding, opportunistic conditional cooperator who occasionally experiments with full contribution. Across the game, they choose only the extreme options allowed (all or nothing) and end up favoring zero contribution much more often than full contribution. Their behavior indicates they recognize that, at the given multiplier and group size, individually it is always more profitable to keep their endowment when at least one other player contributes. They show some volatility early and mid-game: they alternate between contributing everything and contributing nothing in a way that appears exploratory rather than driven by a stable fairness norm. Full contributions occur both when others are contributing and when others are not, suggesting they test whether their own cooperation can “jump-start” group contributions, but they quickly revert to free-riding when this does not yield good relative payoffs. They respond strongly to payoff incentives: when others contribute and they do not, they obtain high payoffs, and they subsequently repeat the free-riding choice in similar circumstances. When they contribute and the others do not, they incur the worst payoff and are less likely to repeat that pattern in the immediately following round. There is no stable norm of equal sharing or reciprocity; the player appears comfortable benefiting disproportionately from others’ cooperation. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing a few times to risk full contribution even after having been exploited or after seeing that unilateral cooperation is costly, but they do not sustain such risks. Over time, their strategy converges toward exploiting cooperative actions by others while avoiding being exploited themselves. By the end of the game, they act as a near-pure defector, with no final cooperative “gesture” despite the absence of punishment or reward.

This player is a moderately prosocial, strategically flexible contributor who oscillates between high and low inputs rather than following a simple trend. They repeatedly give at or above the group average, including multiple full-endowment contributions, yet occasionally drop to very low levels. These downturns follow phases where cooperation is already reasonably high or where they have just benefited from others, suggesting opportunistic free-riding rather than confusion. They do not appear to follow a strict tit-for-tat rule with any single peer; instead, they condition on the overall contribution climate. When the group as a whole cooperates more, they are generally willing to cooperate as well, but they are not locked into a purely altruistic role and are willing to take advantage of cooperative surroundings. They show some tolerance for efficiency loss: they contribute generously even when it is not individually optimal, implying at least partial concern for group outcomes or social norms rather than pure self-interest. At the same time, they are payoff-aware; after periods of heavy spending or low returns they pull back, indicating a sensitivity to net gains. Overall, they behave like a “conditional cooperator with opportunistic deviations,” balancing social preference and material payoff.

This player is a stable, low, near-constant contributor who free-rides moderately but not maximally. They keep their contribution at a small fixed level most of the time and only make very small adjustments. Their behavior indicates a preference for taking a reliable private payoff over pushing for high group efficiency, even when others frequently contribute much more. They show no strong evidence of conditional cooperation: they neither clearly raise their contribution when group contributions are high nor reduce it when others contribute very little. Instead, they appear to follow a simple rule-of-thumb contribution that they regard as “enough” to avoid being a complete defector while still maximizing personal earnings. There is no sign that they use contribution levels to enforce fairness or to match others’ behavior. Their persistence in this low but nonzero pattern, even in the face of wide variation in others’ contributions, suggests low sensitivity to social norms of strong cooperation and a primary focus on own payoff with mild concern for not looking entirely selfish. Risk tolerance and loss aversion are unknown; however, their unwillingness to experiment with larger contributions, even when high contributions are common, hints at a cautious, payoff-protective stance.

This player is a moderately cooperative, payoff-attentive contributor who experiments and adapts. Contributions stay strictly above zero and span from very low to full endowment. The player starts with low contributions, then tests higher and even maximal giving, and later oscillates between low and medium amounts. This pattern indicates conditional cooperation rather than a fixed strategy: they are willing to increase contributions when the surrounding group appears relatively cooperative, but they do not maintain high contributions when the environment looks mixed or deteriorating. They seem to track others’ average behavior and adjust rather than blindly free-ride or consistently cooperate. They do not adhere to a strict fairness norm of “matching the group average” or “proportional to others”; instead, they move their contribution up or down more coarsely, suggesting a simple heuristic like “when cooperation looks high or rising, try giving more; when it looks low or unstable, pull back.” They display some willingness to take on a large personal cost for the group by occasionally contributing very high amounts, which shows at least moderate prosociality and some tolerance for risk in pursuit of potential group gains. However, they also clearly value their own payoff and are not a pure altruist: in lower-contribution environments they revert to more self-serving contributions. Overall, the player’s contribution behavior fits a “cautious conditional cooperator with occasional exploratory generosity”: they cooperate, but do so in a way that appears sensitive to the recent cooperative climate and to their own payoff returns.

This player is a moderately prosocial but payoff-conscious contributor. They almost always give a positive, mid-range amount, typically near or slightly above the emerging group average. They rarely exploit low-contributing environments by dropping to minimal contributions, but they also do not move to the very highest levels even when many others do so. Their behavior suggests a “conditional cooperator with a floor”: they appear willing to support the public good, but only within a band that maintains their own payoffs. Contribution levels adjust modestly in response to changes in group generosity, but not in a highly volatile or extreme way; they remain in a narrow range rather than oscillating between full free-riding and full cooperation. They show no clear evidence of applying strict fairness or matching rules toward specific others; their pattern fits more with reacting to the general contribution climate and guarding against being too far above it. Risk attitude toward cooperation seems moderate: they repeatedly invest in the public good even when others free-ride, but they do not “push” cooperation aggressively by moving to maximal contributions.

This player is a mostly self-interested, low-to-mid contributor with sporadic higher contributions and no clear conditional cooperation pattern. Their baseline tendency is to keep the endowment, contributing nothing or very little in most rounds. Occasional spikes to moderate or high contributions occur without a stable link to others’ average behavior or to changes in the group’s cooperation level. The player does not appear to follow a fairness norm such as “match the group average” or “reward high contributors by also contributing more.” Instead, contribution choices suggest either experimentation with different levels or opportunistic giving when it feels low-risk, but not a sustained commitment to group welfare. They tolerate exploiting cooperative others: they frequently free-ride while many peers contribute substantial amounts. There is no evidence of strong aversion to benefiting from others’ generosity. Risk tolerance toward the public good appears low: once they see that they can earn solid payoffs with low or zero contributions, they revert to that pattern rather than investing into the common pool to try to shift group behavior.

This player is a highly self-interested, opportunistic contributor who alternates between free-riding and high contributions with no clear cooperative norm. They sometimes contribute nothing despite many others contributing substantially, indicating a willingness to exploit the public fund when others are generous. They also occasionally contribute the maximum or near-maximum, but this does not establish a stable cooperative pattern; instead, these high contributions appear sporadic and not contingent on clear thresholds of others’ behavior. There is no evidence that they systematically match others’ contributions or try to move the group toward an efficient cooperative equilibrium. They show no clear fairness norm: they are comfortable taking a full free-rider position even when others contribute a lot, and they do not consistently continue high contributions after group cooperation improves. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they sometimes make large contributions that leave them relatively low in that round’s payoff, but they also often revert to low or zero contributions to secure high personal earnings when others carry the public fund.

This player is a strict, unwavering full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment, with no variation across the game. Their behavior is non-conditional: cooperation does not appear to depend on others’ behavior, even though others also always fully cooperate. They follow a strong efficiency norm: they consistently choose the welfare-maximizing option rather than the individual payoff–maximizing option. There is no evidence of strategic hedging, experimentation, or risk adjustment; they do not test partial defection, even early or late in the game. This pattern is consistent with a stable “principled cooperator” type rather than a conditional or opportunistic cooperator. Fairness seems defined as equal, full contribution by all, which they both experience and maintain. Whether this norm would hold if others defected is unknown.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator under the given parameters. They always contribute their entire endowment to the public fund, without any experimentation, hedging, or apparent conditionality on others’ behavior. Their strategy is highly consistent and does not change over time, indicating a strong internal norm or fixed rule rather than reactive adjustment. They appear comfortable with the risk that others might free-ride, although in this instance others also fully cooperate, so their beliefs remain untested; their willingness to repeatedly expose themselves to potential exploitation is inferred but unconfirmed (low confidence). They behave as if efficiency and group welfare are the primary objective, and they do not display concerns about relative payoff advantages or fairness imbalances, since they make no effort to exploit or protect themselves against possible deviations by others.

This player is a generally high contributor who anchors around a generous baseline and occasionally adjusts downward. They typically give well above the minimum and often at or near the maximum, suggesting a strong cooperative or efficiency-oriented motive rather than pure payoff maximization. Their contributions become especially high when others also contribute fully, indicating conditional cooperation and a preference for mutual full provision when feasible. When a peer contributes noticeably less than the other, the player sometimes lowers their own contribution, but only moderately, not to the level of the low contributor. This reflects an internal fairness norm: they appear unwilling to exploit the group, but also unwilling to fully ignore repeated under-contribution by a specific other. Contribution levels are relatively stable, with only small downward deviations; they do not escalate into a full defection spiral. Risk tolerance appears moderate: the player is willing to commit high contributions even after observing others under-contribute, but uses punishment (rather than sharp contribution drops) to manage the risk of being exploited. Overall, they fit a “strong conditional cooperator with fairness enforcement” profile rather than an unconditional altruist or a pure self-interest maximizer.

This player is a mostly high, near-unconditional cooperator with one early defection. They typically contribute the full endowment, aligning with the group’s all‑in norm despite the presence of a clear low contributor. The one substantial under‑contribution occurs after initial cooperative rounds and during a period when several others also begin experimenting with lower amounts; there is no explicit justification offered, so the motive is unknown (could be curiosity, mild strategic hedging, or a brief lapse in norm adherence; confidence low). After that, they revert to fully cooperative play and maintain it steadily until the end, including the final round, showing no last‑round exploitation. They do not appear to condition their own contributions directly on punishment dynamics: they keep cooperating even when others focus heavily on sanctioning a deviant. They explicitly state that contributing fully “only makes sense” given the high multiplier, indicating they understand the game’s incentives and see full cooperation as individually rational under these parameters. Overall, they exhibit a “principled cooperator” profile with a single unexplained deviation rather than a pattern of opportunistic free‑riding.

This player is a strict, high-cooperation type with essentially unconditional full contribution. They always give the maximum available amount and never experiment with lower contributions, even after repeated free-riding by another player. Their cooperative behavior is stable and not contingent on short-term reciprocity or on whether others perfectly match their level. They endorse the efficiency norm that everyone should contribute all their coins and explicitly frame “everyone all-in” as the correct and obvious strategy. They care about group payoff and appear comfortable foregoing private-pocket gains when the multiplier makes contributions socially (and individually) efficient. They do not display risk aversion about being exploited; instead, they treat persistent full contribution as the baseline and focus on managing norm violators through talk and punishment rather than by reducing their own contributions.

This player is a strongly pro-cooperative contributor with one notable deviation. They usually contribute the full endowment and actively endorse “all-in” as the group norm, aligning their behavior with that stated norm almost every round. Their single clear under-contribution occurs when the group’s coordination frays and several others also lower contributions; this looks more like a momentary drift with the group than a calculated long-term free ride. Once the group re-establishes near-unanimous high cooperation, they return to full contributions and maintain them through to the end, including the final round, showing little classic end-game defection. They appear motivated by maximizing the group pot and by having a “good group” experience, accepting that their personal best response is to keep contributing even when one player (Sloth) defects. Their fairness norm is inclusive: they pressure the low contributor socially but keep cooperating for the sake of the majority, tolerating some inequality rather than undermining the group outcome.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They always give the full endowment to the public pool in every round, including early, mid, and final rounds, and they never test partial or low contributions. Their behavior indicates that they prioritize group efficiency and seem comfortable taking on the small individual risk that others might free-ride, even after clear evidence of a persistent low contributor. They appear to adopt a norm of “always all-in” and stick to it, rather than condition contributions on others’ behavior. There is no sign that they adjust their giving in response to defection, punishment debates, or end-game proximity. Fairness concerns show up not in reducing their own contributions but in supporting punishment for the repeated low contributor. They tolerate the inequity of being exploited as long as the group pot remains high, suggesting they value collective maximization and consistency more than strict reciprocity at the contribution stage.

This player is a highly cooperative, near “all-in every time” contributor, with one notable deviation. They normally match or exceed the emerging group norm of maximal contribution and clearly endorse the collective “all-in” coordination scheme. Their behavior suggests they internalize a fairness norm that everyone should contribute fully when the multiplier is high. The single low-contribution episode appears as an outlier rather than a strategy shift; there is no accompanying justification in chat, and afterward they revert to full contributions and remain there through the end, including the final round. They tolerate the presence of a low contributor in the group as long as overall cooperation is high, and they do not reduce their own contributions in response to that free-riding; instead, they continue to prioritize group efficiency. Risk-wise, they are willing to rely on others’ cooperation despite clear evidence of at least one persistent defector, indicating relatively high social trust and low fear of being exploited at the contribution stage.

The player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They always give the entire endowment, with no downward adjustment in response to free‑riding by others and no opportunistic end‑game deviation. Their behavior suggests they value group efficiency and are willing to sacrifice individual coins for the public pot, even when others occasionally under‑contribute. They do not appear to condition their contribution on past deductions, rewards, or Sloth’s erratic behavior. Fairness concerns are present in chat (they recognize Sloth “not caring” and see punishment as “pointless”), but these concerns never translate into reducing their own contribution. Risk tolerance is high: they continue to invest fully in the public good despite uncertainty about others and the evident presence of a persistent low contributor.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They always contribute the maximum available amount in every round, without deviation, regardless of others’ behavior, the presence of a persistent low contributor, or the game stage. Their stated and enacted norm is group-efficiency maximization: they argue that everyone should always contribute everything to maximize the total pot and explicitly say they will keep contributing the maximum “no matter what.” They do not appear to condition their own contribution on others’ cooperation, punishment, or fairness considerations; instead, they treat full contribution as the dominant rule given the multiplier. They show relatively high risk tolerance in the social sense: they keep fully cooperating even when others are talking about defecting, punishing, or end-game betrayal. They prioritize expected value from the mechanism over guarding against exploitation or free-riding.

This player is a predominantly self-interested free-rider with one brief, non-sustained cooperative deviation. Their default is to take the entire private endowment and contribute nothing, even when they publicly endorse full investment and when most others are cooperating. This pattern is stable across almost all rounds, indicating a deliberate strategy rather than confusion. They show no evidence of conditional cooperation: others’ high or low contributions do not systematically change their own choice, aside from a single instance of contributing fully that they do not repeat. That single contribution occurs in a context where overall cooperation has already eroded and does not lead to any lasting shift. The move appears either experimental, reactive to group pressure, or momentary, not a change in underlying preference. They tolerate substantial inequity in their favor: they are willing to benefit repeatedly from others’ contributions without reciprocating, despite being fully aware that universal contribution would raise group payoffs. Risk tolerance regarding trust in others’ cooperation seems low; they consistently choose the individually safe payoff of non-contribution. Their behavior is consistent with a payoff-maximizing, low-trust defector who may occasionally test the waters but quickly reverts to free-riding when cooperation seems unstable or when they do not expect universal adherence to “all in.”

This player is a mixed-type, outcome-sensitive contributor who alternates between free-riding and full cooperation rather than following a stable pattern. They only ever choose the two extremes offered (all or nothing) and switch several times over the game. Their behavior suggests they track how much others are putting in and adjust accordingly: when others’ cooperation is high and socially reinforced, they are willing to contribute everything; when there are salient free-riders or when coordination looks fragile, they withdraw to zero and take the high private payoff. They do not appear to hold a strict fairness norm (“I always give if others give”), since they free-ride even in periods where most others are contributing. Their willingness to return to full contributions after previously free-riding indicates some openness to cooperative norms, but this is conditional and unstable. Overall, they balance self-interest and group interest in a pragmatic, opportunistic way, rather than following a principled cooperative or purely selfish strategy.

This player is a mostly high-contribution, somewhat erratic conditional cooperator with occasional free-riding. After an initial defection, they shift quickly toward frequent full contributions and maintain that for most of the game, even when others do not consistently reciprocate. They are willing to invest fully into the public fund when there is at least some social pressure or precedent for cooperation, and they do not appear to systematically match others’ contribution levels. Instead, they often keep contributing fully even when average contributions fall, suggesting a relatively high tolerance for being “used” by partial defectors, or at least a willingness to prioritize group efficiency over strict payoff maximization in many rounds. Their pattern includes a couple of clear free-riding choices embedded within a majority of full-contribution decisions, indicating that they are neither a pure altruist nor a strict egoist. The free-riding choices do not obviously align with a clear trigger (e.g., no visible punishment or explicit retaliation), so their use of defection seems exploratory or opportunistic rather than strongly rule-based. They do not show an obvious strategy of punishing low contributors via their own defection, because they continue contributing fully in multiple situations where some others free-ride. Fairness concerns are not strongly expressed in behavior: they do not systematically reduce contributions in response to others’ low contributions, and they accept payoff losses relative to free-riders. Risk tolerance is moderate to high—contributing fully in a large group with mixed behavior exposes them to coordination risk, but they keep doing so for most of the game.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. Throughout the game, they always invest their entire endowment into the public fund, without reacting negatively when many others free-ride or reduce contributions. They prioritize maximizing group efficiency over their own immediate payoff, even when the marginal per-person return is below 1 and others visibly exploit contributors. There is no sign they condition their contribution on past group behavior, individual histories, chat cues, or end-game proximity. They exhibit low sensitivity to exploitation risk, high tolerance for unfair payoff splits, and no observable attempt to punish low contributors via withholding future contributions. Their internal norm appears to be “cooperate fully regardless of others,” rather than reciprocity-based fairness.

This player is primarily a high contributor with one notable deviation. They repeatedly choose the maximal contribution in most rounds, even when others free ride or partially defect, indicating a strong cooperative inclination and willingness to bear short‑term individual cost for group benefit. Their contributions are not mechanically constant, however: they switch to non‑contribution once, coinciding with a broader collapse in contributions by many others, suggesting a conditional element—when perceived norms of cooperation break down, they briefly follow the emerging low‑contribution norm rather than standing out as a lone cooperator. After that, they return to maximal contributions, even near the end of the game, which shows that the deviation is not a permanent shift to defection, but more of a temporary reaction or experiment. They do not appear to engage in fine‑grained conditionality based on who exactly contributes; they continue to contribute fully even when some specific players persistently free ride. This suggests they operate with a relatively “global” rather than individual‑targeted notion of fairness. Their behavior is consistent with someone who values efficient group outcomes and is prepared to act cooperatively even in the face of imperfect reciprocity, but who is not completely unconditional and is willing to adjust when cooperation seems futile or eroding.

This player is a high, near-unconditional cooperator with mild early opportunism. They quickly align with a group norm of full contribution and then adhere to it consistently, even when others free-ride and when peers explicitly raise the option of reducing contributions. Their initial behavior shows a brief tension between self-interest and group norms: they verbally endorse a full-contribution strategy but under-contribute relative to that stated standard, suggesting some early strategic hedging or experimentation. Once the cooperative norm feels established, they stop deviating and maintain the full-cooperation level without further visible recalibration. Their contributions become highly stable over time, with no clear downward adjustment in response to persistent free-riding or to others’ expressed frustration. This indicates relatively low sensitivity to short-term payoff disparities and a willingness to accept being exploited to preserve a cooperative pattern. They appear to hold a fairness norm that “everyone should give the maximum,” and they track who is persistently below that norm, but they do not themselves retaliate by reducing contributions. Instead, they rely on social pressure and moral judgment rather than direct material response. Risk-wise, they tolerate the risk that others will not reciprocate. Even after clear evidence that some participants consistently free-ride or give very little, they continue to contribute fully. This implies either a principled or identity-based commitment to cooperating, or a belief that sustained cooperation is still the best available response even in a mixed group. Conditionality is present only weakly: they adjust their commentary based on others’ behavior, but not their own contribution levels after the early rounds. In summary, they are a “norm-committed cooperator”: they may momentarily hedge but ultimately choose and stick to a high-cooperation, high-trust strategy despite imperfect reciprocity.

This player is a high, mostly stable contributor who quickly aligns with a cooperative group norm and then largely sticks to it. They explicitly commit in chat to always choosing the maximum contribution and, aside from a single mid-game deviation to a moderate contribution, they consistently follow through on this stated plan. Their behavior indicates a strong willingness to incur an individual cost to support group cooperation, even when many others free-ride and there is no punishment or reward mechanism available. The brief deviation to a lower contribution occurs after they have already observed substantial free-riding from multiple others, suggesting some sensitivity to fairness and reciprocity: they are not mechanically unconditional; they test defection once when cooperation seems unstable. However, they immediately revert to maximal contributions thereafter and maintain this until the final round, indicating that their default preference is to cooperate fully, even in the face of persistent exploitation. They appear to accept the structural risk that they will subsidize others; their sustained high contributions despite ongoing defections show relatively low risk aversion about being the “sucker.” They seem more motivated by maintaining a cooperative identity and supporting the cooperative subgroup than by optimizing narrow self-payoffs in response to others’ behavior. Overall, their contribution strategy can be characterized as “principled cooperator with a brief experiment in partial defection”: they prefer to contribute fully, test deviation once when norms are undermined, and then reaffirm high contributions for the remainder of the game.

This player is a mostly high contributor who intermittently free-rides in a calculated, opportunistic way. They begin by fully endorsing and enacting the “everyone contributes the maximum” norm, and for the majority of the game they stick to max contributions even when others deviate. However, on several occasions they contribute nothing while others are still largely cooperative, capturing very high payoffs. These defections are not random: they occur after norms are established, when the group remains highly cooperative, and when the player sees little personal downside to exploiting that trust. Their behavior indicates conditional cooperation mixed with self-interested opportunism. They appear to value fairness in principle but are willing to violate it when the personal gains are salient and social sanctions are weak (no punishment/reward tools, anonymous context, very small real-money stakes). They explicitly downplay the monetary importance of the game, yet still care enough about “not giving free money” to low contributors to contemplate strategies that avoid subsidizing them. Contribution levels are not stable: they alternate between full cooperation and full defection, with no gradual tapering. This all-or-nothing switching suggests deliberate strategic experimentation or calculated exploitation rather than confusion. They are aware of group-level efficiency (they talk about maximizing collective payout and fairness) but will prioritize individual payoff when they believe the system allows costless free-riding, especially when they feel the “bad actors” are fixed and unavoidable. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to free-ride even while being publicly visible as a defector, suggesting low concern about social judgment in this anonymous lab setting. At the same time, they often revert to full contributions, which may reflect caution about possible future punishment mechanisms or norms turning against them. Overall, their type is “principled conditional cooperator with opportunistic cheating when enforcement is weak and stakes feel trivial.”

This player is a strict, unconditional full cooperator in this environment. They always contribute the entire endowment and never adjust downward despite repeated evidence of free-riding and shifting group norms. Their behavior is extremely stable and shows no volatility or experimentation. Their choices indicate that they prioritize the cooperative norm and group efficiency over individual payoff maximization. They continue to cooperate even when influential group members openly question whether continued cooperation is wise, and even when exploitation by low contributors is explicitly recognized. This suggests they either do not conditionalize their cooperation on others’ behavior or they assign very high intrinsic value to maintaining the cooperative rule once it is established. They appear comfortable accepting lower personal returns to sustain a norm they see as “right,” implying a fairness or principle-based motivation rather than a purely outcome-based or risk-averse strategy. They do not show evidence of strategic hedging, retaliation, or “testing” others by varying contributions. If they update beliefs about others’ trustworthiness, this does not translate into behavioral change. Overall, they behave as a rule-following, high-commitment cooperator.

This player is a strongly pro-cooperative, near-conditional cooperator with one early exploratory defection. Their default is to contribute the full endowment, and they do so in almost every round. The single partial contribution occurs after it becomes clear that some group members repeatedly free-ride despite a verbal agreement to fully contribute. In that instance, they briefly test a more self-interested choice, then immediately return to full contributions thereafter. They hold a clear fairness norm favoring universal full contribution and are willing to personally sacrifice payoffs to uphold that norm, even when others continue to free-ride. Their behavior suggests they prioritize group efficiency and norm-consistency over strict payoff maximization. Once they recommit to full contributions, their pattern is stable and not volatile: they do not escalate defection in response to others’ selfishness, nor do they “punish” defectors by withholding their own contributions later. They appear to tolerate the risk of being exploited by a minority of low contributors in order to sustain cooperation with the majority. They accept that some players will not follow the cooperative agreement but still choose to contribute fully, indicating relatively high risk tolerance for being taken advantage of, combined with a preference for moral or consistency-based payoffs over narrow monetary gain. Their strategy is “cooperate almost unconditionally, with minimal experimentation,” rather than “strictly reciprocate others’ average behavior.”

This player is best described as a “principled cooperator with one early defection.” Their default pattern is to contribute the maximum possible amount and to keep doing so even when others free‑ride and even when peers openly debate withdrawing contributions. Across the game, their behavior is highly stable: they almost always pick the full contribution and do not gradually decay toward selfishness as the game progresses. The only clear deviation is one early low contribution that conflicts with their simultaneous verbal statement of intending to keep contributing fully. After that, they revert immediately to full contributions and remain there. Because this low contribution is isolated and not repeated despite clear opportunities to exploit others later, it looks less like a strategic pivot and more like either experimentation, a one‑off opportunistic slip, or an input error. Motivation for that anomaly is unknown. They demonstrate strong adherence to an equal‑contribution fairness norm. They align themselves with the group that wants everyone to put in the maximum, and they continue contributing fully even when it is financially disadvantageous relative to free‑riding. This suggests they are willing to trade off some personal payoff to sustain a cooperative norm or to remain consistent with their stated intentions. Their conditionality is limited: they do see that some players are not cooperating, but they do not respond by lowering their own contributions in a systematic tit‑for‑tat way. They are not strongly risk‑averse about being exploited; instead, they act as a “cooperative anchor,” holding to high contributions despite exploitation risk and actual exploitation. They may be motivated by a mix of norm compliance, preference for group efficiency, and a desire to maintain a pro‑social self‑image, but exact internal motives are unknown. Overall, their strategy is simple and robust: contribute fully, signal willingness to do so, and persist with that plan even under moderate social and material pressure to defect. They do not appear to actively hedge against others’ selfishness or to fine‑tune contributions based on round‑to‑round behavior by others.

The player is best described as an “ideological high contributor with one early defection.” Their default is to contribute the maximum possible amount and to stick with that almost unwaveringly. Across the game they consistently choose the cooperative, high-contribution option even when others free-ride and even when peers explicitly discuss defecting in response to low contributors. They show one early deviation from their stated intention: after publicly committing to keep contributing the maximum, they briefly contribute less than that. This suggests some willingness to experiment with self-serving behavior or to test whether others notice, but they quickly revert to full cooperation and then maintain it. After that, their behavior is stable and not volatile. Their contribution rule appears primarily norm- and efficiency-based rather than purely payoff-maximizing in the narrow sense. They repeatedly insist that contributing the maximum “makes more” for everyone, indicating they understand and care about joint surplus. At the same time, they show frustration with free-riders and briefly question what the group should do, but they do not follow through on retaliatory under-contribution. This suggests a conditional-cooperator mindset in rhetoric, but a near unconditional-cooperator pattern in action. They display strong fairness norms: they condemn low contributors and describe them in negative moral or intellectual terms, framing low contribution as both unfair and irrational. They seem risk-tolerant with respect to exploitation: they keep sending high contributions despite clear evidence that some players are persistently free-riding and despite discussion that some might never change. They appear more motivated by upholding a principled cooperative stance and maximizing possible earnings than by protecting themselves against being taken advantage of. Overall, the player’s strategy in contribution choices is: aim for full cooperation, push for a group norm of maximal contributions, and personally uphold that norm even when others defect. Punishment or reward mechanisms are not used in this game, but from their rhetoric, they likely support sanctioning low contributors if it were available.

This player is a persistent, unapologetic free-rider with occasional, short-lived bursts of cooperation that do not survive negative feedback. Their default choice is to contribute nothing, even when others contribute high amounts and even when this clearly increases group payoffs. They sometimes contribute moderately or fully, but these instances do not lead to a stable cooperative pattern; instead, they quickly revert to zero contributions after being targeted with punishment. Their behavior suggests that personal payoff maximization dominates any fairness or reciprocity considerations: when not punished, they prefer taking the entire endowment and benefiting from others’ contributions. They do not condition their contributions positively on others’ cooperation; even sustained and substantial contributions from others fail to elicit consistent co-contribution. When they do contribute a lot, they accept that others are contributing less and do not appear to require equal matching, showing little concern for outcome equality. They show no sign of “strategic cooperation” to build a reputation, despite identifiable punishment and reward, indicating low concern for being perceived as fair or pro-social. Overall, they behave as a payoff-seeking, low-conditional cooperator who only sporadically deviates from pure free-riding and does not systematically learn from social sanctions.

This player is a highly volatile, opportunistic contributor who alternates between extreme free-riding and near-full cooperation. They do not follow a stable norm of “always high” or “always low” giving. Instead, they appear to treat contribution choices as a flexible lever for individual gain under changing social conditions. They sometimes match or exceed the group’s high-cooperation standard by contributing almost their full endowment, but at other times they contribute almost nothing despite others continuing to give generously. These shifts do not map cleanly onto obvious retaliation or gratitude patterns; instead, they suggest an experimental or exploitative strategy: test how much they can earn by free-riding when others are cooperative, and switch back to high contributions when that also remains profitable or socially tolerated. There is no strong evidence of a fairness norm such as “match the group average” or “match minimum contributors.” The player occasionally makes very low contributions even when everyone else contributes very high amounts, indicating low intrinsic aversion to advantageous inequality. At the same time, they are not a pure selfish optimizer, because they also choose full or near-full contributions in many rounds when free-riding would still be profitable. This mix points to a flexible, payoff-minded type that is willing both to cooperate and to exploit cooperation, rather than a principled cooperator or a consistent defector. Risk tolerance appears moderate to high: they repeatedly free-ride in a group where others heavily punish low contributors, absorbing substantial punishment deductions yet still returning to similar low-contribution behavior later. This implies either low sensitivity to social sanctions or a deliberate willingness to gamble on others’ continued generosity while accepting punishment as a cost of that strategy.

This player is a volatile, strategically experimental contributor who mixes high contributions with occasional full free-riding. They clearly understand that group cooperation is valuable (they frequently contribute at or near the maximum, especially when others are cooperating strongly), but they do not commit to a stable norm. Instead, they alternate between joining the cooperative majority and exploiting it. They condition their behavior on others’ contributions: when peers are almost all high contributors, they sometimes match or exceed the group, and other times they drop to zero and ride on others’ efforts. They do not systematically “punish” low contributors through their own contributions; they continue to cooperate alongside obvious low contributors at several points, and sometimes reward them. This suggests that fairness norms (in the sense of strict contribution matching) are weak or secondary. Risk-wise, they tolerate large swings in their own payoff to test different strategies. They accept substantial payoff losses in some rounds (via free-riding plus becoming a target of punishment, or via heavy punishment spending in cooperative rounds) rather than maximizing steady earnings. This indicates a higher weight on expressive or strategic motives than on simple payoff security. Overall, their type is: a high-variance, payoff-sacrificing experimenter who alternates between cooperative and exploitative roles and seems comfortable deviating from both pure selfishness and pure fairness.

This player is a near-unconditional full cooperator for almost the entire game. They consistently give their entire endowment to the public fund, apparently treating maximum contribution as the appropriate norm regardless of others’ average behavior. Their cooperation is highly stable, not reactive to short-term changes in group contributions, punishment patterns, or their own payoffs. The only deviation is a single late-round defection where they contribute nothing while most others still contribute substantially; this appears tied to a desire to punish a chronic low contributor without further “subsidizing” them. Outside of that exception, they prioritize social efficiency over individual earnings and tolerate free-riding while continuing to contribute. Their behavior suggests a strong fairness and contribution norm—those who give much are acceptable, those who give markedly less are norm violators—but they enforce that norm primarily with punishment/reward rather than by adjusting their own contributions. Risk tolerance for being exploited is high; they accept repeat exposure to low contributors without reducing their own giving, and only once briefly switch to non-cooperation in a context of very salient frustration with a particular target.

This player is a generally high contributor who rarely free-rides and usually stays at or above the group’s modal behavior. They alternate mostly between medium-high and full contributions rather than extremes, suggesting a preference for cooperation combined with some self-regarding caution. Contributions are relatively stable and do not collapse over time; even after being penalized and observing erratic behaviors by others (very low contributions, heavy punishment/reward spending), they continue to give substantially to the public fund. They occasionally contribute less than most others, but they never exploit sharply cooperative phases by dropping to zero or very low levels. This pattern indicates a cooperative, efficiency-oriented type who is nonetheless not rigidly altruistic and is willing to keep some margin for themselves. There is no clear conditional tit-for-tat pattern tied to any single peer’s behavior; instead they seem to adopt a “default high” cooperative rule and only adjust modestly upward or downward, perhaps in response to aggregate noise or perceived instability. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they keep contributing even when the social environment becomes volatile (intense punishment storms, very low contributions by some), implying some willingness to risk being exploited for the sake of maintaining cooperation, but they do not always choose the maximum contribution when the group is uniformly cooperative.

The player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They always choose the maximal contribution available and never deviate, even when free-riding clearly pays more and persists across many rounds. Their behavior reflects a strong internal norm that “everyone should contribute fully,” and they continue to follow this norm even when that norm is not fully met by others. They express moral disapproval of low contributors and label them as “greedy,” indicating a fairness-based rather than purely outcome-based standard. They do not appear to condition their own contributions on others’ past behavior; instead, they maintain full cooperation across the entire interaction, including in late rounds when they are aware that the game might be long but finite. They seem risk-tolerant with respect to exploitation: they keep investing in the public good even while recognizing that some players systematically gain higher payoffs by contributing less.

This player is a near-pure high contributor with one deliberate deviation. They almost always give the full endowment, even when free-riding by others is salient and repeatedly discussed. This suggests a strong cooperative norm or a working assumption that “everyone should” contribute fully. Their contributions remain stable across early, middle, and late phases, so they do not visibly adopt endgame defection or conditional cooperation in response to others’ behavior. They do, however, switch to a one-shot free-ride in a single instance, coinciding with another known low contributor. This looks more like a brief test of defecting or a payoff-maximizing probe than a full strategy shift; they immediately revert to full contributions thereafter. There is no direct communication around this switch, so the underlying motive is unknown, but the isolated nature implies low volatility overall. They show no sign of explicit conditionality on specific individuals’ actions (e.g., they keep contributing fully even when some peers are repeatedly called out as low contributors). Their behavior is consistent with a “cooperative but not punitive” type: they uphold a fairness norm for their own play but do not use their contribution level as a tool to sanction others. Risk-wise, they tolerate being exploited by defectors rather than protecting themselves with sustained defection, which points to relatively high social-risk tolerance and low concern for short-term payoff dominance.

This player fits an “inconsistent conditional cooperator / opportunistic experimenter” type. They are willing to make full contributions often, but they do not lock into a stable norm. Instead, they alternate between fully cooperating and fully free‑riding without announcing or justifying the switches. They show that they understand and accept the cooperative norm early: they repeatedly choose the cooperative option even when a small subset of others free‑rides, and they explicitly align with chat messages that call non‑contributors “greedy” and advocate full contributions. At the same time, they sometimes choose to free‑ride while most others are fully contributing, indicating that maximizing own payoff is a live motive. Their pattern is volatile rather than smoothly adaptive: they do not gradually reduce contributions in response to others; rather, they toggle between extremes. The alternations do not clearly track any single partner’s behavior, and there is no explicit statement that they are “testing” the group, so any hypothesis that they are systematically probing responses is low confidence. They attend to group fairness norms in language, but they do not consistently obey those norms in their own choices. They are willing to benefit from others’ cooperation after positioning themselves verbally as a cooperator. Overall, they combine a genuine willingness to contribute with a readiness to exploit cooperative environments intermittently.

This player is a persistent low contributor and opportunistic free-rider. Their default is to give nothing, with occasional token amounts that remain far below the group’s main contributors. They contribute the full endowment only once, and even that appears more like a one-off experiment or joke than a shift in norm. Their pattern is highly asymmetric: they repeatedly benefit from others’ high contributions while withholding their own. When others rally around contributing everything, they mostly continue to give little or nothing, even after explicitly stating they will “give 20 a try and see who goes in,” and then not following through. This shows low adherence to announced cooperative plans and little internalized fairness norm toward matching others’ efforts. Their behavior is weakly conditional: they do sometimes raise their contribution slightly when others are earnestly trying to coordinate on full contributions, but their increases are minor and inconsistent, and they quickly revert to zero after any cooperative gesture. They do not reciprocate high contributions in any stable way. Their strategy emphasizes maximizing personal payoff rather than group efficiency, and they tolerate visible inequity in which others consistently earn less than they would if they also free-rode. Risk tolerance appears low in the social sense: they do not “risk” sacrificing coins to build a cooperative norm, even when repeated verbal commitments from others make high cooperation plausible. Instead, they exploit the presence of high contributors, indicating comfort with social risk (being seen as selfish) in exchange for monetary security.

The player is a predominantly exploitative low contributor who occasionally experiments with higher contributions when it seems safe or socially convenient. Across the game, they most often choose zero, interspersed with a few isolated high or medium contributions. Their default is to free‑ride on others’ cooperation rather than reciprocate it. Their contribution pattern is not stable but also not truly conditional in a responsive, tit‑for‑tat sense. They do not systematically reward others’ cooperation with more giving or punish defection by cutting back; instead they oscillate between full free‑riding and sporadic “bursts” of high giving that appear more like tests, mood shifts, or attempts to appear cooperative than sustained strategy. After experimenting with full cooperation several times, they quickly revert back to zero despite others continuing to contribute, indicating that maximizing own payoff dominates any fairness or efficiency concerns. They show low adherence to fairness norms. They openly endorse the logic of full contribution as the socially optimal choice but do not behave in line with that norm. They appear comfortable benefiting from others’ high contributions without matching them, and there is no evidence of guilt, apology, or compensatory behavior when called out indirectly. Risk tolerance appears asymmetric. They are willing to “risk” a full contribution a few times when many others are already contributing high amounts and are vocally committed to doing so, suggesting comfort with risk when it is strongly socially scaffolded. However, they are not willing to sustain that risk when free‑riding is clearly profitable, even once a cooperative cluster exists. This points to a payoff‑maximizing, opportunistic orientation rather than a trust‑based or norm‑driven one. Overall, their type is best characterized as a strategic free‑rider who occasionally donates to maintain some ambiguity about their motives or to see if cooperation can be exploited more profitably, but who reliably reverts to self‑interested minimal contribution once they see that others will continue to give.

This player is initially a strong unconditional cooperator at the verbal level and for a long stretch at the behavioral level. They repeatedly give the full endowment, even when facing obvious free-riding and voicing frustration. Their contributions are high and relatively stable through most of the game; deviations toward self-interested play occur only after prolonged exposure to perceived unfairness and near the end of the game. They show a clear fairness norm: they explicitly object to “freeloaders” and describe non-contributors in moralized terms. However, they do not quickly retaliate; instead, they keep contributing fully through many rounds after expressing anger, which suggests a tension between a principled cooperative identity and a growing sense of being exploited. When they finally reduce contributions, they do it sharply (contributing nothing in a few late rounds) and in ways that are strategically advantageous to themselves given the group’s behavior. Cooperation is conditional in a nuanced way. They say at one point that they will contribute everything regardless of what others do, yet later condition their willingness to continue on whether others stop free-riding. In practice, they tolerate quite a lot of defection before changing behavior, but eventually adjust downward when they feel their generosity is only rewarding selfish agents. Near the final rounds they are willing to free-ride themselves, especially when they infer that high contributors and non-contributors are locked in a pattern that they cannot fix and when the end of the game limits the future value of a cooperative reputation. Overall, they are a “frustrated high-cooperator”: strongly inclined to maximize the social surplus, but with a threshold of tolerance beyond which they are willing to protect their own payoff and even exploit others’ residual cooperation. Risk tolerance around trust is moderately high early on—they risk being exploited by continuing to contribute fully despite clear evidence of free-riding—but decreases once they conclude that norms will not improve.

This player fits a “quiet, stable free-rider with minimal cover” type. They almost always contribute the smallest nonzero amount that plausibly avoids standing out, with occasional zero contributions. Their contributions are low and remarkably stable over time; they do not show meaningful upward adjustment even when others repeatedly argue for full cooperation and many players do contribute high amounts. A single mid-game deviation to a larger contribution is isolated and not followed up; it does not signal a real strategy shift. Their behavior is strongly conditional on others in an exploitative way: when many others cooperate heavily, they maintain or even drop to very low contributions, thereby reaping high personal payoffs. They do not reciprocate generosity, even when cooperation among others becomes quite strong and repeated. They also do not appear to punish defectors by increasing their own contribution when others drop; instead, they simply continue their low contributions regardless. They do not express fairness norms or concern for equality in the transcript. Their pattern indicates that they prioritize personal payoff over group efficiency, even when the group clearly tries to coordinate toward efficient, high-contribution outcomes. They tolerate free-riding on highly cooperative others and appear comfortable maintaining that position despite explicit social pressure against it. Risk tolerance appears low with respect to cooperating: they show little willingness to “take the risk” of matching high contributors, even after others demonstrate repeated prosocial behavior. However, they show comfort with social risk (being suspected as a “bot” or freeloader) as long as it does not lead to material consequences, which in this setup it does not. Overall, they exhibit a stable, payoff-maximizing free-rider strategy, with modest cosmetic variation (oscillating between 0 and a token 1) rather than true conditional cooperation.

The player is a persistent free-rider: they consistently take the entire private payoff and never contribute to the public fund, despite others repeatedly contributing the maximum. This behavior is stable over all observed decisions; there is no sign of experimentation, conditional cooperation, or adjustment in response to others’ high or changing contributions. They do not appear to follow an equality or reciprocity norm regarding contributions; they accept being the unique or one of very few non-contributors among mostly full contributors without visibly correcting or softening that stance. They appear comfortable taking on the role of someone who benefits from the group without directly adding to the common pool, even when the environment is highly favorable to collective efficiency and others are behaving cooperatively. Their risk tolerance in contribution decisions is low toward social risk (they do not “risk” contributing to match the group), and high toward reputational or moral risk (they tolerate being the free-rider when everyone can see contributions). There is no evidence that they condition their own contribution on rewards received or on others’ payoffs; contribution choices seem decoupled from the social feedback structure.

This player is a strict, stable full cooperator under these payoff conditions. They always contribute their entire endowment, regardless of others’ behavior or prior free-riding. Their cooperation is unconditional across the observed history: they do not reduce contributions in response to repeated zero contributions by some group members, nor do they escalate contributions because of rewards (since they are already at the maximum allowed). This suggests they treat full contribution as a fixed rule or norm rather than a contingent strategy. They appear risk-tolerant with respect to exploitation: they continue to cooperate even when others persistently free-ride. There is no evidence that they adjust contributions based on fairness or proportionality; instead, they maintain a principled high-cooperation stance and seem willing to accept unequal payoffs.

This player is a high, mostly stable contributor who treats near-full contribution as the default. They repeatedly give their entire endowment to the public pool, deviating only once to a moderate contribution. That single reduction coincides with a general dip in group contributions rather than being a unilateral move, suggesting conditional cooperation: they are willing to bear high costs for the group as long as others broadly do the same, but they do not rigidly insist on full altruism when the group environment worsens. They appear to adopt a norm that “good play” is to contribute at or near the maximum. They do not mirror free riders or low contributors; instead, they stay at the cooperative end of the spectrum even when some others repeatedly contribute little or nothing. This indicates a relatively high tolerance for exploitation, at least in the short run. The brief mid-game reduction in their own contribution looks more like a cautious test of how low they can go while maintaining reasonable group returns, not a shift to selfishness. They do not systematically fine-tune their contribution level in response to marginal payoff changes, which implies they are not optimizing narrowly for personal earnings. Instead, they behave as if maintaining a cooperative group norm is more important than small gains from free riding. There is no clear evidence of risk aversion or risk seeking beyond this: they accept the “risk” that others may under-contribute, and still keep contributing highly across the game. They likely hold a fairness belief that high contributors, themselves included, “ought” to support the common good, but they do not attempt to equalize payoffs through their own contribution choices. Overall, they fit the profile of a conditional, norm-driven cooperator with a strong bias toward continuing to cooperate even when others’ behavior is mixed.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They consistently allocate their full endowment to the public good, showing no adjustment in response to changes in others’ behavior, average contributions, or the presence/absence of rewards to them. They tolerate free-riding and moderate contributions from others without reducing their own level, suggesting either a strong internal norm of “always cooperate” or a deliberate choice to ignore short-term payoff optimization. Their behavior fits a high-idealism / low-conditionality type rather than a conditional cooperator: they do not appear to condition on others’ contributions or on being rewarded. They exhibit high social-risk tolerance: they keep contributing fully even when many others clearly deviate downward, and they do not hedge against possible exploitation. The pattern is consistent with a “principled cooperator” who values maintaining contribution norms for their own sake, or believes that stable full cooperation is best for the group and behaves accordingly regardless of strategic incentives.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They always give the full endowment to the public pool and do not adjust their contribution in response to fluctuations in others’ behavior, overall group contributions, or their own payoffs. They behave as a committed cooperator who prioritizes group efficiency over individual payoff maximization, even when others contribute much less or when low contributors benefit disproportionately. There is no visible evidence that they condition their contribution on fairness, reciprocity, or marginal per capita return; instead, they maintain full cooperation regardless of apparent free-riding or the presence/absence of rewards. This suggests a strong internal norm or preference for full cooperation and a high tolerance for payoff risk and exploitation. They do not appear to experiment with lower contributions, so their strategy is low-volatility and rule-like rather than exploratory.

This player is a reliably high contributor who rarely drops below the apparent group norm and often matches or exceeds it. They do not play as a strict unconditional cooperator, but they clearly favor cooperative provision of the public good over free-riding. Their contribution pattern shows moderate adjustment rather than volatility: they range within the upper half of the possible contribution spectrum, sometimes stepping up to the maximum and sometimes moderating downward when group contributions or dynamics look less stable. They appears to condition their behavior on others’ cooperation in a “soft conditional cooperator” style: they maintain relatively high contributions even when some group members contribute very little, but they do not lock in at the maximum. When the environment looks more cooperative, they are willing to increase their own stake; when cooperativeness is patchier, they still contribute generously but not at the top level. They seems to hold a fairness or reciprocity norm that “most should give a lot,” but they do not insist on strict equality or punish low contributors through their own contributions. Instead, they continue to help sustain the public good, indicating tolerance for some free-riding and a longer-term or group-focused view. There is no sign they are strongly risk-averse: they consistently place a substantial share of their endowment into a mechanism where returns depend on others, suggesting comfort with social risk when it supports cooperation.

This player is a moderately prosocial but self-protective contributor whose behavior oscillates between full cooperation and calculated free-riding. They are willing to contribute their entire endowment early on, and they repeatedly match or exceed the group’s typical contribution level. Over time, however, they adjust downward in a way that suggests conditional cooperation: they do not lock into a single level, but instead vary their contributions between low, medium, and high depending on recent group behavior and apparent norms. They do not insist on strict equality or “perfect fairness” in each period; they tolerate the presence of several persistent low contributors without clearly retaliating through drastic under-contribution. At the same time, they show no commitment to unconditional altruism: when contributing less still yields them high payoffs, they take advantage of that, indicating some comfort with opportunistic free-riding when the environment allows it. Overall, their risk tolerance around cooperation appears moderate. They initially accept the risk of being exploited by contributing fully in a large group without prior evidence, which implies some trust or optimism about others. But as the game unfolds and heterogeneity in contributions remains, they occasionally scale back, protecting their own payoffs while still keeping contributions at or above many others’ levels in most periods. The pattern fits a “pragmatic conditional cooperator”: they like cooperation and are willing to support it, but they are not willing to consistently sacrifice large amounts if the group is mixed.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator within the observed environment. They always contribute their entire endowment, regardless of others’ behavior, including when several peers free ride. There is no sign of conditional cooperation, downward adjustment, or experimentation with defection. This suggests a strong internal norm for “doing the socially efficient thing” that is not contingent on short‑term payoff optimization. They tolerate exploitation risk: they continue to cooperate even when multiple others give nothing, indicating either a principled commitment to cooperation, optimism that group norms will stabilize at high contribution, or both. They appear comfortable relying on the high marginal per capita return to justify full provision, and there is no evidence that they track fairness in a fine‑grained way beyond distinguishing clear contributors from repeat non‑contributors. Overall, they fit a “principled high cooperator” type rather than a payoff‑maximizing conditional strategist.

This player is a strict, stable full contributor. They always contribute the maximum allowed amount and never deviate, regardless of how others behave. Their contribution pattern does not react to fluctuations in group cooperation or to the presence of free riders. This indicates a strong internal norm or principle favoring full cooperation rather than a responsive, conditional strategy. They appear willing to accept short-term personal losses from others’ low contributions in order to maintain their own cooperative standard. Their behavior is consistent with a norm-driven or rule-following cooperator rather than a payoff-maximizing conditional cooperator. Risk tolerance regarding potential exploitation seems high: they continue to cooperate even when some others contribute nothing, suggesting they do not require assurance of reciprocity to uphold their contribution level. Their fairness norm is one-sided: they behave fairly toward the group but do not try to enforce fairness through adjusting their own contribution. They show no volatility and no evidence of experimentation with alternative contribution levels.

The player is a high, near-unconditional contributor who generally favors full cooperation. They repeatedly choose the cooperative option even when others partially free‑ride and when they themselves could profit more by withholding. Their contribution pattern is very stable, with only brief deviations from full cooperation.
