Those deviations do not appear to be clear responses to deteriorating group behavior or to direct incentives, so conditional cooperation (matching others) seems weak at best. Fairness norms favor “everyone should contribute,” but the player does not consistently adjust their own behavior to punish low contributors via defection; instead, they keep contributing and rely on the punishment mechanism to address norm violations. Risk tolerance regarding being exploited is relatively high: they continue to contribute cooperatively even after observing multiple noncontributors. Their strategy suggests they value maintaining or enforcing a cooperative norm more than strictly maximizing short‑run earnings from free‑riding.

This player is a primarily cooperative, near-conditional cooperator with a late-game opportunistic deviation. They usually choose the fully cooperative option when they believe others are also contributing at high levels, even when some mild free-riding is present. Their contributions are mostly stable and high, indicating that they value group efficiency and are willing to sacrifice private payoff to support it. They do not react sharply to occasional low contributors; isolated defections do not immediately change their own behavior. However, once full or near-full cooperation appears to be well established, they briefly exploit the cooperative environment with a single defection, then return to contributing. This suggests a mix of pro-social preference and payoff sensitivity, with some willingness to free-ride once the public good seems “safe.” Their risk tolerance for cooperating while others defect is moderate: they continue to contribute despite repeated under-contributions by a subset of players, so they do not require perfect fairness to maintain cooperation. Their fairness norm appears to be “mostly everyone should contribute; a few defectors are tolerable, but persistent zero contributors are norm violators.” They do not fully unravel into non-cooperation, implying they either do not strongly expect end-game collapse or they prioritize cooperative identity over strict profit maximization.

This player is a mostly stable, high contributor who shows a brief, strategic test of free-riding early on and then commits to cooperation. They use the full endowment or nothing, never intermediate amounts, and overwhelmingly choose to contribute fully. The single deviation to non-contribution appears exploratory or payoff-driven rather than the start of persistent defection: they immediately return to full contributions and then maintain that pattern despite later opportunities to free-ride on high group contributions. They tolerate the presence of some freeriders without adjusting downward, suggesting relatively strong internal or efficiency-based norms favoring full contribution. Their behavior indicates they recognize that the marginal per-capita return on contributions is above 1 and are comfortable foregoing short-term private gains to support a high group payoff. There is no clear evidence of conditional cooperation tied tightly to specific others’ behavior; instead, they remain cooperative even when some players defect, and they do not “reward” high-contributing periods with extra generosity since they are already at the contribution ceiling. Overall, they fit a “principled or efficiency-minded cooperator with brief early experimentation” profile, with moderate risk tolerance and low volatility once a strategy is chosen.

This player is a mostly high contributor with occasional, calculated free-riding. They reliably give the full endowment in the large majority of rounds, even after observing others defect earlier or intermittently. Their deviations from full contribution are infrequent and appear opportunistic rather than retaliatory: when they keep their entire endowment, they do so without any accompanying attempt to sanction or persuade, and they revert back to full contribution soon after, even if others continue to partially defect. They do not systematically match others’ lowest contributions; instead, they tolerate some degree of free-riding by others while personally maintaining a high-cooperation standard. Their pattern suggests they value the cooperative outcome and are willing to bear the local cost of contributing, but are not morally rigid—occasional self-serving deviations coexist with an overall cooperative strategy. They do not chase fairness aggressively: they keep contributing fully even when another player repeatedly contributes nothing and earns more. This indicates relatively low inequality aversion and a willingness to accept being exploited if the group outcome remains reasonably good. Their risk tolerance appears moderate: they do not test extreme strategies or escalate defection; instead, they oscillate between full cooperation and isolated free-riding episodes, then quickly return to cooperation once the environment looks stable again.

This player is a stable, unconditional free-rider. They consistently keep their entire endowment in every round, never experimenting with contributing, even when the rest of the group repeatedly contributes fully and even after explicit explanations that full contribution benefits everyone. Their behavior suggests a clear preference for maximizing private payoff under the assumption that others will (or should) continue to contribute. There is no evidence of concern for fairness: they repeatedly exploit a highly cooperative group without adjustment when their non-cooperation becomes salient. Risk tolerance around contribution is unknown because they never deviate from the safest self-interested choice. They appear insensitive to social pressure, group norms, or reciprocity expectations regarding contribution.

This player is a “conditional learner-cooperator with slow start.” They initially free-ride when the environment is still uncertain, then rapidly shift to full cooperation and remain stable once they infer that high contributions are the emerging norm. After the first decision, their pattern is simple and consistent: they always contribute fully when they expect most others to do the same. They appear sensitive to both group behavior and peer messaging. When others demonstrate strong cooperation and at least one player explicitly explains that contributing yields higher returns, this player aligns with that advice and does not revert to defection later. They do not appear to enforce strict reciprocity on individual defectors; they keep contributing fully even while a few others persistently contribute nothing. This suggests that their cooperation is guided more by a desire to follow the apparent group strategy and gain from the public good than by a personal fairness rule that punishes every non-cooperator. Risk tolerance is moderate: they are initially cautious (keeping their full endowment) until they see strong cooperative signals, then they are willing to “risk” full contributions for the remainder of the game. Across the game, they show low volatility after the first adjustment and do not experiment further, consistent with a satisficing strategy: once convinced that “everyone giving” pays, they stick to it.

This player is a highly stable, unconditional cooperator under the presented options. When the choice set is binary between full contribution and none, they consistently choose full contribution in every opportunity, regardless of others’ previous behavior. They show no evidence of reactive defection in response to group under-contribution, and instead appear committed to the efficiency logic of full cooperation. Their chat content indicates they understand that contributing maximizes group and individual returns given the multiplier, and they emphasize that everyone “gets more” if all contribute. They display a fairness/efficiency norm: they seem to expect that everyone should contribute fully, but when some do not, they still maintain their own high contribution rather than punishing by withholding. This suggests relatively high risk tolerance for being exploited in the short term, anchored in an expectation that persuasion and rewards can move others toward full cooperation rather than needing to protect themselves by free-riding.

The player is a pure free‑rider. They always keep their entire endowment and never contribute to the public fund, despite being in a relatively high-return environment for cooperation. This pattern is completely stable; there is no experimentation with contributing, no conditional reciprocity, and no apparent response to fluctuations in others’ contribution levels. They tolerate receiving substantial benefits from others’ cooperation while remaining uncooperative themselves, indicating weak or absent fairness concerns toward the group. Risk tolerance regarding payoff variance appears low: they choose the individually safe, dominant strategy of zero contribution rather than seeking higher joint payoffs through mutual cooperation.

This player is a perfectly stable full cooperator: they always contribute their entire endowment, with no experimentation or decay across the game. Their behavior is consistent with someone who treats full contribution as the default, not as a conditional response to others’ behavior. They continue to fully cooperate even when many others free‑ride, and even when they themselves are punished. There is no sign that they are sensitive to short‑term payoff risk or to immediate exploitation; they appear payoff‑insensitive relative to standard self‑interest, instead following a fixed cooperative norm. Whether they think others “should” always contribute is not explicit, but their persistence in full cooperation in both high- and low-contribution environments suggests a strong internalized fairness or efficiency norm that they follow regardless of others’ choices.

This player is a highly cooperative, near-unconditional contributor who treats full contribution as the default norm. They repeatedly give their entire endowment to the public fund across almost all rounds, including late in the game, despite frequent under-contribution by others and repeated exposure to antisocial punishment. There is only a single observed deviation to zero contribution; this looks like an isolated test or momentary reaction rather than a strategic shift, since they immediately revert to full contribution afterward and maintain it through the end. Their behavior suggests they prioritize social efficiency and norm-consistent cooperation over short-term self-interest, even when the environment is noisy and sometimes hostile. They appear tolerant of free-riding to an extent; they do not sharply reduce their own contributions in response to others’ low contributions or to being punished. Any conditionality is weak: they may notice contribution patterns, but they do not strongly condition their own giving on others’ behavior. Risk tolerance toward exploitation is high; they are willing to keep contributing fully even when it is clear that some players consistently contribute little or nothing and that some punishers target them despite their cooperation. They implicitly endorse a strong fairness norm where full contribution is “how one ought to play,” but they do not enforce this aggressively through their own contribution choices.

This player is a near-unconditional high contributor who strongly favors full cooperation over personal payoff. They typically give their entire endowment or very close to it, even as average group contributions fluctuate and even decline. Their baseline is to uphold a “everyone should contribute a lot” norm and to personally stick to it despite evidence that others sometimes fall short. They do not appear to condition their own contribution tightly on others’ previous behavior: they keep contributing at a high level through both cooperative and more defective phases of the group. Only very late do they deviate substantially from this pattern once, suggesting either experimentation, end-game behavior, or temporary frustration; their usual preference clearly favors cooperation. Fairness norms seem strong but tolerant: they keep contributing fully even when some others contribute very little, signaling that they prioritize group efficiency and norm signaling over short-term fairness in material outcomes. Risk tolerance is high: they repeatedly expose themselves to being exploited by lower contributors and to spending a lot on rewards, indicating they accept large personal payoff variance in pursuit of social goals. They do not show clear evidence of learning to “protect” themselves by lowering contributions when others under-contribute; instead, they appear anchored to a prosocial identity and only occasionally diverge from it. Conditionality is thus weak in a narrow payoff-maximizing sense but strong in a normative sense: they behave as if “I cooperate because that’s the right thing,” not because others have proven themselves first.

The player is a strict, unconditional high contributor. They always contribute the full endowment, showing no sensitivity to declines in others’ provision or to personal payoff losses from punishment and reward spending. They appear to follow a strong internal norm that “the right play” is maximal cooperation, independent of others’ behavior or endgame considerations. There is no evidence of conditional cooperation or strategic withholding; even when group contributions drop substantially or particular players persistently free-ride, the player never reduces their own contribution. This pattern suggests they prioritize norm adherence and public-good efficiency over individual earnings and are willing to accept being exploited by lower contributors rather than compromise their cooperative standard. Risk tolerance is high with respect to being the “sucker”: they repeatedly expose themselves to being taken advantage of but do not adjust away from that stance. Fairness for them centers on correcting others via punishment/reward rather than adjusting their own contribution level.

This player is a moderately prosocial but non-maximal contributor who anchors around mid-to-high contributions and adjusts within a bounded range rather than in extreme jumps. They rarely give nothing or everything; instead, they vary within a band from clearly below the group’s dominant full-cooperation level up to near-full contributions. Their pattern suggests they value contributing to the public good but also protect their own payoff, avoiding systematically matching the highest contributors even when those high contributions are stable. They appear conditionally cooperative in a loose sense: when the environment looks strongly cooperative, they are willing to raise their own contribution, and when cooperation weakens they are willing to reduce it. However, their adjustments are relatively modest and never fully align with the most generous peers, indicating a personal contribution norm that sits somewhat below “everyone gives everything.” They occasionally contribute substantially more than the current group average, which implies some willingness to take on a bit of social risk, but they do not persist at that level across time. Their contribution sequence shows exploratory behavior rather than strict adherence to a fixed rule. They test both lower and higher contributions while continuing to participate positively in the public good. They do not appear driven by strict equality or matching behavior; they tolerate contributing less than most others at times and more than others at other times. Overall, they fit a “cautious cooperator who experiments around a moderate baseline” rather than a pure free-rider or a strict altruist.

This player is a mostly high-contribution, but strategically opportunistic and occasionally deceptive cooperator. They usually match the group’s norm of full contribution when a clear consensus for maximizing the public fund emerges, and they demonstrate that they understand and accept the efficiency of full cooperation. However, they intermittently defect by contributing nothing (and once a partial amount) while others mostly cooperate, and they do so without announcing any change in strategy or tying it to others’ misbehavior. Their pattern is not purely conditional on average group behavior: they defect even in periods when almost everyone else is contributing the maximum, and they later return to full contribution while those same others continue cooperating. This suggests a willingness to “skim” extra payoff when they believe they will not be punished and group cooperation is strong enough to sustain high returns despite their free-riding. They show some instability: long stretches of maximal contributions are punctuated by abrupt zeros or low contributions without explanation. The timing suggests opportunism rather than confusion: they have already demonstrated comprehension of the rules and of the effectiveness of full cooperation, and their defection coincides with situations where others remain cooperative. Their fairness norm appears self-serving: they endorse and benefit from the cooperative norm, but they do not adhere to a strict reciprocity or equality principle, instead tolerating asymmetric gains in their own favor. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they defect a handful of times in a highly communicative environment where free-riders are noticed and criticized, but they do not defect persistently. This pattern is consistent with calibrated risk-taking—extracting extra benefit occasionally while avoiding becoming an obvious chronic defector.

This player is a steadfast full cooperator. They always contribute the entire endowment and never deviate, even when others free‑ride or reduce their contributions. Their behavior is highly stable and non‑volatile across the game. They appear to understand that maximal contribution is individually profitable given the parameters (implied by their repeated insistence on going “all in” and acceptance of the high multiplier) and treat full cooperation as both the efficient and morally correct baseline. They do not condition their own contribution on others’ previous behavior; instead, they behave as an unconditional cooperator in practice. They tolerate substantial inequality in favor of free‑riders without retaliating or experimenting with lower contributions. This suggests low willingness to sacrifice group welfare to discipline defectors, and a strong commitment to the cooperative norm once adopted. Their risk tolerance with respect to others’ opportunism is high: they repeatedly expose themselves to exploitation for the sake of preserving the cooperative outcome.

The player is a predominantly self-interested, strategically flexible free-rider with limited but situational willingness to cooperate. They show no stable norm of always contributing or always withholding; instead, they shift between the two options based on recent group behavior and payoffs. Their overall pattern favors keeping their endowment when they expect others to withhold, and occasionally contributing when they expect at least some group cooperation. They appear moderately responsive to fairness and relative payoffs: they contribute once in a setting where withholding yields no extra advantage over others, and withhold when contributing would clearly leave them relatively worse off than freeriders. This suggests a preference to avoid being the “sucker” who contributes when others do not, more than a desire to maximize total group welfare. Risk tolerance is moderate and calculated: they are willing to take a one-shot cooperative risk when group behavior is uncertain or somewhat mixed, but revert quickly to safe self-regarding choices when cooperation either fails to materialize or becomes exploitable. Their actions indicate they understand the payoff structure and exploit opportunities to benefit from others’ contributions when those arise. Overall, their contribution rule is best described as “conditional opportunist”: they may cooperate if they believe enough others will as well, but they readily free-ride on cooperators and revert to full defection when mutual defection seems likely or when cooperation becomes costly relative to others.

The player is a strict, unconditional full cooperator. They consistently give their entire endowment to the public good in every situation shown, without experimenting with lower contributions or reacting to minor deviations by others. Their behavior indicates that they prioritize maximizing group returns over individual short-term gain, even when the marginal per-capita return makes free-riding individually profitable. They appear highly stable and non-volatile: once they align with a cooperative plan, they do not revise it. There is no evidence of conditional cooperation (e.g., no adjustment in response to a temporary drop by one peer); instead, they maintain full contributions regardless of others’ momentary behavior. This suggests a fairness or norm-following motive: they seem to endorse “everyone gives everything” as the appropriate standard and personally adhere to it. Risk tolerance appears relatively high in social terms: they are willing to expose themselves to potential exploitation by any defector and do not hedge against that possibility. Their choices are compatible with either a principled cooperative type (valuing collective efficiency and norm consistency) or a payoff-maximizer under the assumption that others will also fully cooperate; distinguishing between these is uncertain, but their refusal to test or protect against defection leans toward a norm-driven or intrinsically cooperative type.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator in this environment. They always choose the maximal contribution, never experimenting with free-riding or partial cooperation even when others reduce their contributions or seem inattentive. Their strategy prioritizes maximizing group payoffs over short‑term individual gain, despite the marginal per‑coin return being less than 1. They appear to anchor on the simple rule “everyone contributes fully, every time” and do not revise this rule in response to occasional defections, exits, or accidents. They treat full contribution as both a moral and efficiency norm: contributing maximally is “what we do” and is also the way “we all get more.” They maintain this pattern even when it is clear that there are intermittently selfish or absent players, suggesting relatively low sensitivity to exploitation and high tolerance for minor inequities as long as the group as a whole stays mostly cooperative. They show no evidence of conditional cooperation (e.g., adjusting their own contributions contingent on others’ behavior). Instead, they behave like a steadfast norm enforcer via talk, not via changing contributions. They are risk‑accepting in the sense that they consistently invest their entire endowment into the public pool, trusting others to keep cooperating, and they do not hedge even later in the game when boredom and dropout become salient. Fairness for them is primarily “everyone chips in fully” rather than fine‑grained reciprocity; as long as that broad pattern holds, they are satisfied and encourage continuation.

This player is a high, near-unconditional cooperator with two notable lapses. They overwhelmingly choose the maximal contribution even when a few others free-ride, indicating a strong preference for maintaining group efficiency over short-term individual gain. Their baseline strategy is to match or exceed the cooperative norm rather than hedge against possible defection. They do not visibly condition their contributions on specific others’ past behavior; instead they persist in contributing fully despite some zeros in the group. The isolated zero-contribution choices appear to be opportunistic or mistaken deviations rather than the start of a new strategy: they immediately return to full contribution and even apologize after one such instance, which suggests they care about being seen as cooperative and norm-abiding. They tolerate moderate free-riding without retaliating by lowering their own contributions, implying relatively low use of strategic punishment via contribution withholding and a higher tolerance for inequality when it preserves overall group earnings. Overall, they fit a “pro-social, mostly unconditional cooperator” type with minor, non-escalating deviations.

This player is a “mostly cooperative, mildly opportunistic norm-enforcer.” Their default pattern is to contribute the full endowment consistently; non-contribution is rare and appears to be either deliberate but infrequent opportunism or accidental button errors. They clearly understand that mutual full contribution is payoff-maximizing and adopt that as their baseline rule. Even after observing others give nothing, they keep returning to full contributions instead of shifting into systematic free-riding. This indicates relatively high conditional cooperation with a strong bias toward maintaining group efficiency, rather than reacting in a tit-for-tat way to every deviation. Their few zero contributions cluster in situations where (a) others are also deviating, and (b) they later express uncertainty or embarrassment about possibly not having contributed. They also explicitly report not being sure whether a previous low-contribution behavior was accidental, and later state that a zero was an accident. Given that they remain strongly pro-contribution before and after these events, it is plausible that at least some of their own zeroes are unintended rather than strategic. However, this cannot be confirmed with certainty (low confidence). They uphold fairness norms: they describe those who do not contribute as “selfish” and publicly draw attention to specific non-contributors. Yet they do not escalate into sustained retaliation; they do not switch into persistent free-riding even when that would be profitable in the short term. This suggests that fairness matters, but long-run group earnings and maintaining the cooperative norm matter more. Risk tolerance is moderate. They are willing to contribute fully even when a couple of others defect, trusting that the group will mostly continue cooperating and that the high multiplier makes contributions worthwhile. They do not experiment systematically with strategic free-riding, nor do they appear to probe the group’s tolerance for exploitation; instead, they predominantly lock into the full-contribution equilibrium. Overall, their strategy in a new PGG environment is: aim for universal full contribution, reinforce that norm verbally, occasionally express or experience lapses (possibly accidental), but rapidly return to full cooperation rather than exploiting the group.

This player is a highly stable, unconditional full cooperator in this environment. They always choose the maximum contribution, never deviating despite occasional free-riding or accidents by others. Their behavior suggests they internalize the efficiency logic of full contribution and stick to it, even when others temporarily reduce contributions or when the group composition changes. Their contributions do not appear contingent on short-term fluctuations in others’ behavior; they continue to give the full amount through both high- and lower-payoff phases. This indicates low sensitivity to mild social dilemmas and an absence of retaliatory or “teaching” motives. They neither attempt to punish by withholding nor to strategically free-ride near the end, implying weak strategic opportunism and strong preference for consistent cooperation or for minimizing cognitive effort in decision adjustment. Fairness norms seem to be present but not behaviorally enforced via contribution changes. When others lag or free-ride, the player does not respond by reducing their own contribution; instead, they sustain contribution while expressing mild frustration or impatience in chat. This pattern is consistent with someone who values group efficiency and possibly the simplicity of a fixed rule (“always give full”) more than fine-tuned conditional cooperation. Risk tolerance appears moderate to high in social terms: they repeatedly expose themselves to exploitation risk (always giving full contribution) and do not hedge even when some others are clearly not fully reliable. They seem more concerned with moving the game along and securing the predictable cooperative payoff than with optimizing against potential defectors. Overall, their strategy is: pick the socially efficient, high-cooperation action and stick with it throughout, regardless of noise, boredom, or occasional selfish play by others.

This player is a rigid, unconditional full cooperator. They always contribute the maximum allowed amount and never deviate, regardless of others’ behavior, group instability, or signs of free-riding. Their pattern is extremely stable: they do not experiment with defection, partial cooperation, or strategic withholding, even when others give less or when average payoffs drop. They behave as if following a simple, fixed rule: put in the maximum every time. They appear to treat the game primarily as a coordination task with an obvious efficient norm rather than as a strategic environment requiring contingency plans. They do not visibly condition their behavior on past group outcomes or individual deviations; even repeated low contributions by some players do not alter their choice. This suggests low sensitivity to social risk and exploitation: they tolerate free-riding rather than respond in kind. Given the very high group return from full cooperation, their behavior is individually payoff-maximizing as long as they expect most others to contribute, and they act as if that expectation is stable. When deviations occur, they still maintain the cooperative norm, indicating a strong internalized fairness/efficiency norm or a desire to keep the group outcome as high as possible irrespective of short-run inequity. They show no evidence of risk-hedging or end-game effects. Despite not knowing when the game ends, they never “cash out” by defecting; their contributions remain uniformly maximal. This suggests either a principle-driven style (“always cooperate when cooperation is efficient”) or a simple strategy that ignores temporal considerations. Overall, their type is a high-commitment, norm-following cooperator who prioritizes maintaining a clear cooperative standard over adapting to noisy or opportunistic behavior.

This player is a highly stable, unconditional full cooperator within the available action space. They always choose the maximal contribution in every observed round, without deviation, even when others free-ride or when average contributions drop noticeably. Their behavior does not appear to be contingent on short-term payoff changes, specific individuals’ defections, or temporary coordination failures; they maintain the same choice under both perfect and imperfect group cooperation. They clearly understand that mutual high contributions yield high returns and act consistently with that framing. There is no evidence of strategic experimentation, retaliation, or conditional cooperation; they never switch to the selfish option to “test” the group or discipline defectors. Instead, they appear to adopt a rule-like heuristic of “always give the full amount” and stick to it. Fairness norms seem to be group-focused rather than individual-focused: they do not complain about defectors or call anyone out, and they never threaten to change their own behavior in response to others. They implicitly tolerate some free-riding as the cost of keeping the cooperative norm intact. Risk tolerance is high in social terms: they repeatedly expose themselves to exploitation by low contributors but do not adjust their own contributions, suggesting they value sustaining group cooperation and/or normative consistency over protecting themselves from occasional unfairness. Overall, their contribution type is: reliable, norm-following full cooperator with low conditionality and high tolerance for minor deviations by others.

This player is a highly stable, unconditional full cooperator. They always contribute the maximum allowed amount and never deviate, even when others free‑ride or when group contributions drop. Their behavior does not appear contingent on short‑term fluctuations in others’ behavior; they maintain the same strategy across the entire game rather than experimenting with selfish play or partial contributions. They implicitly follow a strong efficiency and fairness norm: everyone should give the full amount so that the group payoff is maximized and equalized. Their own actions are fully aligned with that norm, and they do not punish defectors by reducing their own contributions. They tolerate occasional free‑riding or mistakes from others without changing their own behavior, indicating low sensitivity to short‑term exploitation risk and a high willingness to accept being “taken advantage of” if it maintains the cooperative equilibrium. They display relatively low risk aversion with respect to being exploited: despite seeing others sometimes give nothing, they keep giving everything, suggesting they either expect cooperation to prevail on average, value adherence to the norm more than protecting themselves from defectors, or both. They also do not appear to strategically time their contributions or respond to perceived end‑game effects; their pattern is flat and unwavering rather than anticipatory or opportunistic. Overall, as a type, they are a “principled cooperator”: they consistently play the fully cooperative action, use it as their only strategy, and do not condition their behavior on others’ deviations.

This player is a stable, unconditional full contributor. They always give the maximum possible amount, never deviating, even when others defect or when time pressure and end-of-task uncertainty arise. Their behavior suggests a strong cooperative norm: they continue to contribute fully despite seeing some players give zero and despite knowing they could earn more per round by free-riding. They actively advocate that everyone should keep contributing the maximum and frame this as a shared profit-maximizing strategy for the group. They appear comfortable with the risk of being exploited by occasional defectors, prioritizing maintaining a cooperative norm and group gains over short-term individual advantage. There is no evidence that their contribution is conditional on any specific other player's behavior; rather, they treat full contribution as the default, “correct” action throughout.

This player is a highly stable, unconditional full cooperator. They always choose the maximum contribution despite multiple opportunities to free ride once others defect or when end-game effects could justify pulling back. Their cooperation does not appear contingent on precise fairness (for example, they keep contributing fully even when others occasionally give zero or when they themselves are punished). They seem motivated by the clear efficiency of everyone “maxing out,” implicitly following the group’s early coordination norm and never deviating from it. They tolerate some inequality and individual misbehavior as long as the overall cooperative structure holds. They do not hedge or experiment with partial or zero contributions, suggesting low concern for short-term individual gain relative to the group-optimal outcome and/or a desire to keep behavior simple and predictable under time pressure.

This player is a strongly prosocial, near-unconditional high contributor. They consistently give the maximum allowed amount almost every round, including when some others temporarily reduce contributions. They only free‑ride once, and immediately return to full contributions thereafter, even though they are then clearly targeted with punishment for that deviation. Their willingness to resume full cooperation despite being punished and despite some others lowering contributions later suggests that they prioritize maintaining the cooperative norm over short‑term individual gain. They appear comfortable accepting lower personal payoffs in order to keep the group outcome high, indicating low risk aversion with respect to exploitation by others and a strong internal fairness/cooperation norm. There is weak evidence of any contingent adjustment to others’ exact contribution levels; their pattern looks more like a default rule of “just max out” rather than fine‑tuned conditional cooperation.

This player is a **conditional high contributor who quickly stabilizes at full cooperation once reciprocity is evident**. They begin by contributing substantially less than the informal “all-in” norm that others verbally endorse, indicating initial caution, mild free-riding, or uncertainty about group behavior. After observing that others overwhelmingly follow the cooperative plan and that high contribution yields high returns, the player switches to contributing the entire endowment and then maintains that level consistently for the remainder of the game. Their behavior is **highly stable once a cooperative equilibrium is established**: there is no sign of opportunistic deviation later, even when group cooperation becomes extremely predictable and profitable. This suggests that, once convinced that others are reliably contributing, the player prioritizes maintaining that mutual-gain structure over exploiting it. They act as a **payoff-responsive cooperator** rather than a purely altruistic one: they do not lead with maximal generosity, but they fully match a cooperative norm when it appears trustworthy and sustainable. There is no evidence of spite or punishment-driven motives, and no attempts to test the group by deviating after cooperation stabilizes. The player appears **risk-aware but not risk-averse**. Early behavior shows they are unwilling to immediately expose themselves to maximal downside if others defect, but once they infer that full contribution is both the socially preferred strategy and payoff-dominant, they accept and repeatedly play it. There is no volatility or random switching; adjustments are directional and grounded in observed group outcomes. They implicitly endorse a **fairness norm of symmetric full contribution**: when everyone is contributing at the same high level, they match it exactly, neither trying to outdo others with excess generosity nor undercutting them with slight under-contribution. They show no signs of exploiting small margins or “shaving” contributions once cooperation locks in.

This player is a highly cooperative, near-unconditional high contributor once coordination around full cooperation is established. They quickly move from a slightly cautious but generous contribution to effectively full contributions and then maintain maximal giving with no signs of backsliding. Their behavior stabilizes at the cooperative norm very early and remains extremely consistent, with no experimentation, no strategic under-giving, and no apparent attempts to exploit others’ generosity. They appear strongly conditional on the emergent group norm but not on precise fairness adjustments. After observing that others are willing to contribute very generously, they align to that standard and then fully match the group’s cooperative pattern. Once they see that “everyone goes all in,” they treat that as the equilibrium and act accordingly. There is no evidence that they track small deviations by others or adjust in response; they act as if maintaining a simple, high-contribution rule is sufficient. Their fairness norm seems group-focused rather than individualistic: they are comfortable contributing at or near the maximum as long as others are broadly doing the same, and they do not show any concern about fine-grained payoff equality or about slightly over- or under-contributing relative to the group in the early phase. They behave as if the important thing is that the group achieves and keeps a fully cooperative state, not that their own share is perfectly fair each round. Risk tolerance is high in social terms: they are willing to commit fully to cooperation once they have a basic indication that others are inclined to cooperate. They do not hedge against potential future defection, and they do not slow-walk contributions upward over time. Instead, they jump to full cooperation and stay there, even though they cannot see how many rounds remain or other players’ full outcome details. This suggests they prioritize maintaining a clear cooperative strategy over guarding against potential exploitation. They follow a simple strategy: “once cooperation is working, always contribute everything.” They do not appear to test alternative contribution levels, seek to optimize narrowly, or react to small deviations. Adaptation is front-loaded: after initially responding generously but not perfectly maximally, they quickly converge to full contributions and then stop adapting, as if they believe the optimal pattern has been found. Overall, they are a stable, norm-following, high-trust cooperator who strongly favors and sustains efficient, group-beneficial outcomes.

This player is a highly cooperative, near “all-in contributor” type who quickly aligns with a group contribution norm and then sticks to it with strong stability. After initially agreeing in chat to a full-contribution strategy, they almost always contribute the maximum possible amount and do not experiment with low contributions or opportunistic free-riding once the group norm is clearly cooperative. The one early non-maximal contribution suggests they are not mechanically fixed to “always-max,” but rather are willing to adjust once they observe others’ behavior. Their contributions become fully stable once it is clear that others are consistently cooperative. They appear strongly conditional on the perceived group standard: when others advocate and then demonstrate all-in behavior, this player matches and maintains it. They do not retaliate against early low contributors nor try to undercut the group later; instead, they behave as a loyal cooperator who values sustaining the high-contribution equilibrium over short-term individual gain. Fairness norms seem important: the player behaves in line with a “we should all do the same, high” standard and never attempts to exploit others’ trust. Risk tolerance appears moderate to high in the pro-social direction. They are willing to risk being exploited by potential defectors, especially early, once they have verbally committed to cooperation. They do not hedge or gradually increase contributions; they rapidly move to a fully cooperative profile and stay there when the environment remains favorable. There is no evidence of payoff-maximizing deviation at the end, suggesting limited focus on endgame exploitation and a stronger emphasis on maintaining group harmony and predictable mutual gains.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator in this environment. They always contribute their entire endowment and show no sign of hedging, even early on when others are not yet uniformly cooperative. Once the group coordination norm emerges, they maintain maximum contributions without wavering, indicating high behavioral stability and no observable volatility. Their strategy appears rule‑based (“always put everything in”) rather than finely contingent on minor deviations by others. They demonstrate a strong efficiency orientation: given the high multiplier and absence of punishment or reward mechanisms, they seem to recognize that full contribution is both individually and collectively profitable, and they act accordingly. They accept and reinforce a fairness norm where everyone contributes equally at the maximum level, and they do not attempt to exploit others by free‑riding once the cooperative pattern is clear. Their decisions suggest low risk aversion in the face of possible defection: they are willing to go all in from the start, even before universal cooperation has been established. They also show no evidence of strategic end‑game defection, despite not knowing how many rounds remain, which implies either a strong preference for consistent cooperation, a simple heuristic of “always cooperate when it’s profitable,” or low salience of end‑game incentives. Overall, their contribution profile fits a “principled efficiency‑seeker”: they prioritize maximizing joint returns under transparent conditions, and they do so in a consistent, non‑opportunistic manner.

This player is an uncompromising, stable full cooperator in this environment. They always contribute their entire endowment and never test lower amounts, even when others deviate early on. Their behavior suggests they prioritize maximizing total group payoffs once they recognize that the game’s structure makes full contribution individually profitable. There is no evidence of conditional or retaliatory cooperation; they contribute fully regardless of earlier free-riding and do not adjust in response to others’ changes. They appear comfortable with the strategic risk of cooperating despite initial defects, indicating either confidence that others will align or a willingness to accept short-term vulnerability for potential long-term gains. Their fairness norm seems to favor uniform full contribution across all players; they do not complain about early lower contributions by others, but their steady behavior aligns with a strong expectation that “everyone should put everything in.” They show no opportunism toward the end of the game and do not attempt to exploit the group once strong cooperation is established. Overall, they fit a “principled efficiency-maximizer” profile: once they see that cooperation is both socially and individually beneficial, they lock into the cooperative optimum and remain there.

This player is a moderately high, fairly steady cooperator who anchors near the middle-to-upper range of possible contributions rather than at extremes. They avoid persistent free-riding and instead keep giving a substantial share of their endowment even when others contribute less, indicating a stable internal norm of “do your part” rather than purely payoff-maximizing behavior. They adjust slightly around a personal reference point rather than tracking every fluctuation in the group, suggesting conditional cooperation that is weakly sensitive to others’ behavior but not tightly matched to it. They are willing at least once to contribute the full endowment, showing readiness to sacrifice personal payoff for collective benefit when they perceive cooperation as either desirable or needed. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they do not swing wildly in response to being under-rewarded or to the presence of low contributors, and they do not chase short-term personal gain by cutting contributions to zero. Their fairness norm seems to be that everyone should contribute roughly a meaningful, non-trivial amount, but they do not insist on perfect equality and continue to cooperate even alongside some free-riding.

This player is a generally low-to-moderate contributor who mixes limited cooperation with clear free-riding. Their typical contributions sit below the group average, and they never move into high-contribution territory. They occasionally match the rough “middle” of the group but also choose outright zero, indicating that maximizing private payoff sometimes overrides any fairness or reciprocity concerns. Their pattern suggests conditional cooperation with a strong self-interest bias: they are willing to give something when others seem broadly cooperative, but they do not try to lead or match the more generous contributors. They appear sensitive to the cost–benefit structure: given that the marginal per-capita return from contributing is below 1, they lean toward keeping more for themselves, especially when they can still benefit from others’ higher contributions. Contribution behavior is somewhat stable around a low baseline, with a few deviations toward full free-riding. There is no sign of strong fairness enforcement (they tolerate both higher and lower contributors without adjusting upward in response), and they do not seek to equalize contributions. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they do not gamble on very high contributions in the hope of full-group cooperation, preferring the safer path of modest or zero giving.

This player is a generally cooperative, moderately conditional contributor who experiments with both high and low contributions. They usually give at or above the group average but occasionally drop below it when they seem to test how much they can free‑ride without harming outcomes too much. Their contributions are not monotonic over time; instead, they alternate between full or very high contributions and more moderate ones, suggesting active adaptation rather than simple fatigue or linear decay. They appear to care about maintaining a reasonably high public good while also preserving personal earnings. They do not rigidly follow a “always full‑cooperate” rule; instead, they flex their contribution around a personal norm of substantial giving, sometimes pulling back when others’ behavior looks less cooperative or when they discover that they can earn comfortably with moderate contributions. They do not push contributions to the minimum, even in later rounds, which indicates they maintain a prosocial or norm‑respecting stance rather than turning into a pure free‑rider. Their behavior suggests a fairness‑oriented but payoff‑sensitive type: they are willing to contribute more than many others, but they do not martyr themselves once it is clear others are not fully aligned with maximal cooperation. They exhibit moderate risk tolerance: they are willing to invest heavily in the public good even after being punished and even when some others persistently contribute very little, implying they accept the strategic risk that their cooperation may not be reciprocated. Over time, they seem to learn that moderate contributions can still yield good payoffs in this environment and occasionally revert to such levels. However, they also show renewed bursts of high contributions, which implies they have not abandoned cooperative ideals. Overall, they behave as a “pragmatic cooperator”: inclined to help the group, but willing to shade contributions downward when the social environment looks imperfect.

This player is a moderate, self-regarding cooperator with stable but below-average contributions. They rarely give nothing, but also avoid high contributions that match or exceed the group norm. Their typical pattern is to contribute a small to mid-range portion of the endowment and keep a substantial private share. They appear sensitive to the general contribution climate: when peer contributions look broadly cooperative, they are willing to contribute in the low-to-mid range; when they likely perceive themselves as being targeted or exploited, they are willing to drop to very low or zero contribution. They do not chase “full cooperation” levels even when many others appear highly cooperative, suggesting that maximizing group efficiency is not their main priority. Instead, they seem to balance some willingness to cooperate with a clear concern for their own payoff and for not being the “sucker” who contributes much more than low contributors. Their behavior is relatively stable over time, with only a few clear adjustments downward that look like reactions to punishment or perceived unfairness. Overall, their fairness norm seems asymmetric: they accept being slightly below group average and appear more concerned with avoiding personal loss or humiliation than with strict equality or full provision of the public good. Risk tolerance in contributions is moderate: they do not experiment with extreme highs or sustained free-riding, but they do occasionally test lower contributions once the social environment feels hostile.

This player is a stable, moderately high contributor who anchors on a preferred “fair” contribution and adjusts only cautiously. They typically give more than the group average but less than the maximum, signaling a cooperative orientation combined with some self-interest. Their behavior is consistent rather than volatile: they hold one main contribution level for many rounds, altering it only after meaningful shifts in the social environment, such as perceived changes in others’ cooperation or being targeted by punishment. They appear to use others’ contributions as a reference point. When the group’s cooperation seems reasonably strong—especially with multiple high contributors—they are willing to match or modestly exceed what they see as a fair share. When they perceive defection or instability, they reduce their contribution somewhat, but they do not collapse into full free-riding. They also show that, under some circumstances, they can increase their contribution again after a lower phase, suggesting they are responsive to signals of renewed or sustained cooperation. They tolerate personal risk to maintain cooperation: they keep contributing at relatively high levels even when there is noise, heterogeneity, or some players who give very little, indicating that they value the public good above strict payoff maximization. However, they do not fully sacrifice their own payoff; they avoid extreme maximal contributions as a baseline and only move upward briefly, which suggests they balance efficiency with self-protection. Their fairness norm seems to be “contribute clearly above low contributors and roughly in line with other cooperators,” rather than strict equality with any single benchmark. Overall, this is a conditional cooperator with a pronounced fairness concern, moderate risk tolerance, and a preference for stable, predictable contribution patterns.

This player is a “conditional high contributor with pragmatic cutbacks.” They show a clear willingness to cooperate strongly, but only up to a point and only while they see enough cooperation and/or feel fairly treated. They start by contributing the full endowment and return to full contributions multiple times, indicating that they accept strong cooperation as a desirable baseline and are not trying to free‑ride from the outset. Over time, they settle into a pattern where they alternate between full and half‑endowment contributions, and then predominantly contribute around half. This suggests they internalize a norm that contributing a meaningful, non‑minimal share is appropriate, while also preserving self‑interest. Their contributions track the emerging environment: when others’ cooperation appears weaker or they are on the receiving end of deductions, they do not escalate to defection, but they do reduce contributions from maximal to moderate levels. They never experiment with zero or very low contributions, which indicates they are not purely payoff‑maximizing in the narrow sense and maintain some commitment to the group good even when incentives to exploit exist. They appear moderately risk tolerant: they repeatedly risk contributing fully despite having been punished earlier, and despite variability in others’ contributions. At the same time, their later shift to mid‑level contributions suggests some hedging against continued unfairness or declining cooperation. Fairness norms seem central. They behave as if a “reasonable” contribution is at least around half their endowment, and they personally exceed that on many occasions. They never respond to others’ apparent under‑contributions by dramatically undercutting the group; instead, they move to an intermediate, still pro‑social level. This is consistent with a player who values fairness and reciprocity but does not want to be systematically exploited. Overall, they are best characterized as a conditional cooperator who starts from a generous position and then adjusts downward to a more guarded but still cooperative contribution strategy as they observe the social and punishment environment.

This player is a high-but-flexible contributor with clear conditional and exploratory tendencies. They frequently give the maximum or near-maximum, but occasionally drop to very low or zero contributions. Their behavior suggests they understand the basic payoff structure: they sometimes exploit cooperative environments by reducing their own contribution while others still give a lot, and they sometimes free ride fully when group cooperation collapses. They do not rigidly follow one rule; instead, they adjust contributions in response to recent group behavior and likely in response to perceived fairness. When others contribute strongly and consistently, they are willing to match or nearly match that level. When group contributions become erratic or low, they are willing to sharply reduce their own contribution, including to zero, and take the benefits if any others still cooperate. Their pattern shows alternating phases of strong cooperation and opportunistic defection, indicating a pragmatic rather than purely altruistic norm: they care about maintaining beneficial cooperation but are quick to stop paying costs if the cooperative standard looks unstable or unfair. Overall, they tolerate some inequality and some free riding in others, but not indefinitely. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to risk contributing fully even after experiencing punishment and contribution fluctuations, but they periodically test lower contributions, indicating a concern with their own earnings.

This player is a moderately high, flexible contributor who aims to support cooperation but is sensitive to perceived group behavior and payoff efficiency. They tend to contribute more than the group’s typical middle range, but they do not commit to unconditional full cooperation. Their contribution levels move within a band rather than at extremes: they sometimes give the full endowment, sometimes a bit below the apparent group norm, and sometimes right around it. This suggests a contingent, payoff‑aware approach rather than a fixed rule. They appear to track how profitable the public good is and respond to changes in overall efficiency. When returns to cooperation (as reflected in their own payoff) are strong and group contributions look robust, they are willing to contribute very generously, even up to their full endowment. When overall contributions and resulting payoffs weaken, they scale back their own giving to a moderate level while still staying clearly cooperative. This pattern indicates conditional cooperation with a “floor”: they rarely, if ever, drift toward near‑zero contributions, implying some intrinsic preference for mutual benefit or fairness rather than pure free‑riding. Their behavior also shows a mix of social and strategic motives. They do not strictly match the average contribution; instead, they sometimes overshoot it and sometimes undershoot it, suggesting they balance concern for the group with attention to individual payoff outcomes. They accept personal payoff reductions when the environment seems cooperative, indicating some tolerance for inequality against themselves if it helps sustain a high‑contribution state. At the same time, when the environment becomes more uneven or looks less cooperative, they protect themselves by contributing less, but not so little as to abandon the public good entirely. Stability is moderate: they adjust contributions multiple times across the game, but changes are incremental rather than erratic. This points to a learning and updating process rather than impulsive reactions. They seem risk‑tolerant in cooperative directions (willing to commit high amounts when others appear to be contributing well) but risk‑averse against being exploited (pulling back once signals of weaker contributions or lower payoffs appear). Fairness norms are present but not absolute: they value high joint payoffs and are willing to be somewhat more generous than many others, yet they also expect some reciprocity and reduce their contribution when that expectation is partially violated.

The player is a moderately pro-social but strategic conditional cooperator with medium volatility. They begin with mid-level contributions relative to the group, then briefly shift to more generous giving, then experiment with full free-riding, and finally return to an above-average but not maximal contribution. This pattern indicates that they care about collective efficiency but also monitor their own payoff and adjust when they feel the public good is being exploited or when cooperation seems fragile. Their contributions track the overall cooperation climate: when others, on average, contribute at least moderately, the player is willing to contribute in that same range or higher. When apparent cooperation drops or looks unstable, they are willing to sharply reduce their own giving, even down to zero, to protect their payoff. This suggests they are not a pure altruist; they value reciprocity and proportionality. They do not insist on perfectly matching the group’s top contributors. At times they contribute less than the most generous players while still benefiting from the public good, so they accept some degree of advantageous inequality in their favor. However, they also move up to high contributions when many others do so, which implies an internal norm that they “should” help sustain cooperation if others are doing their part. Overall, they show: - Medium-to-high average contribution. - Willingness to both increase and decrease their contribution in response to perceived group behavior. - Some concern with fairness, but not so strong that they refuse to free-ride when they feel justified. - Moderate risk tolerance: they are willing to “test” both high cooperation and free-riding, seeing how outcomes change, rather than locking into one strategy.

This player is a highly stable, strongly cooperative contributor. After an initial mid-level contribution, they immediately move to full endowment contributions and then remain at that maximum level without deviation for the rest of the game, regardless of fluctuations in group behavior or in their own payoffs. This pattern indicates a clear preference for maximizing group welfare over short-term individual gain, even when others contribute less and free-riders earn more in some periods. Their behavior is minimally conditional on others’ contributions. They do not reduce their own contribution when the group average falls, nor do they escalate further when the group becomes more cooperative, suggesting a simple, fixed cooperation rule rather than a finely tuned conditional strategy like proportional response or tit-for-tat. They appear to accept the risk that others may exploit their high contributions and do not hedge or diversify their behavior; this exposes them to payoff disadvantage but they do not adjust. That implies comparatively low sensitivity to exploitation risk and a willingness to tolerate payoff inequality in favor of sustained group provision. Fairness norms, if present, lean toward efficiency rather than strict reciprocity: they keep contributing at the maximum even when lower contributors earn higher payoffs, implying they do not insist on equalizing benefits before continuing to cooperate. Their contribution rule looks intentional and consistent with a “principled cooperator” or “unconditional cooperator” type: once they decide that high contribution is right or beneficial, they stick to it across time, despite evidence that opportunistic behavior would personally pay off. This suggests high internal rule-following or norm adherence and low strategic volatility.

This player fits a “calculating conditional cooperator / opportunistic free-rider” type. They do not have a stable contribution level; instead they vary contributions widely, including using the full range from full free-riding to full endowment. This indicates high volatility and active experimentation rather than a fixed norm or habit. They clearly recognize and exploit the incentive structure. Their behavior shows that they understand they earn more by contributing less than others when others are contributing substantially. They repeatedly choose low or zero contributions while surrounded by many high contributors, which reliably gives them above-average payoffs. They are therefore willing to free-ride when they expect others to maintain the public good. At the same time, they are not a pure selfish optimizer in the narrow sense. They are occasionally willing to contribute moderate or high amounts, including once contributing the entire endowment. This suggests they do not hold a strict “never contribute” rule. Instead, they appear to condition their behavior on the recent history of group cooperation and on payoff comparisons. When the group appears cooperative and payoffs from the pool are strong, they sometimes match that with moderate or high giving; when they see that low/zero contributions still yield strong personal payoffs, they revert to minimal giving. There is no clear evidence that they are following a simple “match-the-average” heuristic or strict reciprocity; their individual choices sometimes move opposite to the emerging contribution trend. This implies a focus on optimizing individual payoff under uncertainty more than on maintaining a fairness standard. Any fairness concerns, if present, appear weak and easily overridden by payoff considerations. Risk tolerance appears moderate to high. They are willing to make stark shifts in contribution (including fully free-riding) even in a group where many others are contributing heavily, suggesting comfort taking advantage of others’ efforts despite the risk that the group might collectively reduce contributions later. They also briefly make a maximal contribution despite not being able to see how many rounds remain, indicating a willingness to risk short-term sacrifice, possibly to test whether higher cooperation can be sustained or to see the impact on payoffs. Overall, they are adaptive and payoff-sensitive, with a tendency toward strategic opportunism rather than stable cooperation or stable defection.

This player is a generally high, prosocial contributor with occasional moderate downward deviations. They typically either give the full endowment or a large majority of it, indicating a strong cooperative baseline and comfort with bearing individual cost for group benefit. Their contribution pattern is not purely mechanical: at several points they reduce their contribution to a mid-range level when group contributions have recently been lower or more uneven, then return to high cooperation when others’ behavior improves. This suggests conditional cooperation anchored to a personal “high but not blind” fairness standard rather than strict equality matching. They tolerate being the highest contributor in the group at times, so they do not require perfect reciprocity, but they are sensitive to pronounced free-riding or sudden drops by others and briefly adjust downward in response. The variability is limited and never reaches full defection, which points to a reluctance to exploit others even when their own previous high contributions were not fully reciprocated. Overall, they combine a cooperative orientation with mild, situational downward adjustment rather than a sharp strategic switch.

This player is a strict, stable full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment, with no deviations, regardless of others’ behavior, punishment received, or stage of the game. Their strategy is not conditionally cooperative in the usual sense; they do not reduce contributions in response to free-riding, slow play, or being punished. They appear to treat full contribution as the default “right” action, even when group contributions drop, and they keep this up through the final round. This suggests a strong internal cooperation norm that outweighs short-term payoff optimization or frustration. Their risk tolerance for exploitation is high: they continue to pay the cost of cooperation even when some others chronically do not contribute or play slowly.

This player is a flexible, profit-sensitive conditional cooperator who alternates between full cooperation and full free-riding rather than sticking to one mode. They clearly understand that everyone contributing increases group payoffs, yet they are willing to deviate when it is personally advantageous or when others also deviate. They never use intermediate contributions; they treat the decision as binary: either fully in or fully out. Their pattern shows responsiveness to the group’s contribution climate: when most others contribute, they sometimes join them and sometimes exploit them, indicating a mix of cooperative inclination and opportunism rather than a strict fairness norm. They do not appear strongly driven by equality or reciprocity per se; they tolerate persistent under-contributors in the group without systematically matching or punishing them, and they themselves accept benefiting from others’ cooperation. There is no clear trend of increasing or decreasing cooperation over time; instead, they switch modes multiple times across the game, including after having seen repeated group coordination and chat appeals. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to free-ride even when that may attract social disapproval or punishment, but they also sometimes “play along” with the cooperative majority, suggesting some sensitivity to social and strategic risk. Overall, they prioritize a balance of personal payoff and low social friction more than strict consistency with any one normative rule.

This player is a predominantly self-regarding free-rider with rare, poorly reinforced cooperation. They overwhelmingly choose to keep their entire endowment and contribute nothing, even when others are consistently contributing fully and creating a profitable public fund. Their default is to exploit others’ contributions rather than to join them, indicating little intrinsic commitment to efficiency or collective welfare in this environment. Cooperation is not stable: on a few occasions they contribute fully, but these are isolated and do not lead to a sustained shift in behavior. These cooperative choices do not appear to be contingent on others’ simultaneous cooperation; they sometimes contribute fully when others give nothing and sometimes when others are cooperative. Because they revert immediately to free-riding afterward, these contributions look more like sporadic experimentation or curiosity than a principled norm of fairness. They are willing to accept being the clear beneficiary of others’ generosity, repeatedly taking the entire personal benefit while others contribute substantially. There is no sign of reciprocal matching of high contributions or of a desire to equalize payoffs. They endure repeated third-party punishment for their low contributions without changing their core strategy, suggesting a relatively low sensitivity to social sanctions and a higher weight on immediate personal payoff than on conforming to cooperative norms. Taken together, their contribution type is: “resilient low contributor / opportunistic free-rider, with occasional non-learning experiments in full contribution.” Their fairness norm toward the public good is weak or absent, and they do not adjust behavior in the direction of conditional cooperation even when cooperation by others is clearly observable and sometimes rewarded or enforced.

This player is a highly inconsistent, experiment-oriented contributor who alternates between full free-riding and full contribution without settling into a stable pattern. They never split the endowment; they only choose extremes, which suggests they simplify the decision to “all or nothing” rather than fine-tuning. They repeatedly free-ride when others contribute, indicating low concern for outcome fairness in favor of own payoff and/or curiosity about how much they can extract while others carry the public good. At other times, they fully contribute when others give nothing, accepting clear short-term disadvantage. This willingness to take the worst individual payoff in order to test how others respond points to strong exploratory motives and low risk aversion regarding immediate earnings. Their contributions do not reliably track any simple fairness norm such as “match others” or “reward cooperators,” because they sometimes cooperate while others defect and sometimes defect while others cooperate. Instead, contribution choices appear loosely conditional on recent history in a way that suggests they are probing the environment rather than following a fixed norm: they try both “I cooperate while you don’t” and “I don’t while you do,” and they return to both modes multiple times. They do not appear to be driven primarily by efficiency concerns, as they often choose options that clearly reduce their own payoff relative to easy free-riding opportunities. Overall, their contribution style fits a “strategic experimenter with weak fairness constraints”: they accept short-term losses to explore, but when a safe high-payoff free-riding opportunity is salient, they take it.

This player is a strategically flexible free-rider who is willing to switch to full cooperation when others establish a strong cooperative norm. They initially take everything for themselves, showing no baseline preference for unconditional cooperation or fairness toward contributors. Their behavior shifts only after a clear majority of others repeatedly contribute, suggesting conditional cooperation: they are more willing to contribute when they believe group cooperation is stable and likely to persist. The change from non-contribution to full contribution is abrupt rather than gradual, indicating that once their internal threshold for “this group is cooperative enough” is crossed, they commit fully rather than hedging. They are comfortable exploiting early cooperation, so they do not follow a strict equality or reciprocity norm over the whole game; instead, they appear to weigh immediate self-gain against the perceived reliability of others' efforts. Their risk tolerance regarding cooperation seems moderate: they avoid early “risking” of contribution in a partially cooperative environment but later accept the risk of contributing once cooperative behavior looks entrenched and the horizon is short.

This player is a selective, opportunistic cooperator who alternates between free-riding and full contribution. They do not follow a simple always-cooperate or always-defect rule; instead they mix both choices in a patterned way. Their behavior suggests they track that cooperation is individually costly but socially beneficial, and they are willing to contribute fully only in some situations. They accepts inequality in contributions: they contribute alongside both higher and lower contributors without visibly trying to match others’ levels or enforce strict fairness. They tolerate being a free-rider when others contribute more, and they also accept being slightly more generous than some peers at other times. Stability is moderate rather than high: they shift between non-contribution and full contribution multiple times rather than converging to one stable strategy. This points to exploratory or contingent behavior, not a fixed norm. They does not show strong risk aversion in the sense of “never contributing”; instead, they are willing to risk a full contribution even when not everyone cooperates, suggesting at least some hope that group cooperation can be sustained, or that they occasionally prioritize group benefit or reciprocity over short-term payoff maximization. Their conditionality appears weakly present but is hard to fully infer: they sometimes cooperate when a substantial subset of others is cooperating and sometimes defect even when cooperation around them is high. This indicates either mixed motives (balancing payoff optimization and social concerns) or experimentation with different contribution patterns. Exact underlying fairness norms and beliefs about what is “right” are unknown.

This player is a strong, almost unconditional cooperator. They contribute the full endowment in essentially every round, including the very last one, despite multiple opportunities to free‑ride profitably. The only deviation is a single early round in which they contribute nothing while the entire group also withholds; they then immediately revert to full contributions and maintain that pattern stably. Their cooperation does not depend on others’ short‑run behavior: they continue to give fully even when some peers free‑ride and when cooperation occasionally collapses. This indicates a stable internal norm of full contribution rather than a reactive “tit‑for‑tat” or purely payoff‑maximizing strategy. Risk tolerance is relatively high: they are willing to keep investing in the public good despite uncertainty about others and despite having experienced punishment themselves. Concern for fairness is oriented toward “everyone should cooperate fully,” but they do not adjust their own contribution downward when others violate that norm.

This player is a strongly cooperative, near-unconditional full contributor. They almost always give the entire endowment to the public good, including very early on and throughout the late and final rounds, despite having full knowledge of the finite horizon. They briefly deviate to non-contribution once when the entire group contributes zero, then immediately return to full contributions and maintain them stably. Their behavior indicates a strong norm in favor of full cooperation, with little evidence of strategic free-riding even when others partially defect or when they themselves are punished. They do not appear to condition their contribution level on others’ previous contributions or on punishment they receive; instead, they maintain cooperation as a default rule. This suggests either a principled “cooperate regardless” stance or a belief that high contributions will generally be reciprocated and are payoff-relevant in expectation. Risk tolerance with respect to being exploited is high: they continue to fully contribute even when some group members deviate or when they are personally punished, implying that maintaining the cooperative norm is more important to them than short-term protection from free-riding.

This player is a highly cooperative, near “always-full-contributor” type. They consistently choose the socially efficient option of full contribution in almost every round. The only deviation is a single early round where they contribute nothing, coinciding with everyone else also contributing nothing; this looks like a brief experiment or synchronized pause rather than a lasting shift. Overall, they behave as if full contribution is the default norm, independent of short-term gains or others’ occasional deviations. Their cooperation is notably stable over time. Early exposure to free-riding and chaotic punishment patterns does not push them into sustained defection. Once they see that the group can maintain high contribution levels, they lock into full contribution and stay there through the mid and late game, including very late rounds when end-game incentives to defect are strongest. This indicates strong conditional cooperation in favor of the group’s best outcome, but with a very high threshold for abandoning cooperation. They appear willing to be “exploited” in narrow monetary terms: they keep contributing fully even when some players contribute nothing or when they themselves are punished.
