This suggests that they either (a) value maintaining a cooperative environment more than strictly maximizing short-run coins, or (b) treat the game as one where sticking to a cooperative heuristic is preferable to constant adjustment. Their fairness norm seems oriented toward “everyone contributes fully,” but they do not personally enforce this norm with punishment and do not visibly exit from it when others misbehave. Risk-wise, they tolerate the strategic risk that others may not cooperate. They repeatedly choose full cooperation even after observing substantial free-riding and heavy punishment exchanges elsewhere. This pattern fits a “high baseline trust / high cooperation norm” profile more than a cautious or opportunistic strategist.

This player is a predominantly high, binary cooperator who occasionally experiments with free-riding but quickly reverts to full cooperation once group behavior stabilizes. They almost always contribute their entire endowment when others are contributing, and their contributions become highly stable for a long stretch once a cooperative norm is clearly established. Early on, they sometimes choose not to contribute even when others do, but this pattern does not persist; after a brief exploratory phase, they settle into nearly universal full contributions, even when a few others defect in mixed-contribution environments. They show little evidence of conditional tit-for-tat style responses to marginal defection; instead, they appear more focused on the overall cooperative climate. Their willingness to contribute fully in the presence of some defectors suggests relatively high tolerance for inequality and a stronger orientation toward maintaining public-good efficiency than toward strict payoff-maximizing free-riding. They do not appear particularly risk-averse regarding the possibility that others might free-ride on their contributions; once they see that cooperation can be sustained, they consistently accept the risk of being exploited and keep contributing fully. Overall, they fit the profile of a “norm-following cooperator who briefly probes but then commits to cooperation.”

This player is a generally cooperative, above-average contributor who adjusts contributions in response to the observed group pattern rather than following a fixed rule. They do not contribute everything; instead they tend to give a substantial but partial share of their endowment, staying consistently on the cooperative side while preserving some private payoff. Their contribution levels move in the same direction as the group’s: when others appear to cooperate more, they increase their own contribution; when group contributions drop, they pull back rather than compensating for others. This suggests conditional cooperation anchored around reciprocity rather than altruistic covering of others’ shortfalls. They appear sensitive to fairness: they are willing to match or roughly keep pace with other contributors but do not markedly out-contribute clear low contributors. They also do not display strong risk-seeking altruism; they avoid making extremely high contributions when the group looks unreliable and instead keep contributions moderate. Their pattern shows ongoing updating rather than random volatility: changes in their contributions align with shifts in the others’ behavior, indicating a reactive, learning-oriented style rather than noise.

This player is a predominantly low contributor with rare, moderate deviations from pure free-riding. Their baseline is to keep their full endowment, occasionally making small-to-medium contributions that remain well below the typical full-cooperation level. Contributions are not random; they cluster around moments when others are contributing strongly, but the player still preserves a clear earnings advantage over cooperators. This indicates conditional responsiveness rather than principled generosity. Stability vs. volatility: Their pattern is mostly stable—zero contribution is the default—with a few isolated increases. These increases do not escalate over time or follow a clear upward or downward trend; instead, they appear as one-off adjustments. This suggests a primarily self-interested strategy with limited experimentation rather than a drifting or highly adaptive cooperative style. Conditionality: The player appears weakly conditional. They do notice and respond to group behavior in that they are more willing to contribute when the group is already contributing at relatively high levels. However, they do not seek to match or closely approach others’ average contributions, nor do they appear to “reward” high group contribution with comparable generosity. Their conditionality operates more as: “If others are cooperating a lot, I might contribute a bit,” rather than “I match or reciprocate cooperation.” There is no evidence that they actively reduce contributions in response to lower cooperation, because their baseline is already minimal. Fairness norms: The observed behavior suggests that fairness concerns are either weak or subordinate to payoff maximization. The player repeatedly enjoys payoffs that are higher than what consistent cooperators would obtain, without any compensating attempt to “even things out” later. Occasional contributions are too small and too infrequent to be read as a strong fairness-driven correction. If any fairness norm is present, it is likely a very low bar—perhaps a sense that contributing a little sometimes is sufficient to feel justified free-riding most of the time. Overall, their operative norm appears closer to “take advantage of the public good while others provide it” than to “share burdens equitably.” Risk tolerance: The player is comfortable relying on others to fund the public good, which implies a willingness to ride on group cooperation without insuring against the possibility that others might sharply reduce their contributions. This leans toward higher social risk tolerance: they accept the risk of group under-provision in exchange for immediate personal gain. At the same time, the occasional non-zero contributions are moderate rather than extreme, suggesting they are not inclined to gamble on very high personal sacrifices for potentially higher group returns. They seem to value immediate, secure private payoff more than uncertain increases in collective benefit. Incentives and strategy: The player behaves like a payoff-focused opportunist. Their strategy can be summarized as: default to free-riding, occasionally contribute modestly when group cooperation is strong, but never enough to sacrifice their clear payoff advantage. They appear to understand that the public-good mechanism allows them to benefit from others’ generosity and they exploit this reliably. The sporadic contributions may serve to test whether small sacrifices materially improve outcomes or to maintain a minimal self-image as a contributor, but they never signal a shift to a cooperative type. Beliefs about others (inferred): The consistent reliance on others’ contributions implies that the player expects a substantial portion of the group to keep contributing even in the presence of visible free-riding. Their willingness to free-ride repeatedly suggests they do not strongly fear collective collapse, or at least they prioritize short-run personal gain over that risk. Occasional contributions when the group is performing well suggest a belief that the group is robust enough that they can “chip in a bit” without needing to be a pillar of cooperation. Their behavior does not indicate faith in strict reciprocity norms or strong punishment, as they do not preemptively cooperate to avoid sanctions. Social/personality style (inferred from decisions only): The player presents as individually rational and advantage-seeking, with limited pro-social motivation. They are not purely ruthless: they do contribute occasionally and never at extreme zero across all rounds. But their pattern shows more calculation than solidarity. They seem comfortable being a net beneficiary of others’ kindness and do not appear driven by guilt or strong identity as a cooperator. They are pragmatic, restrained in generosity, and unlikely to volunteer burdensome contributions without strong personal incentive. Learning/adaptation: The player does not show a strong learning trajectory toward either full cooperation or full defection; instead they maintain a predominantly non-contributory stance throughout. The few non-zero contributions look more like small-scale adjustments or probes than durable shifts. They do not escalate contributions in response to high cooperation, nor do they systematically reduce from some early generous baseline. This suggests that their initial orientation is already near their perceived optimum, and experience mainly confirms rather than transforms their approach. Adaptation is present but shallow: they tweak, they do not fundamentally revise. Overall type label (summary): A “strategic free-rider with minimal conditional cooperation” who primarily maximizes personal payoff by exploiting the public good, occasionally offering small contributions when the group is already doing well, but never adopting a genuinely cooperative or fairness-driven stance.

This player is a moderately prosocial, conditionally cooperative contributor who settles into a mid-to-high, relatively stable contribution pattern. Contributions consistently stay in the middle of the available range, with only modest variation rather than sharp jumps or collapses. They do not fully free-ride even when others contribute very little, and they do not consistently match the highest contributors either. Instead, they position themselves around or somewhat above the apparent group average when cooperation is moderate to high, and they maintain roughly similar levels when cooperation weakens, suggesting a personal contribution norm that only partially tracks others’ behavior. Their behavior indicates a preference for maintaining cooperation without sacrificing all personal payoff. They appear willing to give up some earnings to sustain the public fund but seem unwilling to push toward the maximum contribution unless the group strongly coordinates at high levels, which is not the case here. This suggests a “bounded cooperator” profile: they cooperate, but within limits that preserve self-interest. Adjustment across rounds is present but mild, suggesting low volatility and gradual adaptation rather than reactive swings. After periods of higher group contributions, they tend to remain in a similar or slightly elevated range; after group contributions fall, they reduce somewhat or hold steady, rather than punishing the group with near-zero contributions. This indicates conditional cooperation that is tempered by a stable internal reference point rather than pure mimicry. They seem to follow a fairness norm where contributing something substantial is important, but strict equality with others does not appear to be a binding standard. They neither systematically undercut the group to exploit it nor over-contribute to compensate for low cooperators. Instead, they likely aim for what they perceive as a “reasonable share,” balancing fairness to others with fairness to themselves. Risk tolerance appears moderate. Given a marginal per-capita return below 1, a purely payoff-maximizing, risk-averse player would tend to drastically lower contributions, anticipating others might under-contribute. This player nevertheless maintains non-trivial contributions throughout, implying tolerance for the risk that others may not reciprocate, but they still avoid the extremes (never going to full contributions or full defection). This is consistent with someone who values cooperation and group benefit but remains cautious about being exploited. Overall, this player behaves as a steady, moderately generous conditional cooperator with a relatively stable internal benchmark for “enough” contribution, modest responsiveness to others, and a balanced concern for both fairness and personal payoff.

This player follows an adaptive, opportunistic contribution strategy that oscillates between free-riding and moderately high cooperation, with clear responsiveness to group behavior but no stable cooperative norm. They begin by contributing well above what a pure payoff-maximizer would choose in a linear PGG of this type, indicating initial openness to cooperation and at least some concern for group efficiency or fairness. However, once they observe that others are willing to contribute substantially, they quickly shift toward zero contributions and maintain that stance for an extended stretch. This indicates a strong willingness to exploit the cooperative environment once they see that the public good is being provided without their help. Their contributions are volatile across the game rather than stable: they move from relatively high to zero, then back up, then to full contribution, and finally to zero again. This suggests they are experimenting with different contribution levels, likely tracking how their own payoffs respond to shifts in their behavior against the backdrop of others’ generally high contributions. The pattern is consistent with a player who learns that free-riding is profitable, but still periodically tests whether cooperating again is either personally beneficial or socially “safe.” They are clearly conditional in practice, but the condition is not simple “match the group” cooperation. They sometimes cooperate when others cooperate, but also sometimes defect while others remain generous. This pattern suggests that their condition is payoff- and trend-based rather than purely reciprocity-based: they appear comfortable riding on others’ ongoing contributions once they believe the group will sustain the public good, but occasionally increase contributions when the environment changes or when they may be probing for the effect of their choice. They do not display a strong, consistent fairness norm. At some points they accept large personal gains from exploiting generous co-players without increasing their own contributions. At other points they themselves become a high or even maximal contributor, temporarily bearing more cost than others. This mix suggests that fairness concerns, if present, are secondary to instrumental and exploratory motives. They tolerate being a clear free-rider when it is profitable, which is incompatible with a strict inequality-averse or egalitarian type. In terms of risk tolerance, they show willingness to take payoff risks by occasionally contributing a lot in an environment where others’ future behavior is uncertain and where they cannot know how many rounds remain. Contributing the full endowment at one point especially suggests they are not purely risk-averse in monetary terms. These high-contribution deviations are short-lived, however, implying that they update quickly if such behavior is not clearly rewarded (in the sense of yielding especially high payoffs versus free-riding). Overall, this player is best characterized as an exploratory opportunist: they are not locked into either pure defection or consistent cooperation, but instead adjust contributions to probe the incentive structure and to profit from others’ cooperation whenever possible. Their behavior implies that in new PGG environments, they will likely start with some willingness to contribute, then shift toward lower contributions if the group appears reliably cooperative and especially if they cannot be punished, while occasionally revisiting higher contributions to test whether conditions or returns have changed.

This player is a moderately cooperative but highly adaptive contributor who alternates between generous, moderate, and free-riding choices. Their typical contribution level sits around the group average or slightly above when they perceive others as cooperative, but they are willing to drop to zero when they see ample contributions from others and the environment does not allow punishment or reward. They do not follow a simple increasing or decreasing trend; instead, their behavior is volatile and context-sensitive, toggling between extremes and mid-range values. They appear to condition contributions on recent group behavior: when others are broadly contributing high amounts, they sometimes match or exceed that pattern, but they also occasionally exploit it by contributing nothing while benefiting from the group’s generosity. When contributions by others appear mixed or lower, they often respond with mid-level contributions rather than fully defecting or fully cooperating. This suggests a flexible, payoff-aware strategy rather than a rigid fairness rule. The player does not consistently enforce a strict norm of equal contribution; they tolerate the presence of low contributors and free riders and at times act as a free rider themselves. Their choices indicate a willingness to trade off fairness for personal gain in a setting where sanctions and reputational consequences are absent. Risk-wise, they are comfortable taking payoff risks on both ends: sometimes sacrificing personal coins to support the public fund, other times risking social inefficiency by contributing nothing. Overall, they are best described as a conditional, opportunistic cooperator who is sensitive to the contribution climate but not bound by a strong internal fairness or consistency norm.

This player is a moderately volatile conditional cooperator who mixes sincere contribution with opportunistic free-riding. They do not settle into a stable “always cooperate” or “always defect” pattern; instead, they oscillate between low, medium, and full contributions. Across the game, they both fully free-ride and fully contribute multiple times, indicating flexibility rather than a fixed rule. They clearly respond to others’ behavior. When the group appears generously cooperative, they sometimes exploit the situation by contributing nothing while benefiting from the public fund. At other times, in similarly cooperative environments, they match or exceed typical contributions, suggesting they are not a pure egoist but are willing to sustain high provision some of the time. They also contribute meaningfully in environments where others contribute less. In such states, they still put in nontrivial amounts instead of uniformly dropping to zero, which points to at least a partial internal norm of “doing their share” even when the immediate monetary return on each contributed coin is low. However, this norm is not rigid: they are willing to revert to taking everything for themselves when it is profitable and the group looks especially cooperative, showing that fairness considerations coexist with payoff-seeking. Their contribution levels span the full range (none, partial, and all), and they switch between these levels several times. This indicates experimentation and sensitivity to context rather than consistent adherence to any single fairness rule (such as “always match the group average” or “always split evenly”). They seem to accept substantial risk to their own per-round payoff when they make large contributions, especially in less cooperative contexts, implying at least moderate tolerance for outcome variability and a willingness to “test” whether cooperation is reciprocated. Overall, this player behaves like a payoff-conscious conditional cooperator: they value mutual cooperation and sometimes act as a strong contributor, but they are quite ready to free-ride on others’ generosity when the environment looks favorable and unpunished. Their guiding principle appears to be a shifting balance between reciprocity, experimentation, and opportunistic self-interest, rather than a fixed fairness or efficiency norm.

This player is an unwavering full cooperator. They always allocate their entire endowment to the public fund without deviation, regardless of how others behave or how payoffs turn out. Their contribution pattern is perfectly stable and shows no signs of conditional cooperation, retaliation, or strategic withholding; they do not adjust upward or downward in response to changes in group contributions or to visible free-riding. Their behavior is consistent with a strong internal norm of contributing “what is socially best” rather than “what is individually optimal.” The public-good multiplier is too low to make full contribution payoff-maximizing from a purely self-interested perspective, yet they still contribute fully, suggesting that short-term monetary gain is not their sole or primary objective. They appear willing to accept comparatively low payoffs when others contribute little, indicating high tolerance for being exploited and low sensitivity to inequality that disfavors them. There is no evidence that they attempt to forecast others’ contributions or to condition their own on group averages. Instead, they implement a simple, rigid rule: always contribute everything. This suggests a rule-based or identity-based strategy (“I am a full cooperator”) rather than a responsive, outcome-contingent strategy. They do not engage in “teaching” behavior via contribution changes (e.g., lowering contributions to signal displeasure), even when free-riding is observable and persistent. Their fairness norm appears to emphasize doing their own perceived fair share irrespective of others’ choices, rather than demanding reciprocity or proportionality. They behave as if the right action is to maximize the group’s potential benefit, even when others do not reciprocate. From a risk perspective, they exhibit high social risk tolerance: they repeatedly place their payoff at the mercy of others’ cooperation. From a strategic-risk perspective, they are conservative in the sense of not experimenting or exploring alternative contribution levels; once they adopt full contribution, they never test other options. Overall, this player is best modeled as a principled, unconditional cooperator: they consistently sacrifice private payoff for the public good, do not adapt to exploitation, and follow a stable, norm-driven contribution rule independent of environmental feedback.

The player is a primarily free-riding, payoff-maximizing type with occasional, limited cooperation. They almost always keep their endowment and rely on others’ contributions. Their default is to contribute nothing; contributions occur in a minority of rounds and do not follow a simple monotonic trend (no pure “start-high-then-decline” pattern). Instead, contributions appear sporadically, often when overall group contribution levels are high or rising, suggesting a weak, opportunistic conditional cooperation: they are willing to join in when cooperation is already strong and the personal cost is offset by large group returns. They do not appear motivated by fairness toward consistent contributors; they repeatedly exploit cooperative environments by defecting while others continue to give. At the same time, they are not a pure, inflexible defector: their occasional full contributions indicate they are not ideologically opposed to cooperating and can tolerate short-term personal sacrifice when group payoffs look especially strong or stable. Risk tolerance with respect to cooperation is low. They rarely “invest” by contributing when others’ behavior is noisy or declining. Instead, they seem to wait for clear cooperative conditions or mid/late-game high-contribution phases before contributing, minimizing the risk of paying costs into an uncooperative group. There is no evidence of inequity aversion in the form of “compensatory” contributions after having benefited unusually much from others’ efforts; they do not systematically increase giving after earning high payoffs from free-riding. Overall, the contribution pattern is best described as a strategic, mostly self-interested free-rider who occasionally cooperates when doing so is relatively safe and embedded in a cooperative environment, rather than out of strong norm-following or fairness concerns.

This player is a calculated, alternating free-rider rather than a pure cooperator or pure defector. They consistently oscillate between contributing and not contributing across the whole game instead of following a monotone trend. Their contributions are binary (forced by design) but their pattern is mixed: they sometimes match a cooperative environment with cooperation, and sometimes exploit high contributions by others with defection. They display clear payoff sensitivity: when others contribute at a high rate, they are willing to free-ride for a while, capturing higher payoffs than consistent contributors. When the environment becomes more mixed or contributions fall, they are willing to step in and contribute, even at a personal cost relative to free-riding, which suggests they recognize the efficiency of the public good and are not purely selfish in the short run. Their pattern does not show rigid tit-for-tat with any single peer; instead it looks like a coarse-grained, “state-based” conditionality: in generally cooperative states, they free-ride more; in more unstable or low-contribution states, they increase their own contributions. They do not converge to a stable high- or low-contribution regime; rather, they keep using both actions throughout, including in the final rounds. This implies a relatively high tolerance for outcome variability and a willingness to experiment or remain flexible instead of locking in a single strategy. There is no evidence of strict fairness norms (e.g., always matching the group average or punishing low contributors via own behavior). They are comfortable earning more than those who contribute when the group is cooperative and also sometimes earning less than non-contributors when they themselves choose to contribute. That combination is consistent with a pragmatic, payoff‑aware type rather than a principled fairness maximizer. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they do not always chase the locally highest payoff (defect in highly cooperative environments) but they also do not avoid that move; they balance periods of exploitation with periods of contribution, perhaps to keep the group productive enough to sustain high returns. Overall, they function as a “strategic mixed contributor”: they understand that cooperation is socially efficient and occasionally support it, but they opportunistically exploit cooperative phases with defection and are not bound by a rigid moral rule to always contribute.

This player is a highly stable, unconditional full cooperator under the given binary-choice structure. They always contribute their entire endowment and never deviate, regardless of others’ behavior, group composition, or payoffs. There is no observable conditionality on average group contribution, no tit-for-tat pattern, and no visible attempt to exploit low-contributing periods. They tolerate persistent free-riding by others without retaliating through defection. Given that the marginal per capita return is greater than 1, this pattern is payoff-maximizing for the group and individually rational for a risk-neutral or moderately risk-averse player who trusts the mechanism. Their refusal to switch to defection despite repeated instances where defectors earn strictly more in the same round suggests either (a) a strong prosocial or efficiency-oriented preference, (b) a rule-based strategy (“I always contribute”) that they maintain regardless of local incentives, or (c) low sensitivity to short-run disadvantage from free-riders. Which of these dominates is unknown, but the consistency is high. They appear comfortable bearing inequality in disadvantageous directions: they repeatedly end up with lower per-round payoffs than some defectors and still maintain the same contribution level. This indicates high inequality tolerance when inequality arises from their own cooperative choice, and low concern for strict payoff-maximization in the short term. There is no sign of strategic volatility, experimentation, or threshold behavior; once set, their contribution rule remains fixed.

This player is a high, nearly unconditional cooperator who strongly favors contributing the full endowment. Across the game they almost always give the maximum allowed contribution and switch to non-contribution only once. That lone deviation toward free-riding follows multiple instances where non-contributors earn more than contributors, suggesting a brief experiment with exploitation rather than a random error. Their default appears to be: “contribute fully unless I have a strong reason not to.” They are not finely conditional on short-term fluctuations in others’ behavior; they continue to cooperate even when many group members free-ride and when their own payoff is clearly lower than that of defectors. This indicates that they prioritize maintaining cooperation over short-run payoff maximization. They show tolerance for payoff inequality against themselves: they repeatedly accept earning less than free-riders while still contributing. This points to either (a) a payoff-insensitive cooperative norm (“I should do the socially efficient thing”) or (b) a belief that persistent cooperation is the best long-run response even in the face of some exploitation. Their risk tolerance is moderate to high: they repeatedly choose the payoff-dominant but individually risky cooperative option despite clear evidence that others often defect. Volatility in contributions is extremely low. Apart from one early free-riding trial, their behavior is stable and consistent with a strong internal rule. They do not visibly calibrate contributions up and down in response to changing group averages; instead, they maintain cooperation through both high- and low-contribution environments. This is characteristic of a “norm-following cooperator” rather than a strategic, fine-tuned conditional cooperator. In fairness terms, they are willing to be “more fair” than the group around them, shouldering the cost of cooperation even when others do not. There is no evidence that they seek to match the modal behavior or to mirror defectors directly; instead, they uphold a personal standard of full cooperation that is only weakly sensitive to exploitation.

This player is a calculated, alternating cooperator–defector whose primary objective is to maximize personal payoff while keeping the public-good mechanism viable enough that others continue to contribute. They reliably use both available actions (full contribution or full defection) rather than sticking to a single mode. Their pattern shows: - Frequent switches between contributing and free-riding rather than a monotone trend, indicating active experimentation and conditionality rather than a fixed rule. - Willingness to cooperate even when others’ average contributions are modest, which suggests they understand that the multiplier makes group contribution socially efficient and are not purely short-term myopic. - Equally, repeated episodes of defecting when others are contributing at high levels, taking advantage of the public good. This shows comfort with exploiting cooperative environments when it is clearly profitable. - No strict matching rule (e.g., they do not just “copy” the modal behavior): sometimes they contribute when many defect, and sometimes they defect when most cooperate. This implies they track payoffs and strategic advantage more than simple reciprocity. - Fairness norms appear weak or highly instrumental: they tolerate large payoff advantages over others without any corrective behavior and also accept relative disadvantages when cooperating if they see future or aggregate benefit. They seem more payoff-oriented than inequality-averse. - Risk tolerance is moderate: they are willing to “bet” on cooperation even when others are partially free-riding, but they do not lock into a high-cooperation stance; instead, they periodically pull back to defection to secure higher private returns. Overall, this is a payoff-sensitive, opportunistic conditional cooperator who mixes cooperation and defection to balance personal gain against the danger of collapsing the public good.

This player is a volatile, strategically experimental contributor who oscillates between low and high contributions rather than settling into a stable pattern. They are clearly not a pure free-rider or a pure unconditional cooperator. Early on, they match or exceed the apparent emerging cooperative norm at times, but they also drop to very low contributions even when many others remain highly cooperative. Their choices suggest conditionality, but not in a simple “match-the-group-average” way; instead, they seem to test both cooperative and self-regarding strategies across the game. They appears comfortable deviating from group norms in both directions. They sometimes contribute substantially more than the lowest contributors when many others are giving little, and they also under-contribute relative to most of the group in other periods. This indicates relatively high risk tolerance and a willingness to accept social or payoff uncertainty in pursuit of learning or opportunistic gains. There is no consistent evidence of a strict fairness norm such as always matching the median or punishing low contributors via reduced own contributions. Instead, their behavior fits a “mixed-motive strategist”: they care about payoff and occasionally about sustaining cooperation, but they are prepared to exploit cooperative states by contributing little while others contribute more. Contribution changes over time suggest some learning and adaptation to others’ apparent willingness to cooperate, but it does not converge on a single simple rule; they experiment with a range of contribution levels throughout the game.

This player is a predominantly self-interested, opportunistic low contributor who occasionally experiments with moderate giving. Their default is to free‑ride or near–free‑ride, keeping most or all of their endowment even when others contribute high amounts. They never contribute at or above the prevailing high-contribution norm; when their contribution is relatively high for them, it is still below the most cooperative players. Contributions fluctuate rather than follow a stable trend, suggesting exploratory or reactive behavior rather than a fixed rule. There is no clear evidence that they directly condition their contribution on specific others’ contributions beyond a weak tendency to increase somewhat when cooperative norms are very strong, and to drop back when norms weaken. They appear largely unconcerned with fairness in the sense of matching others’ effort; instead they prioritize maintaining high personal payoffs while taking advantage of others’ generosity. Risk tolerance in contributing is low: even their “higher” contributions remain moderate, and they frequently revert to minimal giving after seeing that they can earn more by keeping coins.

This player is a persistently high contributor who treats near-full contribution as the default. They repeatedly give the entire endowment, with a single clear deviation to a mid-level contribution when group contributions have already become noisy and less cooperative. Their contributions do not track short-run changes in others’ behavior: they keep cooperating even when several others give very little. This suggests a strong internal norm or preference for cooperation rather than a finely tuned conditional strategy. They are not strictly “blind altruist,” however: the one drop to a lower amount indicates they are aware of the environment and willing to test some self-protection once the pattern of others’ free-riding is established. There is no apparent attempt to match the group average, target equality, or punish low contributors via their own under-contribution. Overall, they behave as a robust, norm-driven cooperator with minor, late adjustment rather than a payoff-maximizing free-rider.

This player is a moderately cooperative, payoff-conscious contributor with some conditionality but no clear push toward full free-riding or full altruism. They keep their contribution at a stable, intermediate level for much of the game, rarely matching the highest contributors even when many others give a lot. They also do not drop to zero when others defect, instead trimming their own contribution modestly. This pattern suggests they value contributing to the public good but want to maintain a relatively favorable private payoff and avoid being the “sucker” who contributes the most. They appear somewhat responsive to the general contribution climate: when group contributions are high on average, they are comfortable remaining a mid-level contributor rather than exploiting heavily; when others’ contributions weaken, they reduce their own somewhat, but not to the lowest levels seen in the group. Their behavior is consistent with a cautious conditional cooperator who benchmarks around an intermediate standard rather than matching the group’s top or bottom. The player shows no evidence of strongly enforced fairness norms (e.g., they do not systematically match the group mean or anchor on equal-contribution ideals); instead, they occupy a relatively self-protective but not aggressively exploitative middle ground. Risk tolerance in contribution appears moderate: they are willing to put nontrivial amounts into the public good even when some group members defect, but they do not escalate to high contributions to “save” the group.

This player is a generally high contributor who increasingly leans toward cooperative, above-average giving, but with some early experimentation and mild volatility. They start below what later emerges as their typical level, then quickly move into a pattern of medium-to-high and then consistently high contributions. They do not slavishly match others’ exact behavior but appear broadly conditional: their giving is higher in cooperative environments and dips at least once when the group’s overall contributions are relatively low or erratic. They seem to accept personal payoff sacrifices in order to sustain or elevate group contributions, suggesting that they treat “doing their part” as a norm even when many others free-ride. They also tolerate being among the highest contributors toward the end, indicating relatively low risk aversion regarding being exploited and a willingness to act as a cooperation anchor rather than purely follow the crowd.

This player is a strong but not unconditional cooperator, best described as an “altruistic enforcer who occasionally tests the boundaries.” They usually contribute the full endowment and align their behavior with a clear norm that everyone should contribute fully. Their baseline preference is to sustain high group efficiency rather than maximize short-term personal payoff. Their contribution pattern is largely stable and high, with only a single clear deviation where they contribute nothing. That deviation comes after multiple uses of punishment and after the group has already demonstrated it can coordinate on high contributions. This suggests they understand the payoff structure and are willing to briefly free-ride once cooperation feels established, possibly to test whether they can gain extra without immediately damaging the norm. Their cooperation appears conditional on others’ adherence to a high standard rather than on strict equality. They continue to contribute fully even when others reduce their contributions somewhat, instead using punishment as a response mechanism. This indicates they prioritize maintaining the norm via sanctions rather than retaliating by lowering their own contributions. They show tolerance for short-term personal loss in exchange for enforcing a cooperative norm and seem comfortable with the strategic risk that others may not fully understand the game or may deviate. When they briefly free-ride, they do not continue in that pattern; they quickly return to full contributions once the group is back on track, implying that norm maintenance remains their default. Their fairness norm is: “everyone should give the maximum, and those who give very little or nothing deserve sanctions.” They do not visibly fine-tune contribution levels or attempt sophisticated optimization; instead, they commit to a simple, high-cooperation rule and enforce it.

This player is a mostly full-contributor conditional cooperator with mild end-game and frustration-driven deviations. They quickly infer that universal full contribution maximizes group and individual payoffs and act on that belief. When the group appears coordinated and others’ contributions are high, they reliably contribute the maximum. They explicitly endorse a norm that everyone should give the full amount every time so that all benefit, indicating a clear efficiency and fairness orientation. Their contributions are not completely rigid: they occasionally reduce their own contribution when others deviate or when the environment feels uncertain or unstable. These partial deviations are modest rather than extreme, suggesting they still value group welfare but momentarily relax their own standard, likely in response to perceived unfairness or confusion. They do not attempt to secretly free-ride; even their lower contributions remain within a cooperative range rather than dropping to zero. Overall, they prioritize high cooperation, are willing to incur personal cost to maintain efficient outcomes, and only slightly shade contributions down when norms are violated or when the end of the game may be approaching (low confidence on exact motive for final deviations).

The player is a strongly cooperative contributor with one notable deviation. Their default pattern is to allocate their entire endowment to the public fund and to maintain this even when others reduce contributions or when they themselves have been punished. This indicates a stable prosocial orientation, low sensitivity to short-term exploitation, and a preference for efficiency and group welfare over immediate personal gain. They are not purely unconditional, however: they contribute nothing in one instance despite unanimous high contributions by others. The context offers no direct explanation for this choice; it may reflect confusion, inattention, a misclick, brief experimentation with free-riding, or an attempt to see how others respond. Since they immediately resume full contributions afterward and do not shift toward a lower, conditional pattern, this deviation appears situational rather than a strategic pivot. The player does not visibly adjust contribution levels in response to variation in peers’ behavior or chat proposals; they continue to give the maximum even when others under-contribute or when they are punished. This suggests either: (a) a strong and somewhat rigid fairness norm that “everyone should give the maximum,” which they personally uphold regardless of others’ compliance, or (b) limited engagement with strategic conditionality and a tendency to “set and forget” a cooperative strategy once chosen. They show no overt signs of risk aversion regarding being exploited: they persist with maximum contributions even after being heavily punished when free-riding would be financially safer. Their actions fit a “principled cooperator” or “efficiency-focused cooperator” profile, albeit with one unexplained defection whose motive is unknown.

This player is a high, almost unconditional contributor. They repeatedly give the maximum possible amount to the group and only briefly reduce their contribution once when the group’s overall cooperation and average contributions have clearly fallen. Their default norm is that full contribution is the “right” baseline, and they continue to cooperate even after they personally receive substantial net losses from engaging in punishment and reward. They do not appear to condition their own giving tightly on individual others’ past behavior; instead, they seem to track the general cooperation climate and make one temporary adjustment when cooperation has deteriorated. They tolerate personal payoff sacrifices to maintain a cooperative environment and do not visibly chase short‑term individual profit. Risk tolerance appears high: they invest heavily in the public good without clear assurance that others will match them, and they persist in this even after observing some free riding and mid‑range contributors. They show a fairness norm focused more on “everyone should contribute generously” than on strict proportional reciprocity.

This player is a high, near-consistent contributor who treats full contribution as the default. They usually give their entire endowment to the public good and only occasionally shade their contribution slightly downward. Their behavior suggests a strong cooperative norm and a preference for maintaining a high-contribution environment, even when others reduce their own giving. They do not appear highly reactive or vengeful in their contributions: they keep contributing a lot even when some group members free-ride or drop to very low levels. There is some mild downward adjustment in response to generally lower group contributions, but they never move toward outright free-riding. Risk tolerance is moderate: they are willing to invest heavily in the public good despite uncertainty about others’ future behavior. Fairness norms seem focused on “everyone should contribute roughly high amounts,” with special attention to extreme low contributors and non-contributors as norm violators.

This player is a generally high contributor with moderate responsiveness to the group. They alternate between full and half endowment contributions rather than using fine-grained levels, suggesting a simple heuristic rather than precise optimization. Contributions stay well above the marginally selfish best response, indicating pro-social or efficiency-oriented motives. They reduce their contribution after observing that others’ average contributions are somewhat below full cooperation, but they do not collapse to very low or zero contributions, which implies tolerance for moderate free-riding and an interest in sustaining the public good even when others underprovide. When the group appears more coordinated at higher contribution levels, they return to full contributions, which is consistent with conditional cooperation: they prefer to match or slightly exceed what they perceive as the emerging cooperation standard, but are not rigidly unconditional. They appear willing to sacrifice personal payoff to keep contributions high and stable, and they do not seem particularly risk-averse about being exploited, given that they keep contributing substantially in the presence of some lower contributors.

This player is a generally high contributor with one notable deviation. Most of the time, they give the full endowment or close to it, indicating a strong baseline preference for supporting the public good, even when the marginal per-capita return is below 1. This pattern suggests either a cooperative norm orientation or a belief that high contributions will be reciprocated and sustained by others. They do not simply match the group average; they typically meet or exceed it, which points toward a leadership or “moral anchor” role rather than conditional cooperation in a narrow sense. There is a single sharp drop to a very low contribution while others remain moderate contributors. This looks like a purposeful test or expression of dissatisfaction rather than random noise: it comes after they have spent substantial resources on enforcing norms and after persistent low contributions by specific players continue. That deviation does not persist: they immediately return to high contributions. This “one-shot defection” suggests they are willing to briefly prioritize self-interest or send a signal, but they are not fundamentally opportunistic or chronically free-riding. They do not appear highly risk-averse with respect to payoffs: they repeatedly invest heavily in cooperation and costly enforcement despite already earning decent returns, implying a willingness to trade off some income for maintaining a cooperative environment and enforcing norms. Their fairness norm appears to be: “everyone should contribute at least at a solid, near-high level; very low contributors deserve sanctions, and very high contributors or stable cooperators deserve support.” Overall, the contribution pattern fits a “principled cooperator who occasionally tests or protests” rather than a strictly payoff-maximizing or purely conditional cooperator.

This player is a generally high contributor who occasionally shades down to medium contributions when group norms weaken. They start with full contributions and repeatedly return to them, indicating a strong baseline preference for cooperative, efficiency-enhancing behavior. When the overall contribution environment deteriorates, they are willing to lower their own contribution somewhat but do not move to outright free-riding. Their behavior suggests a conditional cooperator with a relatively high internal standard: they expect others to contribute at least in the mid-to-high range and personally uphold that norm most of the time. They appear comfortable accepting some payoff risk to maintain cooperation; they do not systematically track short-term profit maximization and instead behave as if sustaining a high-contribution norm is important.

This player is a mixed, somewhat erratic cooperator who alternates between full cooperation and full free-riding. They never choose intermediate amounts, only the extremes allowed. Their pattern does not settle into a stable rule like “always match the majority” or “always defect after being punished”; instead they switch several times between contributing everything and contributing nothing. They sometimes contribute fully even when others mostly free-ride, and sometimes free-ride when others contribute, suggesting weak or inconsistent adherence to fairness norms. They do not appear to prioritize payoff maximization in a straightforward way: they willingly choose full contribution in situations where it is clearly individually costly and socially unrewarded. Their risk tolerance regarding potential exploitation is moderate to high, as they are repeatedly willing to expose themselves to being the sole or minority contributor without securing reciprocal behavior from others.

The player is a **high baseline cooperator with a late, strategic defection**. For most of the game, the player consistently chooses the fully cooperative option, regardless of variation in others’ behavior. This suggests a strong initial preference for maximization of group efficiency and/or adherence to a cooperation norm rather than short-term self-interest. Cooperation appears **only weakly conditional** on others’ individual deviations in the early and middle stages. When one peer free-rides while the rest cooperate, the player still maintains full contribution and does not immediately adjust downward. This indicates that a single or small number of defections do not instantly overturn their cooperative stance; they seem willing to “carry” some unfairness in the short run. The player’s final-round behavior departs sharply from this pattern: they switch from full cooperation to full free-riding when the game horizon is immediate and known. This demonstrates clear **end-game rationality and horizon awareness**: when future reciprocity or reputation cannot influence outcomes, they choose the individually profitable option. The pattern suggests that their cooperation is not purely unconditional or altruistic; it is at least partly **strategic and time-contingent**. In terms of fairness, the player tolerates temporary inequality (being worse off than some others) while the game is ongoing, but is not so fairness-driven that they sacrifice in the last round once continuation value disappears. Risk tolerance is **unknown** beyond this: the environment offers only discrete contribution choices with no obvious risk gradient, so it is unclear whether they are risk-seeking or risk-averse in general. Overall, the player behaves like a **norm-following, efficiency-oriented cooperator who is willing to defect once future interactions clearly end**.

This player is a strict, unconditionally cooperative maximizer under these parameters. They always contribute their full endowment and never deviate, even when the game allows late-round defection and everyone can see the horizon. Their behavior is perfectly stable and shows no experimentation between contributing nothing and contributing everything. This indicates that they prioritize the group-efficient outcome over individual short-term gain and either correctly understands or strongly intuits that full cooperation is payoff-maximizing in this setting. They do not appear to condition their contributions on others’ past behavior; they continue to cooperate even though they cannot observe anyone deviating. This suggests a default expectation that others will match the cooperative norm, or a willingness to stick to that norm regardless. There is no observable risk-taking in the sense of trying alternative strategies; instead, they display risk tolerance by trusting that others will keep contributing and by not hedging against potential future defection. Their fairness norm aligns with “everyone contributes fully and shares equally,” and they act consistently with that norm throughout.

This player is an unwavering full cooperator. They always contribute the entire endowment in every situation presented. Their behavior is completely stable: they never probe lower contributions, never hedge, and show no strategic experimentation. Cooperation is unconditional within the observed environment: they continue to contribute fully even when others voice suspicion that the situation might change or tempt defection. They appear comfortable aligning with the group norm when the group is cooperative, and they do not seek to individually optimize short-term payoffs by free-riding, despite clear monetary incentives to do so. Normatively, they seem to accept full contribution as the correct or natural rule of play rather than as a fragile agreement that needs constant renegotiation. Risk tolerance is oriented toward trusting the cooperative outcome: they are willing to stake their entire endowment repeatedly on the assumption that everyone will continue to contribute, and they maintain that even as the end of the game approaches, when incentives to defect are strongest.

This player is a strict, unconditional full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment, never experimenting with holding back, even when several others free-ride or cooperation temporarily collapses. Their behavior indicates a strong internal norm that full contribution is the “right” or default action, not something contingent on others’ immediate choices. They show high tolerance for payoff risk: they keep investing fully into the public good despite visible drops in group efficiency and persistent free-riding by a subset of players. They do not appear to condition their cooperation on punishment activity or on the presence/absence of deviations; they maintain the same cooperative choice under both perfect cooperation and mixed cooperation environments. Their fairness orientation is therefore cooperative but non-conditional: they prioritize maximizing the group’s potential surplus rather than adjusting to maintain payoff equality. Learning or strategic updating in contribution behavior is absent or minimal; they do not test lower contributions even when there are repeated defections from others.

This player is a strict, unwavering full cooperator. They always contribute the full endowment in every observed decision, with no adjustment in response to others’ deviations or to changing incentives over time. Their behavior suggests a strong internal norm that the “right” or “best” strategy is for everyone to contribute maximally to the public good, and they personally adhere to this regardless of short-term disadvantage when others free-ride or reduce contributions. They do not condition their own contribution on others’ previous behavior, showing no evidence of strategic reciprocity, tit-for-tat, or opportunistic defection, even when others repeatedly earn more by contributing less. Fairness in payoff terms appears secondary to maintaining the cooperative norm; they tolerate being exploited rather than violating their own pattern of maximal contribution. Their risk tolerance for being taken advantage of is high, and they show no end-game drop in contributions, indicating they are not playing a backward-induction or finite-horizon strategy. Overall, they fit a “principled unconditional cooperator” type: stable, norm-driven, and non-contingent.

This player is a strongly prosocial, near-unconditional full cooperator. They almost always contribute their entire endowment, even when others free-ride or when cooperation temporarily breaks down. The only clear deviation is a single instance of contributing nothing while still publicly advocating full contributions from others; this looks more like an isolated lapse or experiment than a strategic shift, because they immediately return to contributing fully and maintain cooperative messaging. Their behavior indicates that they use a fairness and efficiency norm rather than narrow self-interest: they treat full contribution as the default “right” action, stress that this setup makes everyone better off when all contribute, and keep contributing fully even when a few others defect. They show high tolerance for inequality: they continue to give even when some players persistently take, and they do not retaliate by reducing their own contributions in response. They appear risk-tolerant regarding exploitation: they are willing to risk being taken advantage of to maintain group-level gains and a cooperative atmosphere. They do condition their expectations on others: when everyone is cooperating, they express satisfaction and celebrate; when a few individuals defect, they call them out and encourage them to join. However, this conditionality affects their verbal responses, not their actual contribution level, which stays maximal. They show stable, consistent behavior over time, suggesting a rule like “always give everything; try to pull others up to that level” rather than a fine-tuned, payoff-maximizing strategy. Overall, their contribution profile fits a “committed group-benefit maximizer”: they prioritize the group outcome and fairness norm, accept some personal payoff loss, and do not systematically exploit opportunities to free-ride.

This player is a strongly cooperative, near-unconditional full contributor with rare, unexplained deviations. They almost always contribute their entire endowment, even when others free‑ride or coordination is imperfect. Their choice to give appears stable, not tightly contingent on short‑term fluctuations in others’ behavior. They keep contributing fully through both high‑cooperation and mixed‑cooperation phases. There are two instances where they keep the whole endowment despite the group’s clear cooperative norm. These deviations do not come with any explanation in chat and are not followed by a longer shift toward selfishness; they immediately revert to full contributions thereafter. This pattern suggests occasional opportunistic or exploratory defection (or possible momentary inattention), rather than a strategic conditional‑cooperator profile. They tolerate persistent free‑riding by a small minority without retaliating via lower contributions. They keep playing the cooperative strategy even when it is not payoff‑maximizing in a narrow, myopic sense, implying a preference for norm adherence and group efficiency over short‑term individual gain. They show no evidence of complex trigger strategies (e.g., grim trigger, tit‑for‑tat). Their behavior is best described as: “default to full cooperation; stay there even if some others misbehave; occasionally defect in isolated rounds without altering the overall cooperative stance.” Risk-wise, they accept the risk that others might not contribute, and still put in the full amount. That is consistent with relatively high risk tolerance in service of collective payoff, or with a simple heuristic of “always give” once they understand the payoff structure. They align with fairness in the sense of wanting everyone to benefit from the multiplier; however, they do not adjust their own contributions to punish perceived unfairness. Their fairness norm is more aspirational (how the group ideally should act) than enforcement‑oriented. Overall contribution “type”: high, robust cooperator with rare single‑round defection and low behavioral volatility.

This player is a fully cooperative, norm-driven contributor who always gives the maximum allowed amount. They never deviate from full contribution, even when group cooperation temporarily collapses or when a few players repeatedly free-ride. Their behavior is stable rather than exploratory; they do not appear to test the returns to selfishness or conditional cooperation. They clearly understand that contributing increases individual payoff under this multiplier and use that logic explicitly, so their cooperation is both prosocial and payoff-rational given others’ contributions. They treat non-contributors as “greedy” once it is clear that the interface is not the issue, indicating a fairness norm that expects everyone to give the full endowment when it is efficient. They tolerate some deviation (calling a single defector “a great result for us”) but still push toward unanimous cooperation. Their risk tolerance for being exploited is high: they continue to contribute fully despite repeated defections by specific players and despite the inability to punish or see full payoff information. They anchor strongly on the efficient, fair outcome and behave as if that is the default they should uphold.

The player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator. They always contribute the full endowment whenever given the choice, with no observable deviation across the game and no visible reaction to short-term drops in group cooperation. Their behavior does not appear to be contingent on recent group contributions or on the presence of free riders; they continue to give fully even when returns temporarily fall and when some others openly withhold. This suggests a strong internal fairness or efficiency norm rather than opportunistic adjustment. Given the game’s parameters, their consistent contributions are payoff-maximizing at the group level and personally profitable as long as others contribute at least moderately. The fact that they never exploit periods where others are contributing highly indicates low willingness to free-ride and a high tolerance for the risk that others may not reciprocate. They do not attempt to time or taper contributions toward the end and do not show endgame defection, implying either limited focus on strategic horizon effects or a preference for maintaining a cooperative norm regardless of remaining rounds. Overall, this player behaves as a high-trust, pro-social cooperator who values group efficiency and norm consistency more than marginal personal gain from defecting. They appear robust to frustration and do not use contribution choices to signal disapproval or retaliate against non-cooperators.

This player is a strict, unconditional full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment each round, regardless of others’ behavior, complaints about bots, or visible free‑riding. Their cooperation is highly stable: there is no decay over time and no evident end‑game defection, even when it is common knowledge that only a few rounds remain. They do not appear to condition contributions on punishment behavior or on who is free‑riding; they keep contributing fully even when many group members give zero, and even when coordination appears “in shambles.” This pattern suggests they value group efficiency and/or task compliance more than exploiting short‑term opportunities to profit from others’ contributions. They accept inequity in the short run (others earning more by free‑riding) rather than reduce their own giving. Their risk tolerance for being exploited is high; they do not hedge by withholding contributions in response to persistent non‑contributors or to erratic punishment. Fairness norms are oriented toward “everyone should contribute fully,” but they do not personally adjust their own contributions to enforce or restore fairness.

This player is a strategically inconsistent, partially opportunistic contributor who alternates between full cooperation and free-riding without a clear pattern of conditional reciprocity. They understand, at least verbally, that contributing is beneficial, but their actual choices do not consistently align with that understanding. They contribute the full endowment in several rounds dispersed across the game, including both early, middle, and late stages, but also choose zero in many other rounds where the group norm is clearly “everyone contributes.” Their zero contributions occur even after others explicitly explain the payoff logic and after the player acknowledges understanding. They sometimes publicly claim to be “putting the 20 in,” yet their observed decisions contradict this; they later admit confusion and external distractions as reasons. This suggests either genuine execution errors or a tendency to downplay self-interested deviations. The pattern indicates moderate concern for earnings (via occasional free-riding) but not extreme payoff-maximization, as they also repeatedly contribute fully when they could continue exploiting. Their contribution behavior is not clearly conditional on others’ contributions: they continue to contribute fully even when some players are chronically at zero, and they also free-ride in periods when almost everyone cooperates. This points to a noisy, unstable strategy rather than a strict “conditional cooperator” or “pure defector” type. Risk tolerance regarding social sanction appears moderately high: they sometimes free-ride despite being visible and discussed in chat, suggesting they accept reputational risk or underestimate its impact.

This player is a near-unconditional full cooperator. They contribute the maximum allowed amount in almost every round, including the final one, even after repeated evidence that some others free-ride or behave erratically. There is a single observed deviation where they keep the entire endowment while most others contribute, which appears as an anomaly given the surrounding pattern and their concurrent pro-cooperation chat; whether this is intentional exploitation, a misclick, or interface confusion is unknown. Overall, they treat “always contribute full” as the default strategy, show no signs of conditional cooperation based on others’ recent behavior, and do not reduce their contributions in response to free-riding or punishment noise. They explicitly accept short-run personal loss for higher group earnings and seem comfortable with risk when it aligns with a cooperative norm. They repeatedly frame full contribution as “the right way” to play, suggesting a fairness and efficiency norm rather than a purely self-interested calculus.

This player is a steadfast full cooperator. They always contribute the maximum allowed amount and never deviate, even when many others contribute nothing and payoffs drop sharply. Their cooperation is unconditional: they continue to put everything into the public good despite persistent free-riding by specific others and despite knowing that punishment is available. They clearly understand that contributing fully is payoff-maximizing for the group and behaves consistently with that understanding. There is no evidence that they condition contributions on others’ behavior, on being punished, or on proximity to the end of the game. Risk tolerance appears relatively high in the sense that they are willing to expose themselves to systematic exploitation by low contributors for the sake of maintaining the cooperative norm.

The player is a strict, unwavering free rider. They always keep their full endowment in the private account and never contribute to the public good, despite a very high marginal per-capita return and a persistent, explicit group norm of full contribution. Their behavior is extremely stable: there is no experimentation, no conditional cooperation, and no visible response to others’ appeals or to social pressure. They exploit the high cooperation level of others to maximize their own payoff, even when this repeatedly draws negative attention and targeted punishment. They show no fairness norm toward equal contribution and appear comfortable receiving a larger share of payoff than others while contributing nothing themselves. Risk tolerance toward social sanctions is high: they continue defecting even after being heavily punished once and repeatedly criticized in chat. They do not appear to trade off short-term gains for long-term group goodwill; instead, they consistently prioritize individual payoff per round over any cooperative or reputational concern.

This player is a near-unconditional high cooperator. They almost always contribute the full endowment and rarely deviate. The only clear deviations are a single low contribution and one intermediate contribution early on; both are brief and not repeated. Overall, the pattern is: start fully cooperative, experiment once or twice with imperfect cooperation, then settle into consistently maximal contributions even when one or two others keep free riding. They do not appear to condition their own giving tightly on others’ behavior. Even when a persistent low contributor continues to benefit, they keep putting in the maximum. They show no evidence of trying to “match” others’ contributions or of downshifting in response to unfairness. This suggests a strong internal norm of contributing rather than a payoff-maximizing or reciprocity-based strategy. Risk tolerance seems moderate to high in the social sense: they are willing to expose themselves to exploitation by free riders and to potential disapproval from punishers rather than hedge by lowering contributions. They do not visibly seek to protect themselves against punishment by exerting extra conformity beyond already being a perfect contributor. Fairness-wise, they seem to interpret fairness primarily as “everyone should put in a lot,” but they enforce this only through their own behavior, not through sanctions.

This player is a highly cooperative, near-unconditional full contributor. They repeatedly give the maximum allowed amount to the public good in almost every round, despite seeing persistent free-riding by at least one other player and despite having the option to hold back. The only clear deviation is a single early round where they contribute nothing; they do not explain this choice, and it does not establish a lasting strategy, since they immediately revert to giving the maximum thereafter. Their behavior suggests they treat full contribution as the “correct” norm when the multiplier is high, and they explicitly encourage others to do the same. They do not appear to condition their own contribution level on others’ behavior, even when someone consistently contributes zero. This indicates a strong internal fairness/efficiency norm and low willingness to personally free-ride, combined with relatively low risk aversion about being exploited. They are sensitive to violations of the norm but choose to address them via punishment and social pressure rather than by reducing their own contributions.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator in this environment. They always contribute their entire endowment and never experiment with lower amounts, even when free-riding is salient, others defect, or punishment is available and frequently used. Their cooperation is not visibly contingent on others’ behavior: they keep contributing fully through early uncertainty, mid-game boredom, and late-game bullying of the persistent defector. They appear to anchor on the high multiplier and on the idea that “big pot” cooperation is both profitable and normatively correct, and they do not hedge or show risk aversion by partial contributions. They do not visibly condition contributions on whether they are personally punished, nor on the precise group average. Their fairness norm is that everyone “should” go all in, but their own behavior does not change when that norm is violated; they prefer to maintain efficient cooperation and let others deal with defectors via talk or punishment.

This player is a stable unconditional full cooperator in this environment. They always contribute the entire endowment and never experiment with lower amounts, even when others free‑ride or when the group discusses the costliness of punishment. They accept that the social optimum is everyone contributing fully and behave as if that norm is already in place. Their cooperation is not visibly contingent on short‑term payoffs or on whether defectors are actually deterred; they keep contributing fully through phases of both harmony and conflict. They show no evidence of risk aversion about being “exploited”; they tolerate persistent free‑riding by one player without ever reducing their own contributions. They implicitly endorse a fairness norm where everyone should put in the maximum, and they act as a benchmark cooperator rather than as a conditional follower.

This player is a generally high, pro-social contributor who occasionally and deliberately deviates from the full-cooperation norm. Most of the time, they match or nearly match the informal “all in” standard that emerges in the group, regularly choosing the maximum feasible contribution when others are doing so and the environment is stable. Early on they experiment with non-maximal but still substantial contributions, then quickly converge to the prevailing cooperative level once they see others are reliably contributing high amounts. Later, when full contributions are strongly established, they occasionally under-contribute by a small or moderate amount while everyone else is at full contribution, and they even contribute zero once, despite having just seen universal high contributions. These deviations are rare, not escalating, and do not transition them into a persistent free-rider: they promptly return to full contributions afterwards. Their behavior suggests they understand the payoff structure and see the collective benefit of high contributions, but they also test the boundaries of what others will tolerate and how the system responds to mild free-riding. They seem comfortable being the unique or near-unique deviator for a round even when that visibly violates the emerging “20 or bust” fairness norm. Being punished for under-contributing does not provoke visible retaliation or defection; instead they continue to behave cooperatively. Overall, they act as a cooperative player with sporadic, controlled opportunism and a willingness to “poke” the system rather than a purely altruistic or purely selfish type.

This player is a very high, near-unconditional cooperator. After an initial moderate contribution, they switch to always giving the full endowment and then treat “max and press OK” as the obvious default. They do not visibly condition their own contribution level on others’ free-riding or partial contributions; they continue to contribute fully even when there is persistent low contribution by one player, sporadic low efforts by a few others, or when the group experiments with punishment. They show awareness of contribution mistakes (once noticing that they must have accidentally submitted less than intended) and explicitly state that they are now watching their input, which indicates a clear intention to maintain full cooperation rather than opportunism. Their behavior reflects a strong efficiency norm (recognizing the high multiplier and wanting to take advantage of it) and a group-benefit orientation rather than strict payoff-maximizing self-interest. They appear comfortable sacrificing some potential earnings (via punishment costs and always contributing 20) in order to maintain or enforce a cooperative, norm-abiding environment. Risk tolerance with respect to others’ defection is high: they do not reduce contributions in response to persistent free-riding by one player, suggesting they prioritize group norms and “doing the right thing” over strict reciprocity.

This player is a volatile, strategically flexible contributor who alternates between full free-riding and full contribution rather than settling at a stable level. They do not display a consistent intrinsic preference for always cooperating or always defecting. Instead, their pattern suggests responsiveness to social context and perceived detection or norms: after an initial choice that exploits the group, they are willing to switch to full cooperation, then revert to free-riding later. This indicates conditional cooperation shaped more by perceived incentives and social pressure than by a fixed fairness norm. Their willingness to fully free-ride despite strong social signals favoring contribution suggests relatively low inequity aversion and a tolerance for appearing exploitative when they believe it is advantageous or safe. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to take social risks (defecting when others may notice) but also willing to conform to cooperative behavior when the social environment seems to demand it. Motivation seems mixed: they value personal payoff but are also sensitive to being observed and judged, adjusting contributions accordingly.

This player is a stable, unconditional free-rider. They consistently keep their entire endowment and contribute nothing to the public fund, even as others publicly claim full cooperation and as the presence of punishment becomes salient. There is no evidence of conditional cooperation or adjustment in response to group norms or sanctions; their strategy remains fixed. They appear willing to accept reputational damage and repeated monetary deductions in exchange for maximizing their private retention each round. This suggests a strong priority on immediate individual payoff over fairness or group welfare, and a low sensitivity to social disapproval within the game context. Risk tolerance appears moderate to high: they persist in free-riding despite observable and realized punishment risk.

This player is a persistently moderate-to-high contributor who anchors around a mid-range contribution and adjusts upward or downward in response to others. They appear to hold a fairness or reciprocity norm: they frequently match or slightly exceed the average contribution of others and occasionally contribute clearly more than peers when others also contribute substantially. They do not free-ride; they never contribute zero and rarely go very low relative to the endowment. Their contributions show conditional cooperation: they increase contributions when others contribute more and sometimes reduce them when others’ average drops, but they do not react in an extreme or punitive way via their own contribution level. They tolerate some level of under-contribution by at least one player without sharply cutting their own contribution, suggesting relatively low risk aversion toward mild free-riding and a willingness to keep the public good going even when not perfectly reciprocated.
