There is no evidence of strategic endgame reduction or of experimenting with lower contributions, suggesting a stable “always cooperate” rule rather than contingent reciprocity. They tolerate some unfairness (others giving less while benefiting from their full contribution) without adjusting their own behavior, implying strong cooperative norms and low sensitivity to short-term exploitation. Risk tolerance appears high with respect to relying on others to maintain cooperation, but they are also protected by the game’s parameters, which make full contribution individually rational when others mostly cooperate.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor who repeatedly chooses the maximum contribution in every observed decision, regardless of others’ deviations or short-term payoff loss. They explicitly endorse full contribution as the norm and argue for it on efficiency grounds, pointing out that low contributions marginally benefit the individual at substantial cost to the group. They do not visibly retaliate by lowering their own contribution after others free‑ride; instead they continue to cooperate and try to bring deviators back into line through communication. This suggests a strong fairness and efficiency norm, low inclination to exploit others, and relatively high tolerance for being marginally exploited if communication might fix the issue. Their risk tolerance appears moderate-to-high in social terms: they are willing to “trust” the group with continuous full contributions even after repeated defections, but they also show awareness of the payoff structure and the incentive to free‑ride.

This player is a highly stable full cooperator. They consistently contribute their entire endowment each round and never experiment with lower contributions, even when several others reduce their own. Their cooperation is unconditional in practice: they keep contributing fully despite repeated free-riding by a specific group member and occasional low contributions by others. They appear to hold and follow a clear efficiency/fairness norm that everyone “should” contribute the maximum, and they do not visibly discount future payoffs or hedge against potential end-game defection. Their risk tolerance is high in the sense that they accept being exploitable rather than reducing their own contribution; there is no sign of strategic shading or self-protection. Overall, their contribution strategy is simple, rule-like, and norm-driven rather than reactive or outcome-sensitive.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator. They always invest their entire endowment into the public good without any detectable adjustment to others’ behavior, even when a few group members reduce their contributions. Their pattern indicates a clear acceptance of the efficiency logic of full contribution and a strong norm that everyone “should” go all in. They do not appear to hedge, experiment, or react to minor deviations; instead, they treat full cooperation as the default and stick with it. There is no evidence they condition contributions on being punished or rewarded, or on how many rounds they think remain. They tolerate some free-riding in the short term, relying on punishment rather than contribution cuts to address it. Risk tolerance regarding exploitation is relatively high: they keep maximizing group payoffs despite recurrent under-contribution by the same teammate.

This player is a highly stable, near-unconditional high contributor who occasionally experiments with low contributions late in the game. For almost the entire interaction, they consistently choose the maximum possible contribution, regardless of whether others contribute a lot, a little, or almost nothing. This indicates a strong intrinsic or principled preference for cooperating in the public good, rather than reacting in a tit-for-tat or contingent way to group behavior. Their contribution pattern is extremely stable for most of the game: they do not adjust downward when surrounded by low contributors, nor do they escalate upward when others increase cooperation, because they are already at the upper bound. This suggests they anchor on a personal rule such as “always contribute everything,” and maintain it under a wide range of social environments and payoff outcomes. Later in the game, they briefly deviate from this rule with a pair of very low contributions, then promptly return to maximal giving. This short-lived change implies some capacity for strategic thinking or frustration-based experimentation: they appear to momentarily test how it feels or pays to contribute almost nothing while others give more, then abandon that approach and re-align with full cooperation. Because this low-contribution phase is short and not repeated, it looks more like a probe or emotional reaction than a full strategy switch. They tolerate substantial payoff sacrifice relative to more self-interested strategies, indicating high risk tolerance with respect to being exploited by free riders. They are willing to bear this risk even in a setting with no punishment or reward mechanisms and no communication, where maintaining high cooperation is especially challenging. This points to a fairness norm focused more on “doing their part” than on enforcing reciprocity or equality: they keep contributing fully even when others give much less, with no visible retaliatory pattern. Overall, their type is best described as a principled high cooperator: they strongly prefer to sustain a cooperative norm, are slow to retaliate or reduce contributions, briefly explore defection when cooperative norms seem unreliable, and then re-commit to high contributions despite that experience. Their behavior suggests they value the group’s potential gains and their own cooperative identity more than short-run maximization of private income, and they do not appear to condition their baseline contribution on detailed tracking of who is free riding or who is most generous.

The player is a predominantly low contributor and consistent free-rider with occasional small, exploratory contributions. Their typical choice is to give nothing, punctuated by modest contributions that never approach full cooperation. Contribution levels stay in a narrow band between zero and small single digits, indicating a stable, low-cooperation profile rather than random noise or strong swings. They appear strongly conditional on others’ behavior: when the group average or visible peers are relatively generous, the player sometimes increases their own contribution slightly, but never to a level that matches high contributors. This suggests a “strategic exploiter” pattern rather than a fairness-seeking conditional cooperator. They seem comfortable benefiting from others’ generosity and do not move toward proportional contributions even when surrounded by repeated high givers. The player shows little evidence of fairness norms in the sense of matching or approximating others’ contributions. Instead, they seem focused on maximizing personal payoff given the known multiplier: they regularly choose the individually dominant action (contribute little or nothing) despite clear indications that many others are sustaining relatively high contributions. Their occasional non-zero contributions are small and sporadic, consistent with either minimal norm compliance, light experimentation, or low-cost signaling rather than a genuine attempt to sustain group efficiency. Risk tolerance regarding contributions is low: they avoid large stakes in the public pool and do not test high-contribution strategies, even when high contributors are numerous and persistent. This points to a cautious, payoff-protective approach, prioritizing secure private payoffs over uncertain long-run collective gains. Overall, the persona is best described as a mostly self-regarding, low-risk, conditional free-rider who opportunistically adds small contributions but does not commit to cooperation or fairness.

This player is a predominantly self-interested free-rider with occasional, exploratory high contributions that do not persist. Their default action is to keep the full endowment and rely on others to fund the public good. Low or zero contributions occur in the vast majority of rounds and are not systematically titrated; the player tends to switch between extremes (all or nothing) rather than fine-tuning around intermediate levels. They does not appear to follow a stable conditional-cooperation rule (such as matching the group average). High group contributions from others often coexist with the player choosing to give nothing. When the player does contribute, it is either small and early, or large “bursts” that are sporadic and not followed by sustained cooperation. These bursts do not map in a straightforward way onto preceding group behavior (e.g., they sometimes contribute a lot even when others are contributing modestly, and then revert to zero despite others remaining cooperative), suggesting that their cooperation is opportunistic or experimental rather than norm-driven. Fairness concerns, if present, are weak. The player consistently accepts payoff advantages generated by others’ giving without “repaying” the group in later rounds, despite repeated opportunities. They shows no pattern of increasing contributions when others are highly cooperative over several rounds; instead, they continues to take the larger private payoff that free-riding yields. There is no sign of “guilt-based” or reciprocity-based compensation. Risk tolerance appears moderate to high in strategic terms: the player is willing to make occasional large contributions that are individually costly and that do not guarantee reciprocation. However, because these large contributions are rare relative to persistent free-riding, their overall risk posture is still tilted toward safeguarding personal payoff rather than jointly maximizing group returns. The lack of gradual adjustment and the preference for sharp switches (from full free-riding to full contribution and back) further suggests a more “all-or-nothing” experimentation style rather than careful, incremental calibration. Overall, this player’s type is best described as an exploitive, payoff-focused participant who intermittently tests high cooperation but ultimately reverts to free-riding, with weak adherence to fairness norms and only loose, inconsistent conditionality on others’ behavior.

This player is a mixed-strategy, opportunistic cooperator who toggles between full contribution and full free-riding rather than settling into a stable pattern. They are clearly willing to contribute fully to the public good, but they do not treat cooperation as a fixed moral rule. Instead, they appear to experiment with alternating between contributing and not contributing, while monitoring how cooperative the group remains. They do not respond myopically to immediate payoff maximization; they repeatedly make choices that are individually costly relative to defecting in the same environment. This suggests they value either sustaining group cooperation, testing the effect of their behavior on others, or aligning with a fairness norm some of the time. However, they also repeatedly free-ride when others are contributing, indicating no strict aversion to exploiting a cooperative group. Their contribution behavior shows some conditionality: they tend to contribute in environments where group contributions are relatively high or appear to be trending upward, and they are willing to withhold contributions even when many others cooperate. They never use intermediate contributions (the game only allows extremes), but within that limitation, they do not lock into a purely cooperative or purely selfish type. Risk tolerance appears moderate. They are willing to take the “risk” of cooperating without any guarantee that others will continue, but they also hedge by switching back to defection in later rounds. They do not behave like a strict payoff maximizer; they sometimes choose the lower immediate payoff option, which is consistent with some weight on efficiency or fairness, but that concern is not strong enough to prevent repeated free-riding when cooperation is well established. Overall, they fit a “selective cooperator / strategic free-rider” profile: they participate in creating and maintaining a cooperative environment but allow themselves repeated episodes of exploiting it.

This player is a “mostly-committed cooperator with brief opportunistic testing.” They overwhelmingly choose the cooperative option despite having only binary choices and no punishment mechanism. After an initial phase in which they fully cooperate, they briefly free-ride when others’ cooperation is already strong, then revert to mostly stable full cooperation for the remainder of the game. They treat contribution primarily as a norm rather than as a calculation problem: they are willing to contribute even when some group members are clearly free-riding, and they do not appear to systematically match or undercut the average. Their few defections occur while high cooperation by others is already established, suggesting they periodically test how much they can personally gain without threatening group outcomes, not that they are trying to drive a general collapse. Their pattern suggests: - A baseline preference for contributing fully to the public good. - Tolerance of some free-riding by others without retaliatory defection. - Occasional self-interested probing when cooperation looks robust, but no escalation into systematic defection. - Low volatility overall: after the early exploratory phase, their behavior is highly consistent. They appear willing to incur personal opportunity costs to maintain a cooperative environment, even though they never see others’ final payoffs and cannot use punishment. Their willingness to keep cooperating under uncertainty indicates moderate risk tolerance in service of group efficiency and a belief that contributing is generally worthwhile in this setting. Fairness concerns are present but not dominant: they do not insist on strict reciprocity and continue cooperating despite persistent low contributors.

This player is a high, but not unconditional, contributor who oscillates between full cooperation and full free-riding. They consistently choose one of the extreme options available and never mix levels, indicating a preference for simple, salient strategies rather than fine-tuned optimization. Across the game, their default pattern is to contribute the full endowment in a clear majority of rounds. They maintain this cooperative stance both when others are contributing strongly and when the overall contribution environment weakens, suggesting an intrinsic or norm-based inclination toward cooperation rather than purely opportunistic behavior. However, they occasionally switch to full non-contribution even in cooperative environments, showing that this norm is neither rigid nor absolute. Their cooperation appears weakly conditional: they are more likely to contribute when others, on average, are also contributing a substantial portion of their endowments, and they do not collapse into persistent free-riding after encountering partial defection. Instead, they return to full contribution multiple times after free-riding episodes. This on–off pattern suggests experimentation or mild strategic adjustment rather than consistent tit-for-tat or strict reciprocity. They show no evidence of strong aversion to inequality or strong insistence that all others be fully cooperative. They continue contributing when some group members give nothing, as long as enough others still appear cooperative. This points to tolerance for moderate levels of free-riding and a focus on group-level outcomes rather than strict fairness enforcement. Risk tolerance appears moderate. They are willing to “risk” contributing fully even when cooperation is imperfect and the marginal per-capita return is known and favorable. At the same time, the intermittent free-riding indicates sensitivity to payoff considerations or uncertainty about the group’s future behavior. They neither play purely risk-dominant (always defect) nor purely payoff-dominant (always cooperate); instead they mix, with a strong cooperative bias. Overall, the type can be summarized as a “cooperation-leaning, occasionally opportunistic contributor” who tends to uphold cooperation but allows themselves periodic payoff-seeking deviations and simple exploratory shifts rather than following a rigid or highly sophisticated conditional rule.

This player is a **moderately erratic conditional cooperator with opportunistic free-riding**. They reliably switch between full contribution and full defection rather than sticking to one extreme. Their choices suggest that they condition their behavior on the group’s apparent cooperation level: when others seem to be contributing strongly, they are willing to join in with full contributions; when they perceive that full cooperation is not assured or uniformly shared, they are comfortable taking the entire endowment for themselves. They never use intermediate contributions, implying a preference for simple, binary strategies over fine-grained calibration. This is consistent with a player who is aware of the tension between self-interest and group benefit and resolves it in a “all-or-nothing” way rather than by adjusting in small steps. They do not display a consistent long-run pattern of increasing or decreasing contributions; instead, their behavior oscillates. This indicates **low contribution stability** and ongoing experimentation or context sensitivity, rather than a firmly fixed rule such as “always cooperate” or “always defect.” They appear comfortable free-riding when the group’s overall contributions are already high enough to produce strong returns. This suggests **non-naive, payoff-aware behavior**: they accept the public good when others provide it, and they are willing to withhold their own contribution even when many others are cooperating. At the same time, they keep returning to full contribution after free-riding episodes, which indicates that they do not fully revert to pure defection. This pattern aligns with a **self-interested conditional cooperator**: they support cooperation when they judge that the group is broadly cooperative and/or their own contribution is not singularly exploited, but they are not committed to fairness in the strong sense of always matching others’ sacrifices. They show no strong evidence of aversion to being slightly above or below the average contributor; they at times contribute while others defect, and at times defect while others contribute. This points to **limited adherence to strict fairness or equality norms**, and more weight on personal payoff and flexible adjustment than on maintaining equal contributions. They accept the risk that their own payoff is lower in cooperative choices than in some previous free-riding situations, which reflects **moderate risk tolerance** regarding immediate earnings in order to maintain or test cooperative states. However, because they also repeatedly free-ride, they do not appear to be sacrificing systematically for long-term group welfare; rather, they trade off between personal gain and occasional support for cooperation. Overall, their type is best described as **instrumental, context-sensitive, and willing to exploit cooperation**, without fully abandoning cooperative behavior.

This player is a strict, unconditional cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment to the public fund, with no deviation and no visible experimentation. Their behavior does not track fluctuations in others’ cooperation: full contribution persists when others contribute less, when they contribute more, and when contributions vary across the group. They prioritize collective efficiency over individual immediate payoff. Given the high multiplier, this is consistent with a focus on maximizing group welfare rather than exploiting others’ cooperation. There is no evidence of conditional cooperation (no signs of “I’ll match the group” or “I punish free-riders by reducing my own contribution”). Instead, they appear to follow an internal rule such as “always contribute everything,” independent of context. Their fairness norm is inclusive and group-oriented: they keep investing fully into the common pool even when a subset of players repeatedly contributes nothing or much less. They do not try to protect themselves against free-riding by defecting; they accept payoff variability as a consequence of their rule. Risk tolerance is high in the sense of exposure to exploitation: they continually take the socially beneficial but individually risky action of full contribution without feedback about others’ payoffs or round progression. There is no sign that uncertainty about the number of rounds or about others’ eventual behavior causes them to hedge or reduce contribution. In learning/adaptation terms, they are rigid rather than exploratory. They do not test alternative strategies, and there is no visible response rule tying their own contribution to past reward received or to shifts in average group cooperation. Their underlying type is best characterized as rule-driven, prosocial, and non-retaliatory.

This player is a consistently high contributor who occasionally shades down their contribution but never free-rides. Their default is to give the full endowment or nearly all of it, with brief dips to moderately lower amounts when the overall environment becomes noisier or less cooperative. They never drop to clearly exploitative levels and quickly return to full contributions once high cooperation is re-established. Their behavior suggests they value maintaining a cooperative norm and are willing to sacrifice personal payoff to support it, but they are not rigid: they modestly adjust downward when overall contributions or stability fall. They do not appear to condition contributions strongly on being punished or on specific individuals’ actions; instead, their contributions track the overall group climate and possibly perceived instability. Fairness for them seems defined as “everyone contributing at or near the maximum,” and they position themselves on the generous side of that norm. Their risk tolerance for being the “sucker” is relatively high: they repeatedly contribute fully even when the environment is volatile and when they themselves have just been punished, indicating a cooperative, somewhat idealistic inclination rather than payoff-maximizing caution.

This player is a generally high contributor with occasional, deliberate under-contribution. They treat full contribution as the default when the group appears broadly cooperative and when recent interactions feel “fair enough.” They periodically drop to half the endowment when recent punishment patterns or low contributions from others signal instability or exploitation. These reductions are temporary rather than a drift into permanent defection; they repeatedly return to full contribution once cooperation looks restored. Their behavior suggests a conditional cooperator who aims for high group efficiency but is willing to shade down contributions to protect against perceived unfairness or to respond to turbulence. They do not appear concerned with fine-grained matching of each individual’s contribution; they respond more to overall climate (general high vs low contributions, heavy punishment vs calm) than to precise numerical deviations. Risk tolerance is moderate: they repeatedly keep contributing fully even after being targeted by punishment, but they also occasionally “test” lower contributions, likely to avoid being the sole high contributor in a hostile environment. Fairness norms center on reciprocity and proportionality at the group level: they want most players to contribute “seriously,” and they become less generous when many players either free-ride or weaponize punishment.

This player is a mostly-high contributor who intermittently free-rides in a calculated, low-profile way. They clearly understand the game’s structure and the gains from cooperation (the group is generally very cooperative, and they see that). They often match the emerging “20 coins” cooperative norm but periodically drop to 10 or even 0 while others mostly stay high. Their pattern suggests strategic opportunism rather than confusion: when group cooperation is strong and stable, they sometimes take advantage by contributing less, yet they never become a chronic low contributor. They oscillate between full cooperation and partial or full free-riding, without announcing any rationale. This implies they weigh personal earnings against group norms and reputation but are willing to risk some social disapproval to gain extra payoff. They do not visibly condition their contributions on being punished; after being hit with notable deductions once, they continue to mix high and low contributions rather than moving to consistent full cooperation. They also do not appear to punish others for low contributions, which makes their own occasional free-riding more consistent with an individualistic, payoff-oriented style than with a strong fairness-enforcement motive. Overall, they fit a “quiet conditional cooperator with opportunistic deviations”: comfortable in a cooperative environment, but occasionally exploiting it, especially when they can blend into a large group.

The player is a very strong, long-run unconditional cooperator. They contribute the full endowment in essentially every round, only briefly dropping to a half contribution twice very late in the game when overall cooperation has visibly eroded. This pattern suggests they prioritize group efficiency and fairness over short‑term individual gain, even when some others undercontribute or free‑ride. Their contributions are highly stable and not tightly contingent on recent deviations or on being personally punished; they keep giving 20 in many situations where purely self-interested or conditional cooperators would lower contributions. When they do reduce contributions, it coincides with widespread decreases and growing “endgame/hell” talk, not with targeted retaliation, so the drop appears more like resignation to a changing group norm than a strategic threat. Overall, they behave as a prosocial, efficiency‑oriented cooperator with high tolerance for noise and mild free-riding, and relatively low sensitivity to marginal incentives for defection.

This player is initially a strong, almost unconditional cooperator. They consistently contribute the full endowment through much of the game, even when a few others free‑ride. Early on, they accept personal cost for the sake of group efficiency and do not appear to condition their contributions on being punished or on others’ minor deviations. Over time, they become more volatile and start experimenting with partial or zero contributions. These deviations correlate with increased use of punishment and with a growing sense that others are strategically exploiting the game (e.g., admiration of a low contributor “knowing how to make money”). In late stages, they sometimes contribute low or zero while also using large punishments, suggesting a shift from norm‑following cooperation to strategic or expressive behavior, possibly driven by fatigue or playfulness rather than pure payoff maximization. They tolerate substantial personal payoff losses (via their own punishment spending) rather than rigidly maximizing earnings, indicating lower risk aversion and a willingness to trade off income for other motives (expression, norm enforcement, or experimentation). Their fairness norm appears to be “high contributions are good, but aggressive profit‑seeking can be impressive,” producing some ambivalence in the late game.

This player is a moderately high, but rarely maximal, contributor who free-rides slightly on highly cooperative groups. They usually contribute well above the social optimum of zero and close to the group average, but they repeatedly stay a bit below the modal full-contribution norm when others are giving the maximum. They rarely exploit dramatically: they do not drop to the minimum even when some others contribute very little; instead they tend to keep their contributions in the low-to-mid range of the cooperative band. There are a few spikes upward to very high contributions when group contributions dip, suggesting some willingness to “lean in” when public good provision looks threatened, but they typically avoid being the very top contributor. Their behavior is relatively stable around a personal norm that is slightly under what the most cooperative majority is choosing. They appear to care about maintaining high group earnings, but also about not “over-paying” relative to others, revealing a mixed motive: partial altruism combined with mild self-interest and concern for relative fairness. Risk tolerance looks moderate: they do not test extreme defection, but they also do not consistently match the most generous possible action even when generous behavior is clearly rewarded.

This player is a generally cooperative but profit-conscious contributor who oscillates between high contributions and strategic free-riding. They clearly understand that full contribution is socially efficient and, for much of the game, usually gives at least half their endowment and often the maximum. They explicitly endorse the idea that contributing the maximum benefits everyone and usually acts in line with that norm. At the same time, they occasionally contribute very little or nothing, especially mid‑game and after social goodwill is established. These low contributions do not appear to be direct reactions to others’ individual behavior (they do not reference specific low contributors or punishments); instead they look like opportunistic tests of how much they can personally gain while the group continues to cooperate. Their contribution pattern is therefore mixed: mostly pro-social with intermittent, quiet exploitation of the cooperative norm. They do not condition finely on small variations in others’ contributions; when others are contributing near-max, they are comfortable contributing somewhat less than the group average without remark. Their risk tolerance seems moderate: they opportunistically free‑ride a few times but do not push this strategy aggressively or systematically even when it pays off. Overall they behave like a “warm cooperator with occasional self-interested dips,” not a strict altruist and not a hardened defector.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They consistently give their full endowment to the public fund, without adjusting to the moderately lower average contributions of others and without reacting to observed free-riding. This indicates a strong cooperative or efficiency-oriented norm that overrides short-term self-interest. There is no evidence of conditional cooperation; their contribution does not track others’ past behavior, nor do they reduce cooperation when facing clear under-contributors. Risk tolerance appears high: they are willing to rely on others to some extent for payoff generation despite uncertainty and visible heterogeneity in contributions. Their fairness norm seems group-focused rather than individually matched: they act as if “the right thing” is to contribute fully, independent of whether others reciprocate.

This player is a moderate, stable cooperator who neither free-rides nor fully matches the highest contributors. They keep their contribution constant across rounds despite variability in others’ behavior, suggesting a simple rule-based or “comfortable midpoint” strategy rather than precise conditional cooperation. They appear willing to sacrifice some private payoff for group benefit but stop short of strong altruism. There is no clear evidence that they explicitly track or match the group average; they hold their own level fixed even when others include both very high and very low contributors. Fairness concerns are present but bounded: they do not try to equalize contributions fully, and they tolerate being slightly below the top contributors while clearly above the lowest. Risk tolerance is moderate: they are willing to invest in the public good in a mixed environment with some low contributors, but they avoid the extreme risk of fully contributing.

This player fits a “guarded free‑rider under social pressure” type. They show a strong preference for keeping their own endowment and are willing to contribute only minimally, even when everyone else contributes much more. Their baseline tendency is clearly low cooperation; they are not aiming at fairness relative to group norms and appear comfortable being an outlier who benefits from others’ generosity. Their change in contributions is volatile and moves in the direction of even lower cooperation rather than convergence toward others. There is no evidence they condition their behavior positively on others’ high contributions; instead, they respond by reducing their own giving further. This suggests low adherence to strong reciprocity norms and a focus on maximizing individual payoff rather than efficiency or equality. Risk tolerance regarding social sanctions appears high: despite seeing that others care enough to spend on punishment, they do not increase cooperation and in fact move further toward free‑riding. Their behavior is consistent with either low belief in effective deterrence (e.g., thinking punishment will be diffuse or limited) or low concern about being seen as unfair, but their exact beliefs are unknown.

This player is a moderately high contributor who calibrates around the group norm rather than leading or lagging strongly. They begin by contributing above the average but noticeably below the highest contributors, then reduce their contribution slightly even as the group becomes, on average, more generous. This pattern suggests conditional cooperation with a self-interested tilt: they are willing to contribute substantially but do not seek to set a maximal cooperative example, and they do not increase contributions in response to others’ strong cooperation. They appear aware of payoff consequences and do not behave in a purely altruistic or purely selfish way. Fairness norms are present but not stringent: they tolerate some heterogeneity and are willing to sit a bit below the emerging high-contribution cluster. Their risk tolerance in contributions appears moderate; they do not test extreme free-riding nor full-giving across all rounds, instead maintaining stable, mid-to-high levels without sharp swings, implying a preference for predictable, balanced returns over either moral “purity” or maximal exploitation.

This player is a moderately high, pro-social contributor who becomes fully cooperative when others’ behavior looks sufficiently supportive. They begin by contributing a bit below the emerging high-contribution cluster, which suggests they balance self-interest with a willingness to support the group rather than immediately maximizing group payoff. After observing strong cooperation from several peers and no apparent downside to cooperating, they escalate to contributing their entire endowment. This pattern indicates conditional cooperation: they are willing to increase contributions when they see that others are, on average, reasonably cooperative, even if there are some clear low contributors present. They do not appear highly risk-averse about being exploited; they tolerate some degree of free-riding rather than dropping their contribution. Fairness norms are present but not absolute: they do not strictly match the highest contributors, nor do they sharply under-contribute to protect themselves from defectors. Instead, they move toward higher cooperation as the game unfolds, despite visible low contributors. Overall, they tend toward cooperative, group-benefit-seeking behavior, updated modestly in response to others’ contributions.

This player is a “cooperative optimizer” who strongly prefers full contribution when it is clearly payoff-dominant for the group and individually beneficial. They immediately align with the emerging group norm of contributing the full endowment and explicitly endorse that strategy in chat. Their baseline pattern is very stable: they consistently choose the maximum contribution whenever others are also doing so and the multiplier is high. They show a single, modest deviation downward when the group briefly becomes more heterogeneous, suggesting they are not purely unconditional; they are at least somewhat conditional on perceived group reliability and may test what happens when contributions drop, but they quickly revert to full cooperation once the all-in norm is re-established. They do not experiment systematically with partial free-riding and never attempt an outright exploitative low contribution. Their choices indicate medium-to-high risk tolerance in the sense of willingly “trusting” the group and tying their payoff to the public fund rather than to private retention, but that risk is carefully justified by the high multiplier rather than driven by altruism alone. They appear motivated by efficiency and common-knowledge reasoning: when the technology makes the public investment obviously superior, they treat mutual full contribution as the default and stick with it.

This player is a strongly cooperative, efficiency‑oriented contributor who quickly aligns with the group’s “max contribution” norm. Once the high multiplier and mutual cooperation pattern are apparent, they almost always give the full endowment. Their behavior indicates they understand that contributing everything is individually profitable when everyone else does the same, and they treat that as the baseline strategy. They briefly deviate downward when contributions become more heterogeneous, but still contribute above the group average at that moment. This suggests concern about fairness and exploitation: they are willing to cooperate, but they reduce their input slightly when free‑riding becomes blatant. The adjustment is modest rather than extreme, so they do not flip into full defection; they balance self‑interest with maintaining the cooperative norm. Their contributions are otherwise very stable across the game: once a cooperative equilibrium is established, they stick to it even in later rounds when some small deviations appear. There is no evidence of end‑game unraveling in their own behavior, despite uncertainty about the number of rounds. This points to relatively low short‑term risk aversion regarding “being the sucker” so long as others mostly cooperate, and a willingness to ride a profitable cooperative equilibrium to the end. They track the payoff structure well and explicitly mention the experimental exchange rate with real money, indicating they are motivated by monetary payoff and think in terms of cumulative earnings. However, they do not attempt to exploit the group by under‑contributing once high balances have accumulated; they continue to cooperate at or near full. Overall, they behave like a conditional cooperator with a high threshold: they are happy to contribute maximally when others do, tolerate minor noise, but will reduce contributions somewhat when faced with clear non‑contributors.

This player is a generally high, near-unconditional contributor with only modest sensitivity to others’ behavior. They usually give the full endowment, occasionally dropping to a medium level but never free-riding. Their pattern suggests they prioritize maintaining a cooperative norm over fine-tuned best responses to group averages. When they do reduce contributions, it coincides with variability and some low contributions from others, but they do not sustain a downward trend or retaliate against specific low contributors. They show no clear evidence of calibrating contributions to fairness or matching the group’s average; instead, they appear comfortable continuing to contribute fully even in the presence of consistent low contributors. Risk tolerance is moderate: they repeatedly accept the risk of being exploited by free-riders in order to keep provision high, implying a prosocial or efficiency-oriented motivation rather than short-term payoff maximization. Volatility is low: their pattern is stable, with only rare deviations from high contributions, and they do not escalate or spiral downward. Conditional cooperation is weak at best; if present, it is overshadowed by a robust cooperative disposition.

Type label: Alternating opportunistic cooperator with simple pattern-based strategy. The player alternates sharply between full contribution and full free-riding rather than using intermediate or mixed levels, despite having only two discrete options. This alternation is not random: they reliably shift behavior in response to the recent contribution pattern in the group. When they perceive widespread cooperation, they are willing to contribute fully even though the marginal per-coin return from the public good is below the individual endowment return. This suggests they value collective efficiency and/or norm-following when they expect others to share the burden. However, their cooperation is fragile and conditional: once they experience a collective breakdown of contributions, they immediately switch to taking the entire endowment and continue to do so while the group remains largely uncooperative. They also show a willingness to free-ride on isolated cooperators. When a single other player contributes while the group is otherwise non-cooperative, they do not reciprocate; instead, they capitalize on the opportunity to gain a higher payoff. This indicates that fairness norms are applied primarily at the group level (willingness to join broad cooperation) rather than at the individual level (rewarding lone cooperators). Overall, the player displays: - Conditional cooperation toward a cooperating majority. - Strategic free-riding when group cooperation is low or when a lone cooperator appears. - High responsiveness to recent group behavior rather than a fixed cooperative or selfish disposition. Their risk tolerance is moderate: they are willing to risk full contribution when others appear cooperative, but they avoid “leading” cooperation by themselves.

This player is a predominantly selfish, opportunistic free-rider with one notable deviation. They usually choose not to contribute when they expect others also not to contribute or when they can profit from others’ cooperation. Their default is to keep their entire endowment and benefit from the public fund only when others supply it. Contribution behavior is binary and extreme: they either give nothing or give the full endowment, with no intermediate pattern observable here (by design of the game). Their contributions are volatile across the game: they shift from full free-riding to a single full contribution and then back to free-riding, indicating that their behavior is conditional and experimental rather than principled cooperation. They do not show a stable fairness norm; they are willing to accept large payoff advantages over fully cooperative peers and also tolerate being the worst-off player when their own attempt at cooperation is not reciprocated. The single full contribution appears as a test or signal rather than a stable commitment to cooperation: after discovering that others do not follow, they immediately return to non-contribution. Risk tolerance is moderate: they are willing at least once to risk a large short-term personal loss by contributing fully when others are not, but they abandon this risk when it is not rewarded. Overall, they act as a calculated opportunist with a brief, unsuccessful cooperative probe.

This player is a highly stable high contributor with a single, brief deviation. They overwhelmingly choose to contribute the maximum allowed amount to the public good, even when others free-ride and even though they clearly understand that keeping the endowment could be more profitable in a purely individual sense. Their one instance of non-contribution appears more like a small experiment or momentary opportunism than a shift in strategy: after that, they immediately resume maximum contributions and maintain them for the rest of the game. They show strong preference for group-oriented efficiency over individual gain: they continue to contribute fully even when it is obvious that several others are persistently free-riding and even late in the game when the horizon is short. They appear to operate under a cooperative norm that is robust to mild exploitation. There is no evidence that they condition their contribution directly on specific others’ behavior; they contribute fully both when cooperation is high and when it partially breaks down. That suggests a “principled cooperator” or “norm-driven cooperator” rather than a strongly conditional cooperator. They demonstrate an understanding of the payoff structure, as indicated by their early interest in whether everyone will contribute and later by their participation in a discussion of the experiment’s purpose. The fact that they continue contributing despite this understanding implies they tolerate some level of unfairness and are willing to be exploited if it sustains or signals cooperation. Their risk tolerance appears moderate to high: they are willing to risk being on the losing side of exploitation for the sake of maintaining a cooperative pattern. There is no clear evidence of strong inequality aversion translating into retaliatory behavior, since they never switch to persistent defection. Their fairness norm seems to be “I do my part even if some others do not,” rather than “I match others exactly.” The small deviation suggests they are not completely rigid; they are capable of self-interested experimentation, but quickly revert to their cooperative baseline. Overall, their type is best described as a mostly unconditional, norm-guided cooperator with a slight willingness to test defection once.

This player is a stable, unconditional full contributor. They always give the entire endowment to the public account in every round, with no deviation, even when free-riding by others is persistent and visible and there is no punishment or reward mechanism. Their contribution rule does not appear to depend on recent outcomes, others’ behavior, or proximity to the final round; they behave as if full cooperation is a fixed norm rather than a contingent strategy. They clearly value group efficiency: they explicitly tell low contributors that they can earn more by contributing, framing contribution as the rational payoff-maximizing choice if enough others also cooperate. At the same time, they express moralized judgments of non-contributors, labeling them as “speculators” or “lazy people” and likening them to those who “eat welfare for free.” This suggests a fairness norm that strongly disfavors free-riding and treats it as both economically and morally inferior. Risk-wise, they are comfortable exposing themselves to exploitation: they continue to contribute fully despite ample evidence that some others will not reciprocate and despite an easy, risk-free option to free-ride themselves. This is consistent with a “principled cooperator” or “ideological cooperator” type: they prioritize maintaining a cooperative identity and shared order over short-term private gain. They show no strategic retaliation in response to under-contribution; their only “sanction” is verbal criticism. Even near the end, when unilateral deviation would be individually profitable and socially costless in expectation, they never defect, indicating low sensitivity to end-game incentives and a strong commitment to a consistent contribution rule.

This player is a highly stable, unconditional full cooperator. They always contribute the maximum allowed amount and never deviate, even when free-riding is persistent, repeated, and explicitly defended by others. Their behavior suggests they anchor on the social optimum rather than on short‑term individual payoff maximization. They appear to internalize a fairness norm that “everyone should contribute” and continue to honor it regardless of others’ deviation. They are clearly aware that some players are free-riding and that this reduces the group payoff, yet they do not retaliate by lowering their own contribution. Instead, their concern about others’ non-contribution shows up only in communication, not in their choices. This indicates low strategic conditionality in contributions: they do not seem to use a contingent strategy like “cooperate only if others do,” but rather follow a principle-based or identity-based strategy of “I contribute because that’s what I do.” They tolerate payoff inequality caused by defectors rather than engage in payoff‑maximizing defection themselves, implying low risk tolerance for undermining cooperation: they do not appear willing to risk the collapse of group cooperation to improve their own individual earnings. Instead, they prioritize maintaining a cooperative norm, even when that yields lower immediate returns than free-riding would.

This player is a generally high contributor who adapts sharply to the group’s cooperation level and to their own payoff position. Early on, they contribute well above the apparent group norm and even at or near the maximum, indicating an initial willingness to cooperate strongly and potentially a belief that high contributions are socially desirable or payoff-relevant. Over time, they reduce contributions noticeably when others settle into lower or inconsistent giving patterns, especially when they earn less than others despite contributing more. When the group’s contributions rise again, they respond by increasing their own, and when others drop or free-ride, they sometimes maintain high contributions but also experiment with low ones. This pattern suggests conditional cooperation combined with self-interest: they are willing to give a lot when others do, but they do not lock into a fixed norm of fairness and will under-contribute when it serves them or when others appear unreliable. The player is not rigidly fairness-driven: they continue to contribute high amounts even in situations where they could justify reducing contributions on equity grounds. At other times, they behave more opportunistically, contributing much less than others while still benefiting from the public fund. The resulting profile is a flexible conditional cooperator with some willingness to free-ride strategically. Contribution levels are volatile rather than stable; they oscillate between very high and low, consistent with a learning or exploratory strategy rather than a fixed heuristic. Their choices indicate moderate risk tolerance: they are willing to keep contributing a lot even after seeing that high contribution does not guarantee the highest payoff, but they also try safe, low-contribution strategies once they see that others will still fund the public good. They do not appear purely risk-averse or purely risk-seeking; instead, they update based on realized payoffs and others’ behavior.

This player is a generally high-but-not-maximal contributor who alternates between moderate and very high contributions rather than free-riding or rigidly sticking to one level. They rarely choose low contributions relative to the social optimum; instead, they hover around a middle value and periodically jump to very high contributions, including fully contributing. Their behavior suggests that they understand that contributing is not individually dominant but still treat moderate to high giving as their default. Contribution levels show conditionality: when others are clearly contributing a lot, they are willing to match or even exceed typical levels; when others’ cooperation becomes more uneven or one player defects, they do not collapse into full defection but instead revert toward their moderate “baseline” contribution rather than punish by undercontributing. There is no clear evidence that they finely calibrate their contribution to exact fairness (e.g., trying to match the group average each round); instead, they seem to operate with a rough internal band of “acceptable” giving and move within that band. They show some willingness to bear personal cost to maintain or test high-cooperation states, even in situations where they end up with lower payoffs than lower contributors. This indicates a relatively high tolerance for payoff risk in pursuit of cooperative outcomes. At the same time, they do not consistently choose the maximum contribution, so they are not purely altruistic; they balance self-interest and cooperation, but with a clear cooperative tilt. Their pattern over time suggests they learn from the environment but do so in a coarse, heuristic way rather than through precise optimization. They appear to notice that contributing more than others can leave them with comparatively worse payoffs, yet they keep returning to high contributions in later situations. This points to a stable pro-cooperation preference rather than short-term payoff chasing.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator in this environment. They always choose the maximum contribution available and never deviate, even when others defect or when defection would not be sanctioned. Their behavior is consistent with an efficiency-focused mindset: the group has quickly agreed on a clearly payoff-dominant norm, and they follow it without experimentation. They do not appear to condition their contribution on individual others’ behavior; instead they seem to treat contribution as a fixed group rule that should be followed regardless of specific defections. There is no visible concern for personal risk or exploitation in their own contribution choices; they continue to cooperate despite persistent free-riding by one participant and occasional deviations by another. This suggests they value norm adherence and group efficiency over short-term payoff maximization for themselves.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor in this environment. They always choose the cooperative, payoff-maximizing option and never deviate, even when others free ride or when cooperation could be “excused” near the end of the game. Their behavior is consistent with someone who internalizes the social norm that full contribution is the default and does not appear to condition contributions on others’ past behavior or on remaining rounds. They seem comfortable accepting the small personal risk that others might exploit their cooperation, prioritizing group efficiency and norm adherence over individual short-term gain. Fairness for them is expressed not by adjusting their own contribution downward in response to defectors, but by supporting sanctions against those defectors while maintaining their own cooperative standard.

The player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They always choose the maximum contribution available and never deviate, even when others repeatedly free-ride or when it is the final round. They treat full contribution as the obvious, focal strategy and explicitly frame it as an easy way to secure high earnings and speed up the task. Their cooperation is not visibly contingent on short-term payoffs or on whether free-riders persist; they continue to contribute fully even when punishment appears not to change the main free-rider’s behavior. They show moderate impatience with delays and implicitly value efficiency: universal max contributions make the game simpler and faster, which they like. Risk tolerance appears relatively high: they commit to full cooperation even when others are not guaranteed to reciprocate, relying on group norms and punishment rather than hedging. Fairness norms are strong and equality-oriented: they expect everyone to contribute the same maximal amount, and their frustration is directed at those who deviate from this shared standard rather than trying to exploit it themselves.

This player is a strict, stable full contributor. They always choose the maximum contribution available, without experimentation or visible hesitation, even when others defect or when punishment is costly. Their strategy aligns with a strong efficiency norm: they clearly value the high marginal per capita return and the group agreement to fully invest. They do not condition their own contributions on others’ misbehavior; instead, they rely on punishment rather than reducing their own contributions. Fairness for them means “everyone should put in the maximum,” and they seem willing to bear costs (through punishment) to sustain that norm instead of shifting toward defection. Risk tolerance around contribution is high: they are not deterred by the presence of multiple defectors or by end-game incentives and never appear to test selfish play.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They always choose the maximum possible contribution and never deviate, even when others free‑ride or defect. Their behavior suggests they reason that full contribution is collectively optimal and personally acceptable given the high multiplier and strong group norm. They do not appear to condition their contribution on others’ past behavior, nor do they reduce contributions in response to persistent low contributions by specific others. They show concern for fairness in chat (e.g., reacting negatively to defection and calling it “betrayal”) but do not translate that into contribution cuts; instead, they preserve cooperation and treat punishment as the corrective channel. Risk tolerance appears moderate-to-high: they are willing to keep investing fully despite the possibility that some may systematically exploit the group. They align their behavior with an equality / norm-following fairness standard: if everyone contributes max, everyone does well and the game is simple and efficient.

This player is mostly a high contributor who explicitly endorses full cooperation, but occasionally defects in a playful, opportunistic way. Early on, they advocate everyone investing the maximum and personally do so consistently for most rounds. Their default strategy is to follow the group norm of full contribution when such a norm clearly exists and is publicly endorsed. However, they also show a willingness to break that norm a couple of times without advance warning, apparently for amusement or to “spice up” the interaction, not out of systematic greed. Their defections are isolated and framed jokingly rather than as a sustained strategy. They appear to understand the efficiency of mutual full contribution and generally align with it, but they do not treat the norm as inviolable and do not seem strongly guided by fairness concerns. Their risk tolerance is moderate: they accept being punished heavily once and then quickly return to contributing fully, suggesting they treat losses as an acceptable cost of experimentation rather than something to avoid at all costs. Overall, they behave like a mostly cooperative player with streaks of light-hearted, low-commitment free riding.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator in this environment. They always contribute the entire endowment despite substantial variation in others’ behavior and in their own resulting payoffs. Their strategy shows no sensitivity to free-riding, to others’ contribution levels, or to changes in group efficiency over time. They appear to follow a strong internal norm of “always contribute everything,” rather than adjusting to incentives or matching others. This suggests a fairness norm oriented toward maximizing group output rather than equalizing individual payoffs or punishing low contributors. Their risk tolerance for exploitation by free-riders is high; they are willing to accept clearly disadvantageous situations relative to defectors and moderate contributors without changing behavior. There is no evidence of conditional cooperation, strategic under-contribution, or experimentation; their contribution policy is rigid and rule-like.

The player is a strict, stable full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment, never deviating despite repeated free-riding by others and despite being punished at least once. Their cooperation is not conditional on others’ behavior in this game: they maintain maximum contributions whether others fully cooperate, partly defect, or oscillate. This suggests a strong internal cooperation norm or a rule-based strategy (“always contribute fully”) rather than a payoff-maximizing or reciprocity-based approach. They show high risk tolerance in that they keep cooperating even when some co-players exploit the public good or when the environment becomes noisy with heavy punishment exchanges. They do not appear to use contribution decisions to express approval or disapproval; sanctioning is handled separately through punishment.

This player is a highly cooperative, norm-following contributor who strongly prefers full contributions to the public good. They almost always contribute their entire endowment, with a single clear deviation in the later part of the game. That deviation aligns with a period where several others are free-riding or partially contributing, suggesting their cooperation is conditional rather than purely unconditional. They appear willing to shoulder the cost of maintaining high group efficiency as long as the overall pattern of contributions is mostly full and stable. Their brief switch to non-contribution indicates they are not rigidly altruistic; they are sensitive to perceived unfairness and are willing to exploit the public good when norms are weak or being violated. They generally show risk tolerance in trusting others to cooperate despite intense punishment conflicts among other players, maintaining contributions even while chaos unfolds. They seem motivated by a fairness norm that “everyone should contribute fully”; when this is broadly satisfied, they stick to maximal cooperation.

The player is a strict, unwavering full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment and never deviate, regardless of others’ behavior, volatility in group contributions, punishment chaos, or changing conditions over the game. There is no sign of conditional cooperation: they do not reduce contributions in response to free-riding, aggression, or being personally punished. They effectively treat full contribution as a fixed rule rather than a strategic lever. This suggests a strong internal norm for cooperation or efficiency, and/or a belief that the public good mechanism is sufficiently beneficial that contributing is always the “right” move. Risk tolerance regarding exploitation by free riders appears high, given they maintain maximal cooperation even when some others repeatedly contribute nothing. Any fairness concerns they may have do not translate into contribution adjustments; they separate contribution decisions from others’ behavior.

This player is a highly stable, near-unconditional full contributor. They almost always give the entire endowment to the public good, regardless of others’ behavior or how many rounds remain. The only deviation is a single instance of free-riding in the mid-game, after which they immediately revert to full contributions and maintain that pattern through to the end. This suggests a strong cooperative norm and low sensitivity to short-term exploitation or noise. Their willingness to keep contributing fully even when some others free-ride, and even after they themselves are punished once, indicates they prioritize group efficiency and norm adherence over strict payoff maximization. They appear risk-tolerant regarding being exploited: they do not systematically condition contributions on others’ past contributions or punishment patterns. There is no clear evidence that they use contribution levels strategically to signal or to retaliate; contribution behavior is largely decoupled from the punishment environment and from end-of-game proximity.

This player is best described as a “quiet, payoff‑maximizing free rider with occasional conformity.” Their default is to keep the entire endowment and rely on others’ contributions to the public good. They very rarely contribute, and when they do, it coincides with full or near‑full cooperation by the rest of the group. This pattern suggests they understand that the mechanism is socially efficient (they continue to benefit from others’ giving even as total contributions fluctuate), but they personally prioritize private payoff over fairness or reciprocity. They show no evidence of conditional cooperation in the usual sense (i.e., “I’ll contribute when others do, and stop when they don’t”), because they continue not contributing even when nearly everyone else contributes consistently and the group repeatedly returns to high‑contribution states. Their few contributions look more like brief conformity to a strong norm than a stable principle: they do not maintain contributing after seeing others’ generosity and rewards, and they revert immediately to free riding. They appear insensitive to inequity in their own favor: they repeatedly accept outcomes where they earn more than high contributors, without adjusting their behavior toward more fairness. There is no sign of risk‑taking in the sense of “testing” lower group contributions; they do not try contributing in low‑contribution environments to move the group. Instead, they accept whatever contribution level others create and position themselves to gain most from it. Overall, they are a stable, low‑effort, opportunistic defector with minimal prosocial motivation.

This player is a stable, uncompromising free-rider. They never contribute to the public good, despite facing a group in which a clear majority of others contribute consistently and at high levels. Their behavior shows no experimentation, no partial cooperation, and no adjustment in response to others’ decisions or changes in overall group contribution. They appear to follow a fixed strategy that prioritizes maximizing individual payoff over any fairness or reciprocity norm. They does not display conditional cooperation: their contribution choice does not vary with how many others contribute, whether contributions from others rise or fall, or whether they themselves are rewarded. They appears insensitive to norms of fairness or proportionality; even when many others contribute, they maintain a zero contribution. Risk tolerance in terms of payoff variance is low: they choose the individually dominant action in every period, which is the least risky in terms of personal cost, ignoring potential social or relational gains from cooperation. Overall, their contribution pattern suggests an instrumental, payoff-maximizing mindset rather than one oriented toward group efficiency, reciprocity, or norm-following. If there is any social preference, it does not manifest through contributing; they systematically exploit the cooperative environment created by others.

The player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator within the discrete choice set available. They always choose the maximal contribution and never deviate, even as the group’s average cooperation fluctuates and sometimes declines. This indicates a strong pro-social or norm-following orientation that does not appear contingent on short-term payoff shifts or others’ free-riding. They do not engage in “tit-for-tat” or threshold-based adjustment; their behavior aligns more with a committed cooperator or moral type who treats contribution as the default, regardless of local exploitation risk. Their risk tolerance is high in the social sense: they repeatedly expose themselves to being taken advantage of by non-contributors, yet do not pull back. This suggests that maintaining a cooperative stance or internal consistency is a higher priority than guarding against relative disadvantage. Fairness norms are likely other-regarding but not enforced through withdrawal: they tolerate asymmetries in contribution without observable retaliation or downward adjustment. Whether this is driven by efficiency concerns, moral duty, or simple inertia is unknown, but there is no sign of opportunism or strategic free-riding.

This player is a mixed, conditional cooperator who alternates between full contribution and full defection rather than settling into a stable pattern. They willingly contribute the entire endowment multiple times, but also choose to free-ride fully in several instances. Their choice set is binary, so the observed variability is about timing, not magnitude. Their cooperation appears loosely contingent on group behavior: they continue to contribute when many others are also contributing at high levels, and they are willing to re-start contributing after periods of defection by some group members. At the same time, they sometimes defect even when most others are cooperating, indicating that they do not follow a simple “cooperate if others cooperate” rule. Instead, they seem to accept some level of free-riding on others’ contributions when those contributions are reliably present. They do not show strict fairness or tit-for-tat norms in their contribution behavior. They tolerate lower-than-unanimous cooperation and still contribute, but they also seem willing to exploit those high contributors when it is profitable. This mix suggests an opportunistic but not purely selfish type: they value the public good and are comfortable supporting it, yet they periodically claim the full private benefit of others’ cooperation. Risk tolerance is moderate: they frequently commit the full endowment despite uncertainty about others’ future behavior, but they also switch to defection rather than “overexpose” themselves when group cooperation seems fragile or when they may feel they have contributed enough. There is no evidence of increasing or decreasing trend over the game; instead the pattern stays volatile, suggesting that they are probing and adjusting locally rather than converging on a single stable strategy.

This player is a mostly high-contribution conditional cooperator with occasional opportunistic defection. After initially free-riding once, they settle into contributing in the large majority of rounds, even when others’ average contributions are only moderate and drifting down rather than fully cooperative. This pattern suggests they value maintaining group efficiency and are willing to incur the individual cost of contributing when they see a substantial cooperative core among peers. Their cooperation is not unconditional: they sometimes withhold contribution when they see substantial free-riding by others, indicating sensitivity to perceived fairness and possible reluctance to be exploited. At the same time, they do not require unanimous or near-unanimous cooperation to continue contributing; a stable subset of reliable contributors appears sufficient for them to stay pro-social. They show moderate tolerance for partial defectors and do not immediately revert to full free-riding in response to others’ inconsistency. They appear payoff-aware rather than naïve: the environment offers a socially efficient return to contributing, and their persistent contributions amid an ongoing cooperative base imply they recognize the mutual benefit of sustaining the public good. However, because they still defect occasionally even when cooperation remains relatively strong, they also pursue self-interested gains when they deem the social cost or reputational risk acceptable. Volatility in contribution is low-to-moderate: most decisions are consistent with a “cooperate by default, defect occasionally” style rather than erratic switching. Their risk tolerance seems moderate: they are willing to keep contributing despite uncertainty about others and about the game horizon, but they hedge with some defections instead of fully committing to unconditional cooperation. Overall, they fit a “strategic cooperator” type: inclined toward building and preserving a cooperative environment, but keeping the option of self-serving deviations when they feel the group is robust enough or fairness is strained.

This player is primarily a high, almost unconditional contributor who briefly experiments with free-riding. They typically choose the cooperative option regardless of moderate variation in others’ behavior, indicating a baseline norm of “I should contribute” rather than a finely tuned conditional strategy. Their contributions are largely stable over time; the single deviation into non-contribution appears exploratory or reactive rather than a full strategy shift. They do not seem to insist on strict fairness or one-to-one reciprocity in contributions: they keep contributing even when several group members persistently contribute nothing. This suggests a tolerance for inequality and some willingness to bear exploitation, at least for a while. At the same time, the lone free-riding episode shows they are not a pure altruist; they are willing to test what happens when they do not contribute, possibly to probe whether their own cooperation is pivotal or to hedge against feeling taken advantage of. Overall, they resemble a “mostly cooperative, mildly conditional” type, but with only weak adjustment of contributions in response to others’ freeriding.

This player is best described as a “long‑run conditional cooperator with late‑game fatigue and some strategic free‑riding.” They almost always contribute the full endowment early and through the mid‑game. Their default is to cooperate fully and reliably as long as the group is broadly cooperating and the situation feels fair and purposeful. They are very stable in this high‑contribution pattern for many rounds, even when a few others free‑ride; isolated defectors do not immediately change their behavior. Later in the game, as boredom, time pressure, and uncertainty about the remaining length become salient, they start alternating between full cooperation and full defection. These zero‑contribution choices appear clustered after they become frustrated with how long the task is taking and once it is clear that overall earnings are already high. At that point, they sometimes exploit the cooperative environment created by others while still staying in the game. They show some strategic awareness: they know that everyone benefits from full contributions, and they understand that the game length and payment structure create a time‑cost tradeoff. Their switch to non‑contribution aligns with a shift in motivation from “maximize joint payoff and finish the task properly” toward “minimize further time/effort while still collecting payment.” They do not, however, try to systematically punish or discipline specific low contributors via their own contributions; their free‑riding is driven more by fatigue and perceived over‑investment than by targeted retaliation. They appear moderately inequality‑averse in the sense that they accept bearing more cost than some others for a long time, but once the environment feels exploitative (many non‑contributors, unclear horizon, low hourly rate) they relax their own fairness standard and allow themselves to benefit from others’ cooperation. Their risk tolerance is mixed: early on they willingly “risk” full contribution because the group norm and logic of the game support it; later, with rising opportunity cost, they are less willing to risk further time for marginal gains and instead protect their own payoff. Behaviorally, they learn over time: initial behavior is purely cooperative; later behavior incorporates information about the real duration, the payment rate, and the researcher’s confirmation that the game is finite but longer than expected. This learning leads to a more self‑regarding strategy while still staying engaged enough to sometimes contribute fully when they feel it is reasonable.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator within the available action set. Whenever a choice exists between taking all 20 or contributing all 20, the player always contributes the full amount and never deviates. This pattern persists even when others free‑ride persistently, when average returns fall, and when the game becomes unexpectedly long and frustrating. They show no strategic “testing” of defection, no retaliation, and no apparent attempt to exploit others’ contributions. Their behavior indicates that they prioritize maximizing total group payoff over short‑term individual gain, and they do not adjust downward in response to others’ selfishness. This suggests a strong internal norm of “everyone should give everything” and a willingness to personally uphold that norm regardless of others’ behavior. There is no sign of risk‑averse hedging; they repeatedly put all their endowment at risk of exploitation and tolerate being paired with multiple low contributors. They appear to treat cooperation as the default, “correct” behavior in this environment, rather than as a conditional response to how fair others are being. They are attentive to efficiency: very early on they explicitly reason that collectively contributing everything is the money‑maximizing strategy. They stick to that efficiency rule for the rest of the game, even when the marginal returns in practice are reduced by others’ partial or non‑contribution. They do not diversify across strategies or try to fine‑tune contributions; they pick “always contribute all” and commit to it for the duration, despite the game going on longer than advertised and despite clear evidence of exploitable defectors.

This player is a predominantly self-interested free-rider who occasionally experiments with high contributions but quickly reverts to non-cooperation when punished. Their default is to contribute nothing to the public good and rely on others’ contributions. They show a few isolated episodes of substantial or full contributions embedded in long stretches of zero contribution. These cooperative bursts do not become a stable pattern and are followed by immediate return to free-riding, indicating that cooperation is not their baseline preference. Their contribution behavior appears weakly conditional on the social environment and strongly conditional on personal payoff experience. When they increase contributions and receive net losses due to punishment, they abandon cooperation. When they free-ride and still earn relatively high payoffs (even while being punished), they stay with zero contributions. There is no clear evidence that they try to match the group average, reward high-contributors, or signal willingness to build a norm; their occasional high contributions look more like sporadic tests or mood-driven deviations than systematic norm-following. They tolerate substantial payoff risk when trying high contributions, but they do not sustain such risk once it is met with sanctions. Over time, their strategy simplifies to a robust “keep everything” rule, regardless of fluctuations in others’ contributions. They appear indifferent to efficiency and fairness at the group level; they do not systematically adjust their contributions upward in cooperative environments, nor do they reduce contributions further (they are already at zero) in less cooperative environments. Their fairness norm, if any, is weak or subordinated to a payoff-maximizing heuristic that prioritizes keeping their endowment and collecting returns from others.

This player is a “conditional, payoff‑focused enforcer” with moderate average contributions and clear responsiveness to the group’s cooperation level.
