They do not pick a fixed norm; instead they oscillate between relatively high and relatively low giving, tracking whether the environment looks cooperative. They repeatedly contribute around half their endowment in more cooperative phases and drop to low single digits or zero when others’ average contributions are low. They neither free‑ride systematically nor act as a pure altruist; they appear to weigh the public return against the private cost and adjust to maintain a favorable payoff rather than to uphold a strict fairness rule. They show no evidence of trying to perfectly match the group average or to target a specific percentage of others’ behavior; instead, they cluster around a few “anchor” contribution levels (0, low single digits, about half endowment). This pattern suggests a simple, state‑dependent rule of thumb rather than a finely calibrated equity norm. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to put in substantial contributions when others look cooperative but do not escalate to full contributions and do not consistently “lead” the group with the highest giving. When the environment turns less cooperative, they quickly deescalate contributions rather than risking sustained losses. They appear sensitive to payoff effectiveness: they invest more into public goods when total contributions (and implied payoffs) are higher and scale back when the group slacks. This indicates that they care about efficiency, but only when it is backed by others’ behavior; they do not sacrifice much to “rescue” an uncooperative group. Overall, their contribution strategy is adaptive and opportunistic: they support cooperation when it seems likely to pay off, but readily protect their own payoff when others defect. Their fairness concern is present but bounded by self‑interest.

The player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They always choose the maximum possible contribution and never deviate, even when others reduce their own contributions or when some players free‑ride. They repeatedly promote a “everyone goes max” norm and explicitly link maximum contribution to mutual gain, indicating that they understand the social efficiency logic and prioritize it over short‑term self‑payoffs. They do not condition their own contribution on others’ behavior; their response to under‑contributors is verbal persuasion rather than reducing their own contribution. Fairness for them is framed as group‑level efficiency and consistency rather than punishment or withholding cooperation. Their risk tolerance appears high: they continue contributing fully even when group behavior is noisy and when they suspect some individuals of being unreliable.

This player is a strongly cooperative, near-maximum contributor. They consistently give their full endowment except for a single instance where they reduce their contribution modestly while the group’s average also drops. There is no evidence they ever free‑ride or react punitively to others’ low contributions by cutting back substantially. Their behavior suggests they adopt “always cooperate” as a default norm and maintain it even when some others deviate. Their one non‑max choice appears situational rather than the start of a downward trend: they immediately return to full contributions afterward and stay there through the end. This points to high contribution stability and low volatility overall. They do not explicitly discuss contribution strategy in detail, but their early affirmation of going “max” aligns with the emerging group norm and they stay consistent with that norm. They appear willing to accept the risk that others might undercontribute, as they maintain high contributions even when others evidently are not all doing maximum. This indicates relatively high risk tolerance in service of group efficiency. Their fairness norm seems to emphasize “everyone should contribute maximum” rather than tit‑for‑tat adjustments; they do not condition their own contribution on specific others’ behaviors.

The player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They consistently give the maximum possible amount to the public fund every round and never experiment with lower contributions, even when others reduce theirs or when free-riding appears. There is no visible conditionality based on others’ contributions; they keep contributing fully through both cooperative and more defect-prone phases. They appear comfortable with the risk that others may not match their cooperation and do not threaten or suggest decreasing contributions in response to others. This suggests a strong internal norm favoring full cooperation rather than a contingent “tit-for-tat” or payoff-maximizing strategy.

This player is a very high and mostly stable contributor. They almost always give the maximum possible amount, with a single clear deviation where they give less and once where they give nothing. That deviation does not appear tied to punishment or explicit retaliation, and there is no chat explanation from them, so the motive is unknown. Overall, they behave as if they accept and follow a “max contribution” group norm and do not test or challenge it. Their contributions do not seem precisely conditional on particular others (they keep contributing even when some players under-contribute), suggesting a cooperative default rather than fine-tuned reciprocity in the contribution stage. They show no visible risk aversion about being exploited in the public fund: their pattern is consistent with prioritizing the collective outcome or at least accepting the cooperative convention as the focal equilibrium.

The player is a highly stable unconditional cooperator. They always contribute the full endowment and never deviate, including when others reduce their contributions or when within-group alliances form. There is no visible conditionality on others’ behavior, no retaliation against low contributors, and no attempt to free-ride on high-contribution norms. They do not appear to adjust contributions in response to reward patterns, chat-based coalitions, or perceived glitches. Their risk tolerance regarding cooperation is high: they persist with maximal contributions even when group coordination becomes fragmented and when some players openly shirk. Fairness concerns, if present, do not manifest as contribution adjustments; instead, they act as if the socially efficient, fully cooperative outcome is the default regardless of others’ choices.

The player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They always contribute the maximum possible amount and never deviate despite variability in others’ contributions. Their advocacy for everyone to contribute fully appears sincere and consistent, not contingent on rewards or on others’ behavior. They exhibit a strong efficiency norm: they treat full contribution as obviously best for the group and encourage others to follow it. There is no evidence of risk-averse hedging or retaliatory under-contribution; they tolerate free-riding by some others without changing their own contribution pattern.

This player is a mostly-high contributor who periodically free-rides in a moderate, opportunistic way. They start fully cooperative, matching the group’s “everyone max” norm without hesitation. After early confirmation that others are contributing at or near the maximum, they begin to undercontribute in several mid-game rounds while the rest of the group largely stays high. Their deviations are partial rather than extreme: they never go to zero, and at their lowest they still contribute a small positive amount. This pattern suggests they understand that high group contributions are robust enough that they can personally take more while still benefiting from others’ cooperation. They show no visible attempt to track individual deviations or punish low contributors via their own contributions; when some players contribute less, they do not clearly respond by increasing their own to stabilize the norm. Instead, their own choices appear more driven by personal gain than by norm enforcement. In fully coordinated, high-contribution situations, they often go back to contributing the maximum, indicating that they accept the high-contribution equilibrium when it is salient and when others are clearly on board. Overall, their contribution strategy mixes conditional cooperation with self-serving opportunism: they participate in high cooperation but intermittently shave off their own input once the public good seems secured.

This player is a “conditional high contributor with selfish fallback and volatility.” They are clearly willing to contribute large amounts—often three quarters or more of their endowment—especially when others appear to be contributing at moderate to high levels. They also occasionally push their contribution very near full cooperation. However, these high contributions are interspersed with stretches of very low contributions, including repeated near-zero or zero choices. Their contribution pattern is not stable; it oscillates between generous and minimally cooperative states. This volatility tracks group behavior: when the general contribution environment looks strong or improving, they tend to re-engage with high contributions; when the environment weakens or seems mixed, they flip back to a low-contribution, payoff-maximizing stance. This indicates conditional cooperation rather than unconditional altruism. They do not appear to follow a strict fairness norm such as “match the group average” or “match the highest contributors.” At times, they contribute well above the average and above many peers, absorbing clear personal cost relative to free-riding alternatives. At other times, they under-contribute relative to a cooperative subgroup. This oscillation suggests they are experimenting with and reacting to the perceived “mood” of the group rather than enforcing a fixed fairness rule. Risk tolerance appears moderate to high in the social domain: they are willing to repeatedly risk substantial personal payoff by contributing heavily even in a group that includes clear low contributors. However, in periods when they revert to near-zero contributions, they reveal that they are not rigidly principled; they are capable of and comfortable with exploiting cooperative environments for higher private payoffs. Overall, their strategy is best characterized as: “I prefer high cooperation and will often invest heavily, but I withdraw into low contributions when I feel the cooperative norm is fragile, inconsistent, or not sufficiently reciprocated by others.”

This player is a generally pro-social but strategically cautious contributor who oscillates between high and low contributions in response to the group’s behavior and to their own payoffs. They are clearly willing to fully cooperate and often do so, but they also revert to partial free-riding when they perceive others under-contributing or when high cooperation appears personally costly. Their behavior suggests conditional cooperation: they tend to increase contributions when many others are contributing moderate-to-high amounts, and they reduce contributions after observing repeated low inputs from a substantial subset of players. They do not settle into a single stable contribution level; instead, they use a small set of “modes” (very high, moderate, low) and switch among them, apparently based on recent patterns rather than on a long-term fixed plan. They seem to recognize that full cooperation is not individually optimal in low-contribution environments and are willing to protect their own payoff by contributing only a small fraction of their endowment under such conditions. At the same time, they do not adopt a pure free-rider strategy: even when others are mostly low contributors, they still often put in a nontrivial amount rather than dropping to zero. This indicates a mix of self-interest and a persistent, if tempered, cooperative norm. The player appears risk-tolerant with respect to testing high contributions: they repeatedly invest heavily despite previously getting relatively low returns when others do not match them. However, this risk tolerance is bounded; after sequences where their high contributions coincide with widespread low contributions by others, they pull back. There is some evidence that they prefer fairness in relative terms: they are more willing to give high contributions when several others are also giving high or at least moderate contributions, and they are more reluctant when a few players are systematically close to zero. Overall, they behave like a “hopeful conditional cooperator” who periodically reverts to partial self-protection.

This player is best described as a “calculating free-rider with occasional exploratory cooperation.” Their default is to contribute nothing and rely on others’ provision of the public good. Non-contribution is highly stable across the game, with only a few isolated episodes of positive contribution. Those cooperative episodes are not sustained and do not escalate into a pattern, suggesting experimentation or momentary responsiveness rather than an internalized norm of fairness. They appears to prioritize maximizing own payoff under the given marginal per-capita return, which is below 1, making free-riding individually optimal in standard game-theoretic terms. Their behavior is consistent with understanding or at least intuitively following this logic: they treat the group as a resource to exploit rather than something to protect. Their contributions do not clearly track group average behavior in a systematic way. They sometimes cooperate in highly cooperative environments and then revert to full free-riding even when others continue to contribute significantly. This suggests low commitment to conditional cooperation; at most, they briefly “test” contributing when others are generous, then return to non-contribution regardless of the continued generosity. There is no obvious attempt to “match” or “punish” others’ contribution levels through their own contribution decisions. They do not show evidence of fairness concerns in the sense of trying to equalize own contribution with others’ or with the group average. They regularly take advantage of high contributors without compensating through future contributions. Risk tolerance regarding payoff seems moderate: they occasionally deviate from the safe, selfish strategy (by contributing mid-to-high amounts) but only in a few isolated instances; once it is clear that they can earn high payoffs by free-riding in a still-cooperative group, they settle into that pattern and rarely deviate. Overall, their contribution profile is: baseline pure free-rider, with sporadic, short-lived cooperative spikes that do not translate into a lasting cooperative strategy.

This player is a predominantly high contributor with intermittent, opportunistic free-riding. They typically give the full endowment to the public fund, especially through the middle of the game, indicating a baseline cooperative disposition and comfort with sacrificing immediate private payoff for group benefit. However, they occasionally switch to taking the full endowment for themselves even when others are contributing robustly, suggesting conditional cooperation that tolerates some personal exploitation of the group. Their shifts between contributing and not contributing do not clearly track any explicit public coordination attempts or obvious collapses in group contributions; instead, they appear to mix cooperation with periodic profit-taking. This pattern points to a “mostly cooperator who occasionally exploits” profile rather than a pure altruist or a consistent defector. They do not appear to be strongly driven by strict fairness norms (they sometimes accept being above or below others in contribution levels without visible adjustment via chat or reward) and show no evidence of sanctioning via available reward options. Their risk tolerance around contribution is moderate: they often contribute even when others’ behavior is heterogeneous and not reliably high, implying a willingness to risk being exploited or at least to accept that possibility for much of the game. At the same time, the existence of several zero-contribution choices indicates that they also value payoff maximization and are not locked into unconditional cooperation.

This player is a mixed, opportunistic contributor with no strong fairness norm. They comfortably alternate between full contribution and full defection and never settle into a stable cooperative pattern. Their choices do not track any clear threshold of group cooperation: sometimes they contribute when others mostly contribute, sometimes they free-ride on a largely cooperative group, and sometimes they continue to defect even after others voice intentions to cooperate. They show no aversion to being the sole defector when others contribute, and no sustained attempt to enforce “everyone contributes” behavior through their own actions. Contribution appears driven more by short-term experimentation and situational whim than by a consistent principle, and there is no visible risk-averse tendency to stick with one safer pattern; instead they oscillate between payoff-maximizing defection and occasional full cooperation.

This player is a high, nearly unconditional contributor. They choose the maximal contribution in the overwhelming majority of rounds, including early on before group norms are clear, in mid-game when others’ cooperation is mixed, and in late and final rounds when defection would be payoff-maximizing. Occasional zero contributions appear but do not develop into a sustained free-riding strategy; instead, the player quickly returns to full contributions and then stays there. Their behavior suggests a stable cooperative disposition rather than responsive tit‑for‑tat or profit-maximizing play. They do not appear to condition contributions strongly on others’ recent behavior or on the group’s average contribution level. Even when others free-ride or when the stakes of end-game opportunism are highest, they keep investing fully. This pattern implies a fairness or norm-based motive (“I should contribute”) rather than a calculated response to incentives or risk.

The player is a strategic free-rider who oscillates between full cooperation and zero contribution rather than settling at a stable intermediate level. They clearly understand that contributing is socially efficient; they repeatedly choose the maximum contribution in many rounds despite no punishment mechanism. However, they also frequently choose to contribute nothing, even when others appear highly cooperative. This pattern suggests they treat contribution as a lever for payoff management rather than as a moral obligation. Their behavior is volatile rather than stable: they switch between extremes (full vs. zero contribution, with occasional mid-level contributions) without a simple monotonic trend. The decisions do not map cleanly onto others’ average behavior; they sometimes cooperate fully when others look cooperative, and sometimes defect fully in similarly cooperative environments. This indicates only weak conditional cooperation at best. They do not seem to follow a strict “match the group average” or “reward high cooperation with high cooperation” rule. They appear willing to bear short-term individual cost (by fully contributing) when they judge that returns from the public fund are sufficiently high or that the environment is likely to remain cooperative. At other times, they fully free-ride despite group cooperation, implying comfort exploiting collective effort. The alternation between high and zero contributions suggests experimentation and payoff comparison: they test what happens when they free-ride amid cooperators and also what happens when they join in. Their risk tolerance seems moderate: they are willing to deviate strongly from the safe, modest-contribution middle ground, but they do not commit to permanent exploitation; instead, they regularly return to cooperation. Fairness norms appear weak. The player accepts getting more than their “fair share” when they free-ride among high contributors and shows no compensatory behavior such as systematically increasing contributions afterward to “make up for” earlier defection. At the same time, they are not purely selfish: the persistent return to full contributions in many rounds is inconsistent with a strict selfish-maximizer type given the option to free-ride entirely. Overall, they behave like a payoff-sensitive, opportunistic mixed strategist rather than a principled cooperator or pure defector.

This player is a very high, near-unconditional contributor. They usually give almost all of their endowment, with only small downward deviations, and they never free‑ride. Their pattern is stable across early, middle, and late stages: contribution levels are consistently at the top of the group distribution and do not track short‑term fluctuations in others’ behavior in any clear, contingent way. When average group cooperation dips or some players contribute very little, the focal player maintains high input, sometimes with only a minor reduction. This suggests a strong internal norm of “do the socially efficient thing” rather than a reactive “match others” rule. Given the marginal per capita return, their behavior is individually costly but socially efficient. They appear willing to accept lower personal payoff relative to a selfish best reply in order to sustain a high public good. They do not show evidence of risk‑averse experimentation or oscillation; instead they quickly move to (and remain near) maximal contribution. Concern for outcome fairness relative to low contributors is not expressed in their actions; they continue to allow free‑riders to benefit from their high contributions without compensating downward adjustment. Conditional cooperation (e.g., tit‑for‑tat style) is weak or absent; any slight reductions seem more like minor calibration than sanctioning. Overall, they match a “principled high cooperator” or “strong altruist / efficiency‑oriented” type rather than a strategic, payoff‑maximizing type.

This player is a volatile, mixed-motive contributor who oscillates between full cooperation and active free-riding. They clearly understand that contributing is socially efficient (given the high multiplier), because they are repeatedly willing to give the entire endowment and at other times to contribute substantial amounts when peers are highly cooperative. However, they do not adopt a stable high-cooperation identity; instead, they alternate between: - fully cooperative moves (contributing all or almost all of their endowment), - low or zero contributions that exploit others’ generosity. Their contribution pattern appears conditional and experimental rather than random. When the group’s visible average is high, they often match or exceed it on some occasions, but they also intermittently drop to very low contributions even when others stay cooperative. This suggests they are not strictly guided by a fairness norm such as “match the group average” or “match high contributors.” Instead, they blend: - episodes of strong pro-social investment in the public good, - episodes of strategic self-interest where they let others carry the load. They seem comfortable with risk: they sometimes sacrifice large immediate payoffs (by contributing fully and/or spending heavily on rewards) despite uncertainty about others’ future behavior. Yet they also show opportunistic risk-taking in the opposite direction, keeping their entire endowment while trusting others to keep contributing. Fairness as an internal constraint is therefore weak or inconsistent; they are willing both to shoulder more than their share and to free-ride when it suits them. The overall pattern fits an “opportunistic conditional cooperator”: someone who responds to a cooperative environment but feels no obligation to maintain a consistent contribution role.

This player is a strict, unconditional full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment and never deviate, regardless of others’ behavior, temporary drops in group contributions, or the presence/absence of particular group members. There is no evidence of conditional cooperation, retaliation, or experimentation; they do not reduce contributions when others give less, nor do they adjust upward because others are generous—they simply maintain the maximum level. This suggests a strong internal norm that full contribution is the “right” or expected behavior, combined with low concern for short-term individual payoff optimization. Their risk tolerance appears high in the sense that they are willing to expose themselves to exploitation by low contributors and absentees without adjusting. Attitudes toward fairness are expressed socially rather than via contribution changes; they keep cooperating even when others slack, implying a preference for maintaining a stable cooperative standard over enforcing equality through their own contribution decisions.

This player is a very high, near-unconditional cooperator. They almost always contribute the full endowment and only briefly deviate once to a moderately high contribution, then immediately return to maximal giving. Their behavior is highly stable rather than exploratory: once they see that others are generally contributing well, they lock into a pattern of full cooperation and maintain it even when a few group members occasionally reduce their contributions or drop out. They do not appear to condition their own contribution on minor free riding; they tolerate some inequality rather than adjusting down. This suggests they prioritize maintaining group efficiency and stability over tracking strict payoff fairness on a round‑by‑round basis. Their risk tolerance toward exploitation is relatively high: they continue to invest fully in the public good even though the game allows others to benefit without matching effort, and even when some players temporarily slack. They seem to adopt a norm that “everyone should contribute fully,” and they personally uphold it robustly regardless of small deviations by others.

This player is a strategic, variable contributor who mixes free-riding with selective full contributions. They alternate between taking everything and contributing everything, rather than using intermediate levels, suggesting a coarse, “all-or-nothing” heuristic. Their contributions show low internal consistency and appear conditionally responsive to the group’s aggregate behavior: they are more willing to contribute when others are already contributing at relatively high levels, and they free-ride when others’ contributions or the marginal returns to them look secure without their help. They do not follow a simple “always defect” or “always cooperate” rule. Fairness norms appear weak or instrumental. They repeatedly accept benefits from the public fund in rounds where they contribute nothing, indicating comfort with exploiting cooperators when possible. At the same time, they occasionally join high-contribution patterns, which can be interpreted as opportunistic cooperation when doing so is likely to sustain a profitable group outcome. There is no clear evidence that they try to match the group average or enforce proportionality in a principled way; their behavior fits better with payoff-oriented experimentation than with strict fairness. Risk tolerance seems moderate to high: they sometimes invest fully in contribution even when previous patterns suggest that many others may not cooperate. Conversely, they also risk social disapproval (via visible behavior) by blatantly free-riding while others bear the cost. This points to a player who is willing to gamble on both cooperation and defection if it might yield higher payoffs, rather than sticking to a conservative, predictable pattern.

This player is a mixed, opportunistic contributor who alternates between full cooperation and full free riding, rather than settling into a stable pattern. They either contribute everything or nothing, never splitting the endowment. Their cooperation does not strictly increase or decrease over time; it fluctuates, suggesting they are experimenting or reacting to the group rather than following a fixed plan. They do not insist on strict fairness or proportionality. They sometimes cooperate when a substantial share of others are defecting, and sometimes defect when many others are contributing. This indicates that maintaining a fair match between their contribution and the group’s average is not their primary concern. They appear willing to accept the risk of short‑term loss from contributing in order to explore or sustain cooperation, but they also repeatedly exploit cooperative environments by free riding once contributions are high. Their final behavior includes both cooperation and defection, even after conditions become quite cooperative, which suggests they do not converge to a purely “conditional cooperator” type; instead they combine conditional responsiveness with opportunism. Overall, they play as a “flexible opportunist”: willing to cooperate, but quick to take advantage of cooperative others, and comfortable with switching modes as the environment shifts.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment, with no adjustment to others’ behavior, overall contribution levels, or apparent stage of the game. Their strategy prioritizes maximizing group welfare over individual payoff, even when others contribute less and when there are repeated opportunities to update. They show no evidence of conditional cooperation: they do not scale their contributions up or down in response to low or high contributions from others. This pattern suggests a strong fairness or moral norm favoring full contribution, applied consistently and without visible concern for exploitation risk. Their risk tolerance is high; they accept the possibility of being taken advantage of by low contributors without changing their own behavior. They appear rule-driven rather than payoff-driven, and they treat full contribution as the “right” action regardless of context.

The player is a moderately cooperative, payoff-conscious contributor who centers around mid-to-high contributions without ever going to full defection or full altruism. They avoid extremes and rarely choose the lowest or highest feasible contributions. Their behavior shows a generally upward shift in giving after being exposed to partners who are reliably contributing nontrivial amounts; they respond to a cooperative environment by raising their own stake rather than exploiting it. Contribution levels adjust in a measured way rather than jumping erratically, indicating a strategic and somewhat calculated style rather than impulsive play. The pattern suggests they track others’ behavior and update their own contributions in the same general direction as group contributions, while still preserving a margin of self-interest: they do not match the highest contributor when one peer is especially generous, but they do move closer to that level over time. They appear to hold a fairness norm where everyone should contribute a meaningful but not necessarily maximal share. When others’ average contributions are clearly positive and stable, the player is willing to contribute more than this perceived “fair baseline,” but only to a moderate extent. When peer contributions dip, the player reduces their own, though they do not retaliate by dropping to zero; they remain conditionally cooperative rather than turning punitive or exploitative. Risk tolerance appears moderate. The player is willing to risk personal payoff by increasing contributions once they infer that others are not chronic free riders. At the same time, they preserve some private coins every round, suggesting ongoing concern about being exploited or about uncertain partner behavior. There is no evidence they ever test the boundaries with extreme under-contribution to see if others will compensate; instead they tend to err on the side of continued, bounded cooperation. Their conditionality is “proportional, not binary”: they neither fully reciprocate high cooperation nor fully withdraw when cooperation softens. Instead, they adjust their level of giving by moderate increments, loosely tracking the group’s cooperative climate while maintaining their own target band between self-interest and group welfare.

This player is a mostly cooperative but strategically flexible contributor. They alternate between fully contributing and fully withholding, rather than using intermediate or mixed patterns (which are not available in this design), suggesting a willingness to switch between pure cooperation and pure free-riding depending on context. Their contributions do not monotonically decay over time; they both increase and decrease across the game, indicating that they do not follow a simple “cooperate early, defect late” heuristic. They show that they are willing to bear a personal cost to support group efficiency in multiple rounds, but they also demonstrate that they are not bound by a strict fairness or equal-contribution norm, since they deliberately free-ride when others contribute more than they do. Their risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to cooperate even after being targeted by costly punishment, but they also test non-contribution when they anticipate that group cooperation may be sustained without their own input. Their contribution behavior is conditional, but the exact rule is ambiguous: it likely depends on perceived chances of maintaining cooperation and on expectations of others’ reactions, rather than on a strict “match others” or “punish free-riders by defecting” mechanism. Overall, they fit a “conditional cooperator with opportunistic episodes” profile.

This player starts as a consistent free-rider, keeping their entire endowment while relying on others’ contributions. Their initial behavior indicates a clear willingness to benefit from the public good without paying into it, despite others contributing substantially. They only switch to full contribution once they have experienced severe and repeated punishment from multiple co-players, suggesting their cooperation is reactive and enforcement-driven rather than intrinsically motivated. The shift from persistent non-contribution to full contribution suggests they update their strategy in response to strong negative incentives, not to fairness norms per se. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they initially risk social sanctions by free-riding, but once punishment becomes very costly for them, they adjust to a safer, more compliant cooperative strategy.

This player is a mostly high contributor who pivots to free-riding once cooperation is well established and the horizon is short. They consistently choose the cooperative action while the group has mixed or imperfect cooperation and while multiple rounds remain. Their choices indicate a preference for full contribution when others are not all cooperating, suggesting they value maintaining or building group welfare and are willing to shoulder that cost. Once the group reaches a state of universal cooperation and the game is about to end, they switch to non-contribution, exploiting the cooperative environment after others have already demonstrated sustained prosocial behavior. This pattern suggests conditional cooperation with an opportunistic end-game twist rather than unconditional altruism. They appear comfortable taking a payoff-risk in early stages to support cooperation but prioritize individual gain when future repercussions are limited.

This player is a “conditional cooperator with rapid norm alignment.” They initially free‑ride when others’ behavior and punishment norms are unclear, then quickly shift to full cooperation and remain stable once they see that high contribution is emerging as the norm. After the initial deviation, they always contribute the full endowment even when some peers still free‑ride or partially defect. This suggests a shift from short‑term self-interest toward alignment with a perceived cooperative standard. They show no signs of experimenting with intermediate or alternating strategies; their pattern is a one‑time adjustment followed by consistency. Their behavior indicates moderate risk tolerance: after seeing that cooperation is enforced and common, they are willing to incur the opportunity cost of full contribution rather than hedge by partially defecting. Their fairness norm appears to favor matching or supporting the majority cooperative behavior once it is unambiguous, rather than insisting on strict reciprocal tit‑for‑tat with each individual peer.

This player is a predominantly self-interested conditional free-rider with mild, intermittent cooperation. They understand that universal contribution is best for the group but rarely follow it themselves. They alternate between contributing and not in the early and middle game, then settle into almost pure non-contribution later, even while others mostly keep contributing. Their cooperation does not steadily increase after being punished, nor collapse entirely after any single event; instead, they show a loose, low-commitment pattern: occasionally matching the cooperative norm, but reverting to zero when there is no immediate, salient social pressure. They show no strong fairness norm in action: they accept high payoffs while others invest more, and they continue not contributing even when reminded by others that “everyone should work together.” They do not appear strongly risk-averse in the economic sense, as they are willing to expose themselves to repeated punishment risk rather than “buy” social approval by contributing. Their behavior fits someone prioritizing personal earnings under uncertainty about the game length, with only weak, short-lived cooperative impulses.

This player is a strict, unwavering full cooperator. They always contribute the maximum allowed amount and never deviate, even when others free‑ride, when average contributions fall, or when they themselves are punished. Their contribution pattern is highly stable and not visibly conditional on others’ behavior in the group; they show no sign of testing defection or experimenting with lower contributions. They verbally endorse the “everyone contribute” norm early on and then simply live by it without further strategic commentary. They appear willing to accept personal cost and unequal outcomes as long as they continue to uphold the cooperation norm. There is no explicit evidence that they track individual defections to adjust their own contributions; their behavior fits a rule‑based or principled cooperator rather than a conditional or calculating one. Risk aversion toward deviation from the group plan seems high: they do not gamble on personal gain by withholding, even when impatience and boredom rise or others openly question how long the game will last.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator under the available choices. They always give the maximum possible contribution and never deviate, regardless of how much others give or how long the game appears to last. They do not condition their behavior on observed free-riding, on being punished, or on fatigue/boredom expressed in the group. Their effective norm is “everyone should contribute fully,” and they personally comply with that norm even when others clearly do not. They tolerate repeated disadvantage relative to low contributors without visible attempts to personally exploit the situation. Risk tolerance with respect to others’ cooperation is high: they keep investing into the public good despite uncertainty about the number of rounds, doubts about the system’s fairness, and evidence of miscoordination. They show no strategic endgame defection once they suspect the game might be very long or unbounded. Overall, their contribution profile fits a “principled cooperator” type: they prioritize the cooperative rule over short-term individual gain or strict reciprocity.

This player is a strict, unwavering full cooperator. They always choose the maximum contribution available and never deviate, regardless of others’ behavior, apparent free‑riding, punishment, boredom, or uncertainty about how long the game lasts. Their cooperation is stable rather than reactive: they do not adjust contributions in response to low contributors, punishment, or changing group size. This pattern suggests that they treat “always contribute” as a fixed rule rather than as a contingent strategy. They accept the individual cost of contributing even when group contributions dip, implying either a strong pro‑social or rule‑following norm, or an unwillingness to engage in strategic defection. Risk tolerance appears relatively high: they continue to invest fully in the public good despite visible evidence that some others regularly contribute zero and despite being punished at times. They do not appear to condition their contribution on fairness or reciprocity; they maintain full cooperation even when the situation would safely justify some self‑protective free‑riding.

This player is a mostly cooperative but opportunistic conditional contributor with clear sensitivity to relative contributions and punishment. They recognize that high contribution from others is the norm and often match or nearly match that norm, but intermittently exploit it. They contribute at the full endowment in many rounds, showing they accept and play along with a high-cooperation standard when it seems stable. However, they also insert low or zero contributions at points where others remain fully cooperative, indicating a willingness to free-ride when they think they can get away with it or when they are testing limits. Their pattern is volatile rather than stable: contributions alternate between full cooperation and marked defection, with some intermediate “compromise” contributions. This suggests they are experimenting around a cooperative baseline rather than following a fixed rule. They appear to use others’ prior behavior as a key input: when others consistently provide high contributions, they oscillate between matching them and exploiting them. When they are punished following their lower contributions, they temporarily increase back to full contributions, which indicates that sanction risk influences their choices. They do not appear to follow a strict fairness norm, because they sometimes contribute much less than others even when others are perfectly cooperative. At the same time, they do not persist in low contributions in the face of cooperation and punishment; instead they move back toward high contributions, implying that they accept the legitimacy or inevitability of the cooperative standard under punishment. Their behavior is consistent with a moderately risk-tolerant player: they are willing to incur a realistic risk of sanctions for the potential earnings advantage of free-riding, but they retreat from that risk when it materializes. Overall, this player’s type is best summarized as a “punishment-sensitive opportunistic cooperator”: they are inclined to cooperate in a high-cooperation environment but periodically defect to test boundaries or gain advantage, and they adjust upward again when sanctions signal that defection is costly.

This player is a highly cooperative, near-unconditional high contributor. They contribute the full endowment in almost all rounds and only very slightly reduce this at the very end, with one isolated mid-game deviation to a moderate contribution. Their default is to prioritize the group account over personal immediate payoff, even when others’ contributions fluctuate widely and some players consistently free-ride. They do not appear to condition their contributions strongly on others’ average behavior: cooperation remains high during both high- and low-contribution phases, and in the presence of obvious free riders. This suggests they follow a stable pro-social or norm-based rule (“I should contribute”) rather than a reactive “matching” or “tit-for-tat” strategy. Their occasional small deviations from full contribution do not become a new pattern and are not systematically linked to clear changes in others’ behavior, so these look like minor adjustments or experiments rather than a deliberate, sustained shift to self-interest. They do not appear to enforce proportionality or strict fairness; they continue giving even when others earn more by contributing less. Risk tolerance is high in social terms: they are willing to risk being exploited by low contributors in order to sustain cooperation. Overall, they fit a “stubborn high cooperator” / “conditional only at the margins” profile: robustly generous, weakly conditional at best, and not driven by payoff-maximizing logic.

This player is a moderately contributing, highly payoff‑oriented conditional cooperator with opportunistic free‑riding. They rarely choose the extremes of always‑cooperate or always‑defect; instead, they oscillate between contributing nothing, half, three‑quarters, or all of their endowment. Contributions cluster around a middle value with intermittent full cooperation and full free‑riding. They appear responsive to the *level* of cooperation in the group and to their own recent payoffs. When the group is clearly sustaining high contributions, they are willing to match or exceed the apparent average, sometimes giving their full endowment. When cooperation is high and their own defection is not identifiable, they show no reluctance to free‑ride and enjoy high returns. They do not show strong evidence of a fairness norm such as always matching the group average or targeting equal payoffs; instead, they tolerate personal gain from others’ high contributions and accept being below top earners when that requires them to contribute more. The pattern suggests risk‑sensitive, payoff‑tracking behavior rather than principle‑driven fairness. They are willing to incur some “sacrifice” by contributing fully in some rounds even when that gives them a lower payoff than low contributors, but they do not reinforce this consistently and readily revert to low or zero contributions once they have experienced the gains from free‑riding. Volatility in contributions is moderate: they do not change amount erratically every round, but they do move between low, medium, and high contributions over the game, consistent with an adaptive, outcome‑contingent strategy rather than a fixed rule. They show no clear attempt to sort good vs. bad contributors and then permanently “reward” good contributors via their own contribution level. Contributions appear chosen primarily as a direct response to how profitable cooperation vs. defection looks in the recent environment, not as an attempt to enforce a particular cooperative norm.

This player is a payoff-focused, opportunistic conditional cooperator with substantial free-riding. Contributions span the entire range, from full free-riding to giving the full endowment, and vary frequently rather than stabilizing on a fixed rule. The player clearly understands that contributing can be profitable when others are contributing a lot: they are willing to make high or even maximal contributions in environments where cooperation is strong. At the same time, they repeatedly take advantage of others’ high contributions by contributing nothing and keeping their full endowment, suggesting limited internalization of fairness norms and a comfort with exploiting cooperative surroundings. Their contribution choices do not appear random: they cluster around a pattern of “sometimes join the high contributors, sometimes free-ride on them,” with intermediate contributions used as well. There is no evidence that they systematically match others’ exact average behavior, but they do seem responsive to the general cooperative climate: they are more willing to contribute when many others are giving a lot and still generating high returns, and they free-ride more in periods where they can do so without collapsing contributions altogether. There is no sign of altruistic sacrifice beyond what is individually profitable; when they do contribute heavily, it appears compatible with self-interest under the existing high group contribution level. Overall, this type uses contribution levels instrumentally rather than normatively. They tolerate inequity in their own favor and do not consistently sacrifice payoff to uphold equal effort or fairness. Risk tolerance regarding others’ future cooperation is moderate: they repeatedly free-ride without immediately testing whether this will crash group contributions, implying either a belief that others will keep cooperating or a willingness to ride out volatility while maintaining their own high payoffs.

This player is a moderately cooperative conditional contributor with clear sensitivity to group norms and personal payoff. They contribute frequently and often at or above the group average, but they never commit to unconditional full cooperation. Their contributions oscillate mainly between low–medium and full endowment, suggesting deliberate experimentation rather than random noise. When the environment appears strongly cooperative, they are willing to give the full amount for extended stretches. When apparent free-riding or lower average contributions emerge, they scale back to an intermediate level rather than defecting completely, indicating an internal fairness norm that accepts some personal cost but resists being exploited. They show no evidence of strongly risk-seeking behavior; instead they seem to balance risk and return by staying near what they infer to be a “reasonable” contribution rather than extremes. Overall, they act like a pragmatic cooperator: they want a successful public good, but they also protect their own earnings and adjust to what others do.

The player is a pure, unwavering free-rider. They always keep their entire endowment and never contribute to the public fund in any round. This behavior is completely stable and unaffected by others’ contribution levels, by fluctuations in group cooperation, or by the passage of rounds. They do not exhibit conditional cooperation: they do not increase contributions when others contribute more, nor do they reduce contributions when others lower theirs, because their baseline is already zero. There is no evidence of fairness or reciprocity norms guiding their choices; they accept large benefits from others’ contributions without ever matching or approximating the group average. Their risk tolerance regarding future group collapse is effectively irrelevant in practice, because they never “invest” in cooperation even when group contributions are very high and could potentially be sustained by reciprocal behavior. This pattern suggests a strongly payoff-maximizing orientation under the given incentives, with no observed willingness to sacrifice own coins for efficiency, equity, or group welfare.

This player is a consistently above-average contributor, usually giving more than or close to the group average and often exceeding at least one peer. They rarely contribute the minimum and never free-ride; their contributions range in the upper-middle band rather than at extremes of zero or always-max. They appear motivated by a cooperative norm and aim to keep the public good funded, even when others contribute less. Their behavior suggests conditional cooperation with a generosity bias. When partners are highly cooperative, they maintain or increase contributions, aligning themselves with the cooperative majority. When contributions by others fall, they do not match the lowest contributor; instead, they modestly adjust downward or hold steady, preserving a cooperative stance while not over-exposing themselves. This indicates a preference for fairness that is not strictly egalitarian: they tolerate contributing slightly more than others, but not dramatically more on a sustained basis. They show moderate risk tolerance: they continue to invest in the public good even after experiencing unexplained punishment and witnessing volatility in others’ contributions and sanctioning. They seem to believe that cooperative behavior is still likely to be reciprocated in aggregate, or at least that maintaining cooperation is the best available strategy under uncertainty. Their pattern indicates they value efficiency and group welfare but also track their own payoffs enough to avoid maximal, unconditional contributions. Overall, the contribution profile fits a “steadfast conditional cooperator”: cooperative by default, responsive but not reactive to deviations by others, and willing to shoulder a slightly higher share of the public good to keep the system functioning.

This player is a high, norm-focused contributor who occasionally experiments with low contributions as tests or responses, rather than as a stable free-riding strategy. Their baseline appears to be full or near-full contribution: most of the time they give the entire endowment or a large fraction of it. These high contributions persist even after they are punished and even when others contribute substantially less, which suggests a strong internal cooperation norm rather than simple conditional cooperation. However, they intermittently drop their contribution sharply for brief stretches. These low contributions coincide with phases where others’ contributions are mediocre or declining, or where they might be probing the group’s response. They do not lock into defection; instead, after such experiments they revert to high contributions even when others remain mixed. This pattern indicates exploratory behavior and conditionality, but with a clear bias toward cooperation. They show a fairness orientation centered on relative contributions: they appear to expect that partners who are capable of contributing at least modestly should do so, and they differentiate sharply between clear high contributors, moderate contributors, and very low contributors. Yet they are willing to be more generous than others in contributing, tolerating being slightly “above” the group if they believe others are generally trying. They tolerate strategic risk: they keep contributing high amounts even after being punished themselves and even when some partners remain unreliable. This suggests that they prioritize sustaining a cooperative environment over short-run maximization of their own payoff, and that they are not easily deterred from cooperating by adverse outcomes. Overall, their contribution profile fits a “strong cooperator with exploratory dips”: they default to high cooperation, selectively test lower contributions, but repeatedly return to high contributions despite noise and sanctions.

This player is a highly stable, unconditional full cooperator. They consistently choose the maximal possible contribution in every round, including when others briefly deviate, and there is no evidence they ever even consider free-riding. Their behavior indicates that they understand (or quickly infer) that universal full contribution is payoff-maximizing and adopt it as a fixed strategy rather than adjusting to short-term noise. They do not condition their contributions on being (apparently) punished or on others’ mistakes, nor do they reduce contributions when payoffs temporarily drop due to others’ under-contribution. Fairness-wise, they seem to hold a norm that “everyone doing the max is natural / correct” and are somewhat incredulous about being punished despite full cooperation, which suggests an expectation that contributors deserve protection rather than sanctions. Risk tolerance appears moderate to high in social terms: they are willing to keep fully cooperating even when there is uncertainty about remaining rounds, unexplained punishment, and occasional defectors. They also do not switch to self-protective defection at the end, even after they infer (approximately) the horizon, implying either a principled commitment to cooperation or a belief that end-game defection is not worthwhile given the group’s coordination.

This player is a highly stable, unconditional full cooperator. They always allocate their entire endowment to the public fund and never deviate, even when doing so is clearly unprofitable relative to others’ lower contributions. Their behavior suggests they treat full contribution as a rule or norm rather than as a calculation-sensitive choice. They display strong commitment to group efficiency and fairness norms: they persist in contributing fully when others also do so, and they continue to fully contribute even when one peer intermittently free rides. They do not appear to condition their own contribution on others’ past behavior; their cooperation is insensitive to both good and bad behavior from partners. This points to a “principled cooperator” or “norm-following altruist” type rather than a conditional cooperator. There is no evidence of risk aversion about being exploited in contribution decisions. They consistently accept the risk and reality of exploitation by a lower contributor, maintaining full cooperation. They also show no apparent attempt to hedge or test others with partial or zero contributions, even after repeated deviations by a peer, which indicates low focus on payoff-maximizing adaptation in the contribution stage. Because they cannot see how many rounds remain, their unwavering full contributions also indicate that they do not attempt any endgame defection or strategic tapering. They behave as if the appropriate action is to contribute fully every time, regardless of horizon. Overall, their contribution “type” is: rigid, norm-driven full cooperator, non-conditional on others’ recent behavior, willing to absorb personal disadvantage to maintain cooperative norms.

This player is a near-unconditional full cooperator with rare, isolated deviations. They overwhelmingly choose to contribute their entire endowment, even when some partners do not. Their behavior is highly stable across the game: long stretches of full contributions with only two brief instances of free-riding. These deviations do not trigger a lasting shift toward defection; they quickly revert to contributing fully and then maintain that pattern through the end. Contribution choices show weak conditionality. The player does not reliably match or retaliate against lower contributions from others. They keep contributing fully through repeated instances where at least one peer free-rides and also when the group sometimes drops to low total contributions. This suggests that they either prioritize maximizing group efficiency or place relatively low weight on short-term payoff advantages from defecting. The few defection choices look more like occasional tests or momentary opportunism rather than a coherent strategy of exploitation. After free-riding and seeing that others still contribute, the player returns to full cooperation and does not try to repeatedly capitalize on others’ generosity. They appear comfortable with bearing the cost of contributing in the presence of imperfect cooperators, implying a tolerant fairness norm: they accept some inequity without systematically adjusting their own behavior to enforce strict reciprocity. In terms of fairness, they act as if cooperative norms should prevail and are willing to continue cooperating despite evidence of at least one inconsistent group member. They do not move toward systematic “tit-for-tat” or “grim trigger” responses. This pattern is consistent with a prosocial or efficiency-oriented type who views full contribution as the default “right” or best action and only occasionally deviates. Their risk tolerance appears moderate to high in social terms: they repeatedly risk being the “sucker” by contributing when others might not, and they do so without clear evidence that contributions will always be matched. The repeated return to full cooperation after defection by others suggests they are not driven primarily by fear of exploitation. Instead, they behave as if they expect relatively cooperative behavior over time or as if they value maintaining cooperation regardless of short-term risk. Overall, this player can be characterized as a persistent, norm-driven cooperator with low volatility, mild experimentation with defection, and a forgiving stance toward others’ imperfect cooperation.

This player is a predominantly high, near-unconditional cooperator with brief exploratory deviations. After a very short initial phase of testing both defection and cooperation, they settle into consistently contributing their full endowment and maintain that pattern even when it is locally exploited. They clearly recognize that full contribution yields a higher group return than defection when others cooperate, and they are willing to adopt and stick with that cooperative strategy despite experiencing periods where they personally earn less than they could by free-riding. Once they observe that high cooperation in the group is possible, they show strong inertia: they do not drift downward over time and do not visibly react to noise or temporary exploitation by lowering their own contributions. Their contribution pattern is stable rather than volatile. Early on, they briefly alternate between not contributing and contributing, which appears to be instrumental probing: they directly compare the payoffs of free-riding versus full cooperation under different group behaviors. After that initial exploration phase, they treat full contribution as the default and rarely, if ever, reconsider it, even when others intermittently defect or fully free-ride. This indicates a low sensitivity to short-term unfairness and a high tolerance for payoff disadvantage in the service of maintaining the cooperative norm. Conditioning on others’ behavior is present but weak. They do not behave as a pure unconditional cooperator because they do test defection early, and they sometimes free-ride when they see unanimous cooperation from others. However, once they decide that a cooperative equilibrium is attainable, they stop using contingent downshifts (e.g., retaliatory defection) and instead keep contributing even when another player persistently defects or when all others defect in a single instance. Their conditionality is thus asymmetric: they are open to being influenced toward cooperation by others’ good behavior but are not easily pushed toward defection by others’ bad behavior. Fairness norms appear present but non-enforcing. The player repeatedly accepts being the only contributor or one of a minority of contributors. They do not attempt to discipline defectors through reciprocal defection, even when they are clearly aware that others contribute less. This suggests a cooperative fairness ideal (everyone contributing) combined with a reluctance to punish or withdraw cooperation in response to unfairness. They prioritize sustaining public-good efficiency over achieving strictly equal, exploitation-free payoffs. Risk tolerance is moderate to high in social terms. The player willingly risks being exploited by contributing fully when there is uncertainty about others’ future behavior. They also tolerate payoff variance: they continue to choose the cooperative action even after experiencing very low payoffs when others free-ride. However, their initial phase of experimenting with free-riding shows that they are not naïve; they do understand and briefly take the individually risk-dominant option before committing to the socially efficient one. Overall, they behave as a principled cooperator who has done some payoff-based learning and then sticks with a high-cooperation strategy, even in the presence of inconsistent partners.

This player is a strategically flexible, outcome-oriented contributor who oscillates between full cooperation and complete free-riding, with intermittent medium contributions. They clearly understand that high contributions generate large payoffs and often choose to contribute very high amounts or the maximum. They also show no internalized norm of always matching or exceeding the group average: they are willing to drop to zero contributions even when others remain highly cooperative. Their pattern suggests conditional cooperation in a loose, self-serving sense: they cooperate heavily when it aligns with high expected returns and when they appear engaged, but they have no hesitation to exploit a cooperative environment by free-riding once they realize others continue to contribute regardless. The volatility in their contributions indicates low commitment to stable norms and high responsiveness to situational incentives rather than to principles of fairness. They do not appear to punish others, so their “conditionality” is purely via own contributions, not norm enforcement. Risk tolerance seems moderate to high: they frequently put in all or almost all of their endowment, suggesting comfort with trusting the group when they expect the multiplier and others’ behavior to make this profitable, but they are also comfortable flipping to zero when they anticipate they can ride on others’ efforts.

The player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator. They always contribute the entire endowment to the public fund, without deviation and without visible sensitivity to others’ contribution levels. This holds even when some group members contribute nothing, even after they themselves are punished, and even toward the end of the game when incentives to free-ride are strongest. They appear to treat full contribution as a fixed norm or principle rather than as a contingent strategy based on others’ behavior or on remaining rounds. Their approach suggests low concern for short-term individual payoff maximization and high valuation of group efficiency and norm consistency. Risk tolerance is high: they continue to invest fully in the public good despite uncertainty about others’ actions and despite being personally targeted with punishment.

This player is a stable, high contributor who consistently gives very close to the full endowment in every round. They start off highly cooperative and maintain that stance throughout the game, with only small, bounded variation rather than large swings. They appear to treat near-full contribution as the default norm rather than something contingent on others’ short-term behavior. Even when several group members free‑ride or strongly under-contribute, they do not visibly reduce their own contributions in response. Their behavior suggests either a strong internal fairness norm favoring high cooperation or a deliberate earnings-maximizing strategy that recognizes that, given the group’s generally high contributions and the marginal per capita return, it is profitable to keep contributing heavily. They show no evidence of experimenting with low contributions to test others, indicating low tolerance for opportunistic payoff-seeking at the expense of the public fund. Risk tolerance with respect to others’ potential defection appears moderate to high: they continue to invest heavily even when there are clear defectors, suggesting either confidence that others will sustain cooperation or a preference for maintaining their own standard regardless of others’ actions.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They always give essentially their full endowment to the public fund and never test lower contributions, even when some others free‑ride or partially defect. They appear to hold a strong internal norm that “full contribution is the right play,” rather than conditioning on others’ behavior in a fine-grained or reactive way. There is no visible risk‑sensitive adjustment: they keep contributing at the maximum even when it is clear that some players repeatedly contribute very little. They tolerate inequity in the short run and do not try to protect themselves by lowering contributions. Their behavior is consistent with a principled cooperator who values maintaining a high public good more than optimizing narrow payoff in response to free-riding.

This player is a flexible, payoff-oriented conditional cooperator who oscillates between full free-riding and substantial contributions. They do not lock into a single strategy: they sometimes keep their entire endowment, sometimes give almost everything, and often choose intermediate amounts. Their behavior suggests they opportunistically balance two goals: exploiting very high cooperation when their own contribution is pivotal for public returns, and occasionally free-riding when others’ high contributions appear stable enough that their own withholding will not collapse the group outcome. They clearly respond to the group’s contribution level, increasing their own giving when they see most others contributing high amounts, and easing back to zero or low amounts at times when they can still earn high redistributed payoffs from others’ efforts. They tolerate the risk of being punished for free-riding—accepting deductions without changing to a pure “always contribute” stance—indicating relatively high payoff focus and moderate risk tolerance about social sanctions. Their pattern over time reflects learning: early experimentation with extremes, followed by more mid-to-high contributions once the cooperative norm proves robust, with selective reversion to zero when they anticipate little future consequence or wish to test boundaries.

This player is a payoff-focused, flexible contributor who mixes full cooperation with deliberate free-riding and moderate, “middle” contributions. They clearly understand that contributing nothing yields high short-run payoffs when others give, and they are willing to exploit that, but they also repeatedly choose to contribute the maximum, even when others’ average contributions are modest. Overall giving fluctuates substantially rather than following a simple upward or downward trend. They show no fixed contribution anchor: they move between zero, small amounts, mid-range amounts, and the maximum across the game. This indicates high behavioral volatility rather than a stable norm or rule like “always contribute half” or “always contribute zero.” Their choices appear responsive to the perceived level of group cooperation and possibly to their own recent payoffs, not to a strict internal fairness standard. They do not behave as a pure free-rider: they frequently contribute the full endowment, even when others’ average contributions are clearly below that level. This suggests that they accept being more generous than many peers at times, possibly because they believe high group cooperation will be rewarded in aggregate. However, they are also willing to swing back to zero when it is profitable, implying that they do not have a firm commitment to unconditional cooperation. They occasionally choose intermediate contributions (neither zero nor maximum). These mid-level amounts cluster in a modest range rather than smoothly tracking others’ exact average. That pattern is consistent with a rough, heuristic adjustment (“give something, but not too much”) rather than precise conditional cooperation keyed tightly to others’ mean behavior. Fairness concerns are present but secondary: the player sometimes sacrifices own payoff by contributing fully when others are not all doing so, which is consistent with a desire to keep the group effort going or to be “doing their part.” Yet they also repeatedly free-ride when others are contributing a lot, accepting the unfair benefit to themselves. On balance, they appear comfortable with inequality when it benefits them, and only intermittently willing to rectify it by contributing more. Risk tolerance is moderate to high in the social sense: contributing fully when many others cooperate exposes them to the risk that the group will later drop contributions and that they will have “overpaid” relative to others. They take this risk repeatedly. At the same time, they are not locked into a cooperative stance and are quick to protect themselves by reverting to low or zero contributions if they sense that high giving is not consistently in their interest.

This player is a moderately prosocial, payoff-sensitive conditional cooperator with conservative risk-taking and no strong fairness enforcement. They rarely choose extreme contribution levels. Their typical choice lies in the lower-to-middle range of the action space, with only a few excursions to very low or very high amounts. This indicates a general tendency to contribute something to the group rather than fully free-riding, but also a reluctance to sacrifice too much private payoff. Their behavior is somewhat volatile early on, with noticeable shifts between low, medium, and very high contributions. Over time, contributions stabilize into a narrower band around a modest, repeatable level. This pattern suggests ongoing learning and calibration rather than a fixed rule from the outset. They clearly condition their behavior on the observed cooperativeness of the group. When others appear to contribute more, they are willing to increase their own contributions. When the environment looks less cooperative or when high contributions do not translate into especially high payoffs, they pull back. However, their adjustments are incremental rather than drastic, which points to a cautious and measured response style. They do not appear to follow a strict fairness or reciprocity rule (such as matching the group average or strongly punishing low contributors by sharply cutting their own contributions). Instead, they seem to balance “doing their part” with maintaining a comfortable individual payoff. This balance implies a weak fairness norm: they care about contributing above pure self-interest, but they do not push for equal burden-sharing or maximal group efficiency at personal cost. Their risk tolerance is moderate. They are willing to occasionally make high contributions, which are risky in this environment given the incentives to free-ride and the absence of punishment or reward tools. However, these high contributions are rare and followed by more self-protective behavior. They seem to experiment with generosity, observe the outcome, and then retreat toward safer, mid-range contributions when the payoff or group response does not justify sustained high giving. Overall, this player is best characterized as a cautious conditional cooperator who aims for a compromise between benefiting the group and safeguarding personal earnings, updating their contributions based on the emerging cooperative climate but without strong moralistic or enforcement-driven motives.

They are a low, mostly free-riding contributor who occasionally “tests” small contributions but never moves toward genuinely cooperative levels. Their typical choice is at or near zero, with infrequent small amounts that stay well below the group’s prevailing average. Contributions fluctuate within a narrow low range rather than trending upward or downward over time, suggesting a stable preference for keeping most of the endowment. They appear conditionally responsive in a limited, asymmetric way: they are willing to contribute a bit more when others are clearly contributing a lot, but they do not attempt to match or approach group norms. Instead, they treat others’ cooperation as an opportunity to extract higher payoffs while making only token contributions. This indicates a self-interested strategy that is aware of the public good mechanism but prioritizes individual gain over fairness or efficiency. Their behavior is consistent with an understanding that the per-coin private return from contributing is lower than from keeping coins, and they act in line with the dominant-strategy logic of free-riding, though not in its extreme form (pure zero every round). The occasional small positive contributions suggest either a minimal fairness concern, a desire not to appear maximally exploitative, or simple experimentation with how contributions affect payoffs; however, these impulses are weak and never override the baseline tendency to under-contribute. They do not exhibit risk-seeking attempts to coordinate on high-contribution equilibria; they avoid large jumps in contribution and never commit a high amount even when others repeatedly show strong cooperation. This points to low willingness to bear personal risk for potential collective gain and a relatively low tolerance for depending on others’ reciprocity. Overall, they are best characterized as a calculated, payoff-focused low contributor who lightly conditions on group behavior but does not internalize strong fairness or reciprocity norms.

This player is a moderate, payoff-sensitive conditional cooperator with some experimentation and no strong commitment to either full cooperation or full free-riding. They typically contribute a mid-range amount relative to the endowment rather than extreme values. Their contributions cluster around a moderate level, with occasional low (including zero) and high (full) contributions. This indicates neither a pure altruist nor a pure defector profile, but someone balancing self-interest with group-benefit considerations. Their pattern stabilizes over time into a relatively narrow band of medium contributions, after an initial phase with more variance. Early on, they explore different contribution levels, including very low and very high choices. As the game proceeds, they reduce volatility and largely avoid extremes. This suggests learning and calibration rather than random play. They appear conditionally cooperative in the sense that they generally keep contributing even when others’ behavior is mixed, but they do not systematically match the highest cooperators. They occasionally respond to higher group contributions with an increase of their own, but their level rarely exceeds or even equals the group’s more generous members, pointing to a “participate but don’t lead” cooperative stance. They do not show a strict fairness norm such as equalizing their contribution to the group average or to specific partners. Their contributions remain below the most generous players and above the least generous, placing them in a “middle contributor” role. There is no clear evidence that they are attempting to punish free-riders through under-contribution or to reward high contributors through over-contribution; when they shift up or down, changes are modest and not tightly synchronized with visible extremes in others’ choices. The player appears payoff-aware: when lower contributions coincide with especially high personal payoffs and higher contributions coincide with weaker personal payoffs, they do not lock into either extreme; instead, they gravitate to moderate contributions that still secure strong payoffs. This suggests they notice the incentive structure and aim to capture benefits from others’ contributions without completely undermining the public good. Risk tolerance appears moderate. They are willing to test both very low and very high contributions at least once, accepting short-term uncertainty, but they quickly settle into safer, mid-range behavior. There is no repeated gambling with all-or-nothing contributions; instead, they favor predictable, incremental payoffs. Overall, this player behaves as a self-interested yet non-exploitative contributor: they regularly contribute nontrivially, benefit from the public good, and adapt their level over time toward a comfortable compromise between cooperation and personal earnings.
