They appear conditionally cooperative in a loose, aggregate sense: when the surrounding environment looks broadly cooperative, they usually continue to contribute, and brief deviations to zero do not lead to a sustained collapse of their own cooperation. However, they do not seem to condition finely on each individual peer’s past behavior; instead, they treat the group as a whole and maintain high contributions even when several specific others repeatedly contribute nothing. Fairness norms seem present but weakly enforced: the player tolerates the presence of persistent free‑riders without systematically withdrawing their own cooperation or engaging in tit‑for‑tat patterns. Risk tolerance is moderate: they are willing to incur substantial contribution and, at times, nontrivial reward costs even when returns are uncertain, but they also occasionally protect their private payoff by defecting when the group appears able to sustain high total contributions without them.

This player is a “principled cooperator with occasional opportunism.” They treat full contribution as the default, repeatedly choosing the cooperative option despite mixed group behavior and without visible decay over time. Across most of the game they maintain a high, stable contribution level, suggesting they accept the social-efficiency logic of contributing even when some others free-ride. They show one clear deviation into free-riding early on, during a phase where others are contributing strongly, which indicates they are not a pure altruist; they are willing to exploit a cooperative environment at least once to test or benefit from it. However, they do not stick with this exploitative pattern, implying that free-riding is not their dominant strategy. Their behavior appears only weakly conditional on short-term fluctuations in others’ contributions. They continue to cooperate even when a noticeable minority of players persistently contribute nothing. This points to relatively high tolerance for free-riding and a willingness to keep investing in the public good without requiring strict fairness. At the same time, they seem aware of contribution norms: they do not increase cooperation in response to others, but they avoid sustained deviation from the cooperative pattern themselves, which fits an internalized “I should do my part” norm more than a reactive tit-for-tat style. Risk-wise, they accept the risk of being exploited by low contributors repeatedly. There is no evidence of them trying to hedge by withdrawing contributions when cooperation falls. This suggests either a payoff-insensitive commitment to cooperation or a belief that long-run or group-level gains justify continued full contribution. Their one early defection indicates some payoff sensitivity, but their persistence in full contributions even afterward suggests that norm adherence or self-concept as “a cooperator” carries more weight than immediate material incentives.

This player is a stable full cooperator. They always contribute the maximum available amount and never experiment with free-riding, even after repeated exposure to others contributing nothing and even when reward patterns and chat make clear that some players profit more by withholding. Their cooperation is not visibly contingent on short-term individual payoff; instead, it aligns with a group-efficiency norm and a desire to return to “high group earning” situations. They explicitly reference the higher payoffs when everyone cooperates, indicating that they understand the efficiency benefits and care about them. There is no evidence of risk-averse hedging or retaliatory defection; they tolerate others’ partial or zero contributions without reducing their own, suggesting a principled or norm-based cooperator type rather than a conditional cooperator.

The player is a high but inconsistent contributor who oscillates between full cooperation and full free-riding. They reliably choose the extremes available rather than splitting the difference, suggesting a preference for clear-cut strategies over fine-tuning. Contribution behavior is conditionally cooperative but also opportunistic: they sometimes contribute fully when many others do, yet at other times they free-ride despite strong group contributions. They do not appear to punish low contributors via their own contributions; they continue to alternate between giving and taking even when non-contributors are salient. Fairness norms are unclear: they do not voice concerns about fairness, and their mixed pattern suggests they tolerate benefiting from others’ cooperation without explicit justification. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to contribute fully in environments that look cooperative, but also comfortable taking the short-term safe payoff via free-riding. Overall, they fit a “volatile conditional cooperator / opportunistic free-rider” profile rather than a stable cooperator or strict defector.

This player is a predominantly free-riding, payoff-focused type with intermittent, low-commitment cooperation. They consistently face a setting where others almost always contribute fully, yet they mostly keep their own endowment. Their baseline is to contribute nothing and benefit from others’ contributions. Occasional full contributions appear, but they do not escalate into a sustained cooperative pattern; instead, they alternate between free-riding and full contribution without clear upward or downward trend. Their behavior suggests they treat contribution as a tactical choice rather than a moral obligation. They sometimes match cooperative peers, but they do not adopt a stable “always cooperate” norm even when others remain reliably generous. The alternation hints at experimentation or mild conditional cooperation: they are willing to contribute fully when cooperation is high, but they revert to zero contributions frequently, even when others stay cooperative and even when free-riding attracts substantial punishment. They do not appear driven by strong fairness norms. They repeatedly take advantage of partners who contribute more than they do. Even when they are the unique free-rider against cooperative others, they continue to exploit the situation. They appear relatively risk-tolerant regarding social sanctions: they keep free-riding despite accumulating costly punishment in multiple instances. Overall, they prioritize individual payoff over group efficiency or equality, though occasional contributions indicate some responsiveness to cooperative cues or to variation in expected payoff.

This player is a predominantly high contributor who occasionally free-rides in a calculated, pattern-based way. They usually match or exceed the apparent cooperative standard rather than undercutting it. When others both contribute, the player almost always contributes as well, indicating a preference for mutual cooperation and an expectation that full contributions are a reasonable norm. Their deviations from contributing appear deliberate rather than random. They sometimes withhold their contribution even when others are fully cooperating, suggesting a willingness to opportunistically exploit a cooperative environment after it is established, especially once trust and high contributions are stable. They also occasionally withhold when one peer is persistently not contributing, which can reflect frustration, experimentation, or an attempt to test how robust others’ cooperation is. Contribution decisions are clearly conditional on peers’ past behavior. The player seems to track who consistently contributes and who does not, and bases both their own contributions and subsequent social sanctions on those histories. They tolerate some asymmetry (continuing to contribute when one peer is free-riding) but not indefinitely: their later behavior shows more resolve against chronic non-contributors. Fairness norms are complex rather than purely egalitarian. The player appears to think in terms of “roles”: a reliable cooperator deserves support and positive treatment even if another group member is slacking, and a chronic free-rider deserves to be sanctioned even if aggregate earnings suffer. They show moderate risk tolerance: they are willing to sacrifice guaranteed personal payoff through both contribution and costly sanctions to push the group toward their preferred pattern of behavior. Overall, they fit a “conditional cooperator with strategic opportunism”: they prefer high, stable cooperation, but once such cooperation is in place they sometimes exploit it by not contributing, and then revert to cooperation again to maintain the social structure and their moral authority to punish or reward.

This player is a perfectly stable, unconditional full contributor. They always give the entire endowment to the public fund, with no variation across the game. They do not adjust their choice in response to free riding by some peers, to fluctuations in the group’s average contribution, or to changes in active group size. This indicates a contribution rule that is effectively fixed rather than reactive. Their behavior is consistent with a strong efficiency norm: they act as if maximizing the group return is the correct choice regardless of others’ actions. Given the game’s parameters, they forgo guaranteed higher individual payoffs from free riding, so they are not playing the strictly payoff-maximizing best response. Instead, they appear to prioritize either (a) social-welfare maximization, (b) a rigid fairness/“do my part” rule, or (c) a simple non-strategic heuristic such as “always contribute everything.” Which of these is primary is unknown, but their unwavering pattern suggests a strong internal rule rather than ongoing cost–benefit recalculation. They show no evidence of conditional cooperation (no increase when others are generous, no decrease when others shirk). They also show no attempt to discipline low contributors via reduced giving, even when free riding is persistent and identifiable at the individual level. This suggests either low sensitivity to perceived unfairness, a deliberate refusal to retaliate, or a focus on personal consistency over reciprocity. Risk tolerance appears high with respect to exploitation: they repeatedly expose themselves to being taken advantage of and do not hedge by retaining any coins privately. They seem either unconcerned about the possibility of being the “sucker,” or they place overriding value on adhering to their contribution rule. Learning or strategic updating is minimal to nonexistent: once they adopt full contribution, they maintain it, ignoring feedback that would normally push a self-interested or conditionally cooperative player toward partial or zero contributions.

This player is a mixed-strategy, payoff-sensitive conditional cooperator. They are clearly willing to contribute fully at times but do not adopt a simple “always contribute” or “always free ride” rule. Instead, their behavior shifts between full contribution and zero contribution in a way that appears to track both others’ behavior and punishment/reward signals. They show that they are not purely self-interested: they are willing to give up their entire endowment to the group on multiple occasions, including when cooperation appears relatively strong. At other times they fully free ride while others contribute, indicating they do not treat free riding as categorically wrong and are willing to exploit high-contribution environments when it seems advantageous. There is some instability/experimentation early on: they switch from full contribution to zero, then back to full. This suggests they are testing how much cooperation they can sustain while minimizing personal cost, rather than following a rigid norm. Their pattern is consistent with someone who values higher total group contributions but also keeps an eye on personal earnings and is willing to deviate if they perceive the environment as either tolerant of free riding or already highly cooperative without their help. They do not exhibit a strict fairness norm such as “I always match what others do” or “I never earn more than strong contributors.” They tolerate situations where they benefit more than high contributors without self-correcting through extra contributions or rewards. This indicates a relatively high tolerance for advantageous inequality, though not an aversion to cooperation itself. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to contribute fully even when some others are defecting, which carries the risk of being exploited, but they also at times avoid that risk by free riding themselves. Overall, they balance cooperative impulses with opportunistic self-protection.

This player is a late-converting conditional cooperator with an initial free‑riding bias. When contributing is framed as an all‑or‑nothing choice, they start by consistently taking the selfish option even in a cooperative environment, indicating an initial priority on individual payoff over group welfare. They update their behavior after accumulating social feedback via rewards and punishments, eventually switching to full contribution and then maintaining it to the end. Their shift suggests they are responsive to norms: once it becomes clear that high contribution is the prevailing pattern and is socially reinforced, they align with that pattern. The change is abrupt rather than gradual, implying a strategic “test, then conform” style rather than continual fine‑tuning. They tolerate some personal disadvantage (being punished while not contributing, and later contributing despite lingering low contributors) once they perceive a stable cooperative norm backed by incentives. Their risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to experiment with non‑cooperation early, but once they sense that the environment rewards cooperation, they avoid further deviation.

This player is a highly stable, moderate contributor with a strong internal anchor. They fixate on a single contribution level that sits below typical high cooperators but far above free-riders, and they maintain it with striking consistency across almost the entire game. Their contribution barely reacts to fluctuations in others’ behavior, including periods when many peers contribute very little and periods when peers contribute a lot. This suggests they follow a simple personal rule (e.g., “always put in a certain share”) rather than finely adjusting to the group. They appear to care about contributing something meaningful but not maximizing social efficiency. They rarely match the highest contributors and are also not the lowest; they sit in the middle, which indicates a moderate fairness norm: they are not comfortable free-riding, but they also do not feel obliged to fully match the most generous players. Volatility is extremely low. The one clear upward deviation to a higher contribution late in the game is isolated and not sustained, implying either a brief test, a momentary experiment, or a response to a particularly cooperative group state, but not a full strategy shift. They show no evidence of risk-seeking experiments like extremes of 0 or full contribution as a repeated pattern. Overall, their risk tolerance in contributions is low: they prefer a predictable, rule-based input to the public good. Conditioning on others appears weak at best. When others drop their contributions sharply, this player does not retaliate by reducing their own to very low levels, and when others contribute very highly, they do not significantly increase their own beyond their usual anchor. If any conditionality exists, it is faint and overlaid on a dominant “fixed-rule” tendency. They behave more like a rule-following, mildly prosocial type than a reactive conditional cooperator.

This player is a generally prosocial but strategic conditional cooperator with moderate volatility. They are clearly willing to contribute fully and repeatedly when cooperation looks viable and when others’ contributions are moderate to high. They also sometimes contribute only part of their endowment and occasionally free‑ride completely, especially when they appear to test boundaries or when they can exploit a cooperative environment late in the game. Their contributions cluster around three modes: full contribution, partial contribution, and rare zero contribution. They do not show a simple monotonic decay over time; instead, they alternate between high and medium contributions with occasional low or zero ones. This suggests they are not purely myopic or purely altruistic, but are experimenting and reacting to perceived patterns in others’ behavior. They seem to adopt a fairness norm that expects at least moderate contributions from everyone. They often maintain or return to high contributions even after observing free‑riding by some group members, which implies they value sustaining group payoff and may believe punishment can stabilize cooperation rather than needing to personally defect whenever others do. At the same time, they are not rigidly principled: they sometimes under‑contribute when others are contributing well, indicating some willingness to take advantage of the group when it seems low‑risk. Their risk tolerance appears moderate. They are willing to “invest” heavily in the public good many times, trusting that the multiplier makes cooperation worthwhile. But they also occasionally hedge by keeping more for themselves, especially after instability or norm violations. Overall, they behave like a pragmatic cooperator who is sensitive to norms but not self‑sacrificing at all costs.

This player is a moderately strong, though not unconditional, cooperator. They typically contribute a high share of their endowment, most often at a level clearly above the group average. They sometimes move to the maximum possible contribution, and sometimes pull back to a mid-range contribution, but they never free‑ride or contribute very little. Their behavior suggests a preference for substantial cooperation combined with some self-protection. Their contributions are fairly stable across the game: they stay within a relatively narrow band of medium-to-high contributions, with only modest fluctuations. These adjustments loosely track shifts in others’ behavior: when peers appear reliably generous, the player is comfortable being very generous; when peer contributions drop or become mixed, the player moderates but does not abandon cooperation. This indicates conditional cooperation, but anchored at a relatively generous baseline rather than tightly matching others. They appear to hold a fairness norm in which everyone is expected to contribute at least a moderate amount, and they personally are willing to exceed that norm. They continue to contribute well even when some peers repeatedly give nothing, suggesting a tolerance for some inequality and a reluctance to fully punish defectors by withdrawing their own contributions. At the same time, they do not consistently go all-in at the maximum, which suggests some awareness of exploitation risk and a desire to maintain a reasonable personal payoff. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they repeatedly invest substantially in the public good under uncertainty about individual payoffs, implying comfort with cooperative risk. Yet they also avoid the most extreme cooperative stance in many instances (not always contributing the maximum), indicating some caution.

This player is a conditional, strategically flexible cooperator who alternates between full contribution and full free-riding, with clear responsiveness to others’ behavior and to payoff consequences. They strongly prefer coordinated, high-contribution profiles when they believe others are also contributing, and they are willing to carry their share (and at times more than their proportional share) of cooperation. They repeatedly choose the cooperative action even after experiencing low personal payoffs from being more generous than at least one other group member, indicating a relatively high tolerance for short-run disadvantage and some commitment to group-beneficial outcomes. Their contribution pattern shows both stability and targeted volatility: - Over the game, they use both extremes of the action space (all or nothing), never intermediate amounts (which are not available here), and they use both options multiple times. This suggests they think in discrete strategic categories: “cooperate fully” vs “defect fully,” rather than trying to finely tune contributions. - For extended stretches, they maintain full contributions despite mixed or imperfect cooperation from others, suggesting a baseline inclination toward cooperation and a willingness to test whether cooperative norms can be sustained. - They occasionally switch to full free-riding in contexts where others are contributing well or resume contributing after a collective breakdown, indicating they are attentive to payoff structure and group dynamics rather than being purely rule-following or purely selfish. Their behavior is clearly conditional: they respond to the composition of others’ contributions and to recent patterns rather than following a fixed rule. When others are consistently cooperative, they are generally cooperative too, but they also occasionally exploit those cooperative phases by free-riding. After very low personal returns from being the only or dominant contributor, they temporarily withhold contributions, but this withholding is not permanent; they later return to full cooperation when others again appear willing to contribute. From a fairness and norm perspective, they appear to value mutual cooperation and seem comfortable with outcomes where everyone benefits similarly, even if they themselves sometimes earn less than they could by defecting. However, they do not behave as a strict fairness enforcer or unconditional altruist: they are willing to capitalize on cooperative others by defecting when it is clearly profitable, and they do not refuse such gains. Their fairness norm is thus flexible and context-sensitive rather than rigid. Risk-wise, they accept the risk of cooperating in uncertain environments and tolerate being exposed to exploitation for multiple periods. At the same time, their selective free-riding shows that, when evidence accumulates that cooperation is fragile or exploitable, they are willing to reduce that risk and protect their own payoff. Overall, they act as a moderately risk-tolerant, payoff-aware conditional cooperator who balances social and self-interested motives, rather than as an extreme altruist or a purely outcome-maximizing defector.

This player is a strategically alternating free-rider who mixes cooperation with frequent defection to maximize personal payoff. They regularly exploit the group by withholding contributions while most others give, yet they occasionally contribute fully. Their decisions are binary and shift repeatedly rather than following a simple “always contribute” or “always defect” rule, suggesting deliberate experimentation or conditionality rather than randomness. They show a clear comfort with free-riding even when others contribute at high levels, indicating low guilt or fairness aversion toward inequality in their favor. At the same time, they are willing to contribute when others’ cooperation is already strong, which implies they either (a) see some value in sustaining high group contributions over time, or (b) accept short-run personal losses to preserve an environment that is generally profitable for them. Their pattern does not track short-term retaliation risks (there is no punishment) but appears aligned with payoff optimization: when others keep contributing, defecting remains very lucrative, and they take advantage of that many times. Contributions intersperse with defections across the entire game rather than clustering only at the start or end, consistent with an opportunistic mixed strategy rather than a simple “start nice, then exploit” or “end-game only” heuristic. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they repeatedly rely on others’ continued cooperation instead of hedging by contributing more steadily. In normative terms, they do not behave like a strong fairness-oriented cooperator; instead, they resemble a payoff-focused opportunist who is comfortable being richer than others as long as the group keeps the public good high. Their occasional cooperation may reflect either weak pro-social preferences or instrumental signaling/maintenance of the cooperative atmosphere.

The player is a perfectly stable, unconditional full cooperator within the action space available. Whenever a choice is offered between keeping the full endowment or contributing it all, the player always chooses to contribute everything to the public pool and never deviates or experiments with free-riding. This pattern holds irrespective of changes in group behavior or fluctuations in others’ contributions. The player does not condition on the current average contribution level, on the presence of clear free-riders, or on the presence of almost-universal cooperation. They maintain full contribution even when it would be strictly better (in a narrow monetary sense) to keep their endowment, given others’ behavior. The player therefore appears to follow a strong cooperation norm or rule such as “always contribute fully,” rather than a responsive or contingent rule like “match others” or “reward cooperators and punish defectors through my own contribution choices.” There is no evidence of risk-hedging or strategic adjustment; they do not probe the environment by changing their action. They behave as if maximizing collective efficiency takes priority over individual payoff optimization. They accept being exploited by non-contributors rather than reducing their own contribution. This suggests either (a) a strong pro-social or efficiency-oriented preference, (b) a fairness norm defined more as “I do my part” than as “I must equalize payoffs,” or (c) a rule-following style where the player treats contribution as the “correct” action independent of context. Which of these dominates is unknown; confidence is only that they are unwaveringly cooperative in their own actions.

This player is a strict, unconditional full cooperator in this environment. They always contribute the entire endowment and never deviate, regardless of how others behave or how profitable defection is in any given situation. Their behavior is extremely stable across the entire game, with no experimentation, no partial shifts, and no apparent sensitivity to fluctuations in others’ average contributions. They appear to prioritize maximizing the group return over their own relative payoff. Even when some group members visibly earn more by not contributing, this does not trigger any retaliatory free-riding or attempts to protect themselves from exploitation. This suggests a payoff motive that is either (a) group-efficiency oriented, (b) rule/identity driven (“I am a cooperator”), or (c) focused on long-run collective outcomes rather than short-run advantage. Exactly which of these dominates is unknown, but their choices are consistent with a strong internalized norm of full cooperation. Risk-wise, they repeatedly choose the option that is collectively efficient but personally vulnerable to exploitation, and they persist in this even when others occasionally defect. This indicates high tolerance for being exploited, or low weight on that concern, compared to the value they place on contributing. There is no behavioral sign that they are using a conditional cooperation rule (e.g., matching others’ average behavior); their contributions do not track the ups and downs in group contributions. In a new PGG, this type tends to: - Contribute fully by default from the start. - Maintain full contributions even in the presence of moderate or recurring free-riding. - Avoid strategic toggling; they prefer a simple, consistent “always contribute” rule. They are best described as a “steadfast altruistic cooperator” rather than a reciprocal or strategic one.

This player is a one-shot full cooperator in a large group public goods setting. Faced with a single decision and no future interaction, they allocate their entire endowment to the group account. This indicates a strong prosocial or efficiency-oriented preference that outweighs narrow self-interest, even when there is no opportunity for reciprocity, reputation building, or conditional cooperation across rounds. They do not exhibit strategic hedging or partial contributions; instead, they adopt an all-in cooperative stance. This suggests a relatively high tolerance for payoff risk in favor of maximizing group benefit, or a norm-driven commitment to contributing “as much as possible.” Because only one decision is observed, stability across time, responsiveness to others’ past behavior, and nuanced conditionality (e.g., matching, threshold strategies) remain unknown. Their fairness norm is ambiguous: they contribute at the cooperative extreme, but there is no evidence they evaluate or react to the disparity between their own action and others’ more moderate or low contributions. Overall, they fit a type best described as an unconditional or idealistic cooperator in a one-shot context, with high willingness to sacrifice personal payoff for collective gain.

The player exhibits a moderate, neither-minimal-nor-maximal contribution pattern in a one-shot setting. They do not free-ride, but they also do not match the most cooperative individuals. This suggests a mixed motive: they are willing to support the group but appear to balance that against personal payoff considerations. With only one decision observed, stability and volatility across rounds are unknown. Their choice is consistent with a “compromising cooperator” type rather than a pure altruist or pure egoist. They do not appear strongly inequality-averse in either direction based on this single action; sensitivity to fairness norms is unknown. Conditionality (e.g., whether they match or reciprocate others) is also unknown because they act without observing prior contribution behavior. Their willingness to contribute a non-trivial amount in a one-shot environment with no punishment or reward options indicates at least moderate baseline trust or internalized cooperative norm, but their restraint from a full contribution hints at some risk sensitivity or self-protection. Overall risk tolerance, beyond this cautious-but-cooperative level, is unknown.

The player is a full cooperator in this setting, choosing the maximum possible contribution from their endowment. This indicates a strong cooperative orientation relative to the group average, with no evidence of free-riding or hedging. With only a single decision observed, stability over time and volatility across rounds are unknown. Their behavior is not conditional on prior observed actions, as there is no history yet; instead, they act in an unconditionally cooperative way. This suggests either a principled or norm-driven approach to public-good provision rather than a reactive or opportunistic one, but the underlying motivation (fairness, altruism, efficiency, or confusion) remains unknown. They tolerate substantial strategic risk: given the game structure and group size, full contribution exposes them to exploitation by low contributors, yet they proceed anyway. This implies relatively high risk tolerance toward others’ potential non-cooperation, or low salience of strategic risk in their decision-making. Their fairness norm regarding equality of contributions is unclear; they neither comment on others’ choices nor adjust behavior (no subsequent round) in response, so it is unknown whether they prefer strict equality, efficiency, or some other norm. They do appear comfortable being among the highest contributors even when others contribute less, suggesting they do not require immediate reciprocity at this stage.

This player is a one-shot full cooperator. They allocate their entire endowment to the public pool despite the game being a single round with no future interaction, which indicates a strong cooperative or pro-social orientation rather than strategic self-interest. The behavior suggests a willingness to accept personal risk for the group’s benefit, implying relatively high risk tolerance in social dilemmas. There is no evidence that they condition their behavior on others’ expected contributions; they choose the most cooperative action without attempting to gauge or hedge against potential free-riding. Fairness norms are not explicitly expressed, but contributing the maximum available amount aligns with a preference for collectively efficient outcomes over individual profit maximization. Stability or volatility of contributions across rounds is unknown because only one round is played, and conditional cooperation patterns cannot be inferred.

This player is a moderately high contributor in a one-shot public goods setting, clearly above a purely self-interested free-riding level but below the maximum. With only a single round, their willingness to contribute substantially despite no future interaction suggests they place some intrinsic or norm-based value on cooperation rather than acting purely on narrow payoff maximization. Their choice is consistent with a desire to be prosocial while still retaining some private benefit; they neither exploit others nor fully sacrifice their own payoff. There is no evidence of conditional or reactive contribution behavior, because there are no prior rounds or visible pre-contribution commitments to condition on. Fairness norms and inequality concerns are unknown beyond the fact that they are comfortable with contributing more than a purely self-regarding player would. Risk tolerance is moderately high in social terms: they accept the possibility that others may contribute less and that they could earn less than a strict defector, yet they still contribute significantly. Any more nuanced strategy across rounds, such as tit-for-tat or thresholds, is unknown due to the single-round structure.

The player is a one-shot full cooperator in this setting. With only a single interaction and no future rounds, they still give their entire endowment to the group, indicating strong pro-social or efficiency-oriented motives that override narrow self-interest. Their cooperation is unconditional within the observed game: they commit fully without first testing or conditioning on others’ behavior. This suggests a default norm of high cooperation rather than a cautious or retaliatory approach. They appear willing to accept personal risk of exploitation; the contribution choice is strongly altruistic or rule-following, not payoff-maximizing in a strictly individual sense. Views about fairness are not directly expressed, but the action is consistent with an internal standard that favors maximal group benefit. Because only one decision is observed, stability over time and responses to changing group behavior remain unknown or low confidence.

The player shows a moderate, neither-minimal-nor-maximal contribution level. They do not fully free-ride but also do not sacrifice everything to the group, suggesting a “compromising cooperator” type: willing to support the public good, yet retaining a substantial private portion. With only one round observed, stability vs. volatility in contribution is unknown. There is no direct evidence of conditionality because the player does not see others’ choices beforehand, but their mid-range choice is consistent with an implicit expectation that many others will cooperate while some will not. The contribution is below the most altruistic levels yet clearly above pure self-interest, indicating a mixed motive of fairness and self-regard. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they expose a meaningful amount of their endowment to collective uncertainty, but not enough to be classified as risk-seeking or fully trusting. Their behavior aligns with a balanced, hedge-like strategy: participate in cooperation to benefit from amplification of the public good, while protecting against potential group under-contribution.

The player is a strong free-rider in this setting. When presented with a one-shot group interaction with a highly profitable public good, the player keeps essentially their entire endowment and contributes only a token amount. This indicates a clear preference for maximizing personal payoff over matching group norms of high contribution. Their behavior is not conditioned on any prior history, since there is only a single decision, and there is no evidence that they attempt to be fair or proportional to what others might give. They appear comfortable taking advantage of others’ cooperation and accepting a payoff that is boosted by others’ much higher contributions. Risk tolerance regarding social disapproval or moral costs is unknown because there is no punishment or feedback channel tied explicitly to their choice, and they do not discuss or justify their behavior. Overall, their default revealed strategy is to contribute the minimum they can while still participating, not to share in the cooperative surplus.

This player is a moderate, somewhat cooperative contributor. In a setting where full cooperation would be to contribute their entire endowment and full defection would be to contribute nothing, they choose an intermediate amount. This indicates a willingness to support the group but also a clear interest in retaining some private benefit. Their choice aligns loosely with the group’s generally high cooperation, but they contribute less than many of the most generous participants, suggesting they are not an unconditional cooperator. They appear comfortable taking on some risk of being exploited but not to the extreme of sacrificing everything; they balance self-interest and group interest rather than maximizing either. With only a single decision observed, stability or volatility of their contributions across time is unknown. Their behavior is consistent with a conditional cooperator who expects others to contribute and is willing to join in, but with a self-protective buffer. Fairness norms lean toward “pulling some weight” without matching the highest contributors; they do not insist on strict equality or maximal altruism. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they do not choose the safest purely self-interested option, but also avoid the most vulnerable fully cooperative stance. Because they cannot see others’ decisions before acting in this one-shot setting, any conditionality must be based on expectations rather than observed peer behavior; their underlying belief about average others’ cooperation is unknown, but their intermediate contribution suggests they consider significant cooperation plausible.

This player is a mostly conditional cooperator with opportunistic free-riding. After beginning as a pure defector, they shift into a pattern where they often match or exceed the group’s cooperative baseline but intermittently exploit cooperative periods by keeping their endowment. Contribution choices are binary and used flexibly rather than rigidly; they neither lock into always-give nor always-keep. They seem sensitive to whether “enough” others are contributing and are more willing to contribute when the broader environment is cooperative and somewhat stable. When others’ behavior becomes erratic or more selfish, they do not respond with a clear, consistent retaliation rule; instead, they sometimes keep contributing and other times free-ride, suggesting low but present concern for fairness and a stronger focus on personal payoff than on strict reciprocity. Their strategy suggests they understand that contributing in a cooperative environment can be individually profitable, yet they are comfortable deviating to defection even when others are largely cooperative. They exhibit moderate risk tolerance: they are willing to contribute when others seem likely to cooperate, but they also periodically “test” how much they can gain by deviating. The pattern indicates they value both group efficiency and personal advantage, with the balance tilting toward self-interest when a one-shot gain is available and no punishment is present. Stability is medium: they show phases of sustained cooperation punctuated by isolated defections rather than random noise. They do not show strong commitment to equality of outcomes; they are willing to earn more than others when free-riding on their contributions.

This player fits a “calculating opportunist with intermittent cooperation” type. They show no intrinsic preference for contributing; their baseline is to free‑ride whenever that is clearly profitable. They repeatedly choose not to contribute when others are already contributing enough to generate a high group return, suggesting a focus on individual payoff maximization rather than fairness or equal sharing. Their contribution pattern is volatile rather than stable. They switch between contributing and not contributing multiple times, with no simple monotonic trend. These switches seem conditional on the recent group environment: they are more willing to contribute when cooperation around them is generally high or improving, and more inclined to revert to 0 when others reduce contributions or when “no point” attitudes appear in chat. This indicates conditional cooperation, but only weakly and inconsistently; they are not an unwavering cooperator, nor a pure defector. They appear sensitive to marginal payoff logic. The environment is such that full contribution by all is socially efficient, and when others contribute a lot they do sometimes join in, which increases the group return including their own. However, they also frequently exploit that same high-cooperation state by contributing nothing while others contribute fully, taking advantage of the strong multiplier. This pattern suggests they recognize and use the high efficiency of the public good while still prioritizing personal gain. Fairness norms are weak or absent. There is no consistent attempt to match the typical contribution of others, and the player is comfortable being the only or one of the few defectors in otherwise cooperative rounds. They do not show evidence of being troubled by inequity in either direction; instead, they accept advantageous inequity and do not actively try to correct disadvantageous inequity either, beyond occasionally increasing their contribution when overall cooperation falls. Risk tolerance appears moderate. They are willing to contribute in situations where some others are defecting, which exposes them to the risk of low group returns, but they do not persist with high contributions in the face of clear defection. Their pattern suggests “probe and respond”: they contribute in some mixed environments, observe the return, and are willing to pull back when contributions from others drop. Overall, their strategy is best described as opportunistic, conditionally cooperative, and payoff-driven, with contributions used tactically rather than guided by a stable fairness or altruism norm.

This player is a strict, stable full cooperator. They always give their entire endowment to the public pool, without experimenting or adjusting. Their behavior fits a strong efficiency-focused norm: as long as the marginal per-capita return is above the private return, they prioritize maximizing the group’s total surplus rather than their own immediate, deviation-driven gain. They do not condition their contribution on others’ behavior, even when some group members contribute much less or fluctuate substantially. There is no evidence of conditional cooperation, reciprocity, or strategic hedging; instead, they follow a fixed cooperation rule that appears principle-driven. They tolerate being paired with clear low contributors and persist in full cooperation, suggesting a high willingness to be “exploited” rather than compromise their cooperative standard. Their risk tolerance for social exploitation is high; they do not appear to fear being the “sucker” or respond defensively to free riding. Their fairness norm is closer to “maximize collective efficiency by always contributing fully” than to “match what others do” or “adjust to punish unfairness through reduced giving.”

This player is a consistently high contributor who tends toward cooperative, near-prosocial behavior rather than selfish free-riding. Contributions stay in the upper range of the possible amounts and usually meet or exceed the group average, indicating that they prioritize group efficiency and are comfortable giving up private coins for the common pool. Their pattern suggests a quasi-rule-based approach (“contribute a lot”) rather than fine-tuned conditional reciprocity: they do not sharply cut back when others contribute less, nor do they escalate to full-max contributions only when everyone else does. Instead, they hover around a personally acceptable high range, with some moderate variation. They appear to value fairness and collective gains: they never exploit low-contributing others by dropping their own contribution to very low levels, even when clear under-contributors are present. At the same time, they are not a rigid pure altruist; their contributions fluctuate within a high band, which may reflect mild sensitivity to the group’s overall level or to payoff risk, but there is no strong, abrupt reaction to any single deviation by others. Their risk tolerance in contributing is relatively high, as they reliably place substantial resources into the public good without assurance of reciprocity, and they are willing to keep doing so across all rounds despite visible heterogeneity in others’ behavior. Overall, their contribution “type” is a steady, high, cooperative contributor with mild, not extreme, conditionality and a stable internal norm of doing more than the minimum to support group welfare.

This player is a rigid, unconditional medium contributor. They always give the same moderate amount, well below the emerging group norm but far above the minimum. Their contributions do not react to punishment, to changes in others’ contributions, or to shifts in group efficiency. This indicates a pre‑set personal rule (“this is my fair share”) rather than strategic conditional cooperation. They appear unconcerned with aligning to the majority’s higher standard, yet also unwilling to exploit the group by free‑riding fully. They tolerate being a mild under‑contributor despite repeated signals (via punishment and group behavior) that the group favors higher cooperation. They seem risk‑averse to extremes: they avoid both very low and very high contributions, sticking to a stable middle regardless of social or strategic feedback. Whether they consciously calculate payoffs is unknown; however, their lack of adjustment despite clear evidence that higher contributions are typical suggests low responsiveness to payoff optimization and social conformity.

This player is a moderately cooperative, efficiency-aware conditional contributor with some opportunistic streaks. Contributions cluster around the group average or slightly below it, with occasional full contributions when group cooperation looks strong. The player clearly understands that contributing is socially beneficial, given that they never free-ride aggressively and sometimes give the maximum possible amount. However, they also show willingness to under-contribute relative to high contributors when overall cooperation appears secure, which suggests a concern for personal payoff and a readiness to “coattail” on others’ generosity. Their contribution pattern is responsive to the group rather than fixed. When others’ cooperation is high and stable, they are willing to match or exceed the typical contribution. When some group members start contributing very little and total contributions fall, they do not escalate their own giving to compensate; instead, they stay around a moderate level that protects their payoff while still participating in the public good. This indicates conditional cooperation based on observed peer behavior rather than unconditional altruism. Fairness norms appear present but not strict. The player does not insist on exact equality in contributions, tolerating moderate deviations as long as the overall cooperation level remains high. They seem more bothered by very low contributors than by small under-contributions. Risk tolerance is medium: they are willing to risk higher contributions when others appear cooperative, but they do not keep pushing contributions upward when the environment grows more erratic. Overall, their type is a “pragmatic conditional cooperator”: they value the public good and prefer high-contribution equilibria, but they also guard against being exploited and are comfortable contributing somewhat less than the most generous group members when that seems safe.

This player is a generally high, norm-following contributor who occasionally experiments with defection. Across the game they choose the cooperative option far more often than the selfish one, so their baseline type is “conditional cooperator with principled leanings.” They appear to adopt a simple rule: contribute fully when a substantial core of others also does so, and withhold only sporadically when cooperation levels are low or mixed. Their contributions are mostly stable and not strongly volatile. Isolated deviations from full contribution do not cascade into a persistent free-riding strategy; after trying non-contribution, they quickly return to full cooperation even if the group does not uniformly improve. This suggests they value maintaining a cooperative standard more than strictly optimizing against short-run incentives. They display a concern for fairness: they keep contributing even when several others free-ride, and they combine this with selective punishment (discussed below). They seem willing to bear personal cost to support a group norm of high contribution, which is consistent with an internalized fairness or reciprocity norm rather than pure payoff maximization. Their tolerance for risk is moderate. They continue to contribute fully in an environment where some players regularly give nothing, indicating they are prepared to “risk” being exploited for the possibility of sustaining cooperation. However, the occasional zero contribution shows they are not blindly altruistic; they are willing to test what happens when they themselves free-ride, likely to gauge both income effects and social feedback via punishment. Conditioning on others’ behavior is present but not extreme. When the environment looks strongly cooperative, they reliably cooperate; when it is mixed or deteriorating, they still usually contribute but occasionally defect. They do not engage in rapid tit-for-tat or fine-grained matching; their reactions are coarse and slow, consistent with a player who tracks the general cooperative climate rather than individual histories (which they cannot fully observe here).

This player is a mostly high contributor who occasionally free-rides when it is safe and socially unchallenged. After initially withholding and then encouraging full cooperation, they settle into a pattern of almost always contributing the maximum, with only rare deviations. Their behavior indicates that they understand that full group contribution increases collective payoffs and are generally willing to bear the private cost to sustain that outcome. Their contribution pattern is relatively stable over time: once a cooperative norm is established among most others, they repeatedly match it. Deviations from full contribution are infrequent and occur when others are reliably contributing a lot and no social pressure is active, suggesting opportunistic free-riding rather than random error. When their own payoff is clearly worse under full contribution but group cooperation is still strong, they nonetheless continue to contribute, which is consistent with a preference for maintaining group efficiency and social harmony over strict short-term payoff maximization. They appear conditionally cooperative in a lenient sense: they keep contributing even when one or two others systematically contribute less, as long as a core of high contributors remains and the atmosphere stays positive. They do not quickly punish low contributors with defection; instead, they act as a stabilizer of the cooperative norm. However, their early and mid-game free-rides when others are cooperating show that they are not an unconditional altruist; they accept personally advantageous deviations as long as they do not visibly threaten the cooperative structure. Fairness concerns are present but indirect. They advocate that “everyone” should contribute fully and seem pleased when all do so, suggesting an ideal of symmetry and shared effort. Yet they tolerate a persistent low contributor without retaliating, and they themselves exploit the group’s generosity at select moments, implying that they prioritize group-level success and a pleasant interaction over strict equity enforcement. Risk tolerance appears moderate. They are willing to invest fully even when prior returns have been lower due to others’ partial defection, indicating readiness to accept payoff risk to sustain cooperation. At the same time, their few free-riding choices show that when the environment feels secure and cooperative, they are comfortable taking a calculated personal gain at the group’s expense, expecting that the cooperative structure will hold. Overall, this player is best described as a prosocial, norm-promoting conditional cooperator with mild opportunistic tendencies, who values a high-contribution equilibrium but does not rigidly enforce fairness through their own contributions.

This player is a volatile, strategically adaptive contributor who shifts between full cooperation and full free-riding. They do not exhibit a stable norm of always giving or always keeping; instead, they adjust sharply in response to recent group behavior and payoffs. Early on, they are willing to fully cooperate even when others do not all match them, suggesting an initial willingness to contribute to the public good despite imperfect reciprocity. After being disadvantaged and targeted by punishment while cooperating, they switch to pure free-riding while others continue to contribute fully, indicating strong sensitivity to relative payoff and perceived unfair treatment. They maintain this noncontributory stance across multiple opportunities while others keep contributing, which implies a readiness to exploit a cooperative group when they feel wronged, rather than a simple confusion or one-off deviation. Later, when overall cooperation collapses around them, they again choose to contribute fully even though most others hold back, revealing that they still value restoring group efficiency under some conditions and are not a pure unconditional defector. Finally, when the entire group converges on full cooperation, they also fully cooperate, aligning with an apparent preference for mutual high contributions when expectations are clear and symmetric. Overall, their contribution pattern suggests a conditional cooperator with a strong reciprocity element, but with a readiness to defect if they feel exploited or unfairly punished. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to take payoff risks by cooperating when others may not, but they also later protect their own payoff aggressively through defection when they feel trust has been violated. Their fairness norm seems centered on reciprocal and symmetric cooperation; once they perceive asymmetry, they prioritize self-protection and opportunistic gain over maintaining cooperation.

This player is a strict, unconditional high contributor. They always contribute the full endowment in every round, with no visible adjustment in response to others’ free-riding, mid-level contributions, or changing group averages. They appear to operate under a firm internal norm that “the right thing” is to fully cooperate, independent of short-term payoff considerations. There is no evidence of conditional cooperation (they do not reduce contributions even when many others give little or nothing), nor of strategic end-game defection. Risk tolerance regarding exploitation is high: they consistently expose themselves to being taken advantage of but do not flinch. This pattern indicates a strong, stable fairness ideal centered on their own behavior rather than on matching others.

This player is a generally cooperative, rule-following contributor who leans toward full provision of the public good but is not mechanically rigid. They usually give their entire endowment when the environment appears stable and relatively cooperative, suggesting a baseline pro-social tendency and acceptance of the efficiency logic of contributing. Their behavior is mostly stable: they default to full contribution and return to it after briefly deviating. The single instance of withholding contribution indicates they are capable of strategic adjustment rather than being a pure unconditional cooperator. The deviation coincides with a temporary drop in group contributions, implying conditional cooperation: they appear willing to withhold when they see others reducing their effort, but they quickly resume full contributions once the cooperative pattern is re-established. This points to a mild reciprocity norm rather than aggressive exploitation. They tolerate some degree of free-riding from others: one peer consistently contributes nothing, yet the player continues to give fully in most rounds. This suggests they value maintaining group efficiency and/or fairness toward cooperative partners more than strictly maximizing short-term individual payoff against the free-rider. Their brief defection is enough to “test” or react to the changed group pattern, but not sustained enough to label them as opportunistic. They do not insist on strict one-to-one fairness; they accept being more generous than at least one peer. Risk tolerance is moderate. They are willing to contribute fully despite uncertainty about remaining rounds and without direct visibility into others’ final payoffs, which implies comfort with outcome ambiguity as long as observable contributions from at least some peers remain high. At the same time, their single non-contribution suggests they remain attentive to the risk that cooperation could unravel and are willing to protect themselves if signals worsen. Overall, this player fits a “mostly cooperative, lightly conditional” type: they prioritize contributing and helping sustain a high-contribution norm, are somewhat sensitive to others’ behavior, and briefly experiment with non-cooperation when the group norm looks fragile, but quickly revert to cooperation.

This player is a rigid, full cooperator who consistently contributes their entire endowment in every decision, regardless of others’ behavior or payoff consequences. Their behavior is highly stable and non-volatile; there is no evidence of conditional cooperation, retaliation, or strategic adjustment in response to free-riding or shifts in group contributions. They appear to prioritize the public good outcome or an internal cooperation norm over individual material payoff. When the group briefly collapses to minimal cooperation, they still maintain the same maximal contribution pattern, indicating either (a) a strong internal norm of “always contribute,” (b) a rule-based or heuristic strategy that they do not revise, or (c) low sensitivity to short-term payoff losses. They do not exploit opportunities to increase their own earnings by defecting, even when others’ behavior would make defection temporarily more profitable. There is no visible concern with fairness in the sense of matching others’ contributions or punishing free-riders through withholding contributions. They tolerate persistent free-riding by another player without changing their own behavior, which suggests either acceptance of unequal benefit sharing, indifference to inequity, or a strong commitment to cooperation independent of fairness considerations. Risk tolerance appears high with respect to payoff variance: the player repeatedly exposes themselves to the risk of low private returns by contributing fully even when group behavior is unstable. At the same time, they do not experiment with alternative strategies, which suggests low exploration and high adherence to a single, pre-chosen rule. Conditionality is effectively absent in the observed behavior: contributions are not contingent on recent group contributions, on the presence of a consistent free-rider, or on the temporary breakdown of cooperation. Learning or adaptation is either absent or too limited to detect; the player shows no shift from cooperation to defection over time, despite clear opportunities to do so. Overall, this player is best described as an unconditional full cooperator with a strong, stable contribution rule and little to no reactive adjustment to others.

This player is a highly stable, strict full cooperator once coordination on high contributions is achieved. They start with a substantial but not maximal contribution, then quickly shift to and maintain the maximum possible contribution for the remainder of the game, regardless of small deviations by others. Their behavior suggests a strong efficiency norm: they prioritize maximizing group payoffs over personal short-term gain, even when the marginal per-coin return from the public good is below 1 for the contributor. They do not exhibit conditional cooperation in a fine-grained sense; instead, they appear to adopt a simple rule: once most others are fully cooperating, they contribute fully and stay there, tolerating minor free-riding rather than adjusting downward. They show no visible concern for personal exploitation by occasional lower contributors and display no evidence of strategic endgame defection. Risk tolerance seems moderately high in social terms: they are willing to commit to full cooperation early and persistently without first testing whether others will reciprocate beyond an initial partial contribution. Fairness considerations are present but weakly enforced behaviorally: they advocate for equal, maximal contributions but do not change their own contributions when others briefly deviate, suggesting they care more about achieving and maintaining an efficient, cooperative norm than about punishing slight unfairness.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor who consistently gives the full endowment to the public good every round. Their behavior does not adjust to variation in others’ average contributions, nor to shifts in specific peers’ behavior. They appear to hold a strong fairness or efficiency norm favoring maximal group contribution and are willing to uphold that norm regardless of apparent free-riding by specific players. There is no observable risk aversion in their contribution decisions; they accept the risk of being exploited by lower contributors without reducing their own input. Their contribution pattern suggests a principled or rule-based cooperator rather than a cautious, conditional cooperator.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They consistently give their full endowment to the public good in every situation, regardless of others’ behavior, punishment patterns, or proximity to the final round. They appear to follow a strong internal norm that “full contribution is the right thing,” rather than conditioning on others’ average. They do not shade contributions downward even when some group members persistently free-ride or reduce their giving. This suggests a preference for efficient group outcomes and a willingness to bear personal risk that others may not reciprocate. There is no evidence of strategic under-contribution or profit-maximizing adjustment; their cooperative stance is fixed and robust across the game.

This player is a mostly-cooperative but opportunistic contributor who toggles between full cooperation and full defection. They reliably match the group’s cooperative norm for long stretches, even after being punished, which suggests a baseline willingness to support high-efficiency outcomes and a preference for norm compliance when the group is clearly cooperative. They do not shade contributions gradually; instead, they switch discretely between all-or-nothing. This indicates low use of fine-grained reciprocity and a relatively simple contingent strategy. Their occasional defections occur despite stable full contributions from others, so they are not reacting to observable free-riding by peers. This points to either (a) opportunism (testing whether they can free-ride while others stay cooperative), or (b) experimentation/uncertainty about optimal behavior. Because they return to full contribution multiple times after defecting and after being punished, they are not a pure free-rider; they maintain some internal norm or belief that full contribution is acceptable or expected. Fairness norms appear mixed: they sometimes cooperate fully in a group of full contributors (consistent with equality and efficiency concerns), but also sometimes keep their entire endowment while others fully contribute (tolerating or exploiting advantageous inequality). They do not show risk-averse hedging; they are willing to sharply deviate from the cooperative pattern even when this predictably invites punishment and loss, which suggests either low sensitivity to social sanctions, low concern for being disliked, or low anticipation of how others will respond. Overall, they fit a “conditional cooperator with opportunistic deviations” profile, with simple binary choices rather than nuanced reciprocity.

This player is a mostly unconditional full cooperator after an initial free-riding test. They begin by withholding contribution once, then immediately switch to always contributing the full endowment and never deviate afterward, even when another player free-rides or when they themselves are repeatedly punished. Their behavior indicates a strong preference for maintaining a high-public-good, high-payoff environment rather than exploiting it. They do not appear to condition their contribution on others’ average contributions or on being targeted by punishment; contributions remain stable and maximal across cooperative and mildly defective group states. They tolerate some inequity (others free-riding while they cooperate, and unfair punishment) without reducing cooperation, suggesting relatively high social preference for group efficiency and low willingness to use contribution as leverage or retaliation. Their risk tolerance regarding exploitation is moderate to high: they accept the risk of being the “sucker” to keep the public good high, but this occurs in a mostly cooperative group, which lowers the actual cost of that stance.

The player is a stable, unconditional full contributor. They always contribute their entire endowment, regardless of others’ behavior or recent punishment history. They appear committed to a strong efficiency and equality norm: they behave as if the “right” outcome is full contribution by everyone and maintain this even when others occasionally free ride or when they themselves are punished. There is no observable strategic adjustment or hedging based on changes in group contributions; their behavior suggests they prioritize sustaining the cooperative norm over short‑term payoff optimization. Risk tolerance with respect to being exploited is high: they keep cooperating even when some group members contribute nothing, and there is no evidence of conditional cooperation or opportunistic defection.

This player is an opportunistic low contributor with sporadic high-cooperation spikes. Their average contribution is low relative to the endowment and to the group’s cooperative benchmarks, but they occasionally choose very high or full contributions. These high-input decisions are not stable; they appear as isolated experiments or brief cooperative bursts rather than a maintained norm. They adjust contributions in response to the apparent cooperation level of others. When the group’s contributions look strong and widespread, they are willing to join with a substantial or even maximal contribution. When cooperation softens, fragments, or looks unreliable, they quickly revert to minimal giving and often free-ride on others’ efforts. This indicates conditional cooperation in principle, but with a strong self-serving tilt: they are more comfortable benefiting from others’ generosity than bearing the cost of trying to sustain it themselves. Fairness norms appear weak and asymmetric. The player occasionally behaves “generously” and accepts very poor payoffs for themselves, suggesting they are not purely payoff-maximizing and that they understand and sometimes value the public good. However, those moves are not followed by persistence or clear attempts to stabilize a fair, high-contribution norm. Once they see that their own big contributions do not reliably elicit matching behavior or superior outcomes, they shift toward exploiting cooperative environments rather than sacrificing to improve them. Risk tolerance is situation-dependent. They are capable of high-risk cooperative moves that expose them to large relative losses when others don’t match, which implies some tolerance for strategic risk or experimentation. Yet over time, their contribution pattern converges toward low, low-variance contributions with occasional small deviations, consistent with a cautious, payoff-protective baseline. The combination suggests that they test bold strategies but quickly revert to safer free-riding when the social return is uncertain or disappointing. In summary, this player is a conditional, self-protective cooperator: willing to invest heavily under certain perceived conditions but defaulting to low cooperation and strategic free-riding, with limited commitment to enforcing or upholding fairness norms.

This player is a high, but not unconditional, contributor who oscillates between full cooperation and strategic free riding. Their default mode is to give the maximum or near-maximum amount, and this pattern persists over most of the game. They occasionally drop to zero or low contributions, but these moments are rare relative to their overall cooperative baseline. Their behavior suggests they recognize that contributing nothing while others give moderate or high amounts is individually profitable, because they exploit the group fund when they withhold. However, they do not lock into permanent defection after experiencing such profitable free-riding; instead, they quickly return to generous contributions. This indicates they is not purely payoff-maximizing in a narrow sense and likely values maintaining a cooperative group environment. Contribution levels are somewhat volatile in the early-to-mid game but remain within a restricted band: they alternate mainly between very high contributions and complete or near-complete withholding, with a few intermediate contributions. This pattern looks like deliberate experimentation around a cooperative norm—testing what happens when they defect or scale back, then reverting to high contributions rather than systematically ratcheting down. They appears moderately conditional on others’ behavior. When group contributions are relatively strong or surge, they reliably match or exceed that cooperative tone. When the group performs poorly or becomes erratic, they sometimes reduce their own contribution, but not consistently and not to the minimal level. This asymmetry—being much quicker to restore high giving than to maintain low giving—suggests a preference for cooperation tempered by opportunism rather than strict tit-for-tat. They does not track fairness in a finely calibrated, proportional way. There is no clear pattern of matching the group average precisely or punishing specific low contributors with symmetrical reductions. Instead, they seems to apply coarse-grained rules: “stay generous when the group looks reasonably cooperative; occasionally pull back if others seem to drift toward selfishness; re-engage fully when cooperation resurges.” This points to a loose fairness norm: they expects some level of reciprocity but tolerates substantial variation rather than reacting sharply to each deviation. Risk tolerance is high. Contributing the maximum in a large group with a low marginal per-capita return is risky from a narrow self-interest viewpoint, especially when others oscillate or undercontribute. Yet they repeatedly commits large amounts, indicating a willingness to risk being exploited in the hope or expectation of higher collective payoffs. Their occasional free-riding episodes show they understands the incentive structure, but their repeated reversion to generosity suggests a prosocial or at least cooperation-seeking orientation that coexists with payoff awareness. Overall, this player fits a “cooperative opportunist” profile: they prefers to be a strong contributor and help sustain a high-contribution equilibrium, occasionally tests or exploits the public good when conditions look unfavorable, and then quickly returns to generous behavior rather than spiraling into persistent defection.

This player is a mostly cooperative but somewhat inconsistent contributor who understands that group contributions are beneficial yet occasionally deviates when confused or skeptical. They usually choose to contribute fully to the public good, even when others begin free-riding, indicating a baseline pro-social or norm-following inclination and a moderate tolerance for being exploited. Their one major deviation from full contribution coincides with expressed confusion about the role of rewards and a prior statement of intent that contradicts their actual choice, suggesting that their behavior is not purely strategic but influenced by momentary uncertainty or misclick/second thoughts (confidence: low on exact cause, high that it is an anomaly rather than a stable pattern). They are willing to keep contributing even when free-riding becomes more common and when others explicitly advocate for non-contribution, which implies a relatively weak end-game defection tendency and a focus on maintaining cooperation. However, they also show that they are not ideologically committed to always cooperating: they explicitly propose that everyone should refuse to donate in response to rising free-riding, indicating conditional cooperation norms tied to perceived fairness and reciprocity.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They always contribute their full endowment in every round, even when others free-ride heavily, when coordination attempts to “refuse to donate” emerge, and when the game reaches the final round. Their strategy does not appear to adjust to changes in group behavior, observed defection, or horizon effects. This suggests either a fixed norm of “always contribute” or a deliberate choice to prioritize group efficiency over individual earnings, regardless of others’ actions. They tolerate substantial exploitation by free-riders without retaliating or downgrading their own contributions. Their behavior is consistent with a strong cooperative or rule-following type rather than a conditional cooperator or payoff-maximizer.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They always give their full endowment, even when others start withholding and even when there is explicit group coordination pressure to stop contributing. They appear to prioritize collective efficiency over short‑run individual gain, and they do not visibly condition their contributions on others’ past behavior or on end‑game proximity. There is no evidence of retaliation or strategic free‑riding; they tolerate being exploited by low contributors without adjusting downward. Their risk tolerance for being the “sucker” is high, and they seem more guided by a simple cooperative rule (“always contribute”) than by nuanced payoff maximization or fairness balancing.

This player is a generally high contributor with one clear deviation. They usually give the full endowment, even when others start defecting, suggesting a baseline cooperative or efficiency-oriented inclination. Their contributions are not mechanically unconditional, however: after making a costly cooperative move that is not reciprocated via reward, they temporarily switch to free-riding. This indicates conditional cooperation influenced by perceived social reciprocity, not just contribution levels. They explicitly reference the payoff logic later, recognizing that defectors can earn more when others continue to contribute, so they understand the strategic incentives and are not simply naïve. Despite this awareness and explicit peer pressure to defect at the end, they continue to contribute fully, implying some combination of fairness concern, mild social anxiety about defecting, and a tendency toward “doing the proper thing” even when others suggest otherwise. Their behavior is somewhat volatile early on (switching once to zero), but then stabilizes to full contributions, showing fast adaptation and then commitment to a cooperative pattern.

This player is a high, mostly unconditional contributor who occasionally experiments with self-serving deviations. For most of the game, they give either the full endowment or very close to it, even when one group member persistently contributes very little. Their behavior shows a strong cooperative baseline that is not tightly conditioned on exact fairness or full reciprocity from all group members. They are not perfectly rigid: they reduce their contribution a few times, including at least one sharp drop. These deviations do not become a new stable pattern; they return to high contributions afterwards. This suggests opportunistic testing or short-run self-interest rather than a committed switch to defection. They tolerate inequality: they keep contributing a lot despite one clear low contributor and without any visible coordination to punish or isolate that player. They appear more focused on sustaining group provision with the reliable high contributors than on enforcing strict fairness norms on everyone. Risk tolerance is moderate-to-high in social terms. They consistently expose themselves to the risk that others might reduce contributions later, yet they keep cooperating. Economically, they do not exploit the individually rational strategy of free-riding for most of the game. This implies a cooperative orientation or prosocial preference rather than pure payoff maximization. Overall, their contribution profile fits a “cooperative but occasionally opportunistic” type: they default to strong cooperation, are robust to some free-riding, but are not entirely immune to short-term temptation as the game unfolds.

This player is a highly cooperative “steadfast contributor” with a single brief defection. They consistently give the full endowment to the public fund almost every round, indicating a strong preference for collective efficiency over short‑term private gain. Their pattern is extremely stable: they do not gradually decrease contributions in response to a persistent low contributor, and they do not condition their level on small variations in others’ behavior. They appear to treat full contribution as the baseline norm, rather than something contingent on strict fairness or equal treatment. They do briefly switch to zero contribution once late in the game, then immediately return to full contributions.
