The most plausible interpretation is a one‑off test or opportunistic free ride rather than a full strategy shift: there is no sustained lowering or tit‑for‑tat pattern before or after. This suggests they understand that free riding can be profitable but still choose to cooperate almost all the time. Given the public-good multiplier, full contribution is individually costly relative to free riding but socially efficient. Their willingness to keep contributing at the maximum despite chronic undercontribution by one peer shows tolerance for disadvantageous inequity and a low sensitivity to being exploited. They do not seem risk‑averse about others’ cooperation: they continue to bet on group contribution even when evidence of full unanimity is weak. They appear to implicitly endorse a fairness norm that “everyone should give all” but are willing to maintain that norm unilaterally rather than punishing or withdrawing when others fall short.

The player fits a “quiet, profit‑maximizing free rider with occasional conformity” type. Their baseline is to keep almost all of their endowment and contribute minimally, even when others contribute fully and explicitly advocate universal giving. They do not match the prevailing norm of full contribution and never sustain high contributions across time. Their contributions fluctuate between very low and moderate levels rather than converging on a stable pattern, suggesting opportunistic experimentation rather than a principled norm. They clearly prioritize individual payoff over group efficiency: they repeatedly choose contribution levels that maximize their private returns in the given environment, even when a stable, high‑contribution norm is visible and reinforced in chat. Occasional higher contributions appear sporadic and are not tied to any expressed reasoning or explicit coordination, so their underlying decision rule is unknown; however, those higher contributions do not persist when they see that others continue contributing high amounts regardless of their own behavior. They show no visible fairness norm: they regularly contribute far less than the group while benefiting from others’ generosity, without any evident attempt to “make up for it” later or to track proportionality. Their risk tolerance regarding potential social sanctions is high; they free ride openly in the presence of identifiable partners and critical chat but do not adjust towards more cooperative behavior in response to social pressure alone.

This player is a moderately high but very conditional contributor. They oscillate between full or very high contributions and clearly under-averaging choices, rather than settling on a stable level. They appear to track others’ average behavior and adjust: when many group members contribute generously, they are willing to match or exceed that standard; when some persistently contribute less, they sometimes drop their own contribution while still staying within a cooperative range rather than fully defecting. They do not exhibit a simple upward or downward time trend—cooperation rises and falls in response to the pattern of others’ contributions and to recent punishment use. They tolerate some free-riding by others but use their own contribution in combination with punishment to enforce what looks like a fairness norm: low contributors relative to the group are treated as norm violators, yet the player also occasionally free-rides themselves when others are very generous, suggesting that their fairness standard applies more harshly to others than to themselves. Risk-wise, they are willing to “over-contribute” relative to the group early on and in some later rounds, indicating a willingness to take payoff risk in pursuit of cooperative outcomes, but they also show episodes of payoff-maximizing restraint, especially after intense punishment phases.

This player is a generally cooperative but flexible contributor who treats the public good as their default but not absolute priority. They rarely free‑ride completely and almost always give a positive amount. Their modal behavior is to contribute around half of their endowment, but they periodically shift to full contributions, especially once group patterns have stabilized and cooperation from others appears predictable. They also occasionally experiment with low contributions early on, suggesting they test how much they can deviate without provoking excessive punishment or destabilizing the group outcome. They appear to accept that contributing more than the marginal per‑coin return is individually costly yet still do so often, indicating a concern for sustaining group efficiency or conforming to an implicit norm of “everyone gives a reasonable amount.” Simultaneously, their sporadic under‑contributions indicate some self‑interest and a willingness to benefit from others’ higher giving when they see room to do so. Their contributions are moderately conditional: they tend to move upward when cooperation seems solid and stable, and relax downward when the environment looks more chaotic or when punishment noise is high. Fairness norms for them seem to be “contribute something, don’t be an extreme outlier.” They do not systematically match the highest contributors, nor do they join the lowest. Risk tolerance is moderate: they are willing to put in high contributions even when some others consistently contribute nothing, but they offset that risk by occasionally protecting their own payoff with lower contributions or by punishing low contributors rather than by withdrawing to zero themselves.

The player is a “principled high contributor with conditional restraint.” Their default is to give the full endowment or close to it, indicating a strong cooperative orientation and a norm that everyone should support the public good generously. They repeatedly choose contributions at or near the maximum even when average group contributions are clearly lower, which suggests they value sustaining cooperation more than short‑term personal gain. Contribution levels are not completely rigid. The player occasionally reduces their contribution to a moderate or low level after observing clear deviations from high cooperation by others. These downshifts are limited in frequency and size, and they later return to high giving, implying they use contribution cuts as a mild signal or probe rather than as a full defection strategy. They appear sensitive to fairness: when many others contribute substantially more than the lowest contributors but still below their own full-cooperation standard, the player experiments with giving less, yet they do not adopt persistent free‑riding. Their behavior indicates conditional cooperation, but with an unusually high baseline: they are willing to contribute at levels that exceed the typical group member, even when this exposes them to exploitation. They show some risk tolerance in maintaining high contributions despite uncertainty about the remaining number of rounds and despite evidence that some players repeatedly undercontribute. However, they do adjust when they personally incur heavy punishment, briefly lowering their contribution and then re‑escalating to high levels, which suggests they want to preserve both payoffs and norms but prioritize recovering from setbacks before recommitting fully. The player seems to hold a fairness norm that low or zero contributions are unacceptable, moderate contributions are tolerated but not ideal, and full or near‑full contributions are the desirable standard. They do not appear to be purely outcome‑maximizing: many of their choices are inconsistent with narrow self‑interest but consistent with maintaining a cooperative climate. Their giving pattern is relatively stable overall, with occasional reactive deviations that are then corrected back toward high cooperation. They appear to learn and update cautiously, experimenting with lower contributions to test the group and punishment environment, then reverting to their cooperative default once they see the outcome of those tests.

This player is a verbally pro-cooperation “team player” who nonetheless opportunistically free-rides when it is safe and profitable. They initially match a moderately high cooperative norm and then quickly endorse the group’s push toward full contribution. Their stated intentions track the cooperative equilibrium implied by the multiplier, and they explicitly commit to increasing their contribution alongside others. However, their actual pattern is conditional and self-serving rather than principled: after having experienced full mutual cooperation and seeing that the group can coordinate, they later reduce their own contribution while continuing to present themselves as aligned with the high-contribution plan. This indicates a willingness to exploit trust once a cooperative norm is in place, suggesting a strategic, payoff-sensitive cooperative stance rather than unconditional altruism. They tolerate inequality in their favor and show no attempt to correct it through higher giving or punishment, consistent with an egoistic payoff focus under a cooperative façade. Their risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to cooperate early when it is unclear how others will behave, but later they reduce their contribution only after the norm is established, which lowers the risk of coordinated punishment or social backlash.

The player acts as a verbally strong cooperator but with a late deviation. They initially advocate and follow a high, equal-contribution norm, then privately exploit it once it appears solidified. Early on, they push for a specific positive contribution rule and match it with full contributions, signaling commitment to group welfare and efficiency. When the group successfully coordinates on full contributions, they maintain high giving once but later reduce their own contribution while publicly affirming a high-contribution standard. This pattern fits a “strategic conditional cooperator with opportunistic streak”: they support efficient cooperation while it is being built, then quietly free‑ride once trust and expectations are in place. Their choices suggest they understand that full contribution is collectively efficient and use that norm, but are willing to undercut it when they expect others to keep cooperating. Contribution behavior is somewhat volatile rather than rigid; it responds to the social environment and is not purely selfish or purely altruistic.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator in this environment. They always contribute the maximum allowed amount, and this does not change even when several others repeatedly free‑ride or partially defect. Their cooperation is not contingent on others’ current behavior, on recent punishment patterns, or on their own payoff disadvantages relative to defectors. This suggests a strong internal norm in favor of contributing rather than a purely payoff‑maximizing or tit‑for‑tat strategy. They tolerate significant payoff inequality in favor of free‑riders without scaling back their own contribution, indicating low sensitivity to exploitation and a willingness to incur personal cost to maintain the public good. Risk tolerance appears high with respect to being the “sucker” (cooperating while others defect), and they show no evidence of experimenting with lower contributions to probe others’ responses. Their notion of fairness seems to equate “right behavior” with always contributing fully, independent of what others do.

This player is a steadfast free-rider. They always take the entire private endowment and never contribute to the public fund, despite a high marginal per capita return that makes full contribution socially efficient. Their behavior is extremely stable: there is no adjustment in response to others’ consistently high contributions, nor to changes in others’ contribution patterns. They do not appear to follow reciprocity or fairness norms; they accept benefiting from others’ cooperation while giving nothing themselves. There is no sign of conditional cooperation or experimentation. Their risk tolerance with respect to social sanctions is high: they continue to free-ride despite being heavily and repeatedly punished. Overall, they prioritize maximizing immediate private payoff over group efficiency or equity, even when social costs are salient.

This player is primarily a persistent free-rider with occasional, low-frequency, low-to-medium contributions that do not follow a clear reciprocity pattern. Their default is to contribute nothing, even when others contribute high amounts and sustain relatively cooperative environments. They show no stable upward or downward trend in generosity; instead, they insert sporadic positive contributions that look more like brief experiments or impulses than a coherent strategy. They do not appear to condition contributions tightly on others’ average behavior. They keep contributing zero when others are clearly cooperating, and they sometimes contribute a moderate amount even when others’ contributions are relatively low. This suggests weak or absent adherence to a strong fairness norm such as “match the group” or “match the average.” They seem highly payoff-focused: free-riding delivers them consistently high earnings relative to peers in many situations, and they show no attempt to trade off earnings to support group efficiency. When they do contribute, the amounts are typically partial and not obviously risk-seeking (e.g., no repeated full-endowment contributions). This implies moderate risk aversion in terms of not staking everything on cooperation, but combined with willingness to occasionally “test” contributing without clear evidence it will pay off. Overall, they fit a “selfish opportunist” type: they mostly exploit others’ cooperation, occasionally inject contributions without sustaining them, and do not display a principled equity or efficiency motive.

This player is a moderately cooperative, payoff-conscious conditional contributor with some volatility. They rarely choose the extremes of full free-riding or full contribution and hover around a mid-range contribution. They do occasionally test low and zero contributions and also sometimes jump to very high or full contributions, suggesting experimentation rather than a fixed norm. Their behavior indicates conditional cooperation: they tend to contribute more when others as a group contribute more and when several peers show stable, medium-to-high contributions. When group contributions sag, they reduce their own and at times drop to zero, indicating they do not adhere to a strict “always cooperate” fairness rule. They seem sensitive to under-contribution by specific peers (especially chronic low contributors) but primarily respond at the aggregate level rather than targeting matching levels to each individual. They appear to prioritize personal payoff over strict equality. When the environment becomes noisy or mixed—some high contributors, some persistent free-riders—they often move toward lower contributions that are privately profitable, even when that yields higher payoffs for themselves than for most others. In homogeneous high-contribution environments, they are willing to contribute more, but they do not insist on contributing as much as the highest contributors unless others are also clearly investing at that level. Their risk tolerance in contribution appears moderate. They are willing to risk high contributions even when they have previously been punished or seen others punished, but they more frequently choose safe, mid-level values that preserve substantial private payoff regardless of others’ behavior. Their occasional extreme moves (zero or full contribution) look like strategic tests of how the group will react rather than stable preferences. They do not appear to enforce or follow a strict fairness norm such as “everyone should contribute the same absolute amount” or “everyone should contribute half.” Instead, their behavior suggests a flexible, adaptive rule: contribute enough to benefit from the public good and appear reasonably cooperative, but scale up or down depending on how cooperative the group seems and on whether that cooperation appears to be rewarded or punished. Overall, their contribution pattern fits a “cautious conditional cooperator who is willing to opportunistically freeride when trust in the group’s cooperativeness weakens.”

This player is a highly stable, near-unconditional full contributor. They consistently give their entire endowment to the public fund in almost all rounds, including throughout the mid-game and into the final rounds, despite the multiplier being individually unprofitable. They show only a single, isolated instance of free-riding and immediately revert back to full contribution thereafter, with no visible shift in behavior by others needed to restore their cooperation. This pattern indicates a strong internal norm or preference for full cooperation rather than a purely payoff-maximizing or tit-for-tat strategy. They tolerate at least moderate exploitation: when a partner free-rides once, they do not punish through reduced contributions, and when they themselves briefly defect, they do not follow up with a defection streak. Their contributions do not visibly track others’ past behaviors; instead, they maintain a “default cooperate” rule. Fairness concerns are present but appear secondary to maintaining high group efficiency: they contribute fully even after observing that defecting would earn more in the short run, and they do not visibly attempt to restore fairness via contribution adjustments. Risk tolerance is relatively high in the cooperative direction: they repeatedly expose themselves to possible exploitation without trying to hedge by partial or conditional contributions.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator throughout the game. They always place the entire endowment into the public account, with no evidence of experimenting, hedging, or conditioning on others’ behavior. Their cooperation persists even when a small number of others free-ride or contribute less. They clearly internalize a norm that everyone “should” contribute and that this is straightforwardly “best for everyone.” They do not appear to track individual deviations in any systematic way for the purpose of adjusting their own giving; instead, they treat full contribution as the default, morally correct strategy, and they stick to it once a cooperative group pattern emerges. There is no sign of risk aversion about “being the sucker”: they willingly expose themselves to exploitation by defectors but rely on social norms and group pressure rather than changing their contribution. Their behavior is consistent with a prosocial, efficiency-oriented type who values maximizing the group return and is comfortable sacrificing private payoff to sustain a cooperative equilibrium, especially when the group is already highly cooperative.

This player is a strict, unconditional full cooperator in this environment. They always give the maximum allowed amount to the public good, with no deviations and no apparent experimentation. Their cooperation does not depend on others’ short-term behavior: they continue to contribute fully even when a few group members sometimes withhold contributions and when there is uncertainty about how many rounds remain. They show no evidence of conditional cooperation (e.g., “I’ll contribute only if others do”), nor of strategic end‑game defection, despite not knowing the exact horizon. They appear comfortable accepting minor inequality or occasional free-riding as long as the group is broadly cooperative. Overall, they prioritize the efficient, high-return group outcome over individual short-term gain, and they do so in a very stable, consistent manner.

This player is a strategically selfish conditional contributor with clear adaptation over time. They begin with full cooperation, then rapidly shift to much lower contributions once they see substantial free-riding and/or chaotic sanctioning behavior around them. After the initial shift, they mostly keep their own contribution well below the emerging group norm, occasionally increasing it somewhat but never returning to high-cooperator levels. They appear to prioritize personal payoff over sustaining high group efficiency: they repeatedly choose low contributions even when others continue to give generously. Their behavior suggests they understand that contributing less than others while still benefiting from the public pot is profitable, and they systematically exploit this. Their contributions are conditional, but in an asymmetrical, opportunistic way: they reduce sharply after seeing that others’ behavior and sanctioning system are unstable and risky, yet they do not “reciprocate up” to match the high contributions of the many strong cooperators in later rounds. Instead, they maintain a low contribution baseline, with modest variation, likely balancing: (a) the risk of further punishment they sometimes receive, and (b) the desire to keep most of their endowment. They show no clear fairness norm aimed at matching the group or rewarding “deserving” others through their own giving. Their contribution pattern is consistent with a “quiet exploiter” type: they accept high cooperation from others but do not share proportionally in the cost. Risk tolerance looks moderate to high in payoff terms: they are willing to stay a noticeable below-average contributor for many rounds despite being targeted with costly sanctions at times. Overall, their contributions are low, self-serving, and moderately responsive to the environment, but never in the direction of strong, stable cooperation.

This player is a moderate but self-serving conditional cooperator. They mix between free-riding and mid-level contributions rather than ever fully sacrificing or fully defecting. Their contributions cluster around two modes: giving nothing and giving about half of the endowment, with no instances of fully matching the highest contributors. They appear sensitive to the surrounding cooperation level: when the group contributes very high amounts, they sometimes join at a mid-range level, but they also periodically revert to zero even in highly cooperative environments. This pattern suggests they value maintaining high personal payoffs while not fully jeopardizing group returns, rather than following a strict fairness norm or simple mimicry. They do not demonstrate symmetry with others’ efforts: they never match the more generous players, and they do not systematically increase their own contribution when particular individuals contribute heavily. Their behavior fits a “profit-maximizing under a cooperation umbrella” mindset: they are willing to put in enough to blend with the group and not stand out as a pure exploiter, but they remain below the cooperative elite and periodically exploit. Risk-wise, they behave conservatively: they avoid extreme swings in their own contributions, staying between zero and roughly half the endowment, and maintain high round payoffs throughout. They do not test very high-contribution strategies, even when such behavior is clearly common.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment, with no variation and no apparent sensitivity to others’ behavior. They do not reduce contributions in response to clearly visible free-riding, erratic behavior, or heavy use of punishment and reward by others. This suggests a strong internal norm or commitment to maximizing the group public good, independent of short-term personal payoff optimization. They appear risk-tolerant with respect to exploitation: they continue fully cooperating even when some others repeatedly contribute nothing or very little. There is no sign that they condition contributions on fairness, reciprocity, or average group contribution levels; their strategy is simple and consistent rather than contingent. Their type is best characterized as a norm-driven unconditional cooperator rather than a calculating conditional cooperator.

This player is a generally high, steady contributor who experiments briefly with free-riding but then settles into a cooperative baseline. They start at the maximum possible contribution, then oscillate among high amounts, with one clear instance of contributing nothing even when others contribute strongly. After that, they consistently choose contributions in the mid-to-high range, usually above the group average and rarely at the minimum or maximum extremes. They appear to value group efficiency and are willing to forgo private coins to support the public good, as indicated by repeated high contributions despite the presence of low contributors and heavy norm-enforcement behavior by others. At the same time, the one free-ride episode suggests they are not a pure altruist: they test the payoff structure and learn that strong cooperation by others can be exploited without immediate consequences. Nonetheless, they do not adopt permanent free-riding; they return to substantial contributions, suggesting an internal norm of “do your part” rather than strict payoff maximization. Their behavior is only weakly conditional on others’ contributions. Very low contributions by some players coexist with their own continued high giving. When the group is generally cooperative, they maintain or slightly adjust contributions but do not dynamically fine-tune based on specific others’ deviations. This indicates a relatively stable personal standard rather than responsive reciprocity. Risk tolerance appears moderate. They are willing to contribute high amounts even in a noisy, volatile environment with erratic punishers and rewarders. However, they avoid extreme over-contribution relative to most high contributors after the initial round, suggesting some concern about being exploited or overcommitting. Fairness norms are implicit: they exhibit no systematic tendency to lower contributions in response to free-riders or to ramp up dramatically when others become more generous. This points more to a “stably prosocial but self-aware” type than to a strict equity or conditional cooperator type.

This player is a steady, high contributor after a brief early experiment with free-riding. They alternate between medium and zero giving at the very start, then quickly lock into consistently contributing the full endowment in all remaining rounds, regardless of variation in others’ behavior or obvious antisocial punishment patterns in the group. This shows a strong internal norm for cooperation that is not tightly conditional on others’ contributions or on the presence of “fair” punishment/reward systems. They tolerate the risk that others may exploit their generosity, and they do not scale back their giving in response to either being punished when free-riding or seeing erratic punishment in the group later. They appear to prioritize group efficiency and personal norm-following over payoff maximization, suggesting a cooperative, possibly efficiency-oriented or rule-internalized type rather than a payoff-maximizing conditional cooperator.

This player is a consistently high contributor who anchors their behavior around a “good citizen” norm rather than strict payoff maximization. They typically give well above the contemporaneous group average and never free‑ride. Their contributions are very stable, with only small deviations that still remain in the cooperative range. They appear willing to keep contributing fully even when some others give little or nothing, suggesting they prioritize sustaining a cooperative standard over short‑term protection against exploitation. There is no clear evidence that they condition their own contributions strongly on the exact pattern of others’ contributions; even when low contributors persist, they maintain high contributions. This points to a type who sees contribution as a personal obligation or identity (“I am a cooperator”) rather than a tactical response. Their behavior shows moderate risk tolerance with respect to being exploited: they accept the possibility that low contributors will benefit from their generosity, presumably because they expect social control via punishment and reward mechanisms to correct deviations.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator under the given parameters. They always give their entire endowment to the public fund, showing no sensitivity to fluctuations in others’ average behavior and no visible conditionality on past treatment. They act as though the socially efficient norm is full contribution and adhere to it regardless of observed free riding or mid-level contributions by others. They appear strongly fairness- and efficiency-oriented: the game’s high multiplier makes cooperation payoff-dominant, and their behavior aligns with prioritizing group efficiency over own short-term payoff. They do not hedge or experiment with partial or zero contributions, suggesting low concern with exploitation relative to their commitment to the cooperative norm. Their risk tolerance is high in social terms: they continue to expose themselves to potential exploitation by consistent cooperators with no attempt to protect themselves via reduced contributions. They seem to follow a “do the right thing and use sanctions/rewards to shape others” approach rather than adjusting their own giving.

This player is a “strategic freerider with prosocial reinforcement.” They initially cooperate fully, then rapidly switch to full defection while continuing to benefit from group cooperation. Their contribution pattern is extreme and binary rather than gradual, suggesting low concern for fine-tuned fairness and a willingness to accept being a net beneficiary of others’ generosity. Once they switch away from contributing, they maintain that stance consistently, indicating a stable decision rather than round‑to‑round experimentation. Their choices imply that short‑term personal payoff dominates over strict reciprocity norms. They appear comfortable exploiting a cooperative environment as long as the group continues to contribute. Whether they care about long‑run group efficiency beyond the current game is unknown. Their risk tolerance regarding social sanctions is moderate: they freeride despite evidence that others are willing to punish, suggesting they either discount the probability or impact of being targeted, or they believe their prosocial rewarding behavior offsets negative perceptions.

This player fits a “steadfast high contributor” type. They always contribute the maximum available amount and show no experimentation or adjustment over time. Their behavior indicates that they prioritize maximizing group efficiency over individual short-term gain: given the game’s parameters, full contribution is socially efficient but individually risky if others defect, yet they never deviate. They appear insensitive to moderate variation in others’ behavior. Even when some players free‑ride or reduce their contributions, this player maintains full contribution, suggesting that their norm for “appropriate” behavior is unconditional high cooperation rather than conditional reciprocity. There is no evidence that they condition on the exact number or identity of defectors; their contributions are stable despite changes in who withholds. Risk tolerance seems relatively high: they are willing to expose themselves to exploitation by low contributors and to possible punishment, yet they do not hedge by reducing contributions. Their fairness norm appears to be that everyone should contribute fully, but their own behavior is not contingent on that norm being respected by others. Their strategy is simple, consistent, and predictable: always cooperate fully, independent of short-term signals. Motivationally, they seem either strongly pro‑social or strongly rule‑oriented: they behave as if full contribution is the “right thing,” and they stick to it. Whether this stems from altruism, norm compliance, or a focus on long-run collective payoff is unknown, but their lack of adjustment suggests internalized principles more than outcome-based learning.

Type label: “Conditional high contributor with occasional opportunism” The player generally aims to contribute fully when others appear broadly cooperative. Their default in a cooperative environment is to match or exceed the apparent norm rather than exploit it. They tolerate the presence of a few low contributors as long as overall cooperation remains high and stable. They do, however, experiment with free-riding at least once despite ongoing high contributions by others. This suggests that their cooperation is not purely unconditional or purely norm-driven; they are willing to test whether they can profit from others’ cooperation without immediately undermining the group outcome. This opportunistic deviation is isolated and not followed up with systematic defection, indicating low to moderate commitment to cooperative norms rather than strict principled cooperation. They appear comfortable with risk regarding social judgment: they defect once in a context where punishers are active, yet do not retaliate when they themselves are punished. This indicates either a willingness to accept sanctions as a cost of strategy testing, or low concern about others’ evaluations when the personal payoff is attractive. They exhibit no clear pattern of conditional cooperation in the sense of finely matching their contribution to specific others; instead, they adopt coarse strategies (all or nothing), consistent with the binary choice structure. Their behavior fits a “mostly cooperative but willing to probe the boundaries” profile rather than a steadfast altruist or a consistent free-rider. Beliefs about others’ cooperativeness are inferred as moderately optimistic: they repeatedly act as if a cooperative social environment will persist, and after testing defection once, they return to high contribution even though some players continue to free-ride. They do not visibly escalate or withdraw in response to the small number of persistent defectors.

This player is a *strict, stable full cooperator*. They always contribute their entire endowment, showing no experimentation and no apparent sensitivity to others’ occasional low contributions. Their behavior is consistent with a strong internal norm of “maximal cooperation” rather than outcome-based adjustment. They do not appear to condition their contribution on group averages, on the presence of free riders, or on prior punishment/reward received. This suggests a rule-based or norm-following strategy: they decide that full contribution is the “correct” action and stick to it. Risk tolerance appears moderate to high: they accept exposure to exploitation by lower contributors without hedging. Their fairness notion seems oriented to “doing their part” rather than enforcing proportionality or reciprocity in contributions, since they never reduce their own contribution in response to others contributing less.

The player is a strict, unconditional full cooperator. They always allocate their entire endowment to the public fund and never experiment with withholding. This pattern holds regardless of variation in others’ cooperation levels, indicating that their cooperation is not conditional on short-term reciprocity or on immediate fairness from others. They appear comfortable accepting the individual cost of cooperation when the institutional return from the public fund is high, suggesting they understand and prioritize the efficiency gains from contribution. They tolerate the presence of clear free riders and partial contributors without adjusting their own behavior, implying either a strong internal norm for contributing or a strategic commitment to sustaining group efficiency even when others deviate. Risk tolerance is relatively high: they continue to cooperate fully without observable assurance that others will maintain high contributions and without visible knowledge of end-of-round outcomes for others.

This player is a stable, moderate cooperator who anchors on a fixed, “reasonable” contribution and rarely deviates. They consistently contribute about half their endowment, regardless of others’ fluctuations, indicating a rule-based rather than highly reactive strategy. They do not systematically match the group average, nor do they escalate downward in response to free riding. Only once do they substantially increase their contribution toward a near-full amount, suggesting a momentary experiment with stronger cooperation or a response to temporarily high group contributions, but they immediately revert to their usual level afterwards. This pattern implies a personal fairness norm centered on “doing my part” rather than optimizing group efficiency or punishing free riders. Risk tolerance for being exploited by low contributors appears moderate: they tolerate clear free riding without reducing their own contribution to very low levels. Adaptation over time is limited; they maintain a near-constant contribution profile even as others cycle between high and low cooperation.

This player is a consistent, moderately high contributor with occasional full cooperation. Their contributions are usually at or slightly above the group average, but not maximally altruistic in a stable way. At multiple points they contribute their full endowment, indicating a willingness to prioritize group welfare, yet they also choose mid-range contributions that preserve a comfortable private payoff. Contribution levels fluctuate in response to others’ visible behavior: when partners contribute more, the player sometimes increases to full contribution; when others contribute little or free-ride, the player reduces back toward a moderate level rather than persisting at a high sacrifice. They appear to value fairness and mutual effort but not at any cost; they do not push a strict “always equal or above others” rule, nor do they clearly condition contributions on any one individual’s behavior. They tolerate some free-riding and asymmetry without sharp withdrawal. Risk tolerance seems moderate: they repeatedly risk being the highest contributor in the group, but they do not lock into that stance when it is not reciprocated.

This player is a mostly-high, somewhat erratic cooperator who quickly converges to full cooperation once they understand both the interface and the social norm. They begin by matching the stated “all in” cooperative plan and then alternate between full free-riding and full contribution a few times. The pattern suggests initial confusion or experimentation rather than a stable selfish strategy: the free-riding events are interspersed with sincere verbal endorsements of always contributing the maximum and are followed by long stretches of consistent full contributions. Once the group norm of contributing the maximum is clearly established and the interface misunderstanding is resolved, the player settles into stable, unconditional full contributions and maintains them through the end of the game, even when a few others briefly reduce their contributions. They show no evidence of conditional cooperation based on others’ exact amounts; instead they appear to adopt a simple rule: “always max for best group payoff.” Risk tolerance is moderate to high in the sense that they are willing to continue fully contributing even after earlier rounds gave them an opportunity to profit by deviating. Fairness norms appear to favor egalitarian, group-maximizing outcomes rather than individual advantage.

This player is a highly cooperative, near-“always-max” contributor once the group norm is clear. They start the game fully cooperative, briefly deviate to a low contribution in one early round despite group pro-cooperation talk, then quickly lock into full contributions for essentially all remaining rounds, including the final one. The deviation does not appear to be reactive punishment or exploitation of slackers, because it occurs when several others also deviate and there is confusion about the interface and rules; their behavior thereafter is stable and aligned with the cooperative norm even when some others temporarily under-contribute. They do not try to fine-tune their own payoff by conditionally adjusting contributions round-to-round in response to others’ exact amounts; instead, they adopt a simple, stable strategy: contribute the maximum as the default. This suggests that they understand that, with a high multiplier, mutual full contribution is efficient and are willing to follow that even when there is some noise. They show no signs of risk-averse “testing” of lower contributions once cooperation is established, and they do not reduce contributions near the end despite knowing the horizon, indicating resistance to end-game unraveling. Their fairness norm appears to be group-efficiency oriented rather than focused on equalizing payoffs via own deviations; they tolerate temporary free-riding or mistakes by others without retaliating through lower contributions.

This player is a strongly cooperative, mostly unconditional high contributor once the basic incentive structure is clear. They initially match the apparent group norm of full contribution, then briefly experiment with pure free-riding despite ongoing pro‑cooperation chat, and then revert to a near‑always‑max strategy for the rest of the game. After that one deviation and one smaller contribution, they consistently choose the socially efficient contribution level even when others occasionally shirk. They do not visibly condition their later contributions on individual others’ behavior or on the presence/absence of rewards; instead, they appear to lock in a simple, payoff‑dominant heuristic: “always contribute the full amount” (with one later, minor exception). They accept that this sometimes yields lower immediate payoff when a few others free‑ride, suggesting moderate tolerance for inequity as long as the group scheme is mostly intact. Risk-wise, they are willing to expose themselves to exploitation by low contributors for the sake of maintaining a high-output equilibrium, once they have evidence that most players will coordinate on full contribution. Their contributions are stable in the mid‑ and late game, with very low volatility after early experimentation.

This player is a strict, unwavering full cooperator. They always invest their entire endowment into the public good and never deviate, regardless of how others behave or how their own payoffs compare to freeriders. Their behavior is extremely stable: contribution decisions show no volatility and no apparent experimentation, even as group contribution levels fluctuate and as defectors consistently earn more in many instances. They appear to follow an unconditional cooperation rule rather than a contingent or tit-for-tat style. There is no observable sensitivity to changes in the average contribution of others, to shifts in the balance of cooperators vs. freeriders, or to repeated evidence that contributing yields them a lower personal payoff than defecting would. This suggests that payoff-maximization is not their primary decision rule, at least in this context. They accept a payoff structure where freeriders often earn more than they do, continuing to contribute fully. This indicates a willingness to tolerate exploitation and an apparent prioritization of collective efficiency over their own relative earnings. They behave as if they endorse a norm that “everyone should contribute fully,” but there is no indication that they condition their own behavior on whether that norm is met. Their risk tolerance appears high in social terms: they repeatedly expose themselves to the risk of being taken advantage of without ever hedging by defecting. Overall, they fit a “principled cooperator” type: someone who acts in line with a fixed cooperative principle, rather than adjusting strategically to others’ contributions or to changing incentives. Whether this principle is driven by moral preference, intrinsic satisfaction from cooperating, or a misunderstanding of the payoff structure is unknown.

This player is a quickly-learning, stable free-rider with an initial cooperative probe. They start by contributing fully once, then immediately and consistently switch to never contributing again. There is no further experimentation: their pattern is binary and stable (all, then all-zero). Their behavior shows that they understand the payoff structure and act instrumentally. Once they observe that others are willing to contribute at a substantial level, they exploit this by withholding their own contributions while continuing to benefit from the public pool. They do not condition their contributions positively on others’ cooperation; cooperation by others leads them to defect more confidently, not less. They tolerate and even rely on inequity in their favor. They repeatedly choose the option that yields them higher payoffs than the cooperators while keeping group efficiency below what it could be. There is no sign of concern for equalizing outcomes or matching others’ efforts. Risk tolerance appears selective. They are willing to take a one-time strategic risk by contributing at the start, likely to gauge how cooperative the group is, then they avoid further “risk” of being the sucker. Once convinced that others keep contributing even when they free-ride, they lock into the safer, high-payoff selfish strategy. They display no reciprocity: others’ continued contributions, even when they are a clear outlier non-contributor, do not trigger any increase in their own contributions. Their type fits a “strategic egoist with an exploratory first move”: they test the waters with cooperation, but if exploitation is profitable and unpunished, they persistently free-ride.

This player is a near-unconditional full cooperator who eventually experiments with free-riding once, late in the game. They repeatedly choose the maximum contribution even when many others free-ride and even when this leaves them systematically earning less than low contributors. Their behavior is highly stable over most of the game, with a single deviation at the end. They follow a simple, coarse strategy: contribute fully as a default, independent of short-run payoff disadvantages, and maintain that choice across changing contribution patterns of others. There is no clear evidence of gradual conditional cooperation or tit-for-tat style adaptation; the player does not scale their contribution up and down in response to marginal changes in group behavior. Instead, they keep contributing fully across stretches where the environment alternates between relatively cooperative and relatively uncooperative. The late switch to non-contribution indicates they are not a rigid pure altruist: they are willing to try defecting after sustained periods of earning less than many free-riders. This suggests they notice and care about payoff comparisons and are at least somewhat payoff-sensitive. However, this shift occurs only once and only after many cooperative choices, implying a high tolerance for disadvantage before they act on self-interest. Fairness and efficiency norms appear to influence them strongly. Their long streak of full contributions, despite others’ repeated free-riding, is consistent with a robust internal standard that “everyone contributing” is the appropriate behavior and that contributing is worthwhile because the group return per contributed coin is high. They behave like someone who sees full contribution as the default “correct” action and only reluctantly tests free-riding when that norm is repeatedly violated by others. Risk tolerance appears moderate-to-high in social terms: they are willing to expose themselves to exploitation risk by persistent full cooperation in a mixed group. They do not hedge (for example, by alternating between contributing and withholding) and instead commit to a clear cooperative stance. The single late defection suggests they may switch to more self-protective behavior under continued evidence that the group will not align with their cooperative norm, but the transcript is too short to know if that switch would persist in future rounds (unknown). Overall, this player fits a “stubborn cooperator with late, tentative defection” type: they primarily aim for group efficiency and normatively “right” behavior, and only secondarily adjust toward personal payoff protection, and even then, minimally.

This player is best characterized as a *calculated, mostly free-riding conditional cooperator with occasional experimental giving*. Their default is to keep the entire endowment and benefit from the group’s contributions. They very rarely deviate from this pattern; when they do contribute, it appears deliberate rather than random. They display a clear preference for strategies that yield high personal payoffs with minimal personal sacrifice. The dominant pattern is non-contribution even when others are contributing substantial amounts, indicating that maximizing individual gain outweighs concerns about equality or group efficiency. Their few contributions occur when the group is already contributing at relatively high levels and/or when multiple peers are giving. This suggests weak conditional cooperation: they are willing to contribute when they expect the public good to be well funded by others, but they do not maintain a cooperative stance consistently, even in similar group environments. This inconsistency implies either ongoing experimentation with payoffs or opportunistic “appearances” of cooperation rather than a stable fairness norm. In fairness terms, they tolerate and actively exploit outcome asymmetries in their favor. They repeatedly accept earning more than many cooperators without adjusting toward more equitable contributions. There is no sign that they are a strong inequality-averse or reciprocity-driven type; instead, they appear comfortable with advantageous inequality. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they do not contribute in a way that gambles on others suddenly increasing cooperation. When they do contribute, it is in situations where overall contribution levels are already robust and the risk of “wasting” a contribution on a poorly funded public good is relatively low. They avoid aggressive shifts or large cooperative gambles; their behavioral changes are sparse, not volatile. Overall, their contribution profile is stable in its free-rider orientation with rare, strategically placed cooperative acts. These acts look more like testing or conditional signaling than a commitment to sustained mutual cooperation.

This player is a predominantly non-contributing free-rider with rare, isolated cooperation. They consistently keep their entire endowment in most rounds, despite others frequently contributing. Their baseline is to prioritize individual payoff over group efficiency, even when the environment is relatively cooperative and contributions remain high. Their contributions are binary and switch only a couple of times, with no clear increasing or decreasing trend, indicating low sensitivity to gradual patterns and no sustained shift toward cooperation. The cooperative choices appear sporadic rather than part of a stable rule like “match the group” or “reward cooperators.” They do not systematically reciprocate high group contributions, nor do they clearly exploit downturns in contributions; instead, they maintain a free-riding default with occasional deviations. They display low adherence to fairness norms: they repeatedly accept earning more than consistent cooperators and do not appear to move toward equalizing payoffs. Their few contributions do not persist across subsequent rounds, suggesting they are not anchored by guilt, inequality aversion, or a strong fairness principle. Risk preferences are hard to infer with confidence because the action space is discrete and symmetric, but their willingness to contribute only occasionally in a largely cooperative group suggests they may be experimenting or checking payoff consequences rather than avoiding risk; confidence on this interpretation is low. Overall, their stable type is: “profit-oriented, low-commitment cooperator,” whose primary tendency is defection/free-riding, with limited, non-sustained cooperative episodes that do not reshape their long-run behavior.

This player is a predominantly full cooperator with one notable free-riding deviation. They almost always contribute their entire endowment despite facing a relatively low marginal per-capita return and a stable presence of free riders. The single instance of non-contribution appears exploratory or opportunistic rather than the start of a defection cascade: they quickly return to full contribution afterward and maintain it to the end. This suggests a baseline preference for cooperation that is resilient to short-term exploitation. Their behavior indicates a weak form of conditionality. They do not closely track or mirror the average contribution level; they continue to cooperate even when non-contributors repeatedly earn more. The one free-riding move occurs after experiencing sustained exploitation and may reflect a test of how much the group can be exploited, or a response to frustration. However, they do not continue this strategy even when it is clearly profitable, implying that maximizing relative payoff is not their primary objective. They appear tolerant of inequality and willing to accept earning less than systematic free riders. They act as if the cooperative outcome is intrinsically or normatively desirable, even without enforcement mechanisms. At the same time, the isolated defection shows they are not a rigid unconditional cooperator: they are aware of the incentive structure and are capable of self-serving choices when pushed. Stability is high overall: a clear cooperative pattern with a single, contained deviation. They display moderate risk tolerance regarding being exploited; they repeatedly incur personal cost to support the public good, despite clear evidence that others can and do free ride with impunity. In new PGG environments, this type tends to start and remain cooperative, occasionally probing defection if exploitation feels persistent, but generally reverting to contribution.

The player is a perfectly stable, unconditional full cooperator in this setting. They always choose the maximal contribution available, without any observable adjustment to changing group behavior or payoffs. Their pattern indicates that they prioritize group efficiency over individual payoff, even when others systematically free-ride and when it is clear that contributing yields them strictly lower individual earnings than defecting. They do not display conditional cooperation: their contributions do not track the average contributions of others, nor do they react to declines in group cooperation. This suggests that either (a) they adhere to a strong internal norm of always contributing, or (b) they follow a simple, pre-committed strategy not updated by experience. There is no evidence that they experiment with defection or mixed strategies. They tolerate being exploited by free riders without changing their own behavior. This points toward a fairness or efficiency preference that is not implemented in a reciprocal way—if they care about fairness, they do not use their contribution decision to enforce it. Instead, they behave like a pure cooperator in a one-shot sense, every round. In terms of risk, they accept the “risk” of systematically lower individual payoffs to maintain maximum public good provision. Given the parameters of the game, full contribution is individually irrational but socially optimal, so their behavior is consistent with strong pro-social or rule-following tendencies and low sensitivity to short-term material losses relative to internal principles.

This player is primarily a deliberate free‑rider who occasionally switches to over‑contribution, seemingly for amusement or to respond to social pressure rather than to maximize earnings. Early on, they consistently keep their entire endowment while the group coordinates on full contributions, even after the norm is clearly established and explained, which indicates conscious exploitation of the public good rather than confusion. They openly acknowledge not donating and do not show guilt; instead they lean into the role and sometimes try to recruit others to stop contributing. At several points they abruptly move to partial or full contributions, including some rounds where they contribute more than the group’s modal level. These spikes do not follow a clear payoff logic; they appear tied to boredom, mock-cooperation, or momentary engagement with the group rather than a consistent conditional cooperation rule. They show no stable fairness norm: they are comfortable benefiting from others’ generosity without matching it, and when they do contribute substantially it is not sustained. Their risk tolerance with respect to payoff volatility is high: they are willing to sacrifice large amounts of their own payoff (even going very negative in some rounds due to rewards) for idiosyncratic reasons, implying low concern for monetary optimization and high willingness to “play with” the mechanism.

This player is a near-pure, high, stable cooperator. They almost always give the full endowment and only briefly reduce their contribution once when the group’s cooperation level dips. That deviation is modest and immediately reversed, suggesting they treat full contribution as the default norm and only tentatively experiment with lowering it when others pull back. They do not condition their behavior strongly on individual free-riders; one persistently low contributor and occasional partial contributors do not lead them to sustained defection. They seem to prioritize group efficiency and shared payoff over individual short-term gain, and they explicitly value more total earnings over ending the game quickly. They appear comfortable with some exploitation risk: they keep contributing fully despite visible non-contributors and despite having the information needed to chase higher personal profit by reducing their own contributions. Their behavior reflects a fairness norm focused on “everyone should cooperate” rather than “I match each person’s level.” When the group is coordinated and cooperative, they are enthusiastic and supportive; when cooperation frays, they express mild frustration but still behave pro-socially.

This player is a mixed, somewhat erratic conditional cooperator with a clear free-riding streak. Early on, they explicitly state an intention to increase their contribution but initially keep their own endowment, benefiting from a highly generous group. After that, they often match or nearly match the emerging “full contribution” norm, but they periodically revert to zero or partial contributions without clear public justification. They do not appear strongly principled about always contributing; instead, they oscillate between cooperative and self-interested behavior. Their giving is not tightly conditioned on specific individuals’ actions, as they keep contributing even when one identified low contributor is being criticized, and they continue to cooperate while others complain about the game length. At times they apologize for “messing up,” which suggests they recognize their deviations from group norms as suboptimal or socially undesirable, but this does not prevent later free-riding. Overall, they value payoff and are willing to exploit a high-contribution environment, but they also repeatedly participate in sustaining that environment rather than purely maximizing short-term personal gain.

This player is a highly stable, near-unconditional high contributor. They almost always give the full endowment and maintain this even when a known free-rider is present and when others briefly reduce their own contributions. The single deviation to a lower contribution is isolated and not followed up with any explicit explanation or sustained change, suggesting either a brief experiment, inattention, or momentary frustration rather than a strategic pivot. They clearly understand that full contribution is efficient and repeatedly endorse “team” framing, indicating an internalized group-benefit norm rather than narrow self-interest. They tolerate some inequity (others giving zero or less) without retaliating by reducing their own behavior over time. This pattern points to a “principled cooperator” type: they anchor on the cooperative norm and largely decouple their own contributions from short-term exploitation risks.

This player is a steadfast full cooperator. They always contribute the entire endowment and never deviate, even when others free‑ride or start lowering their contributions. Their behavior suggests they understand that maximizing total coins requires full group cooperation and that their own long‑run payoff benefits from maintaining that norm. They do not condition their contribution on specific others’ behavior; they keep contributing fully whether a known low contributor is present or not, and whether the group average is high or slipping. They actively endorse and reinforce the “everyone contributes the maximum” norm in chat, present it as the obvious baseline, and treat deviations as a problem with particular individuals rather than a reason to reduce their own giving. They appear relatively risk‑tolerant in that they keep investing in the public good despite uncertainty about remaining rounds and despite the visible existence of free‑riders. Fairness for them seems to mean “everyone should contribute fully so that the public pot is maximized,” rather than tit‑for‑tat matching or punishing defectors by reducing their own contribution.

This player is a near-pure unconditional cooperator. They almost always contribute the full endowment, deviating only once, and even then still giving more than the group average. Their default assumption appears to be that full cooperation is both normatively appropriate and payoff-relevant, and they maintain this behavior even when a persistent free-rider and some partial contributors are visible. They do not visibly condition their contributions on others’ previous behavior, nor do they respond to fluctuations in group cooperation by scaling their own giving up or down in a systematic way. When they briefly reduce their contribution, it coincides with visible group fatigue and more mixed contributions, suggesting momentary experimentation or impatience rather than a strategic shift away from cooperation. Overall, they prioritize maintaining a high group contribution level over individual earnings, showing low sensitivity to exploitation and a strong preference for efficiency and group welfare.

This player is a conditional, alternating cooperator with a strong norm for full contributions but a willingness to free‑ride intermittently. They are clearly comfortable both fully cooperating and fully defecting rather than using intermediate or “hedging” levels when the option set is binary. Their cooperation is not purely altruistic: they sometimes exploit a cooperative group by withholding their own contribution while others continue to give. At the same time, they repeatedly return to full contribution, indicating that they value group efficiency and do not settle into persistent defection even after being punished. Their pattern suggests a mix of norm compliance and opportunism: they appear to treat full contribution as the default “right” behavior but occasionally test or exploit the environment by defecting when they may feel safe or uncertain about others. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to risk punishment and social sanctions by defecting, but they also invest in costly punishment to uphold norms, which is itself risky in payoff terms. There is no clear evidence of systematic fairness balancing over time beyond a basic expectation that everyone should contribute the maximum.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator within the binary-choice environment. They always choose the cooperative option despite observable variation in others’ behavior, including clear instances of non-contribution by several peers. Their cooperation does not appear contingent on immediate reciprocity, on majority behavior, or on strict equality of contributions. They tolerate the presence of free riders without adjusting their own action, suggesting either a strong internal norm for contributing, a preference for socially efficient outcomes regardless of others, or a desire to avoid complicating their strategy. Risk tolerance is moderate to high: they consistently expose themselves to exploitation risk but also reliably secure the high-return cooperative outcome when others contribute. There is no observable evidence of strategic experimentation or conditional cooperation; their pattern is consistent and inflexible across the interaction. Their fairness norm is unknown beyond a willingness to accept some inequality in order to preserve public-good provision.

This player is a mostly-cooperative but strategically inconsistent contributor who alternates between full cooperation and full defection. They use only the extreme options available rather than intermediate strategies (when constrained to 0 vs 20, they choose one or the other rather than mixing over time in a stable pattern). Their behavior shows that they are not a pure free-rider: they are willing to forgo private payoff and contribute fully to the public good in most rounds. At the same time, they sometimes free-ride even when others are consistently cooperative, indicating that maximizing personal payoff remains an important motive. Their contribution pattern suggests conditionality, but in a non-straightforward way. They do not simply match others’ cooperation; they defect at times when others appear highly cooperative and cooperate even when some others defect. This implies that they are not a naive conditional cooperator who just “follows the crowd.” Instead, they appear to be experimenting with when to free-ride and when to contribute, possibly testing how much they can exploit group cooperation while still maintaining overall gains and/or group stability. The alternation between cooperation and defection suggests they are consciously balancing self-interest and some concern for collective outcomes, rather than committing to one extreme. In terms of fairness norms, they seem to tolerate substantial inequality and personal disadvantage without visible adjustment in punishment or reward. Being heavily targeted by others (as implied by large deductions in some cooperative-context defections) does not lead them to retaliate via the sanctioning mechanism or to permanently switch into purely cooperative or purely selfish behavior. This resilience hints at either low concern for procedural fairness (they accept being sanctioned as a consequence of defection) or a pragmatic focus on outcomes rather than emotional responses to being treated harshly. Overall, they show moderate risk tolerance: they are willing to defect even when the environment is highly cooperative and punishment is powerful, which is strategically risky. Yet they also repeatedly choose full contribution when defection would be individually profitable, showing a willingness to bear short-term cost to participate in the group production process. Their type is best described as a “payoff-conscious, experimenting cooperator” rather than a firm altruist or a strict egoist.

The player is a stable, unconditional high contributor in this environment. They always choose the cooperative option, even when others visibly reduce their own contributions and even when the game is close to ending. Their behavior is consistent with a strong norm of full cooperation rather than conditional cooperation or tit‑for‑tat responses. They appear willing to accept personal payoff risk to sustain public-good provision, indicating relatively low concern for short-term individual profit maximization when it conflicts with group efficiency. They do not visibly adjust contributions in response to moderate or repeated deviations by others. This suggests either a principled commitment to full cooperation, a belief that overall group cooperation will remain high despite some free-riding, or a preference for avoiding the strategic complexity of conditional contribution. Their pattern also indicates tolerance for inequality in payoffs when it arises from others contributing less than they do. Overall, they fit a “steadfast cooperator” type: they prioritize maintaining their own cooperative standard over adapting to others’ opportunism, at least within the time frame and payoff conditions observed. Their fairness norm is oriented more toward “I do my part” than “I match what others do.”

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator under the given binary choice. They consistently choose the cooperative option despite variation in others’ behavior and despite the availability of profitable free-riding. Their strategy indicates a strong efficiency norm: they prioritize maximizing the group return from the public good over private payoff, even when some others defect. They do not visibly condition their contribution on others’ past actions, suggesting a “principled cooperator” rather than a conditional cooperator. Fairness concerns, if present, do not translate into reduced giving when others contribute less; they tolerate some inequity without adjusting downward. They appear risk-tolerant with respect to possible exploitation, behaving as if cooperation is the default regardless of whether others reliably match it. Adaptation across the game is minimal; they maintain the same cooperative choice throughout without experimentation or strategic timing.

This player is a stable, unconditional full contributor. They always give their entire endowment, regardless of others’ behavior or the game stage. Their strategy does not adjust downward when free riding appears, nor upward in response to higher cooperation; instead, they hold a fixed norm of full cooperation. They appear to follow an efficiency-oriented norm: they act as if maximizing group output is the correct behavior, even when others do not perfectly match it. There is no evidence of risk-sensitive hedging or strategic experimentation; they accept the individual cost of contributing in the face of imperfect group cooperation. They show no signs of conditional cooperation (e.g., tit-for-tat style adjustment), suggesting they treat contributing fully as a rule-based or principled action rather than a contingent one. Attitude toward fairness is partially inferred: they punish a clear free rider once, which suggests they value proportional contribution and see stark deviations from the group norm as norm violations, but they still keep contributing fully even when some others contribute less than them.

This player is a stable, unconditional full contributor. They always choose the cooperative option even when some others free ride and even when cooperation levels drop. Their behavior suggests they prioritize maximizing group welfare over short-term individual gain and do not appear to condition their own contributions on others’ deviations. They tolerate the risk of being exploited and accept inequity without adjusting downward, implying either a strong fairness/cooperation norm or a simple heuristic of “always contribute” that they do not revise. They do not display opportunism or volatility and seem insensitive to moderate changes in others’ average contribution so long as cooperation remains broadly present.

The player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator in this environment. They always give the maximum allowed amount, with no variation and no apparent sensitivity to others’ occasional defection or fluctuation. They appear to treat full cooperation as a norm or default rather than as a contingent response to group behavior. Their willingness to keep contributing fully even when some others reduce contributions suggests a strong internal preference for cooperation or norm-following, outweighing short-term payoff concerns. There is no evidence that they attempt to fine‑tune contributions for strategic gain, so their risk tolerance regarding possible exploitation is high or, at minimum, not behaviorally constrained by fear of free-riding. Their fairness norm seems to be “everyone should fully contribute,” but they do not adjust contributions to enforce this.

This player is a mostly high contributor with occasional opportunistic free-riding. After an initial defection, they very quickly align with the group’s cooperative norm and then almost always contribute fully. They defect only when they see that full cooperation is firmly established and others are reliably contributing; in those moments, they take the sure gain from free-riding while still benefiting from the group’s provision. Their pattern suggests they understand the payoff structure well enough to see that mutual cooperation is very profitable, but individual deviation can be even more profitable when others remain cooperative. They do not appear to condition their contributions on specific individuals’ behavior; when one teammate begins to shirk, they continue to contribute fully instead of retaliating by reducing their own contribution. This indicates a preference for maintaining group efficiency over enforcing strict reciprocity through their own contribution decisions. Across the game, their contribution behavior is relatively stable: once they “lock in” to the cooperative frame, they only rarely test defection and otherwise act as a consistent cooperator. They appear comfortable accepting some inequality: they allow one player to free-ride without mirroring that behavior, and they themselves occasionally exploit the group’s cooperation. There is no clear sign that fairness considerations dominate their decisions; instead, they balance personal gain and group gain, with a slight tilt toward exploiting “free money” opportunities when others are reliably cooperative. Risk tolerance is moderate: they do not repeatedly defect when there is any serious chance the group might unravel; instead, they defect only when cooperation seems safe and entrenched.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator across the entire game. They always contribute their entire endowment to the public good, regardless of others’ behavior, apparent free-riding, or uncertainty about how many rounds remain. They do not appear to condition contributions on past deviations by specific players: even when a peer repeatedly withholds contributions or when some players intermittently defect, this player continues to give the maximum. Their behavior suggests either a strong internal norm of “always cooperate when cooperation is obviously efficient” or a simple heuristic of always contributing when the marginal return to the group is high. They show no evidence of experimenting with defection, even late in the game when others are still occasionally free-riding and when some players explicitly discuss fatigue and unknown game length. They appear comfortable taking on personal risk in the sense of trusting the group, but this is not tested by strong group collapse; cooperation stays high in aggregate. Their fairness standard seems group-focused: they support a cooperative norm for everyone but do not adjust their own contributions to punish or mirror defectors.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment, never deviating in response to others’ free-riding or changes in the group pattern. Their behavior fits a strong efficiency norm: they act as if maximizing the group’s total payoff is the right thing to do, regardless of short‑term personal advantage. They do not display conditional cooperation in the usual sense; they continue to fully contribute even when one or two group members repeatedly keep everything or when a previously cooperative member starts freeriding. There is no sign of strategic hedging near perceived endpoints; they keep contributing fully even when other players speculate that the game is close to ending. This suggests either a principled commitment to cooperation or a willingness to sacrifice some earnings for normative consistency. Risk tolerance appears high with respect to exploitation: they accept being taken advantage of by defectors rather than adjusting their own contribution.

This player is a strongly cooperative “full-contributor with one early deviation.” They quickly infer that maximum contribution is overwhelmingly profitable and socially efficient and mostly commit to contributing the full endowment every round. The only exception is an early round where they contribute roughly half of their endowment while many others also contribute less than the verbal “20” norm. After this, they lock into contributing the maximum consistently, including when a few others slip or appear confused. Their cooperation is not fragile: they keep contributing fully even when some players give 0 or lower amounts, and even when they themselves pay a small punishment cost once. They seem to anchor on the group’s “20 club” coordination rather than respond myopically to single defections. They repeatedly endorse “20 always” in chat and continue this strategy even late in the game without visible end effects, suggesting a stable prosocial or payoff-maximizing heuristic rather than strategic endgame defection. They express understanding that the public good multiplier makes full contribution “free money.” They do not demand strict fairness in the sense of matching contributions to others; instead they tolerate occasional undercontribution, interpreting some anomalies as possible game artifacts rather than betrayals. Risk tolerance appears medium–high: they are willing to rely on others’ cooperation and maintain full contributions even under some noise and unexplained punishments.

This player is a stable, near-unconditional high contributor who quickly aligns with full cooperation and then sticks to it. They start slightly above suggested amounts and immediately test higher-than-discussed contributions, then move to always giving the full endowment once the group norm solidifies. After a single moderate under-contribution early on (still above group average), they consistently contribute the maximum feasible amount in every subsequent round, independent of occasional low contributions by others or apparent “glitches.” They appears to understand that with a high multiplier, maximizing group contributions is individually profitable, and they behave as if “20 from everyone” is the obvious equilibrium. Their contributions are not tit-for-tat or reactive; they do not reduce their own giving when others defect or when punishment noise appears. This suggests they prioritize maintaining the cooperative norm and trust that others will either continue or return to it. They tolerate free-riding and noise without changing behavior, indicating low sensitivity to short-term unfairness as long as the cooperative structure broadly holds. Risk tolerance is high regarding exploitation: they keep contributing fully even when some players give 0 or less than the norm, relying on the aggregate group behavior and the strong payoff multiplier rather than protecting themselves by holding back.

This player is a strict, unconditional full cooperator under the available action set.
