They do not exhibit reciprocity or any visible fairness norm; they benefit from others’ generosity without adjusting their own behavior. Risk tolerance in this setting appears low with respect to sacrificing own payoff for collective benefit; they avoid the “risk” of giving up private coins even when the environment is clearly cooperative. Their pattern suggests they either hold a firm belief that free-riding is the rational strategy or they are indifferent to normative expectations of fairness and cooperation in the group. They do not show learning in the cooperative direction; instead, they maintain the same non-contributory strategy despite clear evidence that others are willing to invest heavily in the public good.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They always give the maximum allowed amount and never waver, even when some others contribute nothing or when there is visible heterogeneity in group behavior. Their strategy indicates that they prioritize the group’s efficiency payoff over their own short-term individual gain, consistent with a strong cooperative or efficiency-oriented motive rather than outcome-maximizing self-interest. They do not appear to condition their behavior on the specific contribution pattern of peers; they maintain maximal cooperation despite clear evidence of free riding. This suggests either a norm-based strategy (“I do what I think is right regardless of others”) or a belief that consistent high contributions are the best way to support or stabilize group cooperation. Their willingness to keep contributing fully in the presence of non-contributors indicates relatively high social risk tolerance: they accept being exploited by free riders in order to sustain collective benefits. They also show no sign of strategic adjustment or tit-for-tat behavior; their contributions are both high and rigid rather than reactive. Fairness concerns, if present, do not translate into reduced giving in response to others’ lower contributions. This could reflect a strong internal fairness or duty norm, a desire to avoid personal responsibility for efficiency losses, or a belief that the game’s structure makes full contribution the rational choice regardless of others’ actions. Exact underlying beliefs about others’ motives and future behavior are unknown.

The player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They always give the maximum possible amount to the public good, without visible adjustment to others’ deviations or to the time horizon. Their behavior is consistent with a strong fairness and cooperation norm: they treat “everyone contributes fully” as the obvious standard and do not appear to experiment with free-riding, even when others do so repeatedly and even in the final rounds. Risk tolerance in terms of payoff variance is relatively high; they accept certain personal cost (via punishment/reward spending) on top of full contributions, suggesting they prioritize norm enforcement and social preferences over strict payoff maximization.

This player is a strict, unconditional full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment in every round, without deviation, despite repeated free-riding by some others. Their behavior shows a clear belief that universal high contributions are both possible and desirable: they explicitly endorse that everyone going “all in” is the best outcome and that the group is “doing well” when contributions are high. They do not appear to condition their own contribution on others’ past defection; they keep contributing fully even after clear and repeated under-contribution by specific players. This suggests a preference structure where maintaining cooperative norms and maximizing the social surplus dominates concerns about short‑run exploitation or fairness adjustments. Risk tolerance regarding being exploited is high; they willingly accept exposure to systematic free-riding rather than reduce their own contributions. There is no evidence of strategic contribution tapering toward the end of the game; their cooperation remains stable, with no visible end-game decay.

This player is a calculated, opportunistic free-rider who oscillates between full cooperation and full defection depending on perceived punishment risk and group pressure. They clearly understand the payoff structure: they repeatedly take the entire endowment while others contribute fully, only increasing contributions when punishment against them becomes intense or is explicitly threatened. Their baseline preference is to contribute nothing and reap the benefits of others’ cooperation; intermediate contributions (e.g., partial amounts below the group norm) appear mainly in transitional phases when they are adjusting to recent punishment or anticipating more of it. They do not adhere to a fairness norm of equal contributions; instead, they tolerate being a clear outlier as long as the net payoff is acceptable. Contribution behavior is volatile and history-dependent: they switch repeatedly between zero and near-max contributions over the game, rather than settling into a stable pattern. They show moderate responsiveness to incentives: when punished heavily or explicitly targeted in chat, they temporarily shift to high contributions, but once the pressure eases, they often revert to taking more for themselves. They do not appear highly risk-averse; they are repeatedly willing to invite strong social sanctions for short-term gains and to test whether the group will follow through on threats.

This player is a conditional, oscillating contributor who mixes full cooperation with full free-riding rather than using intermediate levels. They alternate between contributing everything and nothing, and this pattern persists across the game rather than converging to a stable single mode. They are willing to contribute fully even when the group average is low, suggesting either an experimental or hopeful stance toward cooperation, but they are also comfortable exploiting situations where many others contribute while they do not. Their behavior shows no simple “tit-for-tat” rule; instead, they shift modes repeatedly even when the surrounding contribution pattern looks similar across episodes. They appear sensitive to payoff incentives: when non-contribution yields much higher payoffs, they are willing to free-ride, and when the environment is noisy or low-contributing, they sometimes still invest fully, perhaps to test whether cooperation can be sustained or to hedge between moral and monetary motives. Fairness norms are mixed: they sometimes act like a high contributor in a low-contributing group (accepting disadvantage), and at other times they act like a pure free-rider in a high-contributing environment (accepting advantage), so any stable equity preference is unknown. Risk tolerance is moderate to high: they repeatedly choose the dominated cooperative action in an environment where others often contribute little, but they also repeatedly take the safe selfish option when that is clearly profitable.

This player is a mostly high, somewhat stubborn cooperator with occasional strategic free-riding. They usually give the full endowment, even when others contribute very little and even after repeated punishment, indicating a strong cooperative baseline and low responsiveness to short-term payoff signals. They do sometimes choose zero contribution, but these instances do not escalate into a sustained defection phase; instead, they revert to full contributions. Their pattern suggests they value the cooperative norm or see contribution as the “default correct” action, rather than finely tuning to others’ behavior. They appear tolerant of inequality, continuing to cooperate despite being repeatedly targeted and despite others’ low or inconsistent contributions. Their occasional non-contribution looks more like brief experimentation or opportunism than a systematic conditional strategy. Risk tolerance is moderate: they persist in high contributions despite clear evidence that this exposes them to punishment and low payoffs, implying either low sensitivity to risk of exploitation or a preference for maintaining a stable cooperative identity over payoff safety.

This player fits a “predominantly selfish free-rider with occasional, unexplained cooperation” type. By default, they keep their entire endowment and exploit others’ contributions. This pattern is consistent and stable for most of the game, indicating a primary payoff-maximization motive under this low-return multiplier. They do not appear to condition their behavior on others’ cooperation in a standard reciprocity sense: they continue to free-ride even when most peers contribute and group provision is high, and they also free-ride when most peers defect. Their baseline is to contribute nothing regardless of the social environment. At several points, the player abruptly switches to full contribution. These cooperative bursts do not line up clearly with a change in others’ cooperation levels, nor with a consistent punishment pattern, so the triggers are unclear (possible reasons include curiosity about payoffs, momentary fairness concern, or random experimentation; confidence on any specific motive is low). When they do contribute, they do so fully rather than partially, suggesting a simple “all-or-nothing” heuristic rather than fine-grained calibration. Overall, they tolerate significant inequality in their favor for most of the game and do not display a strong fairness norm. They accept earning more than high contributors without compensating them nor matching their behavior. Risk tolerance regarding group collapse is moderately high: they are willing to let contributions drop to very low levels while still maintaining non-contribution themselves, absorbing the lower group returns rather than “investing” into restoring cooperation.

This player is a mixed-strategy, opportunistic contributor who alternates between full cooperation and full free-riding, rather than settling into a stable pattern. They use only the extreme options available and never choose an intermediate level (which is not available here), suggesting a preference for clear-cut strategic stances instead of fine-tuning. They show both phases of sustained contribution and phases of sustained free-riding, with multiple switches over the game. Their contributions are clearly conditional on the evolving environment, not on a fixed principle. They appear willing to contribute fully when others are contributing at moderate-to-high levels, but they also periodically test or exploit the group by free-riding even when others’ contributions are substantial. They do not consistently match the modal group behavior; at times they contribute when many defect, and at other times they defect while several others cooperate. There is no clear evidence that they are purely payoff-maximizing or purely fairness-driven. They tolerate outcomes where they contribute more than many peers and earn low immediate payoffs, but they also accept and even create highly unequal, self-favoring outcomes when they free-ride. That combination points to a player who is experimenting or balancing multiple motives (efficiency, fairness, and self-gain) rather than following a single coherent norm. Their willingness to keep contributing fully, even after repeated low personal returns in some cooperative phases, suggests at least some concern for group efficiency or norm enforcement rather than strict risk-averse profit maximization. At the same time, their recurring returns to free-riding show that they are comfortable exploiting cooperative others and are not bound by a strong fairness norm.

This player is a strategic, self-interested conditional cooperator with early free-riding and later alignment to group norms. Initially, they unambiguously defect despite clear cooperative signals from others, taking advantage of others’ contributions to secure a higher individual payoff. They then shift to consistent contributing once cooperation becomes the clear descriptive norm and once they have already benefited from others’ cooperation. Their contribution pattern suggests: - They are highly payoff-sensitive and willing to exploit unconditional cooperators when it is safe and anonymous. - They are not rigidly selfish; they are willing to contribute once the group is stably cooperative and they have already “locked in” early gains. - They appear relatively risk-averse to being the sole cooperator but fairly comfortable being a rare defector; they initially defect even when most others cooperate but later stop defecting once a couple of others visibly free-ride and tensions emerge. - They accept unequal outcomes in their favor without visible guilt but later seem willing to shoulder their “fair share” once cooperation is established and socially discussed. Overall, their strategy resembles: free-ride while others are strongly cooperative and forgiving, then converge toward cooperation when norms and explicit discussion make continued defection more salient or socially exposed.

The player is a consistent, unconditional full cooperator within the available action set. They always choose the maximal contribution, regardless of others’ behavior or prior outcomes. There is no observable volatility or conditional adjustment in response to free-riding or changes in group averages. Their reasoning appears efficiency-oriented: they explicitly frame contribution as the way for the group to obtain the highest possible monetary outcome. They show no sign of risk aversion about being exploited; they keep contributing fully even when others clearly benefit more from not contributing. Fairness concerns are present but expressed as a collective optimality norm (“everyone should contribute for maximum group gains”) rather than as a trigger for retaliation or withdrawal. They do not experiment with defection, suggesting either a strong internal norm for cooperation or a deliberate commitment to the cooperative strategy independent of short-term payoffs.

This player is an unconditional, full contributor. They always give the maximum possible amount to the public fund and never deviate, despite substantial variation in others’ behavior and in group efficiency. Their strategy is stable rather than exploratory; they do not experiment with lower contributions even when others free-ride or when group contributions drop. This suggests a strong internal norm for cooperation or a rule-based decision style that does not hinge on short-term payoffs. They tolerate the risk of exploitation and do not appear to condition their own behavior on others’ past actions, indicating low sensitivity to fairness violations in terms of contribution choices. Their behavior is consistent with a “principled cooperator” or “moral contributor” type who views contributing fully as the default, independent of others’ moves.

This player is a strongly pro-social, near-unconditional cooperator. They almost always contribute the full endowment and only very occasionally shade slightly below that. Their cooperation is not tightly conditional on others’ immediate contributions: they continue to give high amounts even when some players contribute zero or very little, and even after repeated low contributions from specific individuals. This indicates a stable internal norm for high contribution rather than a purely reciprocal strategy. They appear concerned with group efficiency and are willing to incur personal cost through punishment while maintaining high contributions, suggesting they value maintaining a cooperative environment more than maximizing their short-run private payoff. Occasional small reductions in contribution (rather than a drastic drop to free-riding) suggest a mild willingness to experiment or express disapproval via contribution level, but they do not pivot to exploitation even when others do. Risk tolerance with respect to being exploited is high; they accept being paired with persistent free riders while continuing to cooperate. Fairness norms seem focused on “everyone should contribute substantially” rather than strict equality, since they continue to cooperate fully alongside moderate contributors and only ever materially target extreme under-contributors through punishment rather than by reducing their own contributions.

This player fits a “punished free‑rider with opportunistic reciprocity” type. They frequently contribute nothing despite others’ high and stable cooperation, and they endure large repeated punishments without adjusting reliably toward full cooperation. Their contribution pattern is volatile rather than stable: they alternate between zero, partial, and full contributions with no simple monotonic trend. They do show some conditionality. After being heavily sanctioned for free‑riding, they sometimes respond by contributing partially or fully in subsequent rounds, suggesting they recognize others’ expectations and understand the contribution–punishment link. However, these cooperative responses are short‑lived; they revert to free‑riding even after very severe deductions. This points to either a strong preference for own payoff when not immediately under threat, or a tendency to “test” how much punishment they will actually receive rather than committing to a stable cooperative norm. They do not appear to anchor on fairness in the sense of matching others’ high contributions; even when others contribute near the maximum and the environment is strongly cooperative, they still choose zero relatively often. At the same time, they are willing to contribute fully in some periods, indicating they are not purely selfish automatons; rather, they selectively cooperate when it seems temporarily advantageous or when punishment has just been intense. Risk tolerance appears high: they repeatedly choose blatantly low contributions in a group that has already demonstrated a strong willingness to punish low contributors harshly. They seem willing to accept substantial variance and very low short‑run payoffs to explore or exploit free‑riding opportunities.

This player is a “steadfast cooperator with brief experimentation.” They usually contribute their entire endowment, treating high contribution as a default, not a fragile or tentative stance. Their pattern is largely stable: they do not fluctuate responsively from round to round in ways that mirror the group’s ups and downs. Instead, they maintain high cooperation even when others’ average contributions drop substantially. They do briefly test lower contributions midway through the game, reducing their input for a short stretch. This looks like a limited probe of whether they can free‑ride or whether others will sustain the public good without them. After this experiment, they quickly return to full contributions. That suggests they value consistent cooperation more than short‑term exploitation and that they are willing to absorb some disadvantage when others contribute less. They do not seem to enforce a strict fairness norm via their own contribution level: they keep contributing a lot even when several peers give substantially less. If anything, their behavior reflects a “moral anchor” style—using their own contribution as a stable cooperative signal independent of immediate reciprocity. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they accept the strategic risk and cost of being more generous than some others, relying on the overall group rather than tightly protecting themselves against free‑riding. Overall, they behave as a near unconditional high contributor with only brief, contained deviations, suggesting a cooperative identity and an interest in sustaining a high‑contribution environment even when it is not strictly payoff‑maximizing against the observed group behavior.

This player fits a “cautious conditional cooperator with self-regarding drift” profile. They generally treat intermediate giving as their default, repeatedly choosing a mid-range contribution rather than the extremes. They do sometimes move to full cooperation and sometimes to full free-riding, but those are exceptions around a stable moderate baseline. This suggests they value contributing, but are not willing to consistently sacrifice private payoff for maximum group benefit. Their behavior is clearly conditional on perceived group norms. When others appear highly cooperative on average, they are willing to increase their own contribution substantially, including fully matching the maximum possible contribution. When group contributions look weaker or more mixed, they retreat toward lower giving, including occasional zero contributions. This pattern indicates they monitor the group’s average behavior and adjust rather than following a fixed rule. They are sensitive to fairness in a pragmatic way rather than in a strict norm-enforcing sense. They do not insist on precise equality; instead, they appear content to contribute somewhere near what looks like a “reasonable” middle of the group distribution, even when some players give much more. They do not escalate their own giving simply to match the highest contributors, nor do they systematically punish low contributors by always dropping to the minimum when others slack; instead they move gradually, suggesting a moderate, “satisficing” fairness standard. They show medium risk tolerance. They are willing to invest fully in the public good at times, even when others have recently deviated from perfect cooperation, indicating they can take cooperative risks if the environment still looks generally pro-social. At the same time, they repeatedly pull back when cooperative norms weaken, which protects their individual payoff and shows they are not blindly altruistic. Volatility in their contributions is modest and patterned, not random. Their changes track shifts in the rough level of group cooperation rather than looking like noise. This indicates some attention to the social environment and an adaptive, contingent strategy rather than impulsive or inattentive play. Overall, they are best viewed as a conditional cooperator who aims to avoid being either the “sucker” who over-contributes or the blatant exploiter who always free-rides. They sit near the middle and adjust.

This player is a generally high contributor who treats full contribution as the default, but occasionally deviates to test or exploit the environment. For much of the game, they contribute the maximum available amount, even when others’ average contributions are lower and somewhat unstable. This suggests a cooperative baseline that does not collapse immediately when others reduce their effort. At the same time, they sometimes drop their contribution sharply, including at least one instance of very low giving amidst near-unanimous full contributions from others, indicating a willingness to free-ride opportunistically once they have seen that the group can sustain high provision. They also occasionally switch to moderate contributions rather than the extreme ends, which indicates some sensitivity to changing group behavior rather than blind commitment. Overall, they appear conditionally cooperative with a strong pro-cooperation bias, but they are not strictly principled: they do experiment with lower contributions when it seems safe or informative. Their risk tolerance is moderate to high—continuing to contribute fully even after observing that others do not always match their level of cooperation implies they accept short-term disadvantage in pursuit of a high-public-good outcome, yet they are not a pure altruist because they sometimes prioritize private payoff when cooperation seems robust. Their fairness norm seems to favor rewarding and supporting those who maintain relatively high contributions, rather than insisting on perfect equality or punishing low contributors (which is not available anyway). They exhibit adaptive behavior: they do not converge to a single fixed contribution level but adjust in response to perceived group dynamics and perhaps to balance their own earnings with cooperative goals.

This player is a moderately cooperative, strategically responsive contributor who oscillates between high and low contributions rather than settling on a rigid pattern. They are clearly willing to contribute at or near the maximum in many situations, indicating that they value group efficiency and are not a pure free-rider. At the same time, they occasionally drop contributions very sharply, including to minimal or zero levels, suggesting that they monitor group behavior and adjust to perceived norms or frustrations rather than adhering to unconditional cooperation. Their contributions tend to align loosely with the apparent contribution climate: when others, on average, contribute very high amounts, they are inclined to match or nearly match that standard. When contributions appear more mixed or decreasing, they sometimes cut back substantially. This indicates conditional cooperation: they are more generous when they perceive others as cooperative, and more self-protective when they sense uneven effort or emerging free-riding. They do not display a strict fairness rule such as always matching the group average, but their behavior suggests they care about not being consistently the “sucker” who gives far more than others. The occasional very low contribution in the midst of otherwise generous play hints at reactive experimentation or mild sanctioning by withholding effort rather than a stable selfish type. Their risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to risk high contributions even without punishment options, presumably relying on the high multiplier and the observed cooperation of others to justify that risk, yet they hedge that risk at times with abrupt under-contribution. Overall, the type is best described as an adaptive conditional cooperator: inclined toward cooperation, but with intermittent, sometimes drastic pullbacks when they likely reassess the environment or test the payoff of lower contributions.

This player fits a “conditional high contributor with opportunistic defection” type. They are clearly willing to contribute at or near the full endowment most of the time and do not behave as a pure free-rider. Their baseline pattern is to give a high amount, often the maximum, especially once they see that others also contribute meaningfully. They appear comfortable sustaining high contributions even when some others contribute less, suggesting they value maintaining group efficiency and are not strictly equality-maximizing on every round. At the same time, they periodically drop their contribution sharply, including after previously contributing fully. These drops do not systematically track a clear collapse in group contributions; instead, they look like deliberate tests of free-riding or short-term payoff maximization in an otherwise cooperative environment. This indicates a willingness to exploit cooperative groups intermittently while returning to high contributions to keep the group productive. Their behavior suggests they understand the payoff structure: they use both extremes (very high and very low contributions) and intermediate amounts, implying they experiment and adjust rather than follow a rigid rule. They tolerate being more generous than some peers; mild under-contribution by others does not automatically cause them to defect. Their contribution pattern is moderately volatile but organized enough to look strategic rather than random. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they accept the risk of being punished when they under-contribute but do not permanently switch to full cooperation after being punished, implying they are not highly punishment-averse. Fairness norms seem present but flexible—cooperation is important, yet they are willing to violate a strict fairness norm when it is personally profitable or when they seem to be probing the group.

This player is a conditional cooperator with a tendency to oscillate between strong pro-sociality and opportunistic low provision. They usually contribute at or above the group average when others appear cooperative, and often match or exceed the contributions of the more generous subgroup in the group. They are clearly willing to fully cooperate, repeatedly choosing the maximum contribution when surrounding behavior looks reasonably supportive. At the same time, they are not rigidly altruistic: they occasionally drop contributions substantially, including down to very low or zero, especially after experiencing patterns of low giving by some peers and/or receiving punishment. Their behavior suggests a fairness norm that focuses on relative contributions: they appear comfortable being among the high contributors when others are also contributing robustly, but they reduce their giving when they perceive persistent low contributors and possible exploitation. Contribution levels are not stable; they adapt over time and show moderate volatility. This volatility seems strategic rather than random: high contributions cluster in periods when cooperation from others is relatively high, while low contributions appear after frustration or sanctioning. They show at most moderate risk tolerance: they are willing to “test” low contributions in an environment that previously punished them, but do not persist in extreme free-riding and revert to intermediate contributions afterward. Their overarching type is “pro-social but sensitive to norm violations and sanctions,” combining cooperative ideals with a readiness to protect their own payoff when trust in the group weakens.

This player is a strong, near-unconditional cooperator with mild, late-emerging strategic adjustment. Their default is to give the full endowment to the public pool and they sustain very high contributions across almost all rounds. They do not clearly condition their giving on the exact behavior of specific others; instead, they appear guided by an internal norm that favoring cooperation is “the right thing” and personally acceptable even when others undercontribute. They occasionally reduce their contribution, but the pattern suggests calibration rather than abandonment of cooperation. Even when others’ average contributions are clearly below their own, they keep contributing at or near the maximum, and only rarely step down to a moderate level. These brief reductions do not escalate into defection, indicating they prioritize group efficiency and norm-consistent behavior over strict payoff maximization. They show no clear evidence of risk aversion with respect to contribution: despite fluctuating group behavior, they repeatedly expose themselves to the risk of being exploited by lower contributors. Their fairness norm appears asymmetric: they tolerate being more generous than others for long stretches, suggesting that maintaining cooperation and the group’s total outcome matters more to them than strict equality per round. Over time, they do not trend toward systematic free-riding; any reductions remain partial and temporary.

This player is a mostly high contributor with occasional, moderate free-riding. They repeatedly match or join full-cooperation norms when the group is clearly coordinating, indicating a baseline prosocial or efficiency-oriented preference. However, they intermittently reduce their contribution to a mid-level amount while others mostly keep contributing high levels, suggesting conditional cooperation with some opportunistic shading rather than strict altruism or strict selfishness. Their deviations from full contribution are moderate, not extreme: they do not drop to zero even when others do, which implies some internal fairness or self-image constraint against blatant exploitation. At the same time, they are willing to benefit from others’ higher contributions without matching them, so they balance social efficiency with personal gain rather than prioritizing one absolutely. Their behavior is somewhat responsive to the emerging norm but not rigidly bound by it: they initially align with maximum-contribution coordination, briefly reduce their contribution while many others still contribute high, then re-align with full contribution, and later partially free-ride again. This pattern suggests they track the group climate but also periodically test or exploit it. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to diverge from the explicit “everyone contribute max” coordination even when the norm is salient, but they do not push to extreme defection that might risk social backlash if sanctions were available. They do not appear to apply a simple tit-for-tat rule; instead their pattern fits a “cooperate most of the time, occasionally skim” type, with some sensitivity to the overall cooperation level but no rigid mechanical rule. Under uncertainty about others’ behavior, they err more on the side of contributing than on pure self-interest, but they keep a margin for personal gain.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They always give the full endowment to the public fund and never deviate, even when others free‑ride or when cooperation around them fluctuates. They do not appear to condition their own contribution on group averages, individual deviations, or chat dynamics; their strategy is consistent rather than reactive. This suggests a strong internal norm for full cooperation, potentially driven by either a principled view that “max contribution is right” or by a simple, pre-committed rule they stick to regardless of others’ behavior. They tolerate exploitation by persistent defectors and do not reduce their contributions in response, indicating high cooperation even under adverse conditions and low concern with short‑term payoff maximization. Their fairness norm is likely “I do my part fully” rather than “I match what others do.” They show no visible risk avoidance with respect to being taken advantage of; instead, they accept that risk and continue cooperating.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor who consistently gives the maximum possible amount to the public fund every round, regardless of what others do. They hold a strong norm that everyone “should” contribute fully and explicitly emphasize that mutual full contribution benefits all. They do not appear to adjust their own contributions downward in response to repeated free-riding by specific others, suggesting a combination of prosocial preferences and either low sensitivity to exploitation or a deliberate choice to prioritize group efficiency and norm consistency over personal payoff. Their behavior indicates a high willingness to sacrifice private gain for collective benefit and a low inclination toward strategic defection, even when end-game incentives would favor lowering contributions.

This player is a strict, unconditional full cooperator under the given choice set. When allowed only a binary decision between nothing and everything, they consistently choose to contribute their entire endowment in every situation, regardless of others’ behavior or the aggregate contribution level. Their strategy is extremely stable: there is no sign of experimentation, switching, or contingent adjustment in response to free-riding, partial cooperation, or changes in the average contribution. They appears to anchor on the efficiency logic of the game: each contributed coin yields a return to the group that exceeds its private cost, and they reliably act as if maximizing group surplus is the primary objective. There is no behavioral evidence that they utilize a narrow, self-interested payoff-maximizing rule; instead, they accept lower relative earnings whenever others defect, and they do not exploit situations where others contribute highly by free-riding. Because they never deviate from maximum contribution, their conditionality is unknown in a strict sense (we never observe what they do when others cooperate less across multiple periods in a way that might provoke change). However, their willingness to maintain full cooperation through substantial variation in others’ actions and payoffs suggests a high tolerance for being exploited and a low sensitivity to short-term inequity. They do not appear to chase equality of payoffs or retaliate against defectors through their own contribution choices. Risk tolerance with respect to cooperation appears high: they keep investing fully into the public fund despite substantial uncertainty about others’ future actions and despite observable free-riding. They behave as if the norm “always contribute fully” dominates concerns about being taken advantage of, and they do not hedge this by ever withholding contributions. Overall, this is a “principled, efficiency-focused unconditional cooperator” type: they implement a simple, rigid rule of full contribution that does not adjust to local patterns of behavior or payoffs.

This player is a calculated, alternating free-rider–cooperator. Across the game they reliably switch between taking everything and giving everything, rather than settling into a stable contribution level. They never use intermediate contributions when not forced, suggesting they treat the decision as a binary strategic choice rather than fine-tuned equity. They appear strongly payoff-oriented. When others’ contributions are high, they are willing to free-ride and keep their full endowment, capturing the group benefit. At other times they contribute fully, often when many others are also contributing, which keeps the group return high but does not maximize their own payoff relative to free-riding. This pattern implies they balance two incentives: exploiting cooperative environments and occasionally supporting them. Their cooperation is conditional but not in a simple “cooperate if others cooperate” or “mimic majority” sense. They both free-ride on highly cooperative profiles and fully contribute in similarly cooperative situations at different times. This suggests they monitor the environment but do not follow a fixed, one-dimensional reciprocity rule. Instead, they seem willing to alternate roles—sometimes bearing the cost of provision, sometimes exploiting it—perhaps to avoid being entirely predictable or to test others’ persistence. Fairness norms are limited or flexible. The player is repeatedly willing to earn strictly more than consistent contributors without showing any observable correction toward fairness. At the same time, they occasionally accept earning less than defectors when they choose to contribute in mixed groups, so maximizing relative rank is not their only concern. Overall, they prioritize maintaining high absolute payoffs over enforcing equal shares. Risk tolerance appears moderate. They do contribute fully in environments where defection is materially better for them, indicating a willingness to risk being the “sucker” for one round. However, because they repeatedly revert to free-riding after cooperative episodes, they do not lock themselves into a long-run, exploitable cooperative stance. This pattern is consistent with an opportunistic, experiment-and-exploit style rather than a purely risk-averse or purely altruistic one.

This player is a strategic, profit-oriented conditional cooperator with alternating free-rider tendencies. They recognize that contributing the full endowment is socially efficient but treat cooperation as expendable when exploitation is easy and risk-free. They reliably use only the two extremes (all or nothing) rather than mixing or fine-tuning contributions, suggesting a simple, rule-based strategy rather than continuous calibration. Their pattern shows clear phases: an early willingness to fully cooperate, a substantial mid-game stretch of systematic free-riding despite high group contributions, then a later return to frequent full contributions. They display low fairness aversion: they repeatedly take advantage of others’ high contributions to secure strictly higher payoffs than most contributors. They do not appear deterred by creating or maintaining payoff asymmetries in their favor. At the same time, they are not a pure egoist: they do sometimes choose full contributions even when recent experience shows that free-riding is more profitable, indicating at least some weight on efficiency, reciprocity, or norm-following. Their conditionality is asymmetric. They are quick to free-ride when others contribute a lot, but not equally quick to withhold contributions when others defect. In several cooperative environments they keep cooperating; in others, with very similar peer behavior, they choose to defect, indicating that internal considerations (such as experimentation, boredom with being exploited, or payoff tracking) matter at least as much as current group behavior. This makes them moderately volatile: they switch between contribution regimes multiple times rather than locking into a stable long-term strategy. They show high risk tolerance toward social risk (being the defector among cooperators) because there is no punishment. They exploit this structural safety to secure high relative gains when the public fund is large. Overall, they behave like a payoff-maximizing opportunist who understands the efficiency of contribution but is willing to defect whenever it does not visibly threaten short-run returns.

This player is primarily a persistent free rider with occasional, bounded cooperative bursts. Across most of the interaction, they keep their entire private endowment and rely on others’ contributions to generate public returns. Their default is non-contribution, and this baseline is quite stable. They sometimes switch to full contribution when cooperation by others is already high and the group is large, suggesting they are not categorically anti-cooperative; rather, they cooperate selectively when they can “ride along” with an already-strong public fund and when their own payoff remains acceptable. These cooperative episodes are isolated and do not create a new stable pattern; the player always reverts to non-contribution afterward. They appear insensitive to fairness norms in the strong sense: they repeatedly accept earning strictly more than many higher-contributing peers without making compensating contributions later. There is no consistent effort to match others’ average behavior or to reciprocate past generosity. Any fairness motivation, if present, is weak and clearly dominated by self-interest. Risk tolerance is moderate in the sense that this player is comfortable depending on others’ cooperation to sustain the public fund. However, they avoid the “risk” of being the one who contributes when others might defect; they tend to contribute only when it is clear that many others are already contributing, and they never show willingness to “lead” cooperation from a low-contribution baseline. Conditionality is present but one-sided: they do not appear to punish low contributions by withdrawing their own (since they rarely contribute at all), but they sometimes join in when others are already contributing heavily. They show no pattern of “turning cooperative” to try to rescue declining contributions; instead, their contributions are brief deviations from a consistently selfish strategy. Overall, their contribution profile is best described as “selfish opportunist with occasional symbolic cooperation,” not as a reciprocal cooperator or a fairness-driven type.

This player is a strict, unconditional full contributor. They always allocate their entire endowment to the public fund, with no deviation across the entire game. Their behavior does not track fluctuations in others’ cooperation levels or in personal payoffs; they continue to fully cooperate in both relatively high- and low-contribution environments. They show no evidence of contingent reciprocity, retaliation, or free-riding, even when many others take the endowment for themselves and earn higher individual payoffs. This pattern suggests they prioritize the group-return-maximizing strategy over individual payoff optimization, and they do not adjust based on short-term disadvantage. They appear comfortable with payoff risk in the sense of repeatedly exposing themselves to potential exploitation, but their strategy is actually payoff-dominant at the group level given the high return on contributions. This is consistent with someone who understands (or intuitively accepts) that full contribution can be collectively efficient and chooses that path regardless of others’ behavior. Whether they explicitly compute this is unknown. In normative terms, they act as if there is a firm internal rule to “always contribute everything.” There is no sign that they are trying to match the group average, punish defectors via reduced giving, or experiment strategically. Volatility is effectively zero; the contribution rule is simple, rigid, and stable.

This player is a high-but-not-unconditional contributor who mixes full cooperation with occasional pure free-riding. They only ever choose the extremes available (all or nothing) and do not hover between them, suggesting a clear, discrete mental model of “cooperate vs defect” rather than fine-tuned optimization. Across the game, their modal action is to contribute the full endowment, and they do so in most rounds. However, they periodically switch to taking everything for themselves even when the group is contributing well. These switches are not obviously triggered by sharp drops in others’ cooperation; they sometimes free-ride while others are still contributing heavily. That pattern indicates they are not a strict conditional cooperator who simply mirrors the group average. They appear to understand the efficiency of cooperation in this high-multiplier environment and are willing to bear personal cost for group benefit for long stretches. At the same time, they exploit cooperative states by occasionally free-riding when others continue to contribute. This suggests a “cooperative opportunist”: someone who prefers efficient, high-contribution equilibria but is comfortable taking advantage of them at times. They tolerate inequity when it favors them; there is no sign that seeing others earn less when they contribute more deters their own choices. Conversely, they also accept inequity where they sacrifice for others, without immediately retaliating by sustained defection. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they contribute even when some group members free-ride, trusting that enough others will keep cooperating to make contribution profitable, but they occasionally insure themselves by defecting to lock in higher personal payoffs. Overall, their contribution pattern is relatively stable at a high level, punctuated by a small number of deliberate defections that do not escalate into a general collapse of their cooperation.

This player is a highly cooperative, near-unconditional high contributor. They normally contribute the full endowment and maintain this pattern across almost the entire game, including early, middle, and late phases. Contribution behavior is very stable rather than volatile; there is a single clear deviation where they free‑ride once, surrounded by a long run of full contributions before and after. That isolated free‑ride suggests they understand the economic incentives (they later exploit the group once) but do not adopt a sustained self-interested strategy. Their contributions are only weakly conditional on others’ behavior. They continue to fully contribute even after others reduce their own contributions or when average contributions drop sharply. This indicates a strong internal norm or preference for contributing rather than a purely payoff-maximizing response to others’ actions. They appear to value collective efficiency and maintaining a cooperative standard more than short-term income. They tolerate substantial payoff sacrifice to uphold high contributions, even when others under-contribute or when the marginal per capita return is below 1 for the individual. This implies relatively high risk tolerance with respect to being exploited and a willingness to accept personal losses to preserve cooperation. They do not engage in tit-for-tat style matching of others’ contribution levels; instead, they act as a consistent cooperator who anchors the group’s norm at full contribution whenever possible.

This player is a strong, near-unconditional high contributor, with only a few brief deviations. They usually give the full endowment to the public fund and sustain this pattern across almost all rounds. The rare reductions (including very low or zero contributions) occur sporadically rather than forming a sustained trend, suggesting experimentation or opportunistic defection rather than a full strategic shift. Their cooperation is only weakly conditional on others’ behavior. They continue contributing fully even after observing clear free-riding or partial free-riding by others, and they quickly revert to full contributions after brief personal deviations. This indicates a preference for maintaining a cooperative norm over short-term payoff maximization, at least most of the time. They show a fairness norm in the sense that they are willing to keep contributing fully when others do as well, and occasional under-contributions appear when the group norm is already slightly weakened or when they have just received substantial reward for defecting. However, they do not systematically match others’ contributions; instead they anchor on full cooperation and only sometimes deviate downward. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they repeatedly expose themselves to the risk of being exploited by potential free-riders by continuing to contribute fully. They do not seem to hedge or gradually reduce contributions in response to noise or defection; instead, deviations are abrupt and then quickly reversed. This fits a type who values cooperative structure and is willing to risk being the “sucker” rather than steadily adopting a defensive, low-contribution strategy. Overall, this is a “stubborn high contributor” type: predominantly cooperative, not tightly contingent on others’ choices, with occasional opportunistic or exploratory defections that do not fundamentally change their cooperative baseline.

This player is a near-unconditional full cooperator after an initial mistake. They usually contribute the entire endowment and maintain this pattern stably across the game, even when others occasionally free-ride. The one defection coincides with them stating confusion, and their subsequent behavior aligns with having learned the structure and then committing to cooperation. They appear to accept the fairness norm of equal full contribution and continue to uphold it despite evidence that a specific peer defects later and gains higher payoffs; they do not visibly adjust contributions in response. This suggests relatively low strategic risk-taking and a preference for norm adherence over payoff maximization once the norm is understood. Their contributions look only weakly conditional: they shift from defection to cooperation once they understand the situation, but after that they cooperate even when others don’t, indicating that sustaining the group’s cooperative frame matters at least as much as strict payoff-based reciprocity.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator in this environment. They always contribute the maximum allowed amount, regardless of others’ behavior, including after clear free-riding by specific partners. They do not show evidence of conditional cooperation or tit-for-tat; their own contribution does not drop in response to defectors, nor do they increase it above maximum in any circumstance. This is consistent with a strong internal norm that full contribution is “what one should do,” independent of short-term payoff optimization. Their behavior suggests low sensitivity to individual-round payoff differences and a high valuation of maintaining group efficiency and norm consistency. Willingness to keep contributing fully even when one or two players deviate indicates tolerance for some unfairness as long as the group as a whole mostly cooperates. Risk tolerance toward being exploited appears moderate: they do not change their contribution to protect themselves, relying instead on social pressure or others’ sanctions rather than self-protection via defection.

This player is a strongly cooperation-oriented, near-full contributor who treats high contribution as the default. They repeatedly choose the socially efficient action even when the partner’s behavior would justify self-protection. Their pattern shows a clear norm: mutual full contribution is the “right” baseline, and they are willing to sustain it. They briefly deviate to lower contributions on a few occasions when the partner contributes less, indicating conditionality: they do notice and respond to deviations from full cooperation. However, these adjustments are modest and short-lived rather than a sustained shift to defection, suggesting they are not primarily payoff-maximizing in a narrow sense; they are willing to accept lower payoffs to preserve or restore high cooperation. Their behavior is stable over time: after early experimentation, they settle into a consistent high-contribution strategy and largely maintain it even under repeated, unexplained punishment. This indicates a strong internal norm for cooperation and relatively low volatility. They tolerate some unfair treatment without abandoning their cooperative stance, suggesting risk tolerance in the sense of staying exposed to exploitation and sanctions rather than protecting themselves with defection. Overall, the player fits a “principled cooperator” type: they value high joint outcomes and are reluctant to sacrifice the public good even when experiencing unjustified harm. Whether this reflects altruism, fairness norms, or a desire to follow perceived experimental expectations is unknown, but the net effect is persistent high cooperation.

This player is a near-constant full contributor who treats “all in” cooperation as the default norm. They repeatedly put in their entire endowment whenever they participate, with one clear deviation when they free-ride. Their behavior indicates that they understand the payoff structure: they explicitly point out that contributing yields higher gains and that those contributing zero “have to [be] losing doing that.” They anchor on a strong norm of universal contribution and see the efficient outcome as everyone always contributing their full amount. The single free-riding instance appears opportunistic and experimental rather than a full strategic shift, as they immediately return to full cooperation afterward. They do not condition contributions finely on others’ behavior; instead, they maintain cooperation even in the presence of persistent defectors, relying on punishment rather than reduced contributions to enforce norms. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to keep contributing despite the presence of trolls and occasional mistakes, but they eventually test non-contribution once, suggesting a desire to probe the consequences or recoup some payoff. Fairness norms are strong and efficiency-oriented: they want everyone to contribute and to align individual actions with the group optimum.

The player is a strict, unwavering full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment, with no deviation, regardless of others’ behavior, group contribution levels, or game stage. This pattern suggests a strongly internalized cooperation norm or a fixed strategy chosen in advance, rather than reactive or conditional cooperation. They do not appear to condition on free-riding by others, nor do they escalate cooperation in response to higher group contributions—they simply maintain maximum contribution throughout. Risk tolerance with respect to exploitation is therefore high: they accept being paired with persistent non-contributors and never respond by defecting. There is no evidence that they aim to optimize personal payoff in a narrow, responsive sense; instead, they seem to prioritize a stable cooperative identity and/or the group’s public good regardless of short-term personal gain.

This player is a strict full contributor: they always contribute the maximum possible amount in every round and never deviate. Their behavior is extremely stable; there is no exploration of free-riding or partial cooperation. This pattern suggests that they either adopt a strong internal norm of “always cooperate” or treat full contribution as the default, regardless of what others do. They continue this even when others’ average contributions drop and when it is clear that some players systematically free-ride. There is no visible conditionality on specific peers’ actions: they do not appear to adjust contributions upward or downward in response to any pattern in others’ behavior. Their risk tolerance regarding exploitation is high: they accept being repeatedly taken advantage of by low contributors without changing their own contributions. They do not visibly enforce fairness through their own contribution decisions; instead, they behave as a pure unconditional cooperator.

This player is a steadfast, unconditional full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment in every round, with no hesitation and no adjustment to the group’s fluctuating contribution level. They do not appear to condition on others’ free-riding or on changes in the overall cooperation rate; low average contributions, repeated zeros from specific players, or late-stage declines in group contributions do not alter their behavior. This suggests a strong internal cooperation norm or a fixed rule (“always contribute fully”) rather than payoff-maximizing response to incentives. They tolerate systematic free-riding and repeated under-contribution from specific others without shifting toward self-protection, indicating low sensitivity to exploitation risk and a willingness to bear inequality. Fairness concerns, if present, do not translate into contribution withholding. Their strategy is highly stable over time, with no volatility or experimentation.

This player is a high, mostly unconditional contributor. They almost always invest their full endowment in the public good, with only two clear deviations where they keep everything. Those exceptions do not lead to any lasting change in behavior; they resume full contributions immediately and sustain them through the end. Their cooperation is therefore stable rather than volatile. There is no clear pattern of precise conditionality on others’ behavior. They continue to contribute fully both when average contributions from others are high and when they are relatively low, and they maintain full contributions even after free-riding themselves has paid off. This suggests that they follow a cooperative norm or simple “always contribute” rule more than a strict tit‑for‑tat or equity-based strategy. They appear comfortable bearing the individual cost of contributing even when others do not, which indicates relatively low risk aversion in social settings and a willingness to be the one “carrying” the group. At the same time, they are clearly aware that free-riding is profitable (as shown by the rare zero-contribution rounds) and are capable of exploiting it, so their cooperation seems norm-driven or identity-driven rather than purely naive. They do not systematically reduce contributions in response to low-contributing subgroups, nor do they increase contributions beyond the maximum, so there is no evidence of nuanced fairness calibration beyond a general willingness to be prosocial.

This player is a predominantly self-interested free-rider with occasional, sharp bursts of full cooperation. Their modal choice is to keep the entire endowment, relying on others’ contributions while still collecting the public return. Interspersed among these free-riding choices are a few instances of contributing the full endowment, sometimes when many others also cooperate and sometimes when cooperation from others is mixed. They do not appear to fine-tune contributions in proportion to others; instead, they toggle between “all” and “nothing,” suggesting a coarse, low-effort strategy rather than delicate conditional cooperation. They show no consistent fairness norm; they are comfortable earning substantially more than high contributors in many periods. Their intermittent full contributions do not establish a stable cooperative pattern and are not followed by sustained mutual high contributions. This suggests their cooperative bursts are either exploratory, occasional experiments, or driven by momentary impulses rather than a sustained reciprocity principle. Risk tolerance is moderate: when they do contribute fully, they do so even when others’ behavior is not uniformly trusting, indicating some willingness to risk exploitation, but these episodes are rare relative to their baseline free-riding. They do not systematically reduce cooperation after seeing that others free-ride, because their default is already low contribution. Likewise, they do not gradually ramp up cooperation in response to generous group behavior; instead, they remain mostly at zero, with isolated spikes. Overall, they behave as an opportunistic, payoff-focused player whose contributions are not tightly conditioned on others’ exact choices.

This player is a consistently high contributor with moderate variability around a pro-social baseline. They typically give well above the group average and often choose the maximum or near-maximum contribution. They do not simply fixate on one number: their contributions fluctuate within a high band, suggesting conditional cooperation rather than blind altruism. When others contribute more, they comfortably match or exceed that level; when others contribute less or free-ride, they temporarily reduce their own giving but rarely drop to low or zero. Even after periods of low group cooperation, they resume high contributions, indicating a strong internal norm for sustaining the public good and a willingness to continue “doing their part” despite imperfect reciprocity. They appear to value collective efficiency: they keep contributing heavily even though the marginal private return to contributing is lower than the private benefit of keeping coins, which implies either a prosocial preference, a fairness norm that “everyone should give a lot,” or a belief that high contributions might stabilize group behavior. They show no evidence of opportunistic exploitation of cooperative periods; instead of cutting back when others are generous, they tend to remain generous themselves. Their pattern suggests risk tolerance in the sense of accepting repeated short-term personal disadvantage relative to low contributors for the sake of group-level payoff. At the same time, they do react to very low contribution environments by dialing back, so their cooperation is conditional, not unconditional.

This player is a flexible, payoff-focused conditional cooperator who oscillates between generous giving and near-complete free-riding, guided by short-run incentives and observed group behavior rather than a stable fairness norm. Early in the game, they mix moderate and high contributions with occasional low ones, indicating willingness to invest in the public fund when others also appear cooperative. Over time, they increasingly exploit high-contributing environments by contributing very little or nothing while continuing to enjoy the group’s provision, revealing a clear readiness to free-ride when the environment allows. Their contribution pattern is highly volatile rather than stable: they alternate between full contributions and zero contributions multiple times, suggesting ongoing experimentation and adaptation rather than a fixed rule. They do not appear to target equality or proportional fairness; they are comfortable earning much more than high contributors when the opportunity arises. There is no clear evidence that they match others’ average contributions precisely or follow a strict reciprocity rule. Instead, they seem to respond to broad cues: when cooperation is strong and widespread, they become more willing to drop their own contributions; when cooperation weakens or payoffs fall for contributors, they sometimes increase contributions sharply, consistent with a belief that their own giving can help maintain or restore a profitable public fund. Risk tolerance appears moderate to high: they are at times willing to contribute their entire endowment despite uncertain matching from others, but they are also willing to fully exploit the group by giving nothing while others give a lot, which is risky reputationally in settings where retaliation is possible (though here punishment is unavailable). Overall, they act as a self-interested opportunistic cooperator: they will contribute substantial amounts when they expect it to pay, and readily revert to low or zero contributions when they can benefit from others’ generosity.

This player is a strict high contributor who consistently chooses to put their entire endowment into the public fund every single round, without deviation. Their behavior is stable rather than volatile: they do not reduce contributions even when some peers repeatedly free‑ride or when group contributions fluctuate. They appear to adopt a “full‑cooperation for maximum group payoff” norm and stick to it throughout. They explicitly frame the situation in long‑run, cumulative payoff terms and refer to a high target payoff for everyone, suggesting they view sustained cooperation as the rational money‑maximizing path rather than as a purely altruistic sacrifice. They show no visible experimentation with defection, which implies either low willingness to exploit others or a deliberate choice to ignore short‑term temptation for personal gain. They tolerate inequality in outcomes caused by free‑riders: their contributions do not become more conditional following non‑contributors’ behavior. This points to either high tolerance for being exploited, a focus on maintaining group norms regardless of others’ deviations, or a belief that the overall setup is such that at least one defector is inevitable and should be “absorbed” by the group. They may be somewhat risk‑acceptant in the sense of being willing to rely on others to keep cooperating, but they also hedge that risk by treating the game as “easy money” where contributing remains worthwhile even in the presence of some non‑contributors. They appear to adopt a fairness norm at the level of “everyone should contribute,” but they do not translate that into conditional cooperation or sanctions; instead, they maintain their own standard even when that norm is partially violated. There is no evidence that they attempt to strategically time their contributions to guessed endgame rounds; they continue contributing fully up to and including the final round, even after another player announces that it is the last round. This suggests limited endgame defection and a preference for rule‑consistent behavior over exploiting final‑round opportunities.

The player is a highly stable, unconditional cooperator. They always contribute the maximum possible amount and never deviate, even when several others repeatedly free-ride or partially defect. Their behavior aligns with a strong norm of “everyone should put in everything” and they personally adhere to that norm regardless of others’ shortfalls. They display very high tolerance for exploitation: persistent non-contributors elicit social comments and mild social pressure from them, but never a shift toward self-protection in their own contributions. They do not experiment strategically with withholding or conditional cooperation; there is no sign they adjust contributions in response to changing group behavior or to test others’ intentions. They appear fairness-motivated at the normative level (they call out non-contributors collectively and name specific low contributors), but their fairness concerns are expressed socially rather than through payoff-protecting actions. They seem more motivated by keeping the cooperative scheme intact and playing along with the group contract than by maximizing their own narrow payoff. Risk-wise, they accept the clear risk of being exploited by free-riders, suggesting a preference for group efficiency or norm-following over individual payoff optimization. There is no visible sensitivity to end-game incentives; they maintain full cooperation through the final round, including when at least one peer claims it is the last round. Overall, their “type” is best described as a humorous, norm-enforcing unconditional cooperator who does not translate frustration with free-riding into strategic defection.

This player is a calculated, alternating free-rider who toggles between full cooperation and full defection to exploit a largely cooperative group while maintaining some level of participation. They consistently choose only the extreme options available and never mix strategies within a round. Their default incentive appears to be maximising personal payoff rather than maintaining strict fairness. They free-ride frequently even when the group is highly cooperative and stable, indicating low guilt or constraint from group norms. However, they do not defect in every possible opportunity: they sometimes contribute fully even when others are already contributing at high rates. This suggests they value sustaining the public good enough to keep the “money machine” running, but are comfortable letting others carry the collective effort more often than not. Contribution behavior is moderately volatile in a patterned way rather than random. They alternate between contributing and not contributing across many stretches, with no visible attempt to match any particular other player’s behavior. Their decisions do not track specific defectors; they continue to defect even when the main non-contributors are clearly identified and being discussed by others, and they continue to contribute sometimes while others defect. This points to a primarily self-focused payoff strategy rather than conditional cooperation based on fairness or reciprocity. They accept benefiting disproportionately from others’ contributions and do not protest or comment about “unfairness” in the chat. At the same time, they are willing to incur the cost of contributing in a significant minority of rounds, suggesting they see value in maintaining a cooperative equilibrium and possibly in appearing as a “normal” participant rather than a pure exploiter. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to freeride despite the possibility that others might stop contributing, but they also periodically “re-invest” by contributing, perhaps to reduce the risk of a groupwide cooperation collapse that would hurt their earnings. Overall, they behave like an opportunistic mixed-strategy player: cooperation is instrumental and intermittent, with exploitation of group cooperation being a central component of their approach.

This player is a fully cooperative, all-or-nothing contributor who consistently gives the maximum allowed amount to the public good in every opportunity presented. Their behavior is highly stable and shows no volatility or experimentation; once they adopt full cooperation, they maintain it without deviation. They do not condition their contributions on variations in others’ behavior, since they experience a perfectly cooperative group and simply match that level. Their fairness norm appears strongly egalitarian: they behave in a way that equalizes payoffs among participants and keeps everyone at the same outcome level. Given the payoff structure, their consistent full contribution indicates either a prosocial or efficiency-oriented motive rather than a narrow self-interest focus. Risk tolerance is unknown; because the group is uniformly cooperative from the start, there is no evidence of how they would respond to defection or uncertainty about others.

This player is a verbally pro-cooperation, behaviorally opportunistic conditional cooperator–defector. They understand very clearly that full contribution by everyone maximizes group and individual payoffs and explicitly articulate that logic. However, they do not commit to contributing unconditionally and repeatedly revert to free-riding once they perceive persistent defectors. Their contribution pattern is mixed and reactive rather than stable. Early on, they start as a pure free-rider despite high group contributions. After the cooperative logic and its benefits become salient, they switch to contributing fully a few times, especially when others are also coordinating toward full contribution. When non-contributors remain, they publicly announce that they will stop contributing and then mostly follow through. They then intermittently contribute again, but only in isolated instances, not as a renewed long-term cooperative strategy. They show clear conditionality based on others’ behavior: they are willing to contribute when a near-unanimous cooperative norm seems achievable, but once they judge that some players “refuse to cooperate,” they view further contribution as irrational and revert to maximizing their own payoff, even when reminded that continued contribution would still be socially optimal. They explicitly frame this reversion as “getting more coins” rather than doing the “right” thing, indicating a payoff-maximization motive that overrides their stated social preference once hope for full cooperation collapses. They adopt a fairness norm tied to reciprocity and mutual sacrifice: they expect that if they contribute, others should, too. When that fails, they treat continued giving as being exploited and treat defection as justified retaliation. Their willingness to free-ride while still criticizing other free-riders (especially targetting prominent non-contributors) reflects a self-serving fairness standard: others’ free-riding is framed as morally problematic and socially harmful, while their own late-game defection is rationalized as a reasonable response. Risk tolerance regarding cooperation appears low to moderate. They are not willing to “gamble” repeatedly on cooperation in the face of persistent defectors; once burned a few times, they retreat into safer, individually beneficial choices. They are, however, willing to experiment with contributing during periods of optimism about coordination, so they are not purely risk-averse. Overall, they behave like a pragmatic conditional cooperator who quickly transitions into calculated free-riding when trust in group compliance breaks down.

This player fits a “pragmatic wavering cooperator” type. They clearly understand that full contribution is socially efficient and initially act as a consistent cooperator, repeatedly choosing to contribute fully even when others free-ride. Over the game, their behavior becomes conditional and mixed: they switch back and forth between contributing and free-riding rather than adopting a stable pure strategy. They tolerate being exploited for several rounds, but their cooperation is not unconditional. When persistent non-contributors remain, they occasionally defect to capture higher individual payoffs while still benefiting from others’ giving. These selfish turns are not one-off “errors”; they reappear later, indicating deliberate opportunism rather than confusion. They do not strictly match contributions to the group’s level; instead, they oscillate: periods of prosocial contribution interspersed with periods of clear free-riding even when many others are cooperating. This suggests medium concern for fairness but higher concern for personal earnings. They show no strong aversion to inequality in their favor and at times directly exploit cooperative others. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to keep contributing even after being exploited, which risks continued low relative earnings, but they also hedge by switching to non-contribution when cooperation seems unstable. Overall, they are neither a pure altruist nor a pure egoist; they are a mixed-type who leans cooperative but will defect opportunistically when norms look fragile or when many others are already defecting.

This player is best described as a “hopeful cooperator who drifts into strategic free-riding.” They start as an unconditionally cooperative type: they consistently choose the fully cooperative option even when others free-ride, and they maintain this pattern through a substantial portion of the game despite persistent exploitation. Their contributions are initially very stable and not obviously conditioned on short-term fluctuations in others’ behavior. They continue to cooperate even when there are clear, repeated defectors and when other apparent cooperators begin to express frustration. This indicates a relatively high tolerance for short-run unfairness and a willingness to prioritize group efficiency over immediate personal gain, at least early and mid-game. Later, they begin to mix in defection. The shift is not abrupt abandonment of cooperation but a move to a more opportunistic, alternating pattern. After many rounds of full contributions, they selectively choose zero in some later rounds while returning to full contribution in others, including the final round. This shows they are not purely payoff-maximizing in a narrow sense; if they were, they would be expected to defect more consistently once they start defecting and especially at the very end. Instead, they appear to balance curiosity, fairness concerns, and payoff motives. Their decisions suggest they understand that contributing increases group payoffs. They explicitly stay on the cooperative side even when others give them reasons not to, so they accept a personal disadvantage relative to defectors for long stretches. The later move to zero indicates emerging conditionality: prolonged observation of persistent free riders and other players’ talk about “teaching a lesson” likely weakens their original unconditional stance. However, their final-round contribution suggests they do not fully adopt the retaliatory or purely self-interested norm. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they repeatedly “risk” being exploited by defectors to pursue the efficient outcome, rather than preemptively free-riding. At the same time, their willingness to defect later shows they are not rigidly principled; they are willing to adjust when it becomes clear that full cooperation is not universally shared. Overall, the contribution profile is: early and mid-game high, persistent, largely unconditional cooperation; late-game emergence of selective defection; ending with a return to cooperation, suggesting a mixed, rather than purely opportunistic, payoff orientation.

This player is a high, near-unconditional cooperator. They almost always contribute the full endowment, with only very rare deviations. Those deviations do not appear to be part of a sustained phase of defection or a tit-for-tat scheme; they resemble brief tests or impulses rather than a strategic switch. Overall, they strongly favor maintaining group efficiency even when others occasionally free-ride. Their cooperation is remarkably stable across the game and does not systematically track small fluctuations in others’ average behavior. They tolerate some level of unfairness: they continue to give the maximum even when a small subset of others contributes nothing and when they themselves are punished despite contributing. This indicates a fairness norm that prioritizes the collective outcome over strict reciprocity.
