However, the player does not persist with high contributions when they get no clear relational benefit from it, and they revert to zero contributions after single cooperative episodes. They do not systematically match others’ individual contribution levels; they free-ride even on very high overall group contributions and also on moderate ones. Their own contributions do not track the group average in a smooth way, so they are not a classic “conditional cooperator” who tries to match the group. Risk-wise, they are willing to make large, isolated cooperative bets (full contributions) even after having been punished repeatedly, which suggests some openness to risk/experimentation, but those bets remain rare. Overall, their contribution profile fits a “dominant free-rider who occasionally tests high cooperation but quickly returns to defection when it is not clearly rewarded.”

This player is a moderately high, highly volatile contributor who frequently experiments with both generous and very low contributions. They oscillate between full/near-full giving and minimal giving rather than settling into a stable level. Their behavior is clearly conditional: they tend to contribute more when the group’s overall giving appears higher and when many others are also contributing significantly, and they are willing to free ride when they perceive contributions as low or unstable. They do not consistently track or align with exact fairness norms (such as matching the group average), but they appear comfortable being either above or below others depending on context. They accept substantial payoff risk, occasionally making very unprofitable high contributions that are not protected by reciprocal behavior or rewards, suggesting either a willingness to “test” cooperative norms or a limited concern for short-term payoff maximization. They show no obvious linear trend of cooperation steadily decaying or growing; instead, they switch modes multiple times, indicating adaptive, environment-contingent behavior rather than a fixed contribution rule.

This player is a volatile, opportunistic contributor who mixes occasional high contributions with long stretches of free-riding. They do not follow a simple “conditional cooperator” pattern; instead, they alternate between contributing nothing and making sizable contributions that are often above the current group average. There is no consistent upward or downward trend over the game; rather, their behavior is patchy and switches between modes. They clearly understand that low contribution with high group contributions is profitable, and they exploit that often. However, they are also willing to contribute a lot in some rounds even when others are already contributing well, suggesting that maximizing their own payoff is constrained by other motives (e.g., moments of “doing their part,” testing responses, or reacting to recent social dynamics). They do not appear to adhere to a strong fairness norm; they are comfortable earning high payoffs as a zero-contributor when others bear the cost. At the same time, they occasionally choose relatively high contributions without any obvious necessity for self-interest, indicating that they do not rigidly stick to a purely selfish strategy. Overall, their risk tolerance in contribution decisions is moderate: they sometimes risk over-contributing relative to others, but they frequently revert to the safe, dominant free-riding option once they see that they can earn well by doing so.

This player is a moderately cooperative, payoff-conscious conditional contributor with substantial volatility. Contributions range from full free-riding to high/cooperative levels. They repeatedly show willingness to contribute around or above the emerging group average when the group appears relatively cooperative, but also shift down sharply when others’ contributions or efficiency look low, or when they can benefit from free-riding without social sanction. They do not follow a simple monotonic “cooperation decay”; instead they alternate between low and high contributions across the entire game, including late rounds. They appear to track the group environment and adjust rather than commit to a fixed rule. They sometimes over-contribute relative to the group (contributing at or near the top of the distribution when the group is cooperative), and sometimes under-contribute or free-ride when the group average drops or when high contributors are being punished. This pattern suggests a conditional cooperation norm that is tempered by strategic self-interest: they are willing to support cooperation but not at persistent large personal disadvantage, and they are not strongly fairness-enforcing through punishment. Risk tolerance appears moderate. They accept the risk of contributing in environments where some players are punished for their actions, but they also pivot to low contributions when the environment becomes noisy or hostile. Their behavior indicates awareness that contributing more than the group is vulnerable in the presence of strong punishers, and they occasionally retreat to low contributions after being personally targeted.

This player is a moderate, often below-average contributor with occasional higher contributions. They rarely choose the extremes of always free-riding or always fully cooperating. Instead, they hover around a mid-level contribution and adjust upward or downward in response to the apparent group climate. They tolerate substantial heterogeneity: they keep contributing modestly even when others swing between very low and very high contributions. They do not appear driven by strict equality norms, since they frequently give less than many high contributors while still participating in the public good. Their own contributions do not closely track any single peer’s behavior; the pattern suggests a “participating free-rider” or “cautious cooperator” type: willing to put something in, but also comfortable taking advantage of generous co-players. Risk tolerance is moderate. They do sometimes contribute relatively high amounts even in an environment where non-cooperation and heavy punishment are visible, indicating they are not purely risk-averse. However, they do not sustain high contributions over long stretches and frequently revert to a safer, low-to-mid contribution level, consistent with someone focused on preserving a solid payoff while not completely abandoning cooperation. Their behavior is conditional in a coarse way: when the environment looks more cooperative (widespread high contributions), they are willing to increase their own contribution, but they do not match the group’s level and keep a gap that favors their own earnings. When contributions around them drop, they do not escalate to compensate; they instead stay low or mid, indicating they prioritize self-payoff over group welfare and accept lower group efficiency rather than increasing their own sacrifice.

This player is a stable, moderately prosocial contributor with occasional upward deviations and rare downward adjustments. They typically give a medium, fixed share of their endowment rather than optimizing narrowly for short-term payoff. Their contributions do not track fine-grained changes in others’ behavior; they maintain roughly the same level across very low, mixed, and relatively high group contributions. This indicates a weak but persistent internal norm of “reasonable” giving, not strict conditional cooperation. They sometimes increase to high or full contributions when others are also contributing more, suggesting some responsiveness to a cooperative environment, but they do not sustain maximal contributions and revert to their moderate baseline. They occasionally reduce their contribution when surrounded by clear, persistent low contributors, which suggests some concern about being exploited, but the adjustment is small and temporary rather than a full collapse to free-riding. Overall, they balance self-interest and group benefit, leaning toward steady, rule-of-thumb giving rather than careful recalculation each round. They appear moderately risk-acceptant about being the “sucker”: they continue to give even when others free-ride, but not at the very top level. Their fairness norm seems to be “contribute a fair, mid-level amount most of the time, contribute more when everyone is clearly cooperative, and only slightly pull back when clearly surrounded by chronic low contributors.”

This player is an extremely high, almost unconditional contributor. They consistently give their full endowment to the public fund for nearly the entire game, regardless of how little others contribute or how low their own resulting payoff becomes. Only once do they reduce their contribution, and that happens in a context where others are in fact contributing more than usual, not less. This suggests their contribution pattern is driven more by an internal norm or “do the socially efficient thing” principle than by strategic adjustment to others’ behavior or to payoffs. They show no clear evidence of conditional cooperation: they do not raise contributions when others increase, nor lower them when others free-ride, at least over most of the game. Their behavior is consistent with a strong fairness or efficiency norm (“everyone should contribute fully”), but they do not enforce that norm behaviorally through their own contributions; they simply maintain their own cooperative standard. Risk tolerance appears high in the social sense: they are willing to keep making fully cooperative moves even when surrounded by inconsistent or low contributors and even when they personally earn substantially less than others. They do not appear to experiment with partially selfish contributions to probe responses; there is almost no volatility in their contribution level. This indicates a stable, principle-driven cooperative type rather than a payoff-optimizing learner.

This player is a mostly high contributor who eventually shifts to free-riding when cooperation erodes and norms become unclear. They begin by matching or exceeding the group’s cooperative level, even when full contribution is not individually payoff-maximizing, suggesting a willingness to support collective welfare and/or to signal cooperativeness. As the environment becomes more mixed and less cooperative, they persist in contributing at a high level for a time rather than immediately defecting, indicating some inertia and tolerance for being exploited. Only after sustained drops in others’ contributions do they stop contributing themselves, showing conditional cooperation rather than unconditional altruism. Their behavior suggests they care about fairness and mutual effort: they are willing to contribute when others also seem to be contributing significantly, but once the pattern becomes one of uneven or unreliable cooperation, they prioritize self-payoff and switch to non-contribution even though some others still contribute. The switch is abrupt rather than gradual, hinting at a threshold-based rule (“I cooperate as long as I perceive enough others doing so; once that breaks, I defect”). Their risk tolerance is moderate: they initially accept the risk of being exploited to support a cooperative norm, but when the collective pattern looks unstable, they err on the side of self-protection. They do not appear to be highly payoff-maximizing in a narrow sense early on, but they are also not naïvely altruistic; their eventual defection suggests they are attentive to changing incentives and willing to abandon cooperation when it seems systematically disadvantageous.

This player is an initially cooperative but quickly opportunistic contributor. They start by fully contributing to the public good, then switch to full free-riding for all remaining decisions. Once they observe that high contributions are common and that they personally cannot be punished, they no longer invest their own endowment in the group project. Their choices show no partial or mixed contributions and no return to cooperation, indicating a simple, binary rule: either fully cooperate at the outset or defect completely once exploitation appears feasible. They do not appear to condition contributions on who specifically contributes; they free-ride even when many others still cooperate and when others’ cooperation later drops. Their behavior suggests they prioritize maximizing their own payoff over maintaining high group efficiency, especially once they see that reward flows can offset the social cost of free-riding. They show no explicit fairness norm in contribution behavior: they accept benefiting from others’ contributions without matching their effort. Risk tolerance is moderate: they are willing to begin cooperatively in an environment of uncertainty, but once payoffs from free-riding appear attractive and unpunished, they take the low-risk, self-serving path and do not revert.

This player is a mostly cooperative but strategically flexible contributor. They start by fully supporting the public good when the environment appears highly cooperative and continue to do so as long as broad group cooperation is sustained. When they later observe a clear drop in average cooperation, they are willing to free-ride and keep their entire endowment, indicating that their cooperation is conditional rather than unconditional. They subsequently return to full contribution even when others’ average contributions remain low, suggesting they are not purely self-maximizing and may value either a cooperative identity, fairness, or experimentation with different strategies. Their contribution pattern shows sensitivity to group behavior but not a simple linear “tit-for-tat”; instead, they mix conditional cooperation (withholding when cooperation erodes) and renewed cooperation (even in a partially uncooperative group). Overall, they display moderate risk tolerance: they accept some risk of being exploited when they contribute fully in a partially noncooperative environment, but they also protect their payoff by free-riding when cooperation has sharply deteriorated. Their fairness norm appears to be “high cooperation is desirable, but I’m not obliged to keep paying when many others cut back.”

This player is a conditional cooperator who quickly becomes a strategic free-rider once they infer the environment is reward-heavy and tolerant of low contribution. They start by contributing fully and aligning with the cooperative majority, indicating a willingness to support efficient group outcomes and possibly a default fairness or norm-following inclination. When it becomes clear that others continue to provide substantial public good and that some players reward even defectors, they switch to non-contribution and then maintain that pattern. This shift suggests they actively monitor the aggregate contribution level and payoff structure and are willing to deviate from cooperative norms when they see that defection is safe and profitable. The player appears comfortable exploiting a situation where others keep the public good high and where their own reputation is not directly at risk, indicating moderate-to-high payoff focus and low aversion to free-riding once norms seem unstable. Their contribution pattern thus shows an adaptive, payoff-sensitive strategy rather than rigid pro-sociality or pure selfishness.

This player is a mostly high contributor who later experiments with free-riding. They begin by always giving the full endowment when contributing is possible, even when many others do not fully cooperate and even when the marginal per-capita return is low. This suggests a baseline inclination toward cooperation or norm-following that is not strictly payoff-maximizing. Their behavior is not fully stable, however: after observing a substantial decline in group contributions and seeing that non-contributors can still obtain high payoffs, they switch to contributing nothing. This pattern indicates conditional cooperation: they initially act as a strong cooperator, then revise their behavior downward in response to perceived group deterioration. They appear willing to tolerate some unfairness—continuing to cooperate despite others’ lower contributions—but not indefinitely. Risk tolerance around early contributions seems moderate to high, as they continue to invest fully in the public good even when it is unclear how many rounds remain and when group cohesion is visibly weakening. Overall, they are best described as a “disappointed cooperator”: someone who starts out prosocial and then tests the benefits of free-riding once collective norms look unstable.

This player is a moderately cooperative but self-protective conditional cooperator with opportunistic free-riding spikes. Their typical contribution is around the mid-range of the endowment rather than full cooperation or full defection. They clearly condition on the group’s cooperativeness: when others contribute very high amounts, they tend to contribute at least a moderate amount rather than persistently free-ride, but they often stay below the group’s top contributors, saving more for themselves. They show willingness to both under-contribute and over-contribute relative to their own norm depending on context. At times they drop to very low or zero contribution even while others remain generous, indicating that they are willing to exploit a cooperative group temporarily. At other times they fully match or exceed the group’s generosity. This suggests flexible rather than principled fairness: they do not rigidly aim to match the average; instead, they drift between “taking advantage” and “pulling their weight” based on evolving incentives and possibly perceived future benefits of maintaining cooperation. Their pattern indicates low aversion to inequality when it favors them; they accept being the clear beneficiary of others’ high contributions. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they occasionally test lower contributions even in a cooperative environment, seemingly probing whether the group’s high contributions will persist. They do not panic in response to others’ minor contribution drops; instead they adjust gradually. Overall, they are best characterized as a payoff-sensitive conditional cooperator: they like the efficiency of a high-contribution group but protect their own payoff and are willing to temporarily free-ride if the environment seems “safe.”

This player is a stable, low-to-moderate contributor who consistently gives less than their group peers even when others contribute very high amounts. Their contributions cluster in a narrow band at the low end of the possible range, suggesting a deliberate, not random, pattern. They rarely shift sharply from one extreme to another; instead they adjust in small steps, indicating moderate behavioral stability with incremental adaptation rather than volatility. They clearly respond to the group’s cooperation level but do so in a “tempered” way: when others are very generous, they increase their own contributions only modestly, remaining below the group norm. When others reduce contributions somewhat, they do not collapse to full free-riding; they stay in a low-but-positive range. This pattern is consistent with a conditional cooperator who is systematically under-matching others’ generosity. The player appears to prioritize personal payoff while keeping some minimal sense of participation in the public good. They frequently benefit from high group contributions while contributing less themselves, showing a willingness to free-ride, but they avoid contributing nothing except at the very start, which may reflect either a weak fairness norm or concern about appearing completely exploitative (even if others cannot directly sanction them here). Risk tolerance seems asymmetric: they are willing to “risk” under-contributing relative to others (i.e., risk undermining cooperation) but avoid the risk of over-contributing relative to the group. They do not experiment with very high contributions, suggesting either low altruism, strong payoff maximization, or low trust that others will reliably match high contributions over time. Overall, they are a cautious, payoff-focused under-contributing conditional cooperator who maintains a thin layer of pro-social behavior without ever leading in generosity.

This player is a volatile, strategically self-interested contributor who opportunistically free-rides but is also willing to make substantial or even full contributions when they perceive that cooperation is strong and personally beneficial. They do not display a stable contribution level; instead, their behavior fluctuates widely between almost nothing and the full endowment. Contributions range from minimal amounts up to full cooperation, with multiple switches back and forth. This pattern indicates low internal commitment to a fixed norm and a high sensitivity to context and perceived incentives. They appear to condition their behavior on others’ contributions in a nuanced way. When others as a group contribute very generously, they sometimes respond with very low contributions, exploiting the high group provision to maximize their own payoff. At other times under similarly cooperative conditions, they respond with very high or maximal contributions, boosting the already high group cooperation. This mixture suggests a conditional strategy that alternates between “taking advantage” and “joining in,” rather than a purely consistent free-riding or purely consistent prosocial strategy. They seem to recognize that the marginal per-capita return is greater than one (since total group payoffs under high contribution are repeatedly higher than under low contribution), and they occasionally act in ways that align with internalizing this benefit by contributing a lot even when it does not maximize their immediate individual payoff relative to free-riding. However, they repeatedly exploit cooperative others with low personal contributions while still earning high payoffs, showing that personal gains remain a dominant driver. Fairness norms appear weak or flexible. The player often contributes substantially less than the group average in highly cooperative environments but also, in other instances, contributes as much or more than many others without any external enforcement. This indicates they do not adhere to a rigid equity or reciprocity rule like “match the group average” or “match the minimum,” but rather operate with a pragmatic, situation-specific notion of fairness that can accommodate both free-riding and generosity. Risk tolerance appears moderate to high. The player is willing to deviate sharply from the prevailing pattern—both by contributing much less than cooperative others and by abruptly jumping to high contributions despite uncertainty about future rounds. The absence of punishment or reward opportunities does not cause them to converge to consistent minimal contributions; instead, they continue to experiment across the full range from very low to full contributions. This exploratory variability suggests they are comfortable taking payoff and reputational (if identifiable) risks in pursuit of potential gains, and they do not appear anxious to lock into a single safe, conservative strategy. Overall, this player is best characterized as an adaptive, payoff-attentive conditional cooperator/free-rider hybrid: they respond to the cooperative environment, but not with a simple monotonic rule. They oscillate between exploiting group generosity and supporting it, updating their behavior frequently and treating norms as flexible rather than binding.

This player is a mostly high contributor who intermittently shades down their contribution in response to time pressure, impatience, and mild free‑riding opportunities rather than out of principled defection. They start the game fully cooperative and repeatedly choose the socially efficient contribution, especially once the group norm converges on it. They are not consistently “all‑in,” however: they occasionally contribute less than the apparent group norm, particularly when the discussion shifts toward outside concerns such as earnings, time, and leaving the study. These lower contributions tend to be moderate rather than extreme; they never go to zero and usually stay in the mid to high range. This suggests a type that understands and accepts the cooperative logic but is willing to take incremental advantage when there is little social pressure on them personally. Their decisions are somewhat volatile, not following a simple monotone trend; they fluctuate between full contributions and partial ones throughout the game. This pattern aligns with a conditional cooperator who combines norm following with opportunism: they clearly prefer mutual high contributions and do this often, but they also test and exploit moments when they feel overburdened or impatient. They do not appear highly risk‑averse: they are willing to contribute fully for many rounds even after observing others sometimes contribute less, presumably because the multiplier is strong and group coordination generally holds. Fairness matters to them, but not rigidly; they accept an unequal outcome in their favor in some instances and do not visibly try to correct it via their own contributions. Their main constraint appears to be time and task fatigue rather than fear of exploitation.

This player is a **hesitant conditional cooperator with mild opportunism and social-responsiveness**. They clearly understand that full contribution is collectively optimal and verbally endorse that norm, yet their own behavior oscillates between fully cooperative and partially free-riding. They start by contributing the full endowment, then quickly experiment with keeping everything and later settle mostly on contributing about half. When the group norm of full contribution is strong and salient, they join it; when attention drifts or others reduce contributions, they revert to intermediate contributions rather than staying fully cooperative. Their contributions are thus **conditional on perceived group behavior and social pressure**, not on a fixed internal rule. They show awareness of the efficiency structure of the game and explain to others that maximal contribution is best if everyone does it, indicating at least basic numeracy about the multiplier and a concern with group payoff. However, they also knowingly under-contribute in several phases while publicly promoting the full-contribution norm, which signals **willingness to exploit group cooperation at times**. Stability is moderate rather than high: they switch between full and partial contributions multiple times. This suggests ongoing **strategic experimentation** and/or ambivalence between self-interest and group welfare. They do not display strong risk aversion in the sense of “playing safe” with low contributions; instead, they are comfortable both with fully cooperative and moderately selfish moves, adjusting dynamically as the social context changes. Fairness norms matter to them, but in a somewhat self-serving way. They advocate that all should contribute fully and criticize perceived holdouts, yet they themselves are among the main deviations from that same norm in some stretches. They seem most comfortable when their own behavior roughly matches a cooperative group but with a small personal edge, rather than strict equality. Overall, this player behaves like a **socially-aware, conditional cooperator** who occasionally exploits the group, especially when they believe they can get away with it, but who also converges back to full cooperation once group expectations and scrutiny tighten.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They always allocate the entire endowment to the public fund and never experiment with lower amounts, even when others start contributing less or when total group contributions drop. Their behavior fits a “principled efficiency maximizer”: they appear to understand that full contribution is socially efficient and act accordingly, independent of short‑term individual gain. They do not visibly condition their own contributions on others’ deviations or on being rewarded; they maintain the same behavior whether the group is fully cooperative or partially free‑riding. This suggests a strong internal norm for contributing “what’s best for everyone” and a willingness to bear personal risk for that outcome. They implicitly endorse the view that maximizing the multiplied public fund is the right strategy, and they do not hedge against possible end‑game effects even when the game is advanced (unknown to them, late rounds). Their tolerance for others’ lower contributions seems relatively high in action: they do not retaliate by reducing their own, despite clear evidence of some players contributing less than the maximum. Fairness concerns are present but appear framed as “everyone can be better off if we all do the efficient thing” rather than “I will match or punish low contributors.” Overall, they behave as a consistent, norm‑driven full cooperator.

This player is a strongly pro-social, near-unconditional high contributor who nonetheless experiments briefly with lower contributions. Their default pattern is to give the full endowment to the public fund and to state explicitly in chat that they do so consistently. They appear to accept the group’s emerging norm that maximal contribution is best, and they follow it even when some others contribute less. They does not condition their contributions tightly on others’ behavior: even when several group members free-ride or reduce their giving, this player quickly returns to contributing the maximum and maintains that pattern for the remainder of the game. This suggests a threshold for conditionality that is relatively forgiving; occasional or moderate deviations by others do not push them into sustained defection. The few departures from full contribution look more like short-lived tests or momentary hedging rather than a strategic shift. They lower their contribution in a context where many others also give less, then revert to full giving even though free-riding is still present later. This indicates some awareness of the incentive to hold back but a stronger preference for cooperative play and norm adherence. They shows a fairness norm focused on group efficiency rather than strict reciprocity. They endorse and model the idea that everyone should contribute fully because it benefits the whole group. They does not visibly try to match or punish specific low contributors through their own giving decisions, and they continues to cooperate even when the equality of contributions is imperfect. Risk tolerance appears moderately high in the social sense: they are willing to expose themselves to exploitation by persistent high contributions despite evidence that not everyone is perfectly cooperative. They seems comfortable trusting the group once a cooperative equilibrium is roughly in place and does not appear highly sensitive to short-term payoff risk. Overall, this player fits a “cooperative norm-follower with brief exploratory deviations”: they want to play the socially optimal strategy, are influenced by the group’s stated consensus, briefly test alternative actions when cooperation wavers, but then recommit to high contributions even under less-than-ideal conditions.

This player is primarily a high contributor who displays a strong cooperative baseline even when it is individually costly. They repeatedly choose to contribute fully despite a low marginal per-capita return and despite others visibly free-riding. Early in the game, they maintain full contributions in the face of explicit group suggestions to stop contributing, indicating they prioritize a prosocial or principled strategy over short-run payoff maximization. They do adapt somewhat: after sustained exposure to non-contributors and after personally being punished despite cooperating, they temporarily switch to not contributing. This shift occurs after having had ample evidence that others do not consistently match their cooperative level and that punishment is not reserved solely for defectors. However, the player returns to full contribution immediately afterward, even with mixed contributions by others and with the game about to end. This bounce-back suggests either a strong default preference for cooperation, a desire to “play fair” regardless of others, or possible confusion about payoff-maximizing behavior; which of these motivations is primary is unknown. Their contribution pattern shows conditionality but only weakly so: they tolerate substantial non-cooperation before reducing their own contribution, and the reduction is short-lived. They do not appear to anchor strongly on equal-treatment or strict reciprocity norms; instead they are willing to keep contributing more than many others while getting relatively worse payoffs. Risk tolerance seems moderate to high regarding social risk: they repeatedly expose themselves to exploitation by defectors rather than quickly switching to self-protective free-riding.

This player is a high, norm-driven contributor who oscillates only briefly when a salient group member discourages contribution. Their default is to contribute fully to the public good, even when this is individually costly given the low return rate. They appear to treat full contribution as the “right” behavior rather than as an outcome of payoff maximization. Their one clear deviation from full contribution aligns with an explicit norm message from another player rather than with payoffs per se, suggesting they are somewhat influenceable by perceived group leaders or dominant voices. They do not visibly condition their own contribution on specific others’ past behavior in a fine-grained way; instead, they maintain high contributions even when some others free-ride. Overall, they exhibit a strong cooperative orientation, relatively low concern for short-run monetary self-interest, and some susceptibility to coordination messages even when those messages undermine cooperation.

This player is a strict, stable full cooperator. They always contribute the maximum allowable amount and never deviate, even when others start partially or fully free‑riding and even when that reduces their own payoff relative to defectors. Their behavior is not conditional on recent contributions or payoffs; they do not experiment with lower contributions, and they do not try to “punish” defectors by reducing their own contributions. They appear to operate on a simple cooperative heuristic: “always put everything in” when the multiplier makes cooperation highly profitable for the group. They explicitly reference having seen a variant where greed is penalized across rounds, suggesting they are aware of dynamic incentives, but they still choose to keep contributing fully here where no such penalty exists. They show no risk‑aversion toward potential end‑of‑game exploitation; despite not knowing when the game ends, they continue full cooperation through the final round rather than “cashing out.” The pattern suggests a payoff‑seeking but group‑oriented type who trusts that sustained high cooperation remains best and does not try to strategically time defection.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They always allocate the full endowment to the public fund and never test lower amounts, even when others repeatedly free‑ride or shave contributions. Their behavior tracks a strong fairness/efficiency norm: they appear to adopt “everyone should go all‑in; that’s the right and profitable thing to do” as a fixed rule and do not deviate from it strategically. They tolerate being exploited by free‑riders rather than adjusting downward, suggesting low sensitivity to short‑term payoff disadvantage and a willingness to accept personal loss to maintain group efficiency and norm consistency. There is no evidence that they condition their own contribution on others’ previous behavior; their pattern remains rigidly cooperative in good and bad environments alike. Risk tolerance is relatively high in the social sense: they are willing to keep contributing fully even when it becomes clear that some group members repeatedly under‑contribute and that there is no punishment or reward mechanism. They seem more motivated by sustaining a cooperative equilibrium and adhering to the “all‑in” agreement than by maximizing relative earnings. Strategic sophistication appears moderate: they clearly understand that universal full contribution is collectively profit‑maximizing, but they do not exploit others’ deviations nor hedge against them. Overall, they fit a “principled unconditional cooperator” type: they pick the cooperative norm early and then stick to it, rather than playing a reactive or opportunistic strategy.

This player is a “quiet, opportunistic conditional cooperator.” They clearly understand that full contribution is highly profitable when others also contribute, and they repeatedly join and sustain full-cooperation phases. However, they are not purely cooperative: they occasionally and deliberately free-ride, especially once the group has established a strong cooperative norm and after social tensions about “greedy birds” have mostly cooled. Their default pattern is high contribution: they match the group’s full-cooperation norm many times and help re‑stabilize it after brief disruptions. Interspersed with this, they insert lower contributions—sometimes sharply lower, sometimes only slightly below the norm. These deviations are not random. They tend to occur after the group has been stable and when others have either recently deviated or been called out, suggesting a strategic mix of “blend in most of the time, skim extra some of the time.” They show clear conditionality: when others overall stay generous, they mostly continue contributing generously themselves. They never trigger or escalate a collapse into widespread defection. Even when they under-contribute, they keep group returns high and revert back to full contribution quickly, indicating a concern for maintaining the cooperative equilibrium that benefits them as well. Their fairness norm appears pragmatic rather than moralistic. They accept that others sometimes under-contribute and continue to cooperate rather than retaliate by fully defecting in subsequent rounds. At the same time, their repeated, small-scale free-riding indicates they see “a bit extra for me” as acceptable as long as it does not jeopardize the group’s earnings. Risk-wise, they are moderately risk-taking in the sense of exploiting free-riding opportunities despite social scrutiny (others explicitly talk about watching “the birds”). But they avoid extreme, repeated zero-contribution behavior that could draw sustained ire or trigger group-wide breakdown. Instead, they choose middle or modest deviations (low but not always zero), which are less conspicuous yet still profitable. Overall, the contribution profile fits an adaptive opportunist: high, norm-following cooperation punctuated by calculated deviations to increase personal payoff, while maintaining the overarching cooperative structure.

This player is a highly cooperative, near-conditional cooperator with one brief exploratory deviation. Their default is to contribute the full endowment every round, and they maintain this pattern consistently even when others occasionally contribute less and reduce group payoffs. The single mid-game partial contribution appears exploratory rather than the start of a defection trend: it happens once, is not repeated, and is smaller in magnitude than several peers’ deviations at that time. They clearly understand the monetary incentives and conversion rate and explicitly think in terms of expected earnings, yet they continue to prioritize full contributions, indicating that they internalize the group-efficiency logic of the game. They do not escalate defection in response to others’ greed; instead they re-stabilize on full cooperation, suggesting a strong fairness norm anchored on “everyone puts in the max” and a low propensity to retaliate. There is no evidence they condition their contribution on specific individuals’ past behavior (e.g., “birds”)—they continue contributing fully even when those players under-contribute—so they act more like a principled cooperator than a fine-grained conditional strategist. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to sustain full cooperation despite repeated minor free-riding, trusting that the group will mostly continue to cooperate and that the high multiplier keeps this profitable.

This player is a pure, unwavering free-rider. They consistently give nothing to the public good in every decision, despite being surrounded by high and fairly stable contributions from others. Their contribution pattern is perfectly stable (always zero), showing no experimentation, no conditional cooperation, and no reaction to visible changes in others’ behavior. They appear to adhere to a strictly self-interested payoff-maximizing rule: keep the full endowment and take the redistributed benefits generated by others. The fact that they never adjust their behavior when others are generous, when some peers reduce contributions, or when the active player count changes suggests a fixed strategy rather than adaptive learning. They do not display fairness norms in the usual PGG sense: they accept being a net beneficiary of others’ contributions without ever matching or reciprocating. This is consistent with an instrumental view of the game where the only relevant criterion is maximizing personal earnings, not maintaining proportionality or avoiding exploitation of others. Risk tolerance is oriented toward strategic safety rather than social risk: always keeping the endowment guarantees that their payoff never depends on whether others continue to contribute. They effectively insure themselves against a collapse of cooperation by never putting any of their own resources at stake, relying entirely on the public returns provided by others. Overall, this type is best described as a “hard, non-learning free-rider”: a player who pre-commits to zero contribution, does not revise that rule in light of group behavior, and is comfortable gaining from others’ cooperation without engaging in it themselves. Their choice pattern provides no evidence of conditional cooperation, altruism, inequality aversion, or experimentation; any such motives are either absent or strictly dominated by a commitment to free-riding (confidence: high).

This player is a stable, unconditionally high contributor. They consistently give the maximum possible amount to the public fund, even when others contribute less on average and when some players clearly free‑ride. Their behavior indicates a strong internal norm favoring full cooperation rather than a conditional “match others” strategy. They do not appear sensitive to inequality in contributions, nor do they adjust downward in response to moderate free‑riding. This suggests they either prioritize collective efficiency or adhere to a personal principle of full contribution, regardless of others. Their willingness to forgo all private endowment for the public fund indicates low concern for immediate personal payoff and a high tolerance for the strategic risk that others might exploit their generosity. Whether this is driven by altruism, moral rule-following, or expectation of mutual high contributions is unknown.

This player is a stable, moderate cooperator. They consistently contribute exactly half of their endowment and do not adjust this level in response to others, suggesting a pre-set heuristic rather than reactive strategy. Their contributions are lower than the group’s typical behavior, indicating they accept benefiting from higher contributors while still maintaining a non-minimal, “respectable” level of giving. This points to a mixed motive: they balance self-interest with a baseline fairness norm that avoids extreme free-riding but does not aim for full efficiency. They do not show strong conditional cooperation; even when many others contribute much more, they do not escalate their own contribution. Risk tolerance regarding being “too generous” appears low: they avoid matching high contributors, possibly to protect their own payoff or due to skepticism that full cooperation is sustainable. They appear to anchor on a simple internal rule like “give some, keep some” rather than calculating best responses to the multiplier or observed group behavior.

The player fits a “selectively cooperative opportunist” type. They are clearly willing to contribute fully to the public good but do not make this unconditional. Their contributions switch between full provision and complete free-riding rather than using intermediate levels, suggesting a simple, binary heuristic rather than fine-tuned calibration. They appear sensitive to what others do: they cooperate when group cooperation is strong, defect when cooperation has visibly weakened, then return to full cooperation once cooperation rebounds. This pattern indicates conditional cooperation with opportunistic exploitation rather than a strict fairness norm; they accept benefiting from others’ contributions when average cooperation is lower, and they do not seem to self-correct for that. Risk tolerance in contributing seems moderate: they are willing to risk cooperating even when some free-riding is present, but not when cooperation falls substantially. Their behavior suggests they prioritize personal payoff while still valuing high-cooperation outcomes when others also contribute.

This player alternates between full cooperation and full defection in a way that appears responsive to the group’s average behavior and to dynamic incentives, rather than following a fixed rule. They are willing to contribute everything when the group is relatively cooperative, but they also defect for at least one round despite high previous cooperation levels and in the presence of appeals to keep contributing. This suggests a conditional cooperator with a substantial self-interested streak, rather than a strict unconditional cooperator or unconditional defector. Their choices indicate awareness that contributing everything is socially efficient given the multiplication rate, but they are not committed to maximizing social surplus at personal cost. Instead, they seem to be probing or exploiting: enjoying cooperative payoffs when the group is generous, then opportunistically taking the endowment when cooperation has slipped, and later returning to full contribution when the group becomes highly cooperative again. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to cooperate even when some others free-ride, but not so tolerant that they keep contributing through clear declines in contributions. Fairness norms are present but not absolute: they contribute fully in mostly cooperative environments yet also accept taking a free-rider position when conditions are less favorable.

This player is a volatile, opportunistic conditional cooperator with a strong self-interest focus. They neither free-ride consistently nor cooperate consistently; instead, they alternate between generous and minimal contributions in a way that appears responsive to recent group behavior and payoffs. They show willingness to contribute well above the group’s central tendency when they perceive at least some cooperative foundation, especially when a few peers also contribute nontrivially. However, when the group’s overall contributions look low or erratic, they readily switch to very low or zero contributions, accepting deterioration in public good provision rather than “carrying” the group. They does not appear to follow a simple tit-for-tat or strict matching rule. Instead, they sometimes “test” higher contributions after low-contribution phases, even when others have not clearly reciprocated previously. This suggests a mix of experimentation and residual cooperative motive, not pure payoff-maximizing free-riding. Their choices indicate moderate risk tolerance: they occasionally take the risk of contributing high amounts in an environment with many low contributors, absorbing substantial payoff losses relative to others in those instances. At the same time, after such losses, they tends to swing back toward self-protective free-riding, showing sensitivity to exploitation. Fairness norms appear present but weakly enforced: they is clearly willing to be more generous than many peers at times, but not persistently. When the burden of cooperation becomes too one-sided or when others’ low contributions become evident, they reduces their own contributions sharply instead of maintaining a principled level of giving. Overall, the player’s “type” can be summarized as a reactive, self-interested conditional cooperator: willing to help if others help enough, quick to defect when they feel the environment is uncooperative, yet still occasionally testing for renewed cooperation with nontrivial contributions even after being exploited.

This player is a low-to-moderate, highly conditional contributor who prioritizes individual payoff over stable cooperation. They start with small positive contributions, briefly experiment with medium and even very high giving, but repeatedly revert to very low or zero contributions whenever the group’s behavior becomes noisy or exploitable. Their contributions fluctuate rather than follow a smooth trend; they show no fixed rule like “always give X” or “match the group,” but instead probe different levels and then opportunistically free-ride when others are generous. They are clearly sensitive to payoff consequences: when contributing more leaves them with relatively lower earnings than several lower-contributing peers, they reduce their contributions in later decisions. When contributions from others rise, they occasionally respond with higher giving, but this response is inconsistent and short-lived; they are not a stable conditional cooperator. They appear willing to shoulder some cost for the public good when they expect at least moderate participation from others, but they quickly withdraw support when they see either heavy free-riding or an opportunity to benefit from others’ generosity at low personal cost. Their fairness norm, if any, is weak: they repeatedly end up on the advantageous side of payoff comparisons while contributing less than several peers, and they do not correct this by increasing future contributions in a sustained way. This suggests a tolerance for advantageous inequity. Their risk tolerance is moderate: they are willing at times to make a large, payoff-risky contribution despite prior evidence of freeriding, but they do not stick with that risk; once it fails to secure a clear advantage, they return to safer, low-contribution behavior. Overall, their “type” is best characterized as a payoff-oriented, opportunistic conditional contributor: experimentation with cooperation is present, but the default anchor is low contribution and self-interest, not norm-following or strong reciprocity.

This player is a highly stable, unconditional full contributor. They always allocate the entire endowment to the public fund and never experiment with free-riding or partial contribution. Their behavior does not change in response to being punished, to evidence of one player occasionally not contributing, or to group-level fluctuations in total return. They treat “everyone contributes fully” as the default norm and continue to uphold it consistently, even when others discuss deductions, manipulation, or possible experimental “traps.” They appear risk-tolerant with respect to exploitation: they accept the possibility that others may deviate or abuse punishment, yet they do not hedge by holding back contributions. Their fairness norm emphasizes maximizing group efficiency rather than protecting themselves from opportunistic individuals.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator under the given binary choice structure. They always allocate their entire endowment to the public fund and never deviate, even when others sometimes free-ride or when they themselves receive substantial punishment. They do not appear to condition contributions on group behavior, on their own payoff shocks, or on perceived unfairness; they instead hold a simple, consistent heuristic that putting everything into the pot is the right thing to do. Their behavior suggests they adopt the group-efficiency norm that everyone should contribute fully and that this norm should hold regardless of short-run individual losses. There is no visible evidence of risk-sensitive experimentation or opportunistic defection, even when they express uncertainty about the purpose of the study and about how many rounds there will be. They implicitly accept unequal outcomes (being punished, others earning more or less) without changing their cooperative stance, suggesting they prioritize collective gain and rule-following over short-term self-protection. How they would behave under more granular contribution options than “0 or all” is unknown.

This player is a rigid, unconditional full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment, never deviating despite repeated opportunities to free‑ride or respond to others’ behavior. Their cooperation is stable and not contingent on being punished, on others’ occasional deviations, or on suspicions about the study design. They explicitly frame their behavior as “contributing all to the team” and express enthusiasm for collective success, indicating a strong fairness and efficiency norm: everyone should contribute fully so the group maximizes total payoff. They appear risk‑tolerant with respect to exploitation: even when they are repeatedly deducted against, they do not withhold contributions or hedge. The behavior suggests a principle‑driven or norm‑driven cooperative type rather than a payoff‑maximizing conditional cooperator. There is no evidence of strategic end‑game defection; they maintain full contributions through the final round, despite not knowing when the game ends and despite accumulating negative experiences from punishment.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator in this environment. They always choose the maximal contribution and never deviate, even when personally targeted by deductions and when at least one other player occasionally free-rides or disrupts the norm. Their cooperation is not contingent on short-term personal payoff or on perfect reciprocity from others; they maintain full contributions through both “good” and “bad” phases of the game. They explicitly frame the situation as a team optimization problem and endorse a norm that everyone should contribute fully so the group maximizes the multiplied pot. They show no signs of end-game defection, despite not knowing when the game ends. They appear payoff-aware: they reference how contributions and deductions affect bonuses and note that some behaviors are “just losing [the punisher] money,” indicating they track efficiency implications rather than just following a blind fairness rule. Overall, they fit a “pro-social, payoff-conscious cooperator” type: they value group efficiency and fairness, and are willing to bear individual losses from others’ misbehavior without reducing their own contributions.

This player is a perfectly stable, unconditional full cooperator under these conditions. They contribute the maximum possible amount in every decision, never deviating, even when (a) a known group member intermittently free-rides, (b) they themselves are repeatedly targeted with deductions, and (c) there is active discussion about whether to punish or retaliate. Their behavior fits a “principled cooperative maximizer” type: they appear to accept the group-efficiency logic that full contribution is best and then simply adhere to it. There is no observable conditionality on others’ contributions: they cooperate fully when everyone else does, when one member contributes nothing, and when that same low contributor later resumes contributing. They also maintain cooperation when deductions against them increase in size and frequency. This suggests a strong norm-based or rule-following motive rather than responsive reciprocity. Risk tolerance with respect to exploitation is high: they tolerate being repeatedly deducted without protecting themselves via reduced contribution or counter-punishment. They appear willing to absorb personal losses to preserve the high-return public fund structure. Fairness norms, as far as can be inferred from contributions alone, prioritize collective efficiency over equalization or retaliation.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator under the binary (all-or-nothing) contribution rule. They always invest their entire endowment into the public fund, regardless of being repeatedly punished, regardless of occasional zero contributions by a specific peer, and regardless of uncertainty about how many rounds remain. There is no observable conditionality on others’ behavior: they do not reduce their own cooperation in response to free-riding, suspected experimenter manipulation, or personal loss. Their implicit fairness norm is “everyone should contribute fully”; they align with the group’s pro-cooperation narrative and never deviate from it. They tolerate risk to their own payoff (accepting repeated unexplained deductions) without altering contribution behavior, suggesting that maximizing group efficiency and/or following the perceived ‘right’ rule is more salient than short‑term personal gain.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment and never experiment with free-riding, even when others occasionally deviate or when they themselves are repeatedly targeted with deductions. Their behavior suggests they understand that full contribution maximizes group payoffs and are willing to follow that norm regardless of short-term losses. There is no evidence that their cooperation is contingent on others’ actions; they keep contributing fully when a specific player intermittently contributes nothing and when they suffer repeated deductions. They seem relatively risk-tolerant about being exploited: they do not hedge or reduce contributions in response to noise, punishment, or perceived unfairness. They also appear comfortable with minor inequality in outcomes as long as the cooperative norm is broadly upheld.

This player is a fully cooperative, highly stable contributor. They always give their entire endowment, with no experimentation or deviation, even when others occasionally contribute nothing and when they themselves are repeatedly punished. Their behavior fits a “principled team maximizer”: they act as if the socially optimal outcome (everyone contributing fully) is the correct default, and they do not switch away from it in response to losses or perceived unfairness. They explicitly endorse the norm that universal contribution makes everyone better off and accept this as the focal coordination point. They show no signs of conditional cooperation (e.g., “I’ll stop if others defect”); instead, they cooperate unconditionally, including when one group member acts erratically and when deductions against them increase. Risk tolerance is high in the sense that they keep exposing themselves to free-riding and punishment while sticking to full cooperation, apparently prioritizing group efficiency and/or alignment with the experimenter’s presumed goal over short-run personal payoff insurance. They also entertain the idea that some players may not be “human” or may be operating under different instructions, but this does not change their contribution strategy. This suggests they treat the contribution choice as part of “playing the game correctly” rather than as a narrow monetary optimization problem.

This player is a conditional cooperator with a quick shift toward pro-social behavior once some information about the group is revealed. Initially, they free-ride when cooperation norms are still unclear and costly to them, indicating sensitivity to immediate private payoff and uncertainty rather than an unconditional altruistic norm. After observing that multiple others are willing to contribute, they move to full contribution and then maintain it stably, even when some others continue to free-ride. This suggests that once they infer a minimally cooperative environment, they are willing to incur a definite personal cost to support the public good. They do not appear to condition contributions finely on the exact pattern of others’ behavior; instead they settle into a “cooperate by default” mode once cooperation looks viable. They accept that full contribution is individually suboptimal in strict payoff terms but continue anyway, implying a preference for group efficiency and/or fairness over narrow self-interest, at least once a cooperative norm is salient. Their risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to risk being exploited by a few defectors as long as a cooperative majority is present, but they are not willing to be the first consistent high contributor in a completely ambiguous environment. Overall, they follow a “test, then commit” strategy: start cautiously, then cooperate fully when group-level cooperation seems sustainable.

This player is a mostly cooperative, mildly strategic contributor. They are clearly willing to give the full endowment to the public good and do so in most situations, even when others do not all match their level. They are not a pure unconditional cooperator, however: at least once they withhold their contribution while still benefiting from others’ giving, indicating that they sometimes prioritize payoff maximization or “taking a turn” free-riding. Their contribution pattern suggests conditional cooperation with flexibility rather than rigid fairness enforcement. They tolerate the presence of at least one clear non-contributor without withdrawing their own cooperation, and they later contribute fully even when a persistent low contributor remains, suggesting relatively high tolerance for imperfect group behavior and a focus on maintaining group efficiency. They accept personal payoff sacrifices from contributing more than some peers, so they appear risk-tolerant toward potential exploitation, but not to the point of never free-riding themselves. Their fairness norm appears to be “mostly everyone should contribute, and egregious free-riding can be sanctioned,” rather than a strict requirement that every single player always match them.

This player is a flexible, opportunistic conditional cooperator who alternates between full contribution and full free-riding rather than settling into a single stable pattern. They are clearly willing to contribute fully to the public good even when others do not, indicating some baseline pro-social or experiment-oriented inclination. At the same time, they are also willing to fully free-ride while others contribute heavily, indicating comfort with exploiting cooperative environments when it seems safe or advantageous. Their behavior suggests they pay attention to the group’s overall cooperation level and adjust their own contribution accordingly, but not in a purely fairness-driven way. They tolerate being the only defector among mostly high contributors and do not immediately “correct” that asymmetry by increasing future contributions; they later return to full contribution even though their personal payoffs from free-riding are high. This pattern points to mixed motives: they care about payoff maximization but also about participating in and possibly sustaining cooperation under some conditions. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they accept the risk of being punished when defecting, but they do not persistently exploit; instead, they later move back to contributing fully even after having confirmed that free-riding can be profitable. Their fairness norm is asymmetric: they do not require strict equality of contributions, and they are comfortable benefitting more than others for a time, yet they are not a pure selfish defector and eventually share the cost of provision again. Overall, their “type” in contribution decisions is best characterized as an adaptive, payoff-sensitive conditional cooperator who sometimes tests or exploits cooperative norms but does not abandon cooperation entirely.

This player is a strict free‑rider: they never contribute to the public pool despite consistently receiving high group contributions. Their behavior is stable and not conditional on others’ generosity; even when everyone else contributes fully, they still keep their entire endowment. This indicates that maximizing personal payoff dominates any fairness or reciprocity concerns. They appear comfortable benefiting from others’ cooperation without matching it, suggesting low guilt or low weight on inequality aversion. Risk tolerance regarding being punished for free‑riding is moderate to high: they continue not contributing even after experiencing multiple costly punishments, implying that the expected gains from free‑riding outweigh, in their mind, the risk of further sanctions.

This player is a near-unconditional full contributor who quickly settles into a highly cooperative norm and maintains it. After an initial exploratory deviation to a lower contribution once, they revert to giving the maximum and then keep doing so consistently, regardless of others’ minor deviations or the presence of punishment and reward. They appear to treat full contribution as the default “right” behavior and do not systematically condition their own giving on others’ exact levels, as long as the group remains broadly cooperative. When one peer contributes notably less but still contributes a positive amount, the player continues to give fully rather than experimenting with partial free‑riding or tit‑for‑tat. Even when a peer contributes nothing late in the game, the player still contributes fully, suggesting a strong internal cooperation norm and low appetite for exploiting others’ generosity. Their behavior is consistent with a fairness norm that others should also contribute fully, but they enforce this norm via punishment rather than by reducing their own contribution. Risk tolerance regarding being “the sucker” appears moderate: they accept the risk of over‑contributing relative to some others, and manage perceived unfairness primarily through sanctions instead of withholding contributions.

This player is a strict, unconditional full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment, from the beginning to the end of the game, without any adjustment in response to others’ deviations, group composition, or changing average contributions. They do not appear to condition their behavior on fairness norms (they keep contributing fully even when someone contributes much less or nothing) or on strategic payoff considerations (they never exploit opportunities to free-ride when others are highly cooperative). Their behavior suggests they either prioritize the group outcome or treat “max contribution” as a fixed rule, independent of others’ actions. Risk tolerance regarding exploitation by free-riders appears high; they accept personal payoff risk rather than reduce their contribution. There is no sign of exploratory or volatile behavior; their strategy is highly stable and rigid.

This player is a high, near-fully cooperative contributor with occasional strategic under-contribution. Their default pattern is to match or nearly match the apparent cooperation norm, contributing the full endowment or just below it in most rounds. Deviations downward occur intermittently and are not escalating over time; instead they appear as isolated tests or self-serving adjustments, not a sustained drift toward free-riding. When they under-contribute, they still remain well above classic “defector” levels and quickly return to full contributions. They show no evidence of responding to small variations in others’ contributions, which remain uniformly high; their own few low contributions instead invite strong punishment from others, suggesting they understand they are violating an implicit high-cooperation norm. Despite repeated costly punishment against them for these deviations, they do not permanently shift to pure compliance or pure defection; instead, they sometimes probe again with moderate shortfalls. This suggests moderate risk tolerance and some willingness to trade off fairness norms against personal gain, but within a narrow band. In sum, they are a mostly cooperative, norm-aware player who occasionally experiments with modest self-serving deviations, even in a harsh punishment environment.

This player is a strict, unwavering full contributor. They always choose the cooperative option regardless of past outcomes, the presence of free-riders, or fluctuations in group contributions. Their strategy is stable rather than reactive; they do not reduce their own contributions in response to others’ non-cooperation. They frame the cooperative strategy as the “best profit” approach, indicating that they see full contribution as both normatively right and instrumentally rational. There is no evidence of conditional cooperation, threshold behavior, or experimentation; they appear willing to tolerate exploitation as long as they personally gain and the group-level strategy remains profitable. Risk tolerance is relatively high: they continue contributing even when returns drop close to break-even, suggesting comfort with payoff variability as long as the general cooperative norm is preserved.

The player is a strict, unwavering full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment, from start to finish, regardless of others’ behavior or the stage of the game. Their cooperation is stable rather than conditional: they explicitly state that they keep contributing because they “care for” the group, even while repeatedly observing persistent free-riding by specific others. They show awareness that some participants are not contributing and that this reduces payoffs, but they do not experiment with defection, partial cooperation, or contingent strategies. Their fairness norm appears to be strong: they regard non-contributors as morally inferior “losers” and “wastes,” and they clearly feel that contributing is part of being a “winner” and a decent group member. They tolerate personal payoff losses relative to non-contributors in order to maintain a high-contribution norm, suggesting low concern for narrow monetary self-interest and high concern for norm adherence and group-level efficiency. Their risk tolerance is high in the sense that they are willing to risk being exploited repeatedly rather than reduce their own cooperation.

This player is a strict, unconditional full cooperator in this setting. They always contribute their entire endowment in every round, with no observable deviation or experimentation, even when others free-ride, conditional cooperators peel off, or the group’s overall cooperation level drops. They treat full contribution as the default rule rather than something to be continuously renegotiated. Their behavior shows no sensitivity to short-term profit optimization; they keep cooperating despite clear and repeated evidence that some players systematically contribute nothing. There is no sign of contingent reciprocity, targeted responses to specific free-riders, or strategic withholding to discipline defectors. Their fairness norm appears to be: “I do my part regardless,” not “I match what others do.” This implies a cooperative type that is either strongly norm-driven or intrinsically motivated by contributing, with low responsiveness to exploitation and low aversion to being taken advantage of. Risk tolerance is high with respect to being exploited: they accept predictable losses relative to defectors without protest, exit, or strategic adjustment.

This player fits a “calculating opportunist with light reputational concern” type. They clearly understand that full free-riding is profitable in this setting and most of their decisions follow that logic: they contribute infrequently and predominantly keep their endowment, exploiting a high and fairly stable contribution rate from others. Their pattern is not random; it alternates between short stretches of free-riding and occasional “all in” rounds, suggesting deliberate mixing rather than confusion. They show a weak form of conditional cooperation: early on, after seeing that almost everyone else contributes, they briefly join the cooperative majority, which reduces their material payoff compared to free-riding. Later, even when there are several persistent non-contributors, they still sometimes choose to contribute, implying that pure monetary payoff maximization is not their only motive. These cooperative choices are scattered and do not lock into a stable pattern, so their contribution rule appears to be: default to free-riding, occasionally contribute to maintain some social acceptance or to test whether high cooperation can be sustained. They do not exhibit strong fairness norms. They repeatedly benefit from others’ high contributions, even once they are publicly labeled as a “waste” by cooperators, and this does not induce a lasting shift toward consistent giving. However, they also avoid going to the extreme of never contributing; instead, they contribute enough times to signal that they are “with the group” at least intermittently. Their risk tolerance seems moderate: they are willing to “gamble” on contributing even when it is likely unprofitable given the observed presence of multiple free-riders, but they do not do so frequently or aggressively.

This player is a strict, unconditional full cooperator under the given action set. They always choose the maximum contribution available and never deviate, regardless of how others behave or how the group’s average changes. Their pattern is extremely stable: there is no sign of experimentation, strategic withholding, or contingent response to free-riding or partial cooperation. They behave as if the salient reference point is the socially optimal outcome rather than their own short-run material payoff. The environment makes cooperation individually profitable as well as socially efficient, and their actions consistently align with that structure; they do not attempt to exploit others’ contributions even when free-riding would clearly increase their own immediate payoff. They appear tolerant of risk in the sense of being willing to cooperate fully even when others’ contributions fluctuate and sometimes fall well below full cooperation. They do not hedge or “test” the group by lowering their own contribution when others under-contribute, suggesting either a strong norm of contributing fully or a simple rule: always cooperate when cooperation is payoff-dominant. They show no evidence of strategic conditional cooperation (e.g., matching average contributions, rewarding cooperators or punishing defectors through their own choices). Instead they follow a rigid, one-size-fits-all contribution rule that ignores short-run inequity and potential exploitation. This is consistent with a persona that prioritizes rule-following or efficiency over responsive reciprocity. Overall, they embody a highly stable, principled cooperator type: maximum contributions, no volatility, no detectable opportunism, and no behavioral adjustment to variation in peers’ choices.

This player alternates between full cooperation, partial cooperation, and complete free-riding with no stable pattern. They begin by advocating universal full contribution and initially follow that norm themselves, but quickly switch to contributing nothing even while everyone else continues to fully contribute. Over the game, they repeatedly oscillate: sometimes sending nothing when others all send the maximum, sometimes sending half, and sometimes matching the group. These shifts do not clearly track changes in others’ behavior or punishment intensity; they continue to deviate even after very heavy, repeated sanctions and clear social pressure. Their contributions therefore appear weakly conditional at best and not reliably responsive to either norms or incentives. They sometimes “return” to full contribution after punishment, but soon break the norm again, suggesting they do not internalize the cooperative rule as binding. Fairness norms, if present, are unstable; the player benefits substantially from others’ cooperation when free-riding, yet shows no consistent move toward fairness even after learning that punishment for deviation is severe. Risk tolerance appears high: they persist in norm violations despite being a clear, heavily targeted outlier and facing very large, predictable losses. Overall, they play more like a noisy or contrarian agent than a payoff-maximizing conditional cooperator.

This player is a strict, unconditional high contributor. They always send the full endowment, without any visible experimentation or response to others’ deviations. They anchor on “everyone should send the max” as both a fairness and efficiency norm and do not relax this expectation even when free-riding persists. Their risk tolerance for being exploited appears low in attitude (they dislike defectors intensely) but high in practice: they continue to fully cooperate despite repeated noncompliance by some others. There is no evidence that they ever adjust contributions downward, so they function as a stable “moral cooperator” rather than a conditional cooperator.

This player is a strict, unconditional full cooperator. They always contribute the maximum possible amount in every round and never deviate, regardless of what others do. Their behavior fits a strong efficiency and norm-following motive rather than a responsive, tit-for-tat style: they keep cooperating fully even when some others repeatedly under-contribute or free-ride. They show no apparent risk-averse “hedging” or experimentation with lower contributions, even when noise, bots, or confusion are openly discussed. Fairness for them seems defined as “everyone sends the full amount,” not as matching the lowest or average contribution. They treat the environment as solved very early: contributing the maximum and pressing through quickly is “the” way to play, and they do not adjust this behavior later.

This player is a mostly-high contributor who intermittently free-rides in a way that looks calculated rather than random. They understand that full contribution is individually profitable under this MPCR and explicitly articulate that non-contributors are “losing more,” indicating clear comprehension of the payoff structure and an internalized efficiency norm of contributing fully. They alternate between full cooperation and zero contribution, not gradually shading contributions. The switches to zero do not appear driven by fear of others’ defection: they sometimes defect even when almost everyone else is reliably sending the maximum and strong social pressure to send the maximum exists. This suggests opportunistic “testing” of how much free-riding they can get away with, rather than conditional cooperation based strictly on others’ recent behavior. They quickly align with the stated social norm when it is described (“20 it is”), but then knowingly violate it later. This implies that their private incentive calculus is not perfectly aligned with their public commitments; they are willing to misrepresent their intended behavior in chat and then act differently.
