Overall, they are a high-ability, norm-aware player whose contribution pattern mixes genuine cooperative periods with self-serving exploitative episodes. They are not volatility-driven or chaotic; the pattern is binary (all-or-nothing) and suggests deliberate choices rather than confusion.

This player is a steadfast full contributor. They always contribute the entire endowment, with no variation across the game and no apparent experimentation. Their behavior is highly stable and not conditional on others’ deviations: even when several others free‑ride or partially defect, they still send the maximum. They appear to internalize a strong fairness/efficiency norm that “everyone should send the full amount,” and they adhere to it unilaterally rather than adjusting to protect their own payoff. There is no evidence of risk‑seeking experimentation or strategic under‑contribution; instead, they accept the risk of being exploited for the sake of maintaining cooperative efficiency. Their contributions do not react to punishment dynamics, threats, or changes in the group’s average: they act as an unconditional cooperator anchored on a high norm.

This player is a strong conditional cooperator with a stable, high-contribution norm anchored at the maximum. They consistently give the full endowment whenever they see that the group is broadly cooperating, even when a few others deviate. They only reduce their own contribution once, and then only moderately and opportunistically when they see that someone else is contributing almost nothing and others are also deviating. This deviation is brief and they return immediately to full contributions afterward, suggesting they treat the “take” option as acceptable only when others visibly break the norm. They appear to value group efficiency: they keep contributing fully even though the group is already heavily punishing defectors, indicating a preference for sustaining the public good over personal profit-maximization. Their contributions are extremely stable overall and only weakly sensitive to others’ minor deviations; they respond primarily to clear, persistent free-riding rather than small undercontributions. Risk tolerance in contribution is relatively high: they continue to contribute fully even when the environment is noisy and some players keep defecting, trusting that group punishment and norms will protect cooperation.

This player is a strong, near-unconditional high contributor to the public good. They send the full endowment almost every time, including early, middle, and late in the game, even when others defect or free-ride. The single observed deviation from maximum contribution is when they contribute nothing while still urging a chronic low contributor to “keep doing it” and threatening that they will “get nothing”; this looks more like a one-off expressive move than a shift in strategy. Their default norm is clearly “everyone sends the maximum,” and they repeatedly emphasize that sending the full amount makes everyone better off. They appear comfortable sacrificing private payoff, trusting that maintaining a high-contribution norm (enforced by punishment) is the best strategy. Their contributions are quite stable, not reactive to short-term deviations in others’ behavior; instead, they address deviations with punishment and social pressure rather than changing their own contribution. Risk tolerance with respect to group behavior is high: they keep contributing fully even when several others under-contribute or behave unpredictably, relying on enforcement rather than withdrawing cooperation.

This player is a high, fairly steady contributor who treats near-full cooperation as the default. Across the game, they usually give most or all of their endowment, with only modest fluctuations. They treat full contribution as normal when the group is already contributing well, but they do not rigidly fixate on 20; instead they hover in the mid–high range and occasionally drop a bit, suggesting some attention to personal payoff or experimentation rather than pure unconditional altruism. Their contributions are clearly conditional on the general cooperation climate, but only in a coarse way. When many others give high amounts, they match or exceed the group’s level. When there are a few clear very low contributors, they do not “join them” in low giving; instead they maintain high contributions and sometimes even increase. This indicates a fairness norm anchored on “everyone should contribute a lot,” not on strict equality with every individual. They tolerate some free-riding without sharply adjusting their own giving downward. They appear risk-tolerant and group-optimistic: they routinely put many coins into the public fund even though the marginal per-coin return is below 1 for an individual. This suggests they value the social efficiency of high total contributions and/or expect that enough others will also cooperate to make high giving worthwhile. There is no sign of opportunistic exploitation: they never systematically under-contribute relative to a cooperative majority to gain individually. When they do contribute less than the maximum, the deviations are small and not obviously targeted to exploit high contributors. The pattern fits a “prosocial but not rigidly self-sacrificing” type rather than a pure altruist or a strategic free-rider.

This player is a strict, unwavering full cooperator. They always contribute the entire endowment to the public fund and never adjust this behavior in response to others’ free-riding, changes in group average contributions, being punished, or progression of the game. Their strategy is stable and non-conditional: they do not appear to condition their contribution on peers’ behavior, on realized payoffs, or on any observed deviations from high cooperation. They behave as if guided by a strong internal norm that “the right thing” is to fully contribute, independent of whether this maximizes their own payoff or matches the group’s average. They accept substantial payoff variability caused by others’ under-contribution without any attempt to hedge by reducing their own contribution. This indicates low concern for individual payoff optimization and high willingness to bear the cost of sustaining the public good. In terms of risk tolerance, they effectively ignore the strategic risk that others may exploit them. They do not appear to explore or experiment; instead, they commit to a single, principled strategy from the beginning and maintain it. Fairness norms, if present, are asymmetric: they uphold a fairness standard in their own behavior (never free-riding) but do not adjust their contributions to enforce or mirror fairness in others.

This player is a highly cooperative, near-unconditional full contributor. They almost always give their entire endowment to the public fund and maintain this pattern from early to late game, with a single brief deviation to a lower but still positive contribution that does not persist. Their behavior suggests they treat full contribution as the default, not as a tactical response to short-term variation in others’ behavior. They do not visibly condition contributions on others’ average contributions, on the presence of low contributors, or on having been punished. They appear to follow a strong fairness or efficiency norm: “everyone should contribute a lot,” and they personally adhere to this norm consistently. Risk tolerance seems relatively high: they keep investing maximally in the public fund even when there is some low contribution and when they cannot see others’ payoffs, implying they rely on the game’s structure and group-level returns more than on precise tracking of individual free-riding.

This player is a moderately cooperative, highly stable contributor who anchors on a fixed heuristic rather than finely tuning to others. They generally give about half their endowment and maintain that level across almost all rounds, despite fluctuations in others’ behavior and in how they themselves are treated. They do not chase short‑term profit maximization: given the game’s parameters, their typical contribution is higher than a pure free‑rider would choose, but lower than the high cooperators in the group. Their pattern suggests a “rule of thumb” strategy: pick a comfortable mid‑level contribution and stick to it. The few deviations from this baseline are limited and do not lead to a lasting shift in behavior. When others contribute much more on average, they do not noticeably increase their own contribution to match; when others defect more or contributions drop, they also do not substantially lower their own contributions. This indicates limited conditional cooperation and a low sensitivity to fairness comparisons in their actual decisions, even though the group often contributes more than they do. They appear risk‑averse in terms of changing strategies: once their contribution rule is set, they mostly keep it, even after being punished, and even near the end of the game. They are willing to keep cooperating at a moderate level without strong evidence that this yields maximal payoff, which fits a “satisficing” rather than optimizing mindset. Their fairness norm seems to be “contribute something significant but not maximal,” and they tolerate being below the group’s average without reacting strategically.

This player is a generally high contributor with moderate volatility and clear conditionality. Across the game they usually give well above the group average, frequently near the maximum, suggesting a prosocial or efficiency-minded baseline. They do, however, occasionally drop to medium or very low contributions, indicating that their cooperation is not unconditional. Their contribution pattern appears sensitive to perceived fairness and possibly to others’ behavior. After periods where most peers seem to contribute moderately or highly, they maintain or increase their own contributions. When they sharply reduce their contribution, it occurs in contexts where some players free ride or where they themselves have recently invested heavily in punishment or been heavily punished. This suggests a contingent cooperator who expects reciprocity and becomes willing to withhold cooperation when norms feel violated or when they feel unfairly targeted. They do not consistently match the group mean; rather, they tend to set a cooperative anchor somewhat above typical contributions and then adjust downward in specific reactionary episodes. This points to a norm of “I’m willing to be one of the more generous players, but not if others exploit me or attack me.” Their occasional very low contribution indicates some willingness to test or exploit the public good when they feel wronged, but those instances are rare relative to their generally generous behavior. There is no strong evidence of high risk aversion; they repeatedly choose high contributions despite the availability of punishment and despite not being able to see others’ payoffs. This suggests that they prioritize maintaining the public good and/or upholding norms over narrowly maximizing short-term private earnings.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They always invest their full endowment into the public good, with no evidence of strategic withholding, even when others free-ride or when the game nears its end. Their behavior suggests they prioritize group efficiency and fairness norms over short-term individual gain. They show no visible sensitivity to minor deviations by others; they continue to contribute fully even when multiple others give nothing. Risk tolerance appears moderately high in the social sense: they are willing to rely on group cooperation and enforcement mechanisms rather than hedging by defecting themselves. There is no sign that they condition their contribution on specific individuals’ behavior; instead, they act as a principled cooperator.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They always give the full endowment to the public good, from start to finish, including early uncertain rounds, mid-game turbulence, and the final round. There is no evidence that they condition their contributions on others’ behavior, on recent defection, or on end-game proximity. They behave as if full cooperation is both the norm and the default strategy, even when some others free-ride and overall group contributions drop. This indicates a strong cooperative norm and low concern about being individually exploited. They appear risk-tolerant regarding payoff variance caused by others’ defection and behave more like a principled cooperator than a payoff-maximizing conditional cooperator. Fairness concerns, if present, do not translate into withholding contributions; they rely on other mechanisms (rewards by themselves and punishment by others) to handle unfairness rather than adjusting their own contribution.

The player is a mostly cooperative but opportunistic contributor with clear conditionality. They contribute the full endowment in the large majority of rounds, aligning with the group’s “everyone all‑in” norm and sustaining high group payoffs when others also contribute. However, they occasionally defect fully by contributing nothing, including once early and again in the final round, suggesting they are willing to exploit the public good when (a) others’ cooperation seems secure and (b) retaliation risk or social cost appears manageable. Their behavior indicates that they understand the payoff structure and are not naively generous; they toggle between full cooperation and full free‑riding rather than using intermediate or noisy patterns (though the game only allows 0 or 20). The timing of their defections (after punishment of them for a prior defection, and at the very end) reflects sensitivity to incentives and horizon, not confusion. They appear to hold a fairness norm that everyone should contribute fully each round, but they do not adhere to it unconditionally when they see opportunities to gain personally.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator within the binary-choice environment. They always contribute the maximum allowed amount and never experiment with defection, even when others free-ride or when cooperation temporarily breaks down. Their chat explicitly endorses the idea that everyone contributing fully is “the most efficient way,” and they stick to this regardless of short-term exploitation. They show high tolerance for free-riding in payoff terms: they do not adjust their own contribution in response to defectors, relying instead on the punishment mechanism and group norms to manage non-cooperators. They appear motivated by both efficiency (maximizing group earnings) and fairness (everyone “should” contribute), but they prioritize maintaining the efficient cooperative norm over personal profit optimization or retaliatory defection.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator under the given options. When faced with a binary choice between keeping the entire endowment or contributing all of it, they consistently choose to contribute fully. There is no evidence that they condition their behavior on the average behavior of others or on changes in group composition; they persist with full cooperation even when there are visible free riders and when overall group contributions fluctuate. This suggests a strong internal norm in favor of contributing rather than a purely payoff-maximizing calculus. Their risk tolerance regarding others’ potential defection is high: they remain cooperative despite clear evidence that some peers contribute nothing. Whether this is driven by prosocial preferences, norm-following, or a belief that high contribution is standard behavior is unknown, but their behavior aligns with a “principled cooperator” type who prioritizes group-beneficial action over short-term personal advantage.

This player is a stable, uncompromising free-rider. They systematically keep their entire endowment in every decision, despite a very high collective return to contribution. Their behavior shows no experimentation, no gradual adjustment, and no apparent sensitivity to others’ cooperation levels; they ignore the strong cooperative norm that emerges around them and do not move toward it. This suggests they prioritize individual payoff maximization over group efficiency and are willing to let others shoulder the cost of generating the public good. Their choices indicate low concern for distributive fairness in the sense of “paying their share”; they accept being a clear outlier on the low side of contributions. Risk attitude toward cooperation is unknown, because they never test contributing at all. They appear non-conditional: their contribution rule does not react to high average contributions, to decreasing or increasing cooperation by others, or to being rewarded. They display high strategy rigidity and low behavioral plasticity over the course of the game.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator in this environment. They always contribute their entire endowment instead of free-riding, even when several others consistently give nothing or drop to lower cooperation. Their behavior does not adjust in response to variation in others’ contributions, suggesting they follow a simple, rule-like strategy (“always cooperate”) rather than a contingent one (“cooperate if others do”). They appear to prioritize maximizing the group’s total payoff over their own short-term private gain, indicating a strong efficiency or prosocial norm. They accept the individual cost of contributing despite the presence of clear free-riders, which implies either a fairness ideal that forgives some exploitation, or indifference to being personally taken advantage of in the short run. There is no evidence of risk aversion or experimentation; they never test defection to see how the group reacts or to compare payoffs, even after exposure to repeated non-cooperation by several peers. This suggests a high level of commitment to cooperation that is not easily shaken by social noise or minor norm violations. Their contributions are not strategically tapered near the end of the game, indicating they are either not focused on end-game backward induction or do not base their behavior on expectations about remaining rounds. Overall, their contribution pattern is consistent with a principled cooperator rather than a conditional or strategic one.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They consistently choose the fully cooperative option, even when others contribute less and despite having no information about how many rounds remain. They behave as if cooperation is a standing rule rather than a response to short-term incentives or to specific peers’ behaviors. Their pattern indicates a strong internal norm for contributing, not a conditional “match others” strategy. They do not appear to reduce contributions in response to free-riding, suggesting either low sensitivity to exploitation, a belief that maintaining high cooperation is paramount, or a focus on efficiency over relative payoffs. Their choices are consistent with risk tolerance regarding being taken advantage of, and with a preference for social efficiency over maximizing own narrow monetary payoff.

This player is a conditional cooperator who quickly becomes an efficient free-rider once they feel they have “done enough.” They start fully cooperative, willingly bearing the full cost of contributing even when some group members do not. They then shift sharply to non-contribution, showing low commitment to sustained cooperation when they see that free-riding is both possible and personally profitable. Their contribution pattern is volatile rather than stable: they move from full cooperation to full defection instead of gradually adjusting. This suggests sensitivity to payoff incentives and observed heterogeneity in others’ behavior rather than a fixed fairness rule. They tolerate inequality in the group as long as their own payoff position is favorable; there is no evidence they aim to equalize or condition contribution on strict fairness. They appear risk-accepting with respect to social outcomes, willing to stop contributing even though many others still cooperate and without any guarantee others will continue to do so. The player likely holds a belief that once they have previously contributed and signaled cooperativeness, they can legitimately switch to exploiting the public good while maintaining a positive self-image (inferred from early full contributions followed by later free-riding). Confidence in this inference is moderate; exact motivations are unknown.

This player is a rigid full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment, never deviating once, regardless of variation in others’ behavior or changes in the group’s average contribution. Their strategy is extremely stable and not reactive: they do not lower contributions when some others free-ride, nor do they increase from an already maximal level when cooperation is high. This indicates a strong internal rule such as “always cooperate” rather than contingent reciprocity. They appear to follow an efficiency-oriented logic: full contribution is individually costly in a narrow sense, but highly beneficial at the group level given the relatively high multiplier. Their unwavering cooperation suggests that they prioritize group payoff over individual short-term gain, or possibly that they interpret the task as one where the “correct” choice is to fully invest in the public good. There is no evidence of conditional cooperation; their contribution does not track fluctuations in others’ average behavior. They tolerate repeated free-riding by some peers without any detectable retaliation or withdrawal of cooperation. This shows high social risk tolerance: they continue to expose themselves to exploitation without adjustment, implying either a strong normative commitment to cooperation, indifference to being exploited, or a belief that steadfast cooperation is still best for the group. Their fairness norm seems to be “everyone should contribute fully,” but they do not enforce it by changing their own behavior. They behave as a moral or principled cooperator rather than a strategic reciprocator. Learning or adaptation is minimal or absent: early and later behavior is indistinguishable, suggesting they set a rule at the start and persist with it regardless of feedback. They show no experimentation between available actions. Overall, this player is best described as a stable, unconditional, rule-based full cooperator who values collective outcomes and does not condition on others’ shortfalls.

This player is a strictly unconditional full cooperator in this environment. They always contribute the maximum possible amount and never deviate, regardless of fluctuations in others’ behavior or the stage of the game. Their contribution pattern is perfectly stable: there is no evidence of experimentation, strategic withholding, or reactionary defection after observing free-riding. They appear to operate on a strong efficiency/fairness norm: they consistently choose the action that maximizes total group surplus, even when some peers contribute less or nothing. The choice is not sensitive to short-term payoff variation; periods of weaker group cooperation do not trigger a reduction in their own contributions. This suggests that their internal standard for “what one should do” in such a game is contribution-maximizing rather than payoff-maximizing at the individual level. Conditional cooperation is not supported by the data: although others’ contributions sometimes drop, the focal player does not condition their own behavior on these changes. Instead, they maintain the same cooperative action under both favorable and unfavorable group conditions. Their risk tolerance with respect to being exploited by free riders is high; they accept the possibility that others might free ride without adjusting their own behavior. This is consistent with a player who either (a) places non-monetary value on acting cooperatively independent of outcomes, or (b) believes that persistent full cooperation from themselves helps stabilize or sustain high group contributions over time. Overall, their “type” is a norm-driven, high-commitment cooperator who prioritizes maintaining cooperation and group efficiency over guarding against individual exploitation.

This player is a strict, unconditional full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment, without any adjustment to others’ behavior, payoffs, or the stage of the game. Their strategy is extremely stable: there is no experimentation with free‑riding or partial cooperation, and no sign of conditional cooperation or reciprocity. They appear to treat full contribution as a fixed rule rather than a contingent response. They show no visible concern for short‑term individual monetary optimization and instead consistently choose the socially efficient option, even when some others repeatedly contribute nothing. Their risk tolerance regarding exploitation is high; they continue to cooperate fully despite persistent low contributors and without using available tools to discipline them.

This player is a mostly high, binary contributor who occasionally free-rides in a calculated way. They almost always give their full endowment to the public fund and rarely choose zero, suggesting a strong cooperative baseline rather than opportunistic instability. Their deviations to zero are infrequent and appear reactive: they occur after having themselves free-rode and been punished, or when others’ contributions slip, indicating conditional cooperation moderated by self-interest and sensitivity to group norms. They appear to hold a fairness norm that “everyone should contribute the full amount.” Their own pattern of near-consistent full contributions, combined with targeted punishment of low contributors, supports this. Risk tolerance is moderate: they are willing to incur personal costs through contribution and punishment to support the cooperative norm, but they do occasionally exploit the group by contributing nothing when it seems safe or when others’ behavior gives cover. Overall, they act as a norm-enforcing cooperator with a small, controlled opportunistic streak rather than a pure altruist or pure free-rider.

The player is a strict, stable full contributor. They always give their entire endowment to the public fund and never deviate, regardless of others’ behavior, aggregate contribution levels, or remaining rounds. Contribution is not conditional on observed defection or prior punishment activity. This indicates a strong internal cooperation norm rather than a reactive or payoff-maximizing calculus. There is no evidence of experimentation, risk hedging, or strategic under-contribution; the player appears committed to full cooperation as a default rule. Attitude toward fairness seems focused on enforcing that everyone also contributes fully, but their own giving does not depend on whether that fairness is realized.

This player is a strict, unconditional full cooperator. They always contribute the entire endowment whenever given a binary choice between full contribution and zero, and this does not change in response to others’ behavior. They continue to cooperate even when several others contribute nothing, and even when group contribution levels fluctuate, indicating that their strategy is not contingent on short‑term reciprocity or matching. They appear to follow a strong efficiency/fairness norm: they act as if the socially optimal action is to fully fund the public good, and they adhere to that norm regardless of exploitation risk. There is no evidence of risk‑adjustment or strategic hedging; they do not reduce contributions to protect themselves when free‑riding becomes more common. Their behavior is highly stable and rule‑like across the entire game, suggesting an internalized cooperative rule or identity (“I am a contributor”) rather than a responsive, payoff‑maximizing strategy. Their tolerance for being exploited seems high; they accept being paired with repeat free‑riders without changing course.

This player is a strict, unconditional full contributor. They always contribute the maximum allowed and never deviate, regardless of others’ behavior, fluctuations in group contribution levels, or stage of the game. Their cooperation is highly stable and shows no volatility or experimentation. They appear to operate on a strong fairness or duty norm: contributing fully is treated as the correct baseline, not a contingent strategy. They do not free‑ride even when several others systematically contribute nothing. Risk tolerance regarding exploitation is high; they accept being paired with persistent defectors rather than reducing their own contribution. They seem more motivated by maintaining a cooperative standard (and then using punishment to address deviations) than by maximizing their own payoff in a narrow sense.

This player is a near-unconditional high contributor with one early defection. They almost always give the full endowment and show no systematic conditional adjustment to the group’s average: they continue to contribute fully even when others free-ride or the group total drops. The single zero contribution appears as an isolated deviation rather than the start of a new pattern; afterwards, they immediately revert to full contributions and maintain them through to the end. They clearly understand that cooperation maximizes income and behave in line with an efficiency norm rather than a strict reciprocity norm. Their behavior suggests low sensitivity to short-term exploitation and a willingness to tolerate free-riding if the group outcome remains high. They display low contribution volatility, high internal consistency, and a long-run payoff-maximizing mindset anchored in full cooperation.

This player is a profit‑oriented conditional cooperator with moderate, sometimes strong, free‑riding tendencies. They clearly understand that contributing less while others contribute more increases their own payoff, and they repeatedly exploit that gap. They begin by fully cooperating, matching the group’s “contribute everything” norm. Very soon, however, they start lowering contributions even while the group is still strongly committed to high cooperation. Their pattern after the start is mixed and opportunistic: they frequently choose contributions below the emerging group norm, especially when others are reliably high. At several points they give low or very low amounts while most others give close to the maximum, which raises their own payoff above the rest of the group. This indicates deliberate strategic free‑riding rather than confusion. They are not purely selfish: they periodically return to full or near‑full contributions, particularly when the group is coordinating well and average payoffs are high. They do not chase the absolute minimum (zero) despite that being individually profitable when others give a lot; instead, they often choose intermediate under‑contributions (e.g., half or three‑quarters of the endowment). This suggests they balance self‑interest with a minimal desire to avoid being the most exploitative player. Overall stability is low: their contributions fluctuate widely across the game, with no clear monotonic trend toward lower or higher giving. Changes do not track any clear public discussion cue; they under‑contribute both when others urge cooperation and when they are quiet. They appear mostly insensitive to fairness norms voiced in chat; their choices show little concern for matching others’ contributions or maximizing group welfare. They tolerate significant inequality in their favor and seem comfortable earning more than the cooperators who are keeping the pot high. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they do not test extremes aggressively, but they repeatedly “skim” surplus value by giving less than the group while relying on others to maintain cooperation. They seem to expect that the majority will keep contributing heavily even if a few players, including themselves, shave off contributions.

This player is a strict unconditional full cooperator in this environment. They always contribute the maximum possible amount and never deviate, even as others free-ride or lower their contributions and even when average payoffs drop. Their behavior is extremely stable and not reactive to short-term exploitation; they never attempt to “punish” low contributors by lowering their own contribution. They frame the situation explicitly as a social dilemma but still choose the collectively efficient action every time, suggesting that maximizing group welfare (and the resulting high symmetric payoffs) is their dominant criterion, even if some players earn more by free-riding. They tolerate substantial inequality in favor of defectors without changing their own behavior, implying high cooperation resilience, low spite, and an acceptance that some “leakage” to selfish or inactive players is an acceptable cost of maintaining high group returns. Risk tolerance appears high with respect to being exploited: they repeatedly commit to full cooperation despite uncertainty about remaining rounds, the presence of potential bots, and the ongoing behavior of low contributors.

This player is a generally high contributor with selective, self-interested shading. Their default is to cooperate at or near the full endowment, but they do not commit rigidly to always giving the maximum. Most of the time they match or slightly undercut the informal “full cooperation” norm, often contributing in the high teens or full twenty. At a few points they contribute noticeably less, including one very low contribution early relative to the group’s cooperative framing. Their contribution pattern is moderately volatile rather than perfectly stable: they oscillate between full contributions and somewhat lower values rather than drifting steadily downward. The deviations do not escalate into outright free-riding; instead they seem to test how much they can keep while still benefiting from others’ high cooperation. They appear conditionally cooperative, but in a lenient, opportunistic way. They keep contributing a lot even when others start lowering or when a couple of players systematically free-ride; at the same time, they occasionally lower their own amount during periods when norms are being discussed most strongly by others. This mix suggests they value the cooperative surplus and understand that high group contributions are profitable, yet they are also attuned to the individual payoff incentive to “skim” a bit by contributing slightly less than the most vocal cooperators. They do not show explicit fairness enforcement or strong equality motives in their own contribution choices. Instead, they accept the presence of clear free-riders and still keep their own contributions high, which indicates relatively high tolerance for inequality as long as overall earnings remain good. Their risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to contribute heavily even without punishment or reward instruments and even in the presence of suspected bots and chronic non-contributors, implying comfort with the risk that others may exploit them.

This player is a highly stable, unconditional high contributor. They always give the full endowment and never adjust downward, even when many others free‑ride or reduce their amounts and their own payoff is clearly lower than that of low contributors. They appear strongly committed to the “full cooperation” norm and treat it as a rule rather than a contingent strategy. There is no sign that they condition on recent averages, on specific others’ behavior, or on short‑term payoff changes; they persist with the same choice in both good and bad outcome phases. This suggests a strong fairness or efficiency norm—wanting the group outcome to be maximized—even at clear personal cost. Risk tolerance is high in the sense that they are willing to risk being exploited; at the same time, their behavior is low‑variance and predictable, indicating preference for a simple, principled strategy over responsive optimization. There is no observable attempt to strategically time defection near the end of the game, implying either that they ignore typical end‑game logic or prioritize norm-consistent behavior over strategic deviation. Their contribution pattern is consistent with a “pure cooperator / efficiency maximizer” type rather than a conditional cooperator or selfish type.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator in this environment. They always contribute their entire endowment, and this pattern does not change in response to others’ deviations, group fluctuations, or the game’s progression. Their behavior indicates that they prioritize group efficiency over individual short‑term gain and follow the payoff-maximizing logic of the high multiplier. They do not appear to condition their contribution on others’ past behavior; even when some players free‑ride or contribute less, they maintain full cooperation. This suggests a strong efficiency norm and a high tolerance for being exploited in the short run if the strategy remains collectively rational. There is no observable shift toward self-protection or retaliation, indicating low sensitivity to fairness violations in their own material payoff within the time frame observed. Risk tolerance appears high: they are willing to commit fully to cooperation even in the presence of uncertainty about others’ choices and despite clear evidence that some players occasionally free-ride. Their strategy is simple, consistent, and rule-like rather than reactive: contribute maximally because that is what yields the highest collective return.

This player is a generally high contributor who occasionally deviates downward in a strategic, payoff-improving way. They contribute the full endowment in most rounds, including both early and late in the game, which indicates a strong cooperative or efficiency-oriented baseline rather than pure self-interest. However, they are willing to reduce their contribution when the group is already contributing a lot and when free-riding is clearly profitable; these reductions are moderate rather than extreme. This pattern suggests conditional cooperation with a self-interested tilt: they support a high public fund but will take advantage of others’ generosity to some extent when it is safe to do so. Their contribution pattern is not random; the deviations occur when the group has already demonstrated high cooperation. That implies they monitor others and adapt, rather than following a fixed rule. They do not appear to enforce or insist on strict equal sacrifice; instead, they accept earning more than others when they occasionally give less. This points to relatively low inequality aversion and a tolerance for asymmetric gains as long as group efficiency remains high. Risk tolerance appears moderate to high: they are comfortable matching the group’s full-contribution norm even though any single round could be exploited, but they hedge selectively by undercontributing when they perceive space to gain.

This player is a mostly high contributor with opportunistic deviations. After initially taking everything for themselves despite strong group messaging to contribute fully, they quickly switch to joining the high-contribution norm and then generally maintain it. Their pattern suggests they understand that contributing heavily is profitable when others do the same and are willing to cooperate once that is clear. They are not strictly unconditional: at one point they contribute less than the emerging full-contribution norm while still remaining on the cooperative side relative to pure free-riding. This indicates conditional cooperation with a self-serving margin rather than strict fairness enforcement. They appear sensitive to payoff structure: once it is evident that large group contributions generate very high payoffs, they align with that strategy. Risk tolerance is moderate: they initially exploit a cooperative group to secure a high private gain, then accept the “risk” of contributing fully once cooperation appears stable. Overall, they function as a pragmatic cooperator who is willing to free-ride early and to shade contributions downward occasionally, but who, once coordination is established, usually matches the cooperative standard rather than systematically undercutting it.

The player is a stable, unconditional full contributor in this environment. They always give their entire endowment, without observable adjustment to others’ occasional deviations or to changing average contributions. Their behavior aligns with a payoff-maximizing understanding of the marginal per capita return: they appear to treat full contribution as the dominant strategy when the multiplier makes group returns highly favorable. They do not engage in strategic under-contribution even when free-riding by others is visible and profitable in the short term, suggesting a strong internal norm for efficiency and/or cooperation rather than opportunistic payoff-chasing. There is no evidence of conditional cooperation (no scaling up or down in response to others); instead, they display a fixed, rule-like contribution policy. Fairness concerns, if present, do not translate into reduced giving when others benefit from free-riding. Risk tolerance appears high: the player is willing to fully commit to the public fund even after seeing that some group members exploit cooperation, indicating comfort with relying on the group and accepting exposure to potential exploitation.

The player is a fully unconditional cooperator within this environment. They always contribute the entire endowment, show no experimentation, and do not adjust behavior in response to free-riding or partial contributions by others. Their strategy is stable and non-contingent: they do not appear to condition on group averages, on specific others’ deviations, or on recent changes in cooperation. The behavior aligns with a strong efficiency-oriented norm: they appear to prioritize maximizing the group return over personal short-term gain, even when others sometimes earn more by contributing less. There is no evidence of risk aversion in the sense of hedging contributions; they consistently accept the risk that others may free-ride. Fairness concerns, if present, do not translate into reduced contributions; they tolerate unequal payoffs without adjusting. Overall, they fit the type of a “principled efficiency cooperator” rather than a conditional or strategic cooperator.

This player is a predominantly cooperative but strategically self-interested contributor. They often choose the maximum contribution when group behavior appears cohesive and aligned with high cooperation, but they intermittently shade down their own contribution while continuing to benefit from others’ higher inputs. Their pattern suggests clear understanding that group welfare is maximized by high contributions, yet they are willing to exploit situations where others are reliably cooperating. Contribution levels fluctuate rather than following a simple upward or downward trend, indicating flexible, context-sensitive decision-making rather than a rigid rule. They do not appear purely altruistic: when the group is strongly cooperative, they sometimes lower their own contribution while still remaining within a broadly “cooperative” range, effectively free-riding in a moderate, not extreme, way. This behavior signals a fairness norm that is present but not absolute: they prefer high group efficiency, but they also want above-average personal payoffs when they can obtain them without obviously undermining the system. Their actions are consistent with a conditional cooperator who is willing to nudge toward self-interest when they perceive room to do so without triggering visible breakdown of cooperation. They show no evidence of high risk aversion in the sense of avoiding high contributions; they repeatedly choose the full endowment when it seems collectively supported. At the same time, the selective reductions show they are comfortable taking calculated social risks (quietly under-contributing) if they expect others to maintain cooperation.

This player is a generally high contributor who tends toward full or near-full cooperation when the efficiency of the public fund is clearly favorable. They accept the collective logic of maximizing the group return and usually act in line with it. Their contributions are not perfectly rigid: they occasionally reduce their input while still staying above the group average, indicating conditional cooperation with a mild self-serving adjustment rather than pure altruism or pure free-riding. They appear sensitive to what others are doing—when others largely coordinate on high contributions, they join or exceed that cooperative standard; when there is some slackening or heterogeneity, they trim their own contribution slightly but do not defect hard. This suggests a fairness norm that permits them to gain a relative advantage as long as overall cooperation stays high, rather than a strict equality norm. Their behavior is consistent with a payoff-maximizing cooperative type who understands the game’s structure and is willing to “shade” their contribution to improve their personal payoff, but not to the extent of destabilizing cooperation. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to trust in high group cooperation early and maintain it across the game, but also experiment with partial deviation in mid-game to test how much they can keep while still benefiting from others’ cooperation.

This player is a high, near-max unconditional cooperator with slight strategic shading. They consistently contribute very high amounts relative to the feasible range, often at or extremely close to the maximum. Their behavior shows strong commitment to the group-efficient strategy: they keep contributing heavily even after clear evidence that some others are free-riding or reducing their contributions. They do not appear to condition their own contributions tightly on individual defections; instead, they maintain generosity and only occasionally hold back a few coins for themselves. The small deviations from full contribution are selective rather than volatile: they reduce their contribution slightly in situations where the group is otherwise strongly cooperative, which increases their own payoff while leaving group efficiency essentially intact. This pattern suggests they understand the payoff structure and are willing to extract a modest advantage when cooperation is already secure, but they do not push this into low contributions or aggressive free-riding. Their fairness norm appears to be “everyone should contribute high or maximum,” and they personally live very close to that standard. However, they also show a mild tolerance for exploiting very cooperative environments, indicating they prioritize efficiency and high earnings for all but are not purely altruistic. Risk tolerance toward cooperation is high: they continue to invest heavily in the public fund even after seeing that some players consistently give little or nothing, suggesting they accept some exploitation risk rather than abandoning cooperation.

This player is best described as a “conditional high cooperator with intermittent experimentation and some end-game shading.” They generally treat full contribution as the default and appear intrinsically comfortable with high cooperation, even when some others under-contribute. Across the game, their modal action is to contribute the entire endowment. This pattern is stable for long stretches, indicating a baseline norm of “contribute fully unless there is a strong reason not to.” They clearly condition their behavior on the social environment and on recent experiences with punishment. After being targeted with heavy sanctions when contributing nothing or very little, they promptly return to high contributions and maintain them despite later facing renewed low contributions from others. This suggests (a) they understand that others are enforcing a high-contribution norm, and (b) they are willing to uphold that norm even when not everyone complies. They do, however, periodically reduce contributions sharply, including both a pure free-ride and several partial free-rides. These deviations are not random: they occur after they have already established a pattern of being a high contributor and after they have observed others occasionally giving less. This looks like controlled “testing” rather than a permanent shift in type—brief deviations followed by a return to high contributions. The low contributions are not systematically matched to specific others’ low contributions, so their fairness norm is imperfectly enforced on their own side: they maintain high giving even in the presence of some free-riding, but occasionally probe how much defection is tolerated or profitable. Risk-wise, they seem willing to expose themselves to social and payoff risk: they know that low contributors get punished in this environment (they directly experience this) yet still experiment again later with low or partial contributions. That willingness to re-test suggests a moderate appetite for risk and curiosity about whether enforcement is consistent over time. Toward the end of the game, they shade down contributions somewhat more often, even without clear new shocks. This is consistent with a typical “end-game” relaxation of cooperation norms and a more self-regarding calculus when future retaliation or norm-enforcement may be less relevant. Overall, they integrate both norm-following and opportunism, but the dominant side is cooperative.

The player is a strict, unwavering high contributor. They always give the full endowment and never adjust downward, even when others contribute much less and even when they are personally punished. Their contribution pattern is maximally stable and shows no conditional cooperation: contributions do not respond to changes in group average, presence of free riders, or previous punishments. This indicates a strong internal norm of full cooperation that overrides short-term self-interest. It is consistent with either (a) a rule-based “always do the socially optimal thing” mindset, or (b) a belief that full contribution is the only sensible or acceptable behavior in this environment. They tolerate substantial payoff losses without deviating from full cooperation, suggesting low sensitivity to exploitation risk and a strong commitment to the public-good norm. Fairness for them appears to mean “everyone should contribute fully,” not “I match what others do.”

This player is a conditional high contributor with increasing volatility over time. They begin by contributing substantially more than the self-maximizing minimum and generally prefer contributions at or near the group’s cooperative norm when they perceive others as doing the same. They rarely exploit clearly cooperative groups; when others are uniformly high contributors, they usually match that level or stay only modestly below it. Their behavior is explicitly conditional on peers’ contributions. When others free-ride or substantially under-contribute relative to a high-cooperation benchmark, they reduce their own contribution, sometimes sharply. They do not lock into a single stable contribution rule; instead, they adjust up or down in response to the most salient deviations from high cooperation. This produces a pattern that is initially high and relatively stable, then becomes more erratic as the game progresses and as defection and punishment become more common. They appear to hold a fairness or reciprocity norm: they want high group contributions, but they also want (approximately) equal effort. When they see someone giving much less than others, they react by both reducing their own contributions in later decisions and by using punishment. They do not appear maximally risk-averse regarding being exploited: early on, they are willing to contribute generously even while still learning about others. However, when they start to receive significant punishment themselves, their contributions decline markedly, indicating sensitivity to the social cost and a shift toward more self-protective or retaliatory behavior. Incentive-wise, they seem to trade off material payoff against norm enforcement and reciprocity. They willingly forego some earnings to support high public-good levels and to discipline perceived free-riders, but this willingness weakens once they feel targeted or treated unfairly. In late stages, their contribution choices suggest frustration or disengagement: they sometimes contribute far below the group norm despite others still contributing highly, indicating a breakdown of earlier cooperative intentions rather than a purely payoff-based optimization.
